precursor of steroid hormones, vitamin D, bile acids. - **LDL = "bad"**, **HDL = "good"** cholesterol; LDL:HDL ratio is a key cardiovascular risk metric. - **Energy yield:** Lipids **37 kJ/g** > Carbohydrates **17 kJ/g** > Proteins **17 kJ/g**. - **Hydrolysis of triglycerides** by lipase yields monoacylglycerol + free fatty acids (3 molecules). - **Hydrogenation** (industrial Ni/Pd catalyst) converts unsaturated oils to saturated fats (e.g., vanaspati from vegetable oil). - **Rancidity:** Oxidative (peroxide formation) or hydrolytic (free FA liberation) — quantified by **peroxide value** and **acid value** respectively. > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Mnemonic for Lipid Classes:** "**S**imple **C**ompound **D**erived — **S**hapes **C**ells, **D**efends" (Simple = neutral energy, Compound = membrane builders, Derived = hormones & signals). ---

topics.title: Sugars and Polysaccharides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sugars and Polysaccharides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Carbohydrates?

**Carbohydrates** are polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, or compounds that yield these on hydrolysis. They are the most abundant class of biomolecules on Earth, formed primarily by photosynthetic organisms through the reduction of atmospheric carbon dioxide.

The general empirical formula can be represented as:
$$\text{C}_n(\text{H}_2\text{O})_n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Carbohydrates are the primary substrates in nearly every biochemical engineering process — from fermentation (glucose as the carbon source) to downstream processing (starch hydrolysis, cellulose bioconversion). Mastery of their chemistry is foundational to bioreactor design, media formulation, and product recovery.

## 1.2 Classification of Carbohydrates

Carbohydrates are hierarchically classified based on the number of saccharide units:

| Class | Saccharide Units | Examples | Sweetness Index (vs Sucrose = 1) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| **Monosaccharides** | 1 | Glucose, Fructose, Galactose | 0.7 – 1.7 |
| **Disaccharides** | 2 | Sucrose, Maltose, Lactose | 0.3 – 1.0 |
| **Oligosaccharides** | 3 – 10 | Raffinose, Stachyose | < 0.3 |
| **Polysaccharides** | > 10 | Starch, Cellulose, Glycogen | Tasteless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sweetness Index** is a dimensionless relative measure. Fructose (1.7) is the sweetest natural sugar, while lactose (0.3) is barely sweet — a key reason why lactose-hydrolyzed milk tastes sweeter than regular milk.

## 1.3 Monosaccharides — The Building Blocks

### Aldoses vs Ketoses
- **Aldoses:** Contain an aldehyde group (–CHO). Example: Glucose (an aldohexose).
- **Ketoses:** Contain a ketone group (C=O). Example: Fructose (a ketohexose).

### Structural Isomerism
Monosaccharides exhibit multiple types of isomerism critical to biochemical recognition:

1. **D/L Isomerism (Enantiomers):** Determined by the configuration of the asymmetric carbon farthest from the carbonyl group. D-sugars have the –OH on the right in Fischer projection; L-sugars on the left. Naturally occurring sugars are predominantly **D-sugars**.
2. **Epimerism:** Diastereomers differing in configuration at only **one** chiral carbon. Example: Glucose and Galactose are C-4 epimers.
3. **Anomerism (α/β):** Arises when a sugar cyclizes to form a hemiacetal/hemiketal, creating a new chiral center at C-1 (aldoses) or C-2 (ketoses).

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — LEGO Blocks:** Think of monosaccharides as individual LEGO bricks, disaccharides as two bricks snapped together, and polysaccharides as entire LEGO castles. The way you snap them (which side connects) determines whether you get starch (digestible) or cellulose (indigestible to humans). The same building blocks, different connections, vastly different properties!

## 1.4 Mutarotation — A Dynamic Equilibrium

When a crystalline sugar is dissolved in water, the optical rotation changes until it reaches a constant value. This phenomenon is called **mutarotation**.

$$\alpha\text{-D-glucose} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{Open-chain form} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \beta\text{-D-glucose}$$

- α-D-glucose: $[\alpha]_D = +112°$
- β-D-glucose: $[\alpha]_D = +18.7°$
- **Equilibrium mixture:** $[\alpha]_D = +52.7°$ (approximately 36% α and 64% β at 25 °C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance of Mutarotation:** In industrial glucose syrup production, mutarotation is critical because the specific rotation affects polarimetric quantification (ICUMSA methods) used in quality control. Crystallization processes must account for the dynamic equilibrium.

## 1.5 Glycosidic Bonds — The Linkage

A **glycosidic bond** is the covalent linkage formed between the hemiacetal/hemiketal hydroxyl of one sugar and a hydroxyl group of another, with the elimination of water. This is a **condensation (dehydration) synthesis** reaction.

$$\text{Sugar}_1\text{–OH} + \text{HO–Sugar}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Sugar}_1\text{–O–Sugar}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The two critical descriptors are:
- **Linkage position:** Which carbons are involved (e.g., 1→4, 1→6)
- **Anomeric configuration:** α or β configuration at the anomeric carbon
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Monosaccharide Stereochemistry — In-Depth

### 2.1.1 Fischer Projection Rules
- Vertical lines represent bonds going **away** from the viewer (into the page).
- Horizontal lines represent bonds coming **toward** the viewer (out of the page).
- The most oxidized carbon (C=O for aldehydes, or the carbon nearest the C=O for ketones) is placed at the **top**.

### 2.1.2 The C-3, C-4, C-5 Rule
For aldohexoses, the chiral carbons are C-2, C-3, C-4, and C-5. The D/L designation depends **only on C-5**:
- **D-configuration:** –OH on C-5 is on the **right**.
- **L-configuration:** –OH on C-5 is on the **left**.

Total stereoisomers of aldohexoses = $2^{n-2}$ where $n$ = number of carbons (for hexose, $n=6$).
$$\text{Number of stereoisomers} = 2^{6-2} = 2^4 = 16 \text{ stereoisomers} \quad (8 \text{ D} + 8 \text{ L})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for D-aldohexoses (top to bottom of Fischer):** "**A**ll **A**ltruists **G**ladly **M**ake **G**um **I**n **G**allon **T**anks" → Allose, Altrose, Glucose, Mannose, Gulose, Idose, Galactose, Talose.

## 2.2 Haworth Projection & Cyclic Forms

Sugars with 5 or more carbons spontaneously cyclize via an intramolecular nucleophilic attack of a hydroxyl oxygen on the carbonyl carbon, forming a **hemiacetal (aldose)** or **hemiketal (ketose)**.

- **Pyranose:** 6-membered ring (5 C + 1 O) — e.g., glucose.
- **Furanose:** 5-membered ring (4 C + 1 O) — e.g., fructose.

### Anomeric Carbon Designation
- For aldoses: Anomeric carbon = **C-1**.
- For ketoses: Anomeric carbon = **C-2**.

In Haworth projection:
- **α-anomer:** –OH at the anomeric carbon is **below** the ring plane (trans to CH₂OH).
- **β-anomer:** –OH at the anomeric carbon is **above** the ring plane (cis to CH₂OH).

## 2.3 Disaccharides — Detailed Analysis

| Disaccharide | Monosaccharide Units | Glycosidic Linkage | Reducing? | Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Maltose** | α-D-Glc + α-D-Glc | α(1→4) | Yes | Starch hydrolysis |
| **Isomaltose** | α-D-Glc + α-D-Glc | α(1→6) | Yes | Starch branch points |
| **Cellobiose** | β-D-Glc + β-D-Glc | β(1→4) | Yes | Cellulose hydrolysis |
| **Lactose** | β-D-Gal + β-D-Glc | β(1→4) | Yes | Milk |
| **Sucrose** | α-D-Glc + β-D-Fru | α(1→2)β | **No** | Cane/Beet sugar |
| **Trehalose** | α-D-Glc + α-D-Glc | α(1→1)α | **No** | Yeast, fungi |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reducing vs Non-Reducing Rule:** A disaccharide is a **reducing sugar** if it has a **free anomeric carbon** (i.e., not involved in the glycosidic bond). Sucrose is non-reducing because **both anomeric carbons (C-1 of glucose and C-2 of fructose) are locked** in the glycosidic linkage. This is why sucrose cannot open into the aldehyde form and does not reduce Fehling's or Benedict's reagents.

## 2.4 Polysaccharides — Engineering-Relevant Structures

### 2.4.1 Starch (Storage Polysaccharide in Plants)
- **Two components:**
  - **Amylose** (~20–30%): Linear chains of α(1→4)-linked D-glucose units. Forms a helical structure. Water-soluble. Gives blue-black color with iodine.
  - **Amylopectin** (~70–80%): Branched chains with α(1→4) backbone and α(1→6) branches every 24–30 glucose units. Gives red-violet color with iodine.

### 2.4.2 Glycogen (Storage Polysaccharide in Animals)
- Structurally similar to amylopectin but **more highly branched** (branches every 8–12 glucose units).
- Stored primarily in **liver** (for blood glucose homeostasis) and **muscle** (for ATP generation during exercise).

### 2.4.3 Cellulose (Structural Polysaccharide in Plants)
- Linear chains of **β(1→4)-linked D-glucose** units.
- Chains form **intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonds**, producing rigid microfibrils with high tensile strength.
- **Humans cannot digest cellulose** because we lack the enzyme **cellulase** (β-glucosidase cleaving β(1→4) bonds). Ruminants rely on symbiotic gut microbes.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Analogy — The α/β Bond Difference:** Imagine two railway tracks. The α(1→4) linkage in starch makes a curved, helical track that digestive enzymes (amylases) can easily unclip. The β(1→4) linkage in cellulose makes a straight, tightly-zippered track that requires a special "key" (cellulase) to open. This single stereochemical difference is why wood is inedible but potatoes are!

### 2.4.4 Other Industrially Important Polysaccharides
- **Chitin:** β(1→4)-linked N-acetylglucosamine; found in crustacean shells; used in wound dressings.
- **Pectin:** α(1→4)-linked D-galacturonic acid; gelling agent in jam production.
- **Dextran:** α(1→6)-linked D-glucose with α(1→3) branches; produced by *Leuconostoc mesenteroides*; used as blood plasma volume expander.
- **Xanthan gum:** Microbial polysaccharide; used in food and oil-drilling industries as a thickener.

## 2.5 Chemical Reactions of Sugars (Reaction Toolkit)

| Reaction | Reagent | Product | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Oxidation** | Bromine water (mild) | Aldonic acid (e.g., gluconic acid) | Industrial production of gluconic acid |
| **Oxidation** | Nitric acid (strong) | Aldaric acid (e.g., glucaric acid) | Analytical chemistry |
| **Reduction** | NaBH₄ or H₂/catalyst | Sugar alcohol (e.g., sorbitol) | Artificial sweeteners, diabetic foods |
| **Osazone formation** | Phenylhydrazine (excess) | Osazone crystals | Sugar identification (crystal shape) |
| **Esterification** | Acetic anhydride | Sugar acetate (e.g., glucose pentaacetate) | Protecting group in synthesis |
| **Glycoside formation** | Alcohol + acid | Glycoside (non-reducing) | Synthesis of sugar derivatives |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Empirical formula | $\text{C}_n(\text{H}_2\text{O})_n$ | $n$ = number of carbons |
| Number of stereoisomers (aldose) | $2^{n-2}$ | $n$ = total carbons |
| Number of stereoisomers (ketose) | $2^{n-3}$ | $n$ = total carbons |
| Specific rotation | $[\alpha]_D^{25} = \dfrac{\alpha_{\text{obs}}}{l \cdot c}$ | $\alpha_{\text{obs}}$ in degrees, $l$ in dm, $c$ in g/mL |
| Glycosidic bond formation | $\Delta G \approx -16 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Condensation reaction |
| Cellulose DP (degree of polymerization) | 1,000 – 15,000 | Glucose units per chain |
| Amylose DP | 300 – 3,000 | Linear starch component |
| Amylopectin DP | 2,000 – 200,000 | Branched starch component |
| Glycogen DP | Up to 120,000 | Highly branched, animal storage |
| Mutarotation equilibrium (glucose, 25 °C) | 36% α, 64% β | Aqueous solution |
| Hydrolysis of sucrose (acid-catalyzed) | $\text{Sucrose} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \text{Glucose} + \text{Fructose}$ | Inversion of sucrose; mixture called "invert sugar" |
| Benedict's/Fehling's test | Reducing sugar + Cu$^{2+}$ $\longrightarrow$ Cu$_2$O (red ppt) | Positive for glucose, maltose, lactose; negative for sucrose |
| Degree of sweetness (relative) | $S_{\text{rel}} = S_{\text{sugar}} / S_{\text{sucrose}}$ | Dimensionless; Fructose $\approx 1.73$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Note:** When using absolute value in markdown tables, ALWAYS use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the bare pipe symbol $\vert$ — to avoid breaking the table structure.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fermentation Industry:** Glucose (from corn starch hydrolysis) is the primary feedstock for ethanol, citric acid, lactic acid, and recombinant protein production.
- **Food Industry:** Starch is enzymatically hydrolyzed to glucose syrups, maltodextrins, and high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS).
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Dextran is used as a plasma expander; chitosan (deacetylated chitin) as a drug delivery vehicle.
- **Biofuel Industry:** Cellulose is hydrolyzed to glucose (using cellulases or acid) for second-generation bioethanol production.
- **Paper & Textile Industry:** Cellulose is the principal raw material; understanding its β(1→4) structure is key to pulping and mercerization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Stereoisomers of Aldoses and Ketoses

A carbohydrate with $n$ carbons has chiral centers at every carbon **except** the carbonyl carbon and the terminal –CH₂OH carbon.

**For an aldose:**
- Total carbons = $n$
- Achiral carbons = 2 (C-1 carbonyl + C-n terminal CH₂OH)
- Number of chiral centers = $n - 2$
- Number of stereoisomers = $2^{(\text{number of chiral centers})}$

$$\text{Stereoisomers}_{\text{aldose}} = 2^{n-2}$$

**For a ketose:**
- The carbonyl is at C-2. Achiral carbons = 2 (C-2 + terminal C-n).
- Number of chiral centers = $n - 3$

$$\text{Stereoisomers}_{\text{ketose}} = 2^{n-3}$$

### Worked Example 1: Aldohexose (e.g., Glucose)
For glucose, $n = 6$ carbons.

$$\text{Stereoisomers}_{\text{glucose}} = 2^{6-2} = 2^4 = 16$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
- Chiral carbons in glucose: C-2, C-3, C-4, C-5 (i.e., 4 chiral centers)
- Total stereoisomers = $2^4 = 16$ (8 D-sugars + 8 L-sugars = 8 enantiomeric pairs)
- These 16 stereoisomers are the well-known 8 D-aldohexoses + their 8 L-mirror images

### Worked Example 2: Ketohexose (e.g., Fructose)
For fructose, $n = 6$ carbons.

$$\text{Stereoisomers}_{\text{fructose}} = 2^{6-3} = 2^3 = 8$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
- Chiral carbons in fructose: C-3, C-4, C-5 (3 chiral centers)
- Total stereoisomers = $2^3 = 8$ (4 D + 4 L)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This combinatorial explosion explains why nature's carbohydrate "library" is so vast — the same molecular formula (e.g., C₆H₁₂O₆ for hexoses) corresponds to 16 aldose + 8 ketose = **24 distinct hexose isomers**! Each is recognized by specific enzymes and transporters.

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Rotation and Mutarotation

The **specific rotation** $[\alpha]_D^{T}$ of a sugar solution is given by:

$$[\alpha]_D^{T} = \frac{\alpha_{\text{observed}}}{l \cdot c}$$

where:
- $\alpha_{\text{observed}}$ = observed rotation in degrees
- $l$ = path length of the polarimeter tube in **decimeters (dm)**
- $c$ = concentration in **g/mL**
- Subscript $D$ = sodium D-line (589.3 nm)
- Superscript $T$ = temperature in °C

### Worked Example 3: Calculating Specific Rotation
A 0.5 g/mL solution of sucrose in a 2 dm polarimeter tube gives an observed rotation of $+3.30°$. Calculate the specific rotation.

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.
$$\alpha_{\text{obs}} = +3.30°, \quad l = 2 \text{ dm}, \quad c = 0.5 \text{ g/mL}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the specific rotation formula.

$$[\alpha]_D = \frac{+3.30°}{2 \text{ dm} \times 0.5 \text{ g/mL}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator.

$$l \cdot c = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ (dm} \cdot \text{g/mL)}$$

**Step 4:** Final result.

$$[\alpha]_D = \frac{+3.30°}{1.0} = +3.30° \cdot \text{mL} \cdot \text{g}^{-1} \cdot \text{dm}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $[\alpha]_D = +66.0°$ after unit conversion (since the published value of sucrose is +66.5°; the small discrepancy is due to rounding of input data). The actual reported value is $[\alpha]_D^{20} = +66.5°$ for sucrose.

### Worked Example 4: Mutarotation Equilibrium
At equilibrium in water at 25 °C, D-glucose has an observed specific rotation of $+52.7°$. Given that the pure α-anomer has $[\alpha] = +112°$ and the pure β-anomer has $[\alpha] = +18.7°$, find the equilibrium percentages.

**Step 1:** Let the fraction of α-anomer be $x$ and β-anomer be $(1-x)$.

$$[\alpha]_{\text{equilibrium}} = x \cdot [\alpha]_{\alpha} + (1-x) \cdot [\alpha]_{\beta}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values.

$$+52.7 = x(+112) + (1-x)(+18.7)$$

**Step 3:** Expand.

$$+52.7 = 112x + 18.7 - 18.7x$$

**Step 4:** Combine like terms.

$$+52.7 = (112 - 18.7)x + 18.7$$
$$+52.7 = 93.3x + 18.7$$

**Step 5:** Isolate $x$.

$$93.3x = 52.7 - 18.7 = 34.0$$
$$x = \frac{34.0}{93.3} = 0.3644$$

**Step 6:** Convert to percentages.

$$\% \alpha = 36.4\%, \quad \% \beta = 63.6\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The β-anomer is thermodynamically more stable in aqueous solution because its –OH at C-1 is in the equatorial position (lower steric strain in chair conformation), making it ~1.6× more abundant at equilibrium.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Structural Comparison of Key Polysaccharides

| Property | Amylose | Amylopectin | Glycogen | Cellulose | Chitin |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Monomer | α-D-Glucose | α-D-Glucose | α-D-Glucose | β-D-Glucose | N-Acetylglucosamine |
| Linkage | α(1→4) | α(1→4) + α(1→6) | α(1→4) + α(1→6) | β(1→4) | β(1→4) |
| Branches | None | Every 24–30 units | Every 8–12 units | None | None |
| Solubility in water | Soluble | Insoluble (cold) | Insoluble (cold) | Insoluble | Insoluble |
| Iodine test | Deep blue | Red-violet | Red-brown | None (negative) | None (negative) |
| Digestibility (human) | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** | **No** |
| Primary function | Plant energy storage | Plant energy storage | Animal energy storage | Plant structural support | Animal structural support |
| Approx. DP | 300–3,000 | 2,000–200,000 | $\leq$ 120,000 | 1,000–15,000 | Very high |

## 3.4 Derivation: Hydrolysis Degree of Polysaccharides

For acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of a polysaccharide (e.g., starch to glucose), the reaction follows first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{d[\text{Polymer}]}{dt} = -k[\text{Polymer}]$$

Integrating from initial concentration $[\text{P}]_0$ to $[\text{P}]_t$:

$$\ln \frac{[\text{P}]_t}{[\text{P}]_0} = -kt$$

**Degree of Hydrolysis (DH)** is defined as the fraction of glycosidic bonds cleaved:

$$\text{DH} = 1 - \frac{[\text{P}]_t}{[\text{P}]_0} = 1 - e^{-kt}$$

**Half-life** ($t_{1/2}$) — time for 50% hydrolysis:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

### Worked Example 5: Industrial Starch Hydrolysis
A starch slurry is hydrolyzed with HCl at 100 °C. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.05 \text{ min}^{-1}$. Find the time required for 90% hydrolysis.

**Step 1:** Degree of hydrolysis = 0.90.

$$0.90 = 1 - e^{-kt}$$
$$e^{-kt} = 0.10$$

**Step 2:** Take natural log on both sides.

$$-kt = \ln(0.10) = -2.3026$$
$$t = \frac{2.3026}{0.05} = 46.05 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance:** This first-order kinetic model is the basis for designing **continuous starch hydrolyzers** in glucose syrup plants. A target DE (Dextrose Equivalent) of 95+ (≈95% hydrolysis) typically requires residence times of 45–60 min in jet cookers at 140–160 °C with enzyme cocktails (α-amylase + glucoamylase).

## 3.5 Derivation: Degree of Dextrose Equivalent (DE)

The **Dextrose Equivalent (DE)** is the industry-standard measure of starch hydrolysis:

$$\text{DE} = \frac{\text{Reducing sugar (as glucose)}}{\text{Total dry substance}} \times 100$$

**Relationship to Degree of Polymerization (DP):**
$$\text{DE} \approx \frac{180}{\text{DP} \times 180} \times 100 = \frac{100}{\text{DP}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{DE} \times \text{DP} \approx 100}$$

| Product | DE | DP (approx.) | Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Native starch | 0 | $\infty$ | Thickening |
| Maltodextrin | 3–20 | 30–5 | Fat replacer, filler |
| Glucose syrup | 30–50 | 2–3 | Confectionery |
| High-maltose syrup | 40–50 | 2 | Brewing |
| High-DE glucose | 55–70 | — | Bakery, ice cream |
| Dextrose monohydrate | 100 | 1 | Pharmaceutical, fermentation feedstock |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**DE** = **D**egree of **E**xposure" — higher DE means more bonds broken, smaller fragments, sweeter product.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Carbohydrates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CARBOHYDRATES] --> B[MONOSACCHARIDES - 1 unit]
    A --> C[DISACCHARIDES - 2 units]
    A --> D[OLIGOSACCHARIDES - 3 to 10 units]
    A --> E[POLYSACCHARIDES - more than 10 units]

    B --> B1[ALDOSES - aldehyde group]
    B --> B2[KETOSES - ketone group]

    B1 --> B1A[Glucose C6H12O6]
    B1 --> B1B[Galactose C6H12O6]
    B1 --> B1C[Mannose C6H12O6]
    B1 --> B1D[Ribose C5H10O5]

    B2 --> B2A[Fructose C6H12O6]
    B2 --> B2B[Ribulose C5H10O5]

    C --> C1[Maltose alpha 1 to 4 - reducing]
    C --> C2[Lactose beta 1 to 4 - reducing]
    C --> C3[Sucrose alpha 1 to 2 beta - non-reducing]
    C --> C4[Cellobiose beta 1 to 4 - reducing]

    E --> E1[HOMOPOLYSACCHARIDES - one sugar type]
    E --> E2[HETEROPOLYSACCHARIDES - multiple sugar types]

    E1 --> E1A[STARCH - alpha glucose]
    E1 --> E1A1[Amylose - linear]
    E1 --> E1A2[Amylopectin - branched]
    E1 --> E1B[GLYCOGEN - alpha glucose, highly branched]
    E1 --> E1C[CELLULOSE - beta glucose, linear]
    E1 --> E1D[CHITIN - N-acetyl glucosamine]

    E2 --> E2A[Hyaluronic acid]
    E2 --> E2B[Chondroitin sulfate]
    E2 --> E2C[Xanthan gum - microbial]
    E2 --> E2D[Pectin - galacturonic acid]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Polysaccharide Structures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph storage_polysaccharides[STORAGE POLYSACCHARIDES]
        S1[AMYLOSE] --> S1A[Linear alpha 1 to 4 chain]
        S1A --> S1B[Helical coil structure]
        S1B --> S1C[Soluble in hot water]
        S1C --> S1D[Iodine test: deep blue color]

        S2[AMYLOPECTIN] --> S2A[Branched alpha 1 to 4 and alpha 1 to 6]
        S2A --> S2B[Branches every 24 to 30 units]
        S2B --> S2C[Insoluble in cold water]
        S2C --> S2D[Iodine test: red violet color]

        S3[GLYCOGEN] --> S3A[Highly branched alpha glucose]
        S3A --> S3B[Branches every 8 to 12 units]
        S3B --> S3C[Stored in liver and muscle]
        S3C --> S3D[Rapid mobilization for glucose]
    end

    subgraph structural_polysaccharides[STRUCTURAL POLYSACCHARIDES]
        T1[CELLULOSE] --> T1A[Linear beta 1 to 4 chain]
        T1A --> T1B[Interchain hydrogen bonding]
        T1B --> T1C[Forms rigid microfibrils]
        T1C --> T1D[Major plant cell wall component]

        T2[CHITIN] --> T2A[Beta 1 to 4 N-acetylglucosamine]
        T2A --> T2B[Similar to cellulose but with NHAc groups]
        T2B --> T2C[Exoskeletons of arthropods]
        T2C --> T2D[Second most abundant biopolymer]
    end

    subgraph industrial_polysaccharides[INDUSTRIAL / MICROBIAL POLYSACCHARIDES]
        U1[DEXTRAN] --> U1A[Alpha 1 to 6 with alpha 1 to 3 branches]
        U1A --> U1B[Produced by Leuconostoc mesenteroides]
        U1B --> U1C[Used as blood plasma expander]

        U2[XANTHAN GUM] --> U2A[Produced by Xanthomonas campestris]
        U2A --> U2B[Thickener in food and oil drilling]
        U2B --> U2C[Stable over wide pH and temperature]
    end

    storage_polysaccharides --> shared_monomer1[Common Monomer: ALPHA D Glucose]
    structural_polysaccharides --> shared_monomer2[Common Feature: BETA linkages for rigidity]
    industrial_polysaccharides --> shared_monomer3[Common Feature: Microbial synthesis route]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Mutarotation Dynamic Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pure alpha D Glucose<br>Crystal form] -->|Dissolve in water| P2[Open chain form<br>trace amount]
    P2 -->|Intramolecular cyclization| P3[Beta D Glucose]
    P3 -->|Open via open chain| P2
    P2 -->|Ring closure| P1

    P1 -.->|Observed rotation| R1[+112 degrees]
    P3 -.->|Observed rotation| R2[+18.7 degrees]
    R1 --> E[Equilibrium mixture<br>+52.7 degrees]
    R2 --> E
    E --> P4[36 percent alpha<br>64 percent beta]

    style P1 fill:#FFD580
    style P3 fill:#A8D5BA
    style E fill:#FFA8A8
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Disaccharide Linkage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[DISACCHARIDE FORMATION] --> D2[Two monosaccharides approach]
    D2 --> D3{Anomeric carbons involved?}

    D3 -->|Both anomeric carbons<br>e.g. Sucrose| D4[NON-REDUCING<br>No free aldehyde<br>Cannot open to aldehyde form]
    D3 -->|One anomeric carbon free<br>e.g. Maltose, Lactose| D5[REDUCING<br>Free anomeric carbon<br>Opens to aldehyde form]

    D4 --> D4A[Sucrose: alpha 1 to 2 beta linkage]
    D4 --> D4B[Trehalose: alpha 1 to 1 alpha linkage]

    D5 --> D5A[Maltose: alpha 1 to 4 reducing]
    D5 --> D5B[Cellobiose: beta 1 to 4 reducing]
    D5 --> D5C[Lactose: beta 1 to 4 reducing]

    D5 --> D6[Gives positive<br>Benedict test<br>Red Cu2O precipitate]
    D4 --> D7[Gives negative<br>Benedict test]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In all Mermaid diagrams above, focus on the **linkage type (α vs β) and position (1→4, 1→6, 1→1, 1→2)** — these two descriptors alone determine ~90% of the biochemical, physical, and nutritional properties of any carbohydrate.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define mutarotation. Explain the mutarotation of D-glucose with the structures of α and β anomers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Mutarotation is the phenomenon of change in specific rotation of an optically active sugar with time, until it reaches a constant equilibrium value, when dissolved in water.

**Mechanism (1 Mark):** When crystalline D-glucose (a specific anomer) is dissolved in water, it partially opens to the linear aldehyde form via the breaking of the C-1–O bond. The open-chain form then re-cyclizes to form the other anomer. The α and β forms interconvert through this open-chain intermediate.

**Equilibrium (1 Mark):** At equilibrium (25 °C), the mixture contains approximately 36% α-D-glucose ($[\alpha] = +112°$) and 64% β-D-glucose ($[\alpha] = +18.7°$), giving a constant specific rotation of $[\alpha]_D = +52.7°$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between reducing and non-reducing sugars with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Reducing Sugars (1.5 Marks):** Sugars that have a **free anomeric carbon** (i.e., free aldehyde or potential aldehyde group) and can reduce Fehling's / Benedict's / Tollens' reagents. **Examples:** Glucose, Maltose, Lactose, Fructose.

**Non-Reducing Sugars (1.5 Marks):** Sugars in which **both anomeric carbons are involved in the glycosidic bond**, so the ring cannot open to a free aldehyde form. They do NOT reduce Fehling's / Benedict's reagents. **Examples:** Sucrose, Trehalose.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often write "Fructose is a non-reducing sugar because it is a ketose." This is WRONG. Fructose IS a reducing sugar because, in alkaline solution, it can isomerize to glucose via the Lobry de Bruyn-van Ekenstein transformation, exposing a free aldehyde group. The correct rule is based on **free anomeric carbon**, not on functional group type.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**a) Classify carbohydrates with suitable examples. Explain the structure of glucose in both open-chain (Fischer) and cyclic (Haworth) forms. (7 Marks)**

**b) Describe the structure, properties, and biochemical significance of starch. Differentiate between amylose and amylopectin. (7 Marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Classification of Carbohydrates (3 Marks):**

Carbohydrates are classified based on the number of saccharide units produced on hydrolysis:

- **Monosaccharides** (single unit, cannot be hydrolyzed further): e.g., Glucose, Fructose, Galactose, Ribose.
- **Disaccharides** (two units): e.g., Sucrose, Maltose, Lactose.
- **Oligosaccharides** (3–10 units): e.g., Raffinose, Stachyose.
- **Polysaccharides** (more than 10 units): e.g., Starch, Cellulose, Glycogen.

**2. Open-Chain (Fischer) Structure of D-Glucose (2 Marks):**

D-Glucose is an aldohexose with the formula C₆H₁₂O₆. In the Fischer projection:
- C-1: Aldehyde group (–CHO) at the top.
- C-2, C-3, C-4, C-5: Four chiral carbons with –OH groups.
- C-6: Terminal –CH₂OH at the bottom.
- D-configuration: –OH on C-5 is on the right.

The arrangement of –OH groups (left/right) at C-2, C-3, C-4, C-5 in D-glucose is: Right, Left, Right, Right (R, L, R, R).

**3. Cyclic (Haworth) Structure (2 Marks):**

When D-glucose is dissolved in water, the C-5 hydroxyl attacks the C-1 aldehyde, forming a six-membered **pyranose ring** (5 C + 1 O). This creates a new chiral center at C-1 (the anomeric carbon):
- **α-D-Glucopyranose:** –OH at C-1 is below the ring plane.
- **β-D-Glucopyranose:** –OH at C-1 is above the ring plane.

In solution, the two anomers interconvert via the open-chain form (mutarotation).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Structure of Starch (3 Marks):**

Starch is the **principal storage polysaccharide** of plants, composed exclusively of D-glucose units. It consists of two components:
- **Amylose (~20–30%):** A linear polymer of α-D-glucose units linked by **α(1→4) glycosidic bonds**. The chain adopts a left-handed helical structure with 6 glucose residues per turn.
- **Amylopectin (~70–80%):** A highly branched polymer with **α(1→4) linkages** in the linear backbone and **α(1→6) linkages** at branch points (every 24–30 glucose units).

**2. Properties of Starch (2 Marks):**
- White, odorless, tasteless powder; insoluble in cold water; forms a colloidal suspension in hot water (gelatinization).
- Gives a **blue-black color with iodine** (amylose component) due to the iodine molecules fitting inside the helical amylose coils.
- Hydrolyzed by **α-amylase** (in saliva and pancreas) and by acids.

**3. Differentiation Table — Amylose vs Amylopectin (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Amylose | Amylopectin |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linkage type | Only α(1→4) | α(1→4) + α(1→6) |
| Structure | Linear, unbranched | Highly branched |
| Solubility in water | Soluble (forms hydrated colloid) | Insoluble |
| Iodine test | Deep blue | Red-violet |
| Proportion in starch | 20–30% | 70–80% |
| Degree of polymerization | 300–3,000 | 2,000–200,000 |

**4. Biochemical Significance:**
- Major dietary carbohydrate for humans; broken down to glucose for energy.
- Industrial raw material for glucose syrup, HFCS, ethanol, and biodegradable plastics.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**a) Explain glycosidic bond formation. Describe the structure and properties of three industrially important disaccharides. (7 Marks)**

**b) Discuss the structure, properties, and biological roles of glycogen and cellulose. Why can humans digest starch but not cellulose? (7 Marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Glycosidic Bond Formation (2 Marks):**

A **glycosidic bond** is formed by the condensation of the hemiacetal/hemiketal hydroxyl group (at the anomeric carbon) of one monosaccharide with a hydroxyl group of another monosaccharide (or alcohol), releasing a molecule of water. The reaction is catalyzed by enzymes (glycosyltransferases) in vivo and by acids in vitro.

**2. Sucrose (2 Marks):**
- **Composition:** α-D-Glucose + β-D-Fructose.
- **Linkage:** α(1→2)β — both anomeric carbons (C-1 of glucose and C-2 of fructose) are involved.
- **Properties:** Non-reducing sugar; highly soluble; sweetest common sugar (1.0 reference); hydrolyzed to invert sugar (glucose + fructose) by invertase or acid; $[\alpha]_D = +66.5°$ inverts to $-39.9°$ on hydrolysis.
- **Source:** Sugarcane, sugar beet.

**3. Maltose (1.5 Marks):**
- **Composition:** Two α-D-Glucose units.
- **Linkage:** α(1→4).
- **Properties:** Reducing sugar; obtained from starch hydrolysis; unit of amylose and amylopectin.

**4. Lactose (1.5 Marks):**
- **Composition:** β-D-Galactose + β-D-Glucose.
- **Linkage:** β(1→4).
- **Properties:** Reducing sugar; "milk sugar"; hydrolyzed by lactase; lactose intolerance is caused by deficiency of this enzyme.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Structure of Glycogen (2 Marks):**

Glycogen is the **storage polysaccharide of animals**, found primarily in the **liver** (≈10% of liver mass) and **muscle** (≈1–2% of muscle mass). It has the same basic structure as amylopectin — α-D-glucose units linked by **α(1→4) bonds** with **α(1→6) branches** — but is **much more highly branched** (branches every 8–12 units, vs. 24–30 in amylopectin). This dense branching provides numerous non-reducing ends, enabling **rapid mobilization of glucose** during metabolic demand (e.g., exercise, fasting).

**2. Structure of Cellulose (2 Marks):**

Cellulose is the most abundant organic polymer on Earth, serving as the **principal structural component of plant cell walls**. It is a linear polymer of **β-D-glucose units linked by β(1→4) glycosidic bonds**. Adjacent glucose units are **flipped 180°** relative to each other, producing a flat, ribbon-like chain. These chains form extensive **intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonds**, creating rigid, insoluble **microfibrils** of high tensile strength.

**3. Biological Roles (1 Mark):**
- **Glycogen:** Short-term energy storage in animals; maintains blood glucose homeostasis (liver glycogen) and provides ATP for muscle contraction (muscle glycogen).
- **Cellulose:** Provides mechanical strength to plant cell walls; dietary fiber in human nutrition.

**4. Why Humans Digest Starch but Not Cellulose (2 Marks):**

Humans produce **α-amylase** in saliva and pancreatic juice, which cleaves **α(1→4) glycosidic bonds** in starch. However, we **do not produce cellulase**, the enzyme that cleaves **β(1→4) bonds** in cellulose. Because cellulase is absent in the human digestive tract, cellulose passes through undigested, serving as **dietary fiber** that aids bowel movement. Animals like cows and goats that digest cellulose (e.g., grass) do so because their **rumen** hosts symbiotic microorganisms (*Ruminococcus*, *Fibrobacter*) that secrete cellulase. Thus, the **single stereochemical difference (α vs β) at the anomeric carbon** dictates the digestibility of the entire polymer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mutarotation vs Racemization:** Do NOT confuse mutarotation (interconversion of α and β anomers via open-chain form) with racemization (interconversion of D and L enantiomers). These are different processes.
> 2. **Anomeric Carbon Location:** For aldoses, anomeric carbon is C-1; for ketoses, it is C-2. Students frequently misidentify this.
> 3. **Reducing Sugar Test Logic:** A sugar is non-reducing only when **BOTH** anomeric carbons are locked in the glycosidic bond. Trehalose (α,α-1,1) and sucrose (α,β-1,2) are the classic non-reducing disaccharides.
> 4. **Sucrose vs Fructose Sweetness:** Sucrose's sweetness index is 1.0 (reference), but on hydrolysis to invert sugar (50:50 glucose + fructose), the sweetness is preserved because fructose (1.73) compensates for glucose (0.7). This is why HFCS is used as a cheaper sweetener.
> 5. **"Dextrose Equivalent" Trap:** DE is NOT a percentage of glucose; it is a measure of **reducing power per unit mass** of dry substance. Pure glucose has DE = 100 (by definition), not because it is 100% pure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Sugars and Polysaccharides**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **Carbohydrates:** Polyhydroxy aldehydes/ketones; empirical formula Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ.
- **Monosaccharide:** Simplest unit; cannot be hydrolyzed (glucose, fructose).
- **Glycosidic bond:** C–O–C linkage between two sugars with loss of water.
- **Anomeric carbon:** The newly created chiral C after cyclization (C-1 for aldoses, C-2 for ketoses).
- **Mutarotation:** Change in specific rotation until equilibrium; α-D-glucose (36%) ⇌ β-D-glucose (64%) via open-chain form.

### ✅ Key Classification Rules
- **Number of stereoisomers:** Aldose = $2^{n-2}$; Ketose = $2^{n-3}$.
- **D vs L:** Determined by C-5 configuration (aldoses) or C-5 (hexoketoses) — –OH on right = D.
- **α vs β anomer:** –OH at anomeric C below (α) or above (β) ring in Haworth.
- **Epimers:** Differ in configuration at **one** chiral C (e.g., Glucose vs Galactose = C-4 epimers).

### ✅ Disaccharides — Must Memorize
- **Sucrose:** α-D-Glc (1→2) β-D-Fru — NON-reducing.
- **Maltose:** α-D-Glc (1→4) α-D-Glc — reducing.
- **Lactose:** β-D-Gal (1→4) β-D-Glc — reducing.
- **Cellobiose:** β-D-Glc (1→4) β-D-Glc — reducing (unit of cellulose).
- **Trehalose:** α-D-Glc (1→1) α-D-Glc — NON-reducing.

### ✅ Polysaccharides — Must Memorize
- **Starch (Amylose):** α(1→4) — linear — blue with I₂.
- **Starch (Amylopectin):** α(1→4) + α(1→6) — branched every 24–30 units — violet with I₂.
- **Glycogen:** α(1→4) + α(1→6) — branched every 8–12 units — animal storage.
- **Cellulose:** β(1→4) — linear — UNDIGESTIBLE by humans (no cellulase).
- **Chitin:** β(1→4) N-acetylglucosamine — exoskeletons.
- **DE × DP ≈ 100** (Dextrose Equivalent relationship).

### ✅ Reactions — Must Know
- **Reducing sugar test:** Cu²⁺ (Benedict/Fehling) → Cu₂O (red ppt).
- **Osazone formation:** With phenylhydrazine — used for sugar identification.
- **Reduction:** Sugar → Sugar alcohol (e.g., glucose → sorbitol).
- **Mild oxidation (Br₂/H₂O):** Aldose → Aldonic acid.

### ✅ Numerical & Formula Essentials
- **Specific rotation formula:** $[\alpha]_D^T = \alpha_{\text{obs}} / (l \cdot c)$.
- **First-order hydrolysis:** $\ln([P]_t/[P]_0) = -kt$.
- **Degree of hydrolysis:** $\text{DH} = 1 - e^{-kt}$.
- **Mutarotation equilibrium for glucose:** +52.7° (36% α + 64% β).

### ✅ Engineering Connections
- Glucose syrup, HFCS, ethanol, citric acid → all from starch/glucose.
- Cellulose bioconversion → 2G bioethanol.
- Lactose hydrolysis → low-lactose dairy products.
- Mutarotation → polarimetric quality control in sugar refineries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amino Acids and Proteins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amino Acids and Proteins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Amino Acids — The Building Blocks of Life

### Formal Definition
**Amino acids** are the fundamental monomeric units of proteins. They are small organic molecules containing both an **amino group (–NH₂)** and a **carboxylic acid group (–COOH)** attached to a central **α-carbon (Cα)** atom, along with a hydrogen atom and a variable side chain known as the **R-group** (or residue). Out of the ~300 naturally occurring amino acids, only **20 standard (proteinogenic) α-amino acids** are encoded by the genetic code and incorporated into proteins during ribosomal translation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Students must master:
> (a) General structure of α-amino acids
> (b) Classification based on R-group polarity, nutrition, and metabolic fate
> (c) Zwitterionic nature & isoelectric point (pI)
> (d) Peptide bond geometry and properties
> (e) Four levels of protein structural organization
> (f) Protein denaturation and renaturation

### General Structural Formula

$$\text{H}_2\text{N} - \overset{\alpha}{\text{C}}\text{H}(\text{R}) - \text{COOH}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **α-carbon** (the chiral center, except in glycine)
- –NH₂ = **α-amino group** (basic, proton acceptor)
- –COOH = **α-carboxyl group** (acidic, proton donor)
- R = **Side chain** (defines identity, charge, polarity, and chemistry of the amino acid)
- H = **α-hydrogen**

### Conceptual Analogy — The LEGO® Brick of Life
Think of amino acids as the **LEGO® bricks** of a massive biological construction set. There are only ~20 unique brick shapes, but by snapping them together in different sequences, lengths, and 3D arrangements, biology builds everything from a spider's silk to a human enzyme. The **R-group** is like the unique "stud pattern" on each brick — some are smooth (hydrophobic), some bumpy with charged studs (acidic/basic), and some have special attachments (sulfur, hydroxyl). The **peptide bond** is the click-snap that locks two bricks together. Just as a LEGO model has a 1D parts list (primary), local patterns of bricks (secondary), the whole 3D shape (tertiary), and assembled multi-subunit models (quaternary), so does every protein.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chirality & Optical Activity**
> All 19 standard amino acids (except **glycine**) are **chiral** at the α-carbon because all four substituents (–H, –NH₂, –COOH, –R) are different. Biological proteins exclusively use the **L-isomer (S-configuration)** at the α-carbon — a striking example of life's "handedness." D-amino acids occur rarely (e.g., in bacterial cell walls and some antibiotics).

---

## 1.2 Proteins — The Functional Macromolecules

### Formal Definition
A **protein** is a high-molecular-weight biological macromolecule (typically **10,000 to >1,000,000 Da**) composed of one or more polypeptide chains of amino acid residues linked by **peptide (amide) bonds**. Proteins perform virtually every catalytic, structural, regulatory, transport, and signaling function in living systems.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Molecular Workforce
If DNA is the **library of blueprints**, then proteins are the **engineers, machines, and construction workers** that actually build and run the cell. **Enzymes** (e.g., amylase) are molecular machines; **hemoglobin** is a delivery truck for oxygen; **collagen** is the steel cable in tendons; **antibodies** are guided missiles against pathogens. Each protein's job is dictated entirely by its **3D shape**, which in turn is dictated by its amino acid sequence.

### Key Numerical Benchmarks (KTU must-know)
- **Average molecular weight of an amino acid residue** ≈ **110 Da**
- **Number of standard amino acids** = **20**
- **Peptide bond C–N bond length** ≈ **1.32 Å** (intermediate between single and double bond)
- **Average protein length** = **50–2000 residues**
- **Number of known protein folds (architectures)** ≈ **~1400 distinct fold families**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Amino Acids

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on the R-Group (Polar Character) — *Most Important for KTU*

| Class | Property of R-group | Examples (3-letter / 1-letter) | pKa of side chain | Net charge at pH 7 |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Nonpolar (Hydrophobic)** | Aliphatic or aromatic, no charge | Glycine (Gly/G), Alanine (Ala/A), Valine (Val/V), Leucine (Leu/L), Isoleucine (Ile/I), Proline (Pro/P), Phenylalanine (Phe/F), Tryptophan (Trp/W), Methionine (Met/M) | — | 0 |
| **Polar Uncharged** | Contain –OH, –SH, or amide; H-bond capable | Serine (Ser/S), Threonine (Thr/T), Cysteine (Cys/C), Tyrosine (Tyr/Y), Asparagine (Asn/N), Glutamine (Gln/Q) | ~13 (Cys), ~10 (Tyr) | 0 |
| **Positively Charged (Basic)** | R-group accepts proton at pH 7 | Lysine (Lys/K), Arginine (Arg/R), Histidine (His/H) | 10.5 (Lys), 12.5 (Arg), 6.0 (His) | +1 |
| **Negatively Charged (Acidic)** | R-group donates proton at pH 7 | Aspartate (Asp/D), Glutamate (Glu/E) | 3.9 (Asp), 4.1 (Glu) | −1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Basic AAs:** "**H**appy **L**ittle **R**abbits" → **H**is, **L**ys, **Arg**
> **Mnemonic for Acidic AAs:** Both start with **D/E** which sound like the negative number — "**D**ebt is **E**xpensive"

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Nutritional Requirement (For Humans)

| Type | Definition | Count | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Essential Amino Acids (EAAs)** | Must be obtained from diet; human body cannot synthesize | **9** | Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Thr, Lys, His* |
| **Non-Essential Amino Acids** | Synthesized by the human body | **11** | Gly, Ala, Ser, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln, Asp, Glu, Pro, Arg* |

> *His and Arg are semi-essential (conditionally essential in infants/adults under stress).

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Metabolic Fate (KTU-Favorite Topic)
- **Glucogenic** — carbon skeleton can be converted into **glucose** (via gluconeogenesis) — 14 of 20 AAs
- **Ketogenic** — carbon skeleton can be converted into **ketone bodies / acetyl-CoA** — 2 of 20 AAs (Leu, Lys)
- **Both glucogenic and ketogenic** — Ile, Phe, Trp, Tyr, Thr — 5 of 20 AAs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Purely Ketogenic** = **Leucine & Lysine only** (this is a guaranteed 3-mark question)

### 2.1.4 Special Structural Categories
- **Glycine (Gly, G)** — only achiral AA (R = H); smallest AA; highly flexible in proteins
- **Proline (Pro, P)** — the only **secondary amine**; its R-group loops back to form a **cyclic pyrrolidine ring** that introduces a **kink** in polypeptide chains
- **Cysteine (Cys, C)** — contains reactive –SH (thiol); two cysteines can oxidize to form a covalent **disulfide bridge (–S–S–)**, crucial for stabilizing extracellular proteins
- **Selenocysteine & Pyrrolysine** — the **21st and 22nd** "non-standard" proteinogenic amino acids (co-translationally inserted)

---

## 2.2 The Zwitterion & Isoelectric Point — Core Concept

### 2.2.1 Zwitterion Formation
A free amino acid in aqueous solution at physiological pH exists predominantly as a **zwitterion** (German *Zwitter* = "hybrid") — a **dipolar ion** carrying both a positive charge (–NH₃⁺) and a negative charge (–COO⁻) on the same molecule, with a **net charge of zero**.

$$\text{H}_2\text{N} - \text{C}_\alpha\text{H}(\text{R}) - \text{COOH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{acid}]{\text{base}} \text{H}_3\text{N}^+ - \text{C}_\alpha\text{H}(\text{R}) - \text{COO}^-$$

The zwitterion is the true **isoelectric species** of a free amino acid, and it is the reason amino acids are **crystalline solids with high melting points** (much higher than expected for organic molecules of similar size) — they are essentially internal salts.

### 2.2.2 Acid–Base Behavior: The pKa Values
A free amino acid has **at least two ionizable groups**, with characteristic pKa values:

$$\text{p}K_{a1} \;(\alpha\text{-COOH}) \approx 1.8 - 2.5 \quad ; \quad \text{p}K_{a2} \;(\alpha\text{-NH}_3^+) \approx 8.8 - 10.8$$

The α-COOH is the **most acidic** (lowest pKa) because the resulting carboxylate is stabilized by resonance.

### 2.2.3 Isoelectric Point (pI) — *KTU High-Yield Formula*

The **isoelectric point (pI)** is the pH at which the amino acid or protein carries **zero net electrical charge** and therefore does not migrate in an electric field (a key principle in **electrophoresis** and **ion-exchange chromatography**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU pI Formulas — Memorize These**

**For a neutral amino acid (no ionizable R-group):**

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$$

**For an acidic amino acid (R-group is acidic, e.g., Asp, Glu):**

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR}}}{2}$$

**For a basic amino acid (R-group is basic, e.g., Lys, Arg, His):**

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a2} + \text{p}K_{aR}}}{2}$$

Where:
- $K_{a1}$ = dissociation constant of α-COOH (lowest pKa)
- $K_{a2}$ = dissociation constant of α-NH₃⁺
- $K_{aR}$ = dissociation constant of the ionizable R-group

### 2.2.4 Titration Curve Interpretation
As pH increases from 0 → 14, the amino acid transitions through three regimes:
1. **pH < pKa1** → fully protonated (–COOH, –NH₃⁺), net charge **+1**
2. **pH = pI** → zwitterion dominant, net charge **0**
3. **pH > pKa2** → fully deprotonated (–COO⁻, –NH₂), net charge **−1**

> At pH < pI → AA migrates to **cathode (−)**; at pH > pI → AA migrates to **anode (+)**.

---

## 2.3 The Peptide Bond — The Backbone of Polypeptides

### 2.3.1 Formation (Condensation / Dehydration)
A **peptide bond** is an **amide (C–N) linkage** formed by a **condensation reaction** between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of another, releasing one molecule of water.

$$\text{H}_3\text{N}^+ - \text{CHR}_1 - \text{COO}^- + \text{H}_3\text{N}^+ - \text{CHR}_2 - \text{COO}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{N}^+ - \text{CHR}_1 - \text{CO} - \text{NH} - \text{CHR}_2 - \text{COO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### 2.3.2 Key Geometric & Chemical Properties

| Property | Value / Description |
|:---|:---|
| **Bond length** | 1.32 Å (intermediate between C–N single 1.47 Å and C=N double 1.27 Å) |
| **Partial double-bond character** | ~40% — restricts rotation around C–N bond |
| **Planarity** | The **six atoms** of the peptide group (Cα, C, O, N, H, Cα) are **coplanar** |
| **Trans vs. cis** | Almost always **trans** in proteins (except for proline) due to steric clash |
| **Polarity** | The peptide C=O is a strong **H-bond acceptor**; the N–H is a strong **H-bond donor** |
| **Charge at pH 7** | **Neutral** (no net charge on internal peptide bonds) |
| **Resonance structure** |  |

$$\left[ -\text{C}(\text{=O}) - \text{NH} - \right] \longleftrightarrow \left[ -\text{C}(\text{-O}^{-}) = \text{N}^{+}\text{H} - \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A peptide bond is NOT a true single bond. Its partial double-bond character is the single most important structural reason proteins can form defined secondary structures (α-helix, β-sheet).

### 2.3.3 Nomenclature
- A chain of **2 AAs** → **dipeptide**
- A chain of **3 AAs** → **tripeptide**
- A chain of **< 40–50 AAs** → **oligopeptide**
- A chain of **> 50 AAs** (or one with a defined biological function) → **polypeptide** or **protein**
- Convention: written with the **free α-amino group (N-terminus) on the left** and the **free α-carboxyl group (C-terminus) on the right**
- Residues are numbered from the N-terminus (residue 1, 2, 3, …) — directionality matters!

---

## 2.4 Hierarchical Structure of Proteins

Proteins exhibit **four levels of structural organization** (Anfinsen's dogma). Mastering these is *non-negotiable* for KTU:

### 2.4.1 Primary Structure
- The **linear sequence of amino acids** linked by peptide bonds
- Includes the location of any **disulfide (–S–S–) bonds** between cysteines
- Stabilized by **covalent peptide bonds**
- **Encoded directly by the gene's nucleotide sequence**
- Determines all higher levels of structure (Anfinsen's experiment, 1972 Nobel Prize)
- Example: Insulin — first protein sequenced (Sanger, 1955, Nobel 1958)

### 2.4.2 Secondary Structure
- Local, **regular 3D folding** of the polypeptide backbone into recurring patterns
- Stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** between peptide C=O of one residue and N–H of another
- **Two major types:**

| Type | Geometry | H-bond Pattern | Residues per turn | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **α-helix** (Pauling, 1951) | Right-handed, cylindrical | C=O of residue *i* → N–H of residue *i + 4* | **3.6 residues/turn**, pitch 5.4 Å | Keratin, myosin, tropomyosin |
| **β-pleated sheet** | Extended, zigzag strands | H-bonds between **adjacent strands** (parallel or antiparallel) | ~2 residues/strand segment | Fibroin (silk), β-keratin |

- Other elements: **β-turns** (reverse turns, 4 residues; often contain Pro and Gly) and **random coil** (no regular pattern)

### 2.4.3 Tertiary Structure
- The **overall 3D folding** of a single polypeptide chain in space
- Stabilized by **multiple weak interactions** + sometimes disulfide bonds:
  - **Hydrophobic interactions** (nonpolar R-groups cluster in interior) — major driving force
  - **Hydrogen bonds** (polar R-groups)
  - **Ionic (salt) bridges** (between –COO⁻ and –NH₃⁺ R-groups)
  - **Van der Waals** (London dispersion) forces
  - **Disulfide bonds** (covalent, between two Cys)
  - **Metal coordination** (e.g., Zn²⁺ in zinc fingers)
- Example: **Myoglobin** — first protein whose 3D structure was solved (Kendrew, 1958, Nobel 1962)

### 2.4.4 Quaternary Structure
- Arrangement of **two or more polypeptide subunits** in a multi-subunit protein
- Subunits (also called **protomers**) are held together by the **same forces** as tertiary structure (no new bond type!)
- Examples:
  - **Hemoglobin (Hb)** — tetramer: 2α + 2β chains, 4 heme groups, carries O₂
  - **DNA polymerase** — multi-subunit assembly
  - **Virus capsids** — many identical subunits in icosahedral symmetry
- **Not all proteins have quaternary structure** — monomeric proteins stop at tertiary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Domains** — independently folded compact regions within a single polypeptide chain. Each domain typically has a specific function (e.g., DNA-binding domain, kinase domain).

---

## 2.5 Protein Denaturation & Renaturation

### Definition
**Denaturation** is the disruption of the native (functional) 3D structure of a protein, leading to loss of biological activity — *without* breaking the peptide (primary) bonds.

### Agents that Cause Denaturation

| Physical Agents | Chemical Agents |
|:---|:---|
| Heat (thermal) | Strong acids / bases (pH extremes) |
| UV & ionizing radiation | Organic solvents (ethanol, acetone) |
| High pressure | Heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺) |
| Mechanical shear / agitation | Urea & guanidinium chloride (H-bond disruptors) |
| Ultrasonication | Detergents (SDS — disrupts hydrophobic interactions) |
| Freezing | Reducing agents (β-mercaptoethanol — breaks –S–S–) |

### Renaturation
In many cases, denaturation is **reversible** (e.g., ribonuclease, Anfinsen's classic experiment). When denaturing conditions are removed, the protein spontaneously refolds to its native state, proving that **primary structure dictates higher-order structure**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Denaturation = breaking of peptide bonds. **WRONG!** Peptide bonds are extremely stable; only **hydrolysis** (e.g., by strong acid at high temperature) breaks them. Denaturation breaks **non-covalent interactions** (and sometimes disulfides).

### Functional Consequences of Denaturation
- Loss of enzymatic activity
- Loss of receptor binding
- Change in solubility (often decreases; e.g., cooked egg white becomes opaque because albumin unfolds and aggregates)
- Change in physical properties (e.g., viscosity, optical rotation)

---

| Class | Description | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Simple proteins** | Yield only amino acids on hydrolysis | Albumins, globulins, glutelins, prolamines, scleroproteins |
| **Conjugated proteins** | Contain a non-protein **prosthetic group** attached | |
|    • Nucleoproteins | + nucleic acid | Chromatin, ribosomes |
|    • Glycoproteins | + carbohydrate | Antibodies, mucins |
|    • Lipoproteins | + lipid | HDL, LDL, VLDL |
|    • Phosphoproteins | + phosphate | Casein (milk) |
|    • Metalloproteins | + metal ion | Hemoglobin (Fe), chlorophyll-protein (Mg) |
|    • Chromoproteins | + pigment | Hemoglobin, cytochromes |
| **Derived proteins** | Formed by partial hydrolysis / denaturation of native proteins | Peptones, proteoses, peptides |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating the pI of Glycine

**Problem:** Glycine has pKa1 = 2.34 (α-COOH) and pKa2 = 9.60 (α-NH₃⁺). Calculate the isoelectric point (pI).

### Step 1 — Identify the type of amino acid
Glycine has an R-group of –H. It is **non-ionizable** and uncharged. → This is a **neutral amino acid**.

### Step 2 — Select the appropriate pI formula
For neutral AAs (no ionizable R-group), the formula is:

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the pKa values
$$\text{pI} = \frac{2.34 + 9.60}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Add the values
$$\text{pI} = \frac{11.94}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Divide by 2
$$\text{pI} = 5.97$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer
$$\boxed{\text{pI}_{\text{glycine}} = 5.97}$$

**Physical interpretation:** At pH 5.97, glycine exists predominantly as a zwitterion (⁺H₃N–CH₂–COO⁻) with **zero net charge**. Below pH 5.97, the molecule is net positive (⁺H₃N–CH₂–COOH); above pH 5.97, it is net negative (H₂N–CH₂–COO⁻).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Calculating the pI of Glutamate (Acidic AA)

**Problem:** Glutamic acid has the following pKa values:
- pKa1 (α-COOH) = **2.19**
- pKaR (γ-COOH of side chain) = **4.25**
- pKa2 (α-NH₃⁺) = **9.67**

Calculate pI.

### Step 1 — Classify the amino acid
Glutamate has an **acidic R-group** (extra –COOH). → Use the **acidic-AA pI formula**.

### Step 2 — Apply the correct formula
For an acidic amino acid, the pI is the **average of the two lowest pKa values** (the two acidic groups, since the side-chain –COOH is closer in pKa to α-COOH than α-NH₃⁺ is):

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR}}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$\text{pI} = \frac{2.19 + 4.25}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Add
$$\text{pI} = \frac{6.44}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Divide
$$\text{pI} = 3.22$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer
$$\boxed{\text{pI}_{\text{glu}} = 3.22}$$

**Physical interpretation:** A low pI (3.22) means glutamate is **negatively charged at physiological pH 7.4** — biologically important for its role in negatively charged protein surfaces and as a neurotransmitter.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Calculating the pI of Lysine (Basic AA)

**Problem:** Lysine has the following pKa values:
- pKa1 (α-COOH) = **2.18**
- pKa2 (α-NH₃⁺) = **8.95**
- pKaR (ε-NH₃⁺ of side chain) = **10.53**

Calculate pI.

### Step 1 — Classify
Lysine has a **basic (positively charged) R-group**. → Use the **basic-AA pI formula**.

### Step 2 — Apply the formula
The pI is the **average of the two highest pKa values** (the two amino groups, since they are the groups that retain positive charge near neutrality):

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a2} + \text{p}K_{aR}}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$\text{pI} = \frac{8.95 + 10.53}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Add
$$\text{pI} = \frac{19.48}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Divide
$$\text{pI} = 9.74$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer
$$\boxed{\text{pI}_{\text{lys}} = 9.74}$$

**Physical interpretation:** A high pI (9.74) means lysine is **positively charged at physiological pH 7.4** — critical for DNA-binding proteins (lysine-rich sequences bind the negatively charged phosphate backbone of DNA).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Estimating Molecular Weight of a Polypeptide

**Problem:** A polypeptide chain has 150 amino acid residues. Estimate its molecular weight.

### Step 1 — Recall the average MW of an amino acid residue
Average MW per residue (after loss of water) $\approx 110$ Da.

### Step 2 — Account for water
A free polypeptide of $n$ residues has $(n - 1)$ peptide bonds and therefore has lost $(n - 1)$ water molecules. The full covalent formula is:

$$M_{\text{polypeptide}} \approx (n \times 110) - (n - 1) \times 18$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the formula
$$M_{\text{polypeptide}} \approx 110n - 18n + 18 = 92n + 18$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $n = 150$
$$M_{\text{polypeptide}} \approx 92(150) + 18$$

### Step 5 — Compute
$$M_{\text{polypeptide}} \approx 13800 + 18 = 13818 \text{ Da}$$

### Step 6 — Final Answer
$$\boxed{M_{\text{polypeptide}} \approx 13.8 \text{ kDa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a quick estimate, simply use $M \approx 110n$, which gives $16{,}500$ Da. The more accurate formula above is used in mass-spectrometry-based proteomics.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Net Charge and Migration Direction

**Problem:** Amino acid X has pKa1 = 2.0, pKa2 = 9.0, pKaR = 4.0. Predict the net charge and migration direction at pH 3.0, pH 6.0, and pH 10.0.

### Step 1 — Identify the species
This is an **acidic amino acid** (pKaR = 4.0 is acidic, lower than pKa2 = 9.0).

### Step 2 — Compute pI
$$\text{pI} = \frac{2.0 + 4.0}{2} = 3.0$$

### Step 3 — Analyze at each pH using the rule: "pH < pI → +; pH > pI → −"

**At pH 3.0 (= pI):** Net charge = 0. Zwitterion. **No net migration.**

**At pH 6.0 (> pI):** Net charge = **−1**. The α-COOH and R-COOH are both deprotonated, but α-NH₃⁺ is still protonated. Molecule migrates toward the **anode (+)**.

**At pH 10.0 (> pI):** Net charge = **−2**. All carboxyls and α-amino are deprotonated. Strongly migrates toward the **anode (+)**.

### Step 4 — Tabulate

| pH | Net charge | Migration |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 3.0 | 0 | None (at pI) |
| 6.0 | −1 | Toward anode (+) |
| 10.0 | −2 | Toward anode (+) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Stoichiometry of a Tetrapeptide

**Problem:** How many peptide bonds, water molecules released, and free functional termini are present in a tetrapeptide (4 residues)?

### Step 1 — Count peptide bonds
A linear chain of $n$ residues has $(n - 1)$ peptide bonds.

$$\text{Peptide bonds} = 4 - 1 = 3$$

### Step 2 — Count water molecules released
Each peptide bond formation releases 1 H₂O:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O released} = 3$$

### Step 3 — Count free termini
A linear peptide has exactly **2 free termini** (1 α-amino N-terminus + 1 α-carboxyl C-terminus), regardless of chain length.

$$\text{Free termini} = 2$$

### Step 4 — Generalize
For an $n$-residue linear peptide:
- Peptide bonds $= n - 1$
- H₂O released $= n - 1$
- Free termini $= 2$

$$\boxed{\text{For } n = 4: \;3 \text{ peptide bonds}, \;3 \text{ H}_2\text{O}, \;2 \text{ termini}}$$
