# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms:**
(a) Zwitterion
(b) Isoelectric point (pI)
(c) Peptide bond

### Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 Marks):

**(a) Zwitterion:** A zwitterion (from German *Zwitter* meaning "hybrid") is a dipolar ion of an amino acid in which the **α-amino group is protonated (–NH₃⁺)** and the **α-carboxyl group is deprotonated (–COO⁻)** simultaneously, resulting in a molecule that carries both a positive and a negative charge with a **net charge of zero**. This is the predominant form of free amino acids in aqueous solution near neutral pH.

$$\text{H}_3\text{N}^+ - \text{C}_\alpha\text{H}(\text{R}) - \text{COO}^- \; \text{(Zwitterion)}$$

**(b) Isoelectric Point (pI):** The isoelectric point is the **specific pH** at which a given amino acid (or protein) carries **zero net electrical charge** in an electric field and therefore **does not migrate** during electrophoresis. For neutral amino acids, it is the average of the two pKa values.

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$$

**(c) Peptide Bond:** A peptide bond is a covalent **amide (–CO–NH–) linkage** formed by a condensation reaction between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of another, with the release of one water molecule. It exhibits partial double-bond character (~40%), making the six atoms of the peptide group **coplanar** and restricting rotation.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Differentiate between essential and non-essential amino acids. Give two examples of each.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | Essential Amino Acids | Non-Essential Amino Acids |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Cannot be synthesized by the human body; **must be supplied through diet** | Can be synthesized endogenously by the body in adequate amounts |
| **Number in humans** | **9** | **11** |
| **Examples** | Valine (Val), Leucine (Leu), Lysine (Lys), Tryptophan (Trp) | Glycine (Gly), Alanine (Ala), Aspartate (Asp), Glutamate (Glu) |

**Mnemonic for essentials:** "**PVT TIM HALL**" → Phe, Val, Thr, Trp, Ile, Met, His, Arg*(cond.), Leu, Lys

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — 14 Marks

**"Discuss the classification of amino acids based on the polarity of their side chains. Explain the concept of zwitterion and derive the expression for the isoelectric point of an acidic amino acid."**

**OR**

**"With suitable examples, classify the 20 standard amino acids based on the polarity and charge of their R-groups. What is the significance of this classification in determining protein structure?"**

#### Sub-Part (a) — Classification of Amino Acids based on R-group Polarity — 7 Marks

The 20 standard amino acids are classified into **four major groups** according to the polarity and charge of their side chains (R-groups) at physiological pH (7.4):

**(i) Nonpolar (Hydrophobic) Amino Acids — 9 members:**
These have aliphatic or aromatic side chains that are **uncharged and water-insoluble**. They tend to cluster in the **interior** of globular proteins (hydrophobic core), driving protein folding.

| Amino Acid | Side Chain Feature |
|:---|:---|
| Glycine (Gly, G) | R = H (smallest, achiral) |
| Alanine (Ala, A) | R = –CH₃ |
| Valine (Val, V) | Branched –CH(CH₃)₂ |
| Leucine (Leu, L) | Branched –CH₂CH(CH₃)₂ |
| Isoleucine (Ile, I) | Branched chiral –CH(CH₃)CH₂CH₃ |
| Methionine (Met, M) | Contains sulfur (thioether) |
| Proline (Pro, P) | Cyclic (pyrrolidine), secondary amine |
| Phenylalanine (Phe, F) | Aromatic benzyl |
| Tryptophan (Trp, W) | Bulky bicyclic indole |

**(ii) Polar Uncharged Amino Acids — 6 members:**
Contain polar functional groups (–OH, –SH, –CONH₂) capable of **H-bonding with water**, but carry **no net charge** at pH 7.

| Amino Acid | Polar Group |
|:---|:---|
| Serine (Ser, S) | –CH₂OH |
| Threonine (Thr, T) | –CH(OH)CH₃ |
| Cysteine (Cys, C) | –CH₂SH (thiol) |
| Tyrosine (Tyr, Y) | Phenolic –OH |
| Asparagine (Asn, N) | –CH₂CONH₂ |
| Glutamine (Gln, Q) | –CH₂CH₂CONH₂ |

**(iii) Positively Charged (Basic) Amino Acids — 3 members:**
R-group carries a **positive charge at pH 7** because the side-chain pKa is well above 7.

| Amino Acid | Side-Chain pKa | Charged Group |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Lysine (Lys, K) | 10.5 | –(CH₂)₄NH₃⁺ |
| Arginine (Arg, R) | 12.5 | Guanidinium |
| Histidine (His, H) | 6.0 | Imidazolium |

**(iv) Negatively Charged (Acidic) Amino Acids — 2 members:**
R-group carries a **negative charge at pH 7**.

| Amino Acid | Side-Chain pKa | Charged Group |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Aspartate (Asp, D) | 3.9 | –CH₂COO⁻ |
| Glutamate (Glu, E) | 4.1 | –CH₂CH₂COO⁻ |

**Significance for Protein Structure:**
- Hydrophobic residues → **buried interior** → stabilize folding
- Charged residues → **surface-exposed** → interact with water and other polar molecules
- Cysteine → form **disulfide bridges** → covalent stabilization
- Proline & Glycine → **helix breakers** → introduce kinks and turns
- This classification directly determines the **tertiary and quaternary** arrangement of every protein.

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Zwitterion Concept and Derivation of pI for Acidic Amino Acid — 7 Marks

**Zwitterion Concept:**

A free amino acid in aqueous solution does not exist predominantly in the uncharged form H₂N–CHR–COOH. Instead, the **acidic α-carboxyl group transfers its proton to the basic α-amino group**, producing a **dipolar ion (zwitterion)**:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N} - \text{C}_\alpha\text{H}(\text{R}) - \text{COOH} \;\xrightleftharpoons{\text{intramolecular proton transfer}}\; \text{H}_3\text{N}^+ - \text{C}_\alpha\text{H}(\text{R}) - \text{COO}^-$$

Evidence for the zwitterion form:
1. **High melting point** (200–300 °C) — typical of ionic salts, not organic molecules
2. **High water solubility** — behaves like a salt
3. **Low solubility in nonpolar solvents** (ether, chloroform)
4. **Large dipole moment**
5. **Spectroscopic evidence** — IR shows both –NH₃⁺ and –COO⁻ signatures

**Derivation of pI for an Acidic Amino Acid (e.g., Glutamate):**

An acidic amino acid has **three ionizable groups** with pKa values ordered as:
$$\text{p}K_{a1} \;(\alpha\text{-COOH}) < \text{p}K_{aR} \;(\text{side-chain –COOH}) < \text{p}K_{a2} \;(\alpha\text{-NH}_3^+)$$

As pH increases from 0 to 14, the species transitions through **four microstates**:

| pH range | Net charge | Species |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| pH < pKa1 | +1 | Fully protonated (COOH, COOH, NH₃⁺) |
| pKa1 < pH < pKaR | 0 | α-COOH ionized, side-chain COOH not yet |
| pKaR < pH < pKa2 | −1 | Both COOH groups ionized |
| pH > pKa2 | −2 | All groups ionized |

The **zwitterion (zero net charge)** exists in **two adjacent pH ranges** (0 and −1). The boundary between them, where the concentrations of the two zero-charge species are equal, defines the isoelectric point:

$$\text{pI} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR}}}{2}$$

This is because the zwitterion is the **averaged form** of the species bracketed by the two lowest pKa values (the two acidic groups). For glutamate (pKa1 = 2.19, pKaR = 4.25, pKa2 = 9.67):

$$\text{pI} = \frac{2.19 + 4.25}{2} = \frac{6.44}{2} = 3.22$$

**Verification (Henderson–Hasselbalch at pH = pI):**
At pH = 3.22, equidistant from pKa1 (2.19) and pKaR (4.25), the average fractional deprotonation of the two –COOH groups is exactly 0.5, so one is protonated and one is deprotonated, giving a net charge of **+1 − 1 − 1 + 1 = 0** on the overall zwitterion.

$$\boxed{\text{pI}_{\text{acidic AA}} = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR}}}{2}}$$

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**"Describe the four levels of protein structure with suitable examples. What are the forces that stabilize each level? Explain protein denaturation and the conditions under which it is reversible."**

#### Sub-Part (a) — Four Levels of Protein Structure — 7 Marks

**(i) Primary Structure:**
- The **linear, covalent sequence** of amino acids linked by peptide bonds
- Includes the position of any **disulfide (–S–S–) bonds**
- **Stabilizing force:** Covalent peptide bonds (and disulfide bridges)
- **Encoded by:** the gene's nucleotide sequence via the genetic code
- **Example:** Bovine insulin (Sanger, 1955) — 51 residues in two chains (A-chain = 21 AA, B-chain = 30 AA) joined by 2 disulfide bonds
- **Importance:** Determines all higher levels; **Anfinsen's experiment** (1972 Nobel) showed that ribonuclease spontaneously refolds to its native state after denaturation → **primary structure dictates 3D structure**

**(ii) Secondary Structure:**
- Local, **regular patterns of backbone folding** stabilized by H-bonds between peptide groups
- **Stabilizing force:** Hydrogen bonds between backbone C=O of residue *i* and N–H of residue *i + n*
- **Two principal types:**

| Type | Geometry | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **α-helix** | Right-handed; 3.6 residues/turn; pitch 5.4 Å; H-bond between C=O of residue *i* and N–H of residue *i + 4* | α-keratin (hair, wool), myosin (muscle), tropomyosin |
| **β-pleated sheet** | Extended strands; inter-strand H-bonds; **parallel** or **antiparallel** orientation | Fibroin (silk), β-keratin, many globular protein cores |

- **β-turns** (4 residues; often Pro-Gly) reverse chain direction
- **Random coil** denotes regions with no regular pattern

**(iii) Tertiary Structure:**
- The **overall 3D folding** of a single polypeptide chain into a compact, functional shape
- **Stabilizing forces** (all weak + sometimes covalent):
  1. **Hydrophobic interactions** — the major driving force; nonpolar R-groups cluster inside, away from water
  2. **Hydrogen bonds** — between polar R-groups
  3. **Ionic (salt) bridges** — between R-COO⁻ and R-NH₃⁺
  4. **Van der Waals** (London dispersion) forces
  5. **Disulfide (–S–S–) bonds** — covalent, between two Cys residues
  6. **Metal coordination** — e.g., Zn²⁺ in zinc-finger proteins
- **Example:** **Myoglobin** (Kendrew, 1958, Nobel 1962) — 153 residues, single polypeptide + 1 heme group; the first protein whose 3D structure was solved by X-ray crystallography

**(iv) Quaternary Structure:**
- The **assembly of two or more folded polypeptide subunits** (protomers) into a functional multi-subunit complex
- **Stabilizing forces:** Same as tertiary (no new type of bond)
- **Examples:**
  - **Hemoglobin (Hb):** tetramer (2α + 2β) with 4 heme groups; cooperatively binds O₂
  - **DNA polymerase:** multi-subunit holoenzyme
  - **Virus capsids:** icosahedral arrays of many identical coat-protein subunits
  - **Immunoglobulin G (IgG):** 2 heavy + 2 light chains, 4 subunits total
- **Not all proteins have quaternary structure** — monomeric proteins stop at tertiary

**Summary table of forces:**

| Level | Bonds |
|:---|:---|
| Primary | Covalent peptide (and disulfide) |
| Secondary | H-bonds (backbone) |
| Tertiary | Hydrophobic, H-bond, ionic, VdW, disulfide, metal |
| Quaternary | Same as tertiary |

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Protein Denaturation and Renaturation — 7 Marks

**Definition:**
Denaturation is the disruption of a protein's **native (functional) three-dimensional structure**, leading to loss of biological activity, **without** cleavage of peptide (covalent) bonds.

**Denaturing Agents:**

| Physical | Chemical |
|:---|:---|
| Heat (thermal denaturation) | Strong acids and bases (extreme pH) |
| UV radiation / ionizing radiation | Heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺, Ag⁺) — bind to –SH |
| High pressure | Organic solvents (ethanol, acetone) |
| Mechanical agitation (whipping cream, beating egg) | Urea and guanidinium chloride (H-bond disruptors) |
| Ultrasonication | Detergents like SDS (disrupt hydrophobic interactions) |
| Freezing | Reducing agents (β-mercaptoethanol, DTT) — break disulfide bonds |

**Effects of Denaturation:**
- Loss of **biological activity** (e.g., enzyme catalysis, receptor binding)
- Decreased **solubility** → aggregation and precipitation (e.g., cooked egg white)
- Increased **viscosity** and changes in **optical rotation**
- Loss of **crystallizability**
- Increased susceptibility to **proteolysis**

**Reversibility — Renaturation (Anfinsen's Experiment):**
Christian Anfinsen (1972 Nobel Prize) demonstrated that:
1. Ribonuclease A was **fully denatured** in 8 M urea (breaks H-bonds) plus β-mercaptoethanol (reduces disulfides) → random coil, no activity
2. Upon **removal of urea and reducing agent**, ribonuclease **spontaneously refolded** into its native 3D structure
3. Full enzymatic activity was **restored**, and the **4 correct disulfide bonds** reformed (out of 105 possible pairings)

**Conclusion:** All the information needed to specify the 3D structure of a protein is contained in its **primary structure** (Anfinsen's thermodynamic hypothesis).

**Conditions favoring renaturation:**
- Removal of denaturant (dialysis, dilution)
- Low protein concentration (prevents aggregation)
- Correct redox environment (oxidizing for disulfide reformation)
- Physiological pH and temperature
- Absence of interfering co-solvents

**When is denaturation IRREVERSIBLE?**
- When **covalent peptide bonds are hydrolyzed** (e.g., boiling in strong acid)
- When **irreversible aggregation** occurs (e.g., fried egg white; curdled milk)
- When **cofactors or prosthetic groups** are lost (e.g., heme loss from globin)

$$\boxed{\text{Denaturation} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{remove denaturant}]{\text{add denaturant}}\; \text{Native Protein (active)} \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; \text{Denatured Protein (inactive)}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Ways Students Lose Marks in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Writing "denaturation breaks peptide bonds"** — This is the **#1 fatal error**. Denaturation breaks *non-covalent* interactions (and sometimes disulfides), NOT peptide bonds. Peptide bonds require hydrolysis to break.
> 2. **Using the wrong pI formula** — Mixing up the neutral-AA, acidic-AA, and basic-AA formulas. Remember: pI is the average of the **two pKa values flanking the zwitterionic (zero-charge) species**.
> 3. **Forgetting that proline is a secondary amine** — Its α-amino N is part of a pyrrolidine ring, so it is technically an **imino acid**. This makes it a helix-breaker.
> 4. **Confusing L- vs. D-amino acids** — Biological proteins use **L-amino acids only** (S-configuration at α-C). D-AAs are rare (bacterial cell walls, some antibiotics).
> 5. **Listing Leu and Lys as "only ketogenic" but missing the dual ones** — Purely ketogenic = Leu + Lys only. Isoleucine, Phe, Trp, Tyr, Thr are BOTH glucogenic AND ketogenic.
> 9 essential AAs in humans = Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Thr, Lys, His (often Arg is added as conditional in infants).
> 6. **Drawing the peptide bond as freely rotatable** — It has **~40% double-bond character**; the six atoms of the peptide group are **rigid and coplanar**. Only the bonds to the α-carbons (φ, ψ angles) are rotatable.
> 7. **Omitting units (Da, Å) and pKa values** — KTU examiners reward numerical precision. Always quote pKa to 2 decimal places, MW in Da or kDa, and bond lengths in Å.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Amino Acids and Proteins"**

**Amino Acid Fundamentals:**
- ☐ **20 standard amino acids** are encoded by the genetic code
- ☐ General structure: **H₂N–CαH(R)–COOH**
- ☐ α-carbon is **chiral** in 19 of 20 (glycine is the only exception)
- ☐ Biological proteins use **L-isomers only**
- ☐ **Proline** is the only cyclic (secondary amine) imino acid
- ☐ **Glycine** is the smallest and only achiral AA
- ☐ **Cysteine** forms disulfide bridges (–S–S–) that stabilize extracellular proteins
- ☐ **Selenocysteine (21st)** and **Pyrrolysine (22nd)** are the rare "non-standard" proteinogenic AAs

**Classification (4 R-group categories):**
- ☐ **Nonpolar** — 9 AAs (Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Trp, Met) → interior of proteins
- ☐ **Polar uncharged** — 6 AAs (Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln) → H-bond with water
- ☐ **Acidic (negatively charged)** — 2 AAs (Asp, Glu), pKaR ~ 4
- ☐ **Basic (positively charged)** — 3 AAs (Lys, Arg, His), pKaR ~ 6–12.5

**Nutritional / Metabolic Classification:**
- ☐ **9 essential** AAs in humans: Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Thr, Lys, His (+ Arg as conditional)
- ☐ **Purely ketogenic**: Leu, Lys (only these 2)
- ☐ **Both glucogenic + ketogenic**: Ile, Phe, Trp, Tyr, Thr
- ☐ **Glucogenic only**: the remaining 13 AAs

**Zwitterion & pI:**
- ☐ At physiological pH, AAs exist as **zwitterions** (⁺H₃N–CHR–COO⁻)
- ☐ **pI formulas** (memorize all three):
  - Neutral AA: $\text{pI} = (\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2})/2$
  - Acidic AA: $\text{pI} = (\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{aR})/2$
  - Basic AA: $\text{pI} = (\text{p}K_{a2} + \text{p}K_{aR})/2$
- ☐ At **pH < pI** → AA is **cationic** → migrates to **cathode (−)**
- ☐ At **pH > pI** → AA is **anionic** → migrates to **anode (+)**
- ☐ At **pH = pI** → AA is zwitterion → **no net migration**

**Peptide Bond:**
- ☐ Formed by **condensation** (1 H₂O released per bond)
- ☐ Linear peptide of $n$ residues: $(n - 1)$ peptide bonds
- ☐ **Planar** with ~40% double-bond character
- ☐ Bond length = **1.32 Å**
- ☐ Almost always in **trans** configuration (except for X-Pro)
- ☐ Directionality: **N-terminus on left, C-terminus on right**

**Four Levels of Protein Structure:**
- ☐ **Primary (1°):** AA sequence + disulfide bonds → covalent peptide bonds
- ☐ **Secondary (2°):** α-helix, β-sheet, β-turns → H-bonds (backbone)
- ☐ **Tertiary (3°):** 3D fold of one chain → hydrophobic, H-bond, ionic, VdW, disulfide, metal
- ☐ **Quaternary (4°):** multiple subunits → same forces as 3° (no new bond type)
- ☐ **α-helix:** 3.6 residues/turn, pitch 5.4 Å, H-bond between *i* and *i+4*
- ☐ **β-sheet:** parallel vs antiparallel strands

**Denaturation:**
- ☐ Loss of 3D structure **without** breaking peptide bonds
- ☐ Caused by heat, pH extremes, heavy metals, urea, SDS, organic solvents
- ☐ Often **reversible** (Anfinsen's experiment, 1972 Nobel)
- ☐ 1° structure dictates all higher levels of structure

**Average MW of an AA residue ≈ 110 Da** (use this for quick protein mass estimation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protein Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Protein Structure # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Protein Structure? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > **Protein structure** refers to the three-dimensional arrangement of atoms within a protein molecule. It is organized into four hierarchical levels — **primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary** — each level building upon the previous one and stabilized by a distinct set of physico-chemical interactions (covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic forces, ionic bonds, van der Waals forces, and disulfide bridges). Proteins are **biopolymers of L-α-amino acids** linked together by **peptide (amide) bonds** formed through a dehydration (condensation) reaction between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of the next. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Origami of Life" Think of a protein as a piece of **origami paper**: - The **string of letters** written on the paper is the *primary structure* (the amino acid sequence). - The way the paper naturally *folds into simple creases* (valleys and ridges) is the *secondary structure* (α-helices and β-sheets). - The fully *folded 3D shape* (a crane, a frog) is the *tertiary structure*. - If you then *assemble multiple cranes together* to make a larger sculpture, that is the *quaternary structure*. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The sequence of amino acids dictates ALL higher levels of structure. This was proven by **Christian Anfinsen's experiments** (Nobel Prize, 1972) on ribonuclease A — a foundational concept KTU examiners love to test. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance - **Average molecular weight of an amino acid residue:** ≈ **110 Da (Daltons)** - **Bond length of a peptide bond (C–N):** ≈ **1.32 Å** (intermediate between single and double bond → partial double bond character) - **Trans-configuration** is the preferred geometry of the peptide bond (except for proline). - **Number of naturally occurring standard amino acids encoded genetically:** **20** - **α-helix rise per residue:** **1.5 Å**; **3.6 residues per turn**; **pitch = 5.4 Å** ### 1.4 Hierarchy Snapshot | Level | Description | Stabilizing Force | |---|---|---| | Primary | Linear sequence of amino acids | **Covalent peptide bonds** | | Secondary | Local regular folding (α-helix, β-sheet) | **Hydrogen bonds** (backbone C=O ⋯ H–N) | | Tertiary | Overall 3D shape of single polypeptide | R-group interactions, disulfide bridges, H-bonds | | Quaternary | Assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits | Same as tertiary + subunit interface contacts | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Levels of Protein Structure — Detailed Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Primary Structure - Defined as the **linear, covalent sequence of amino acids** joined by peptide bonds. - Determined by the gene (DNA → mRNA → protein, via the genetic code). - Convention: sequence is written from **N-terminus (free α-amino group)** to **C-terminus (free α-carboxyl group)**. - Any single amino acid substitution (e.g., Glu→Val at position 6 in β-globin) can cause disease — **Sickle Cell Anaemia** is the classic KTU example. > [!NOTE] > **Peptide bond formation reaction:** > $$\text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_1\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_2\text{–COOH} \xrightarrow{\text{condensation}} \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_1\text{–CO–NH–CHR}_2\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ### 2.1.2 Secondary Structure - Local, regular conformations stabilized by **hydrogen bonds between the backbone carbonyl oxygen (C=O) of one residue and the amide hydrogen (N–H) of another**. - Two dominant motifs: **(a) α-Helix (Pauling & Corey, 1951)** - Right-handed spiral (in L-amino acids). - **3.6 amino acid residues per turn**. - Hydrogen bond: C=O of residue *i* to N–H of residue *i + 4*. - Rise per residue = **1.5 Å**; pitch = **5.4 Å**. - Side chains (R-groups) project **outward** from the helical axis. **(b) β-Pleated Sheet** - Formed when polypeptide chains (β-strands) align side-by-side. - **Parallel** (strands run N→C same direction) or **Antiparallel** (opposite directions — more stable). - Hydrogen bonds are **perpendicular** to the strand axis. - R-groups alternate above and below the plane of the sheet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Other secondary structures tested in KTU:** β-turns (reverse turns, ~4 residues, stabilized by a single H-bond; often contain **proline and glycine**), random coil (no regular pattern), 3₁₀ helix, π-helix. ### 2.1.3 Tertiary Structure - The **overall 3D conformation** of a single polypeptide chain. - R-groups that were far apart in primary sequence now interact in space. - Stabilized by: - **Hydrophobic interactions** (nonpolar R-groups cluster inside — major driving force). - **Hydrogen bonds** (polar R-groups). - **Ionic (salt) bridges** (e.g., Lys⁺ ⋯ Asp⁻). - **Disulfide bridges** (covalent S–S bonds between two cysteine residues — strongest). - **Van der Waals** forces in the packed hydrophobic core. - **Metal coordination** (e.g., Zn²⁺ in zinc fingers). ### 2.1.4 Quaternary Structure - The spatial arrangement of **multiple polypeptide subunits (chains)** in a functional protein. - Each subunit is called a **protomer** or **monomer**. - Examples: - **Haemoglobin:** tetramer (α₂β₂) — 4 subunits, MW ≈ 64,500 Da. - **Collagen:** triple helix of 3 polypeptides. - **DNA polymerase:** multi-subunit complex. - Subunits may be **identical (homomeric)** or **different (heteromeric)**. ## 2.2 Forces Stabilizing Protein Structure — Summary Table | Interaction | Strength (kJ/mol) | Bond Type | Example / Location | |---|---|---|---| | Covalent peptide bond | ~200–400 | Covalent | Primary structure | | Disulfide bridge (S–S) | ~200 | Covalent | Cys–Cys in tertiary | | Hydrogen bond | 5–30 | Non-covalent | Secondary structure, solvent | | Ionic / salt bridge | 10–30 | Non-covalent | Asp–Lys, Glu–Arg | | Hydrophobic interaction | 5–15 | Non-covalent (entropic) | Core of globular proteins | | Van der Waals | 1–5 | Non-covalent | Packed interior | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Numerical Relationships | Concept | Formula / Value | Engineering/Biochemical Utility | |---|---|---| | Average residue weight | $M_{\text{res}} \approx 110 \text{ Da}$ | Estimate protein MW from sequence length: $MW \approx 110 \times n$ | | Extinction coefficient (1 mg/mL, A₂₈₀ ≈ 1) | Empirical for Trp/Tyr | Bradford/UV quantification | | α-helix geometry | $n = 3.6$ residues/turn, $p = 5.4$ Å pitch, $d = 1.5$ Å rise | Structural modeling | | Hydrogen bond energy | $E_H \approx 5\text{–}30 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Predicting stability of mutants | | Protein folding free energy | $\Delta G_{\text{fold}} = G_{\text{folded}} - G_{\text{unfolded}}$ | Thermodynamics of denaturation | | Van't Hoff relation (folding equilibrium) | $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{RT^2}$ | Temperature dependence of stability | | Solvent accessible surface area (SASA) | $\Delta G_{\text{fold}} \approx \gamma \cdot \Delta A + \text{const.}$ | Predicting folding energy from buried area | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Convention Note:** In all formula tables, vertical bars inside math expressions (such as absolute value, determinant, or conditional probability) must be written as `\vert` or `\mid`, never as the bare pipe character `\vert x\vert`, to prevent Markdown table parser breakage. ## 2.4 Real-World Biochemical Engineering Relevance - **Downstream processing:** knowledge of quaternary structure helps in designing **chromatography** (size-exclusion separates by subunit assembly). - **Enzyme engineering:** altering primary sequence (site-directed mutagenesis) to improve thermal stability requires understanding tertiary H-bonds. - **Therapeutic biologics (mAbs, insulin):** quaternary stability dictates **formulation, storage, and shelf-life**. - **Bioseparation:** denaturation must be controlled during extraction — primary structure must remain intact. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Molecular Weight of a Protein from Its Sequence This is a **frequently tested 3-mark derivation** in KTU board exams. > **Problem:** A protein has 300 amino acid residues. Estimate its molecular weight and the number of peptide bonds. ### Step 1 — Understanding the chemistry A polypeptide of $n$ amino acid residues contains $n - 1$ peptide bonds, because each bond formation releases one water molecule: $$ \text{Amino acids} \rightarrow \text{Polypeptide} + (n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ ### Step 2 — Average residue weight accounting for water loss The average molecular weight of a free amino acid residue (in a polypeptide, after losing H₂O) is empirically **≈ 110 Da**. This already includes the water loss per residue averaged over the 20 standard amino acids. ### Step 3 — Compute total molecular weight $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{protein}} &\approx n \times M_{\text{residue}} \\ &= 300 \times 110 \text{ Da} \\ &= 33{,}000 \text{ Da} \\ &= 33 \text{ kDa} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion logic:** Each residue contributes ~110 Da on average; multiplying by the total residue count gives the total mass in Daltons. Daltons (Da) = grams per mole (g/mol), so 33,000 Da = 33 kDa. ### Step 4 — Number of peptide bonds $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{peptide}} &= n - 1 \\ &= 300 - 1 \\ &= 299 \text{ peptide bonds} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Number of water molecules released $$ N_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 299 \text{ molecules per protein} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: α-Helix Geometry Verification A standard KTU numerical — verify the pitch of an α-helix. **Given:** rise per residue $d = 1.5$ Å, residues per turn $n_t = 3.6$. **Step 1:** Define the pitch $p$ as the axial distance covered by one complete helical turn: $$ p = n_t \times d $$ **Step 2:** Substitute numerical values: $$ \begin{aligned} p &= 3.6 \times 1.5 \text{ Å} \\ &= 5.4 \text{ Å} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion logic:** Each residue advances the helix by 1.5 Å along the axis. After 3.6 residues, the helix has completed one full 360° turn, having translated 5.4 Å. **Step 3:** The number of residues in a 100 Å stretch of helix: $$ \begin{aligned} n_{100} &= \frac{100 \text{ Å}}{1.5 \text{ Å/residue}} \\ &\approx 66.7 \text{ residues} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Structural Concept in Problems | KTU Problem Statement Clue | Correct Level of Structure to Discuss | Why | |---|---|---| | "Sequence of amino acids…" | Primary | Covalent, linear | | "Hydrogen bond between backbone…" | Secondary | Backbone H-bonds only | | "Subunit assembly…" | Quaternary | Multiple polypeptide chains | | "Mutation Glu→Val…" | Primary, but discuss effect on tertiary | Sequence change alters folding | | "Disulfide bond in insulin…" | Tertiary (within chain) or quaternary (between chains) | Covalent S–S | | "β-pleated sheet of silk fibroin…" | Secondary | Regular H-bonded pattern | | "Denaturation by urea…" | Loss of tertiary (and secondary) | Disruption of non-covalent forces | ## 3.4 The Levinthal Paradox — Folding Time Estimation > **Levinthal's Paradox (1969):** A protein cannot fold by random sampling of all conformations because it would take longer than the age of the universe. **Step 1 — Number of possible conformations:** For a protein of $n$ residues, with $\sim 8$ possible $\phi/\psi$ angles each (discretized Ramachandran states): $$ \Omega \approx 8^{2n} = 8^{2n} $$ **Step 2 — Assume each conformational flip takes $\tau \approx 10^{-13}$ s (typical torsional time):** $$ T_{\text{random}} = \Omega \times \tau = 8^{2n} \times 10^{-13} \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — For a small 100-residue protein:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Omega &= 8^{200} \\ &\approx 10^{180} \\ T_{\text{random}} &\approx 10^{180} \times 10^{-13} \text{ s} \\ &\approx 10^{167} \text{ seconds} \\ &\approx 10^{160} \text{ years} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Observed folding time in nature:** $\sim 10^{-3}$ to $1$ second. > **Conversion logic:** The factor of $\sim 10^{160}$ discrepancy demonstrates that proteins fold via **directed pathways** (folding funnels, chaperone-assisted folding), not blind random search — a major concept in modern biochemical engineering for **recombinant protein production**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Protein Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Primary Structure
Linear amino acid sequence
Covalent peptide bonds] --> B[Secondary Structure
Alpha helix and Beta sheet
Backbone hydrogen bonds] B --> C[Tertiary Structure
3D folded shape of one chain
R-group interactions and S-S bonds] C --> D[Quaternary Structure
Assembly of multiple subunits
Subunit interface contacts] A -.Determined by.-> DNA[DNA Gene Sequence] D --> E[Functional Protein
e.g. Haemoglobin, Insulin, Enzymes] C -.Loss leads to.-> F[Denaturation
Loss of native structure] F -.Can be.-> G[Reversible] F -.Can be.-> H[Irreversible] ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Block-Level Architecture of α-Helix vs β-Sheet ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ALPHA[Alpha Helix Geometry] A1[Residue i
C=O group] -->|H bond to N-H of residue i+4| A2[Residue i+4] A2 --> A3[Next turn 3.6 residues] A3 --> A4[R groups point OUTWARD] A4 --> A5[Right handed spiral for L amino acids] end subgraph BETA[Beta Pleated Sheet Geometry] B1[Strand 1
N to C direction] -->|H bonds perpendicular to strand| B2[Strand 2] B2 --> B3[Parallel or Antiparallel arrangement] B3 --> B4[R groups alternate above and below] B4 --> B5[Forms pleated zigzag pattern] end ALPHA -.both are.-> SS[Secondary Structures] BETA -.both are.-> SS ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Protein Folding Flow (Anfinsen Experiment) ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Native Ribonuclease A] -->|8M Urea plus Beta mercaptoethanol| P1[Unfolded Random Coil State] P1 -->|Remove Urea and Beta ME
dialysis| P2[Refolding Begins] P2 -->|Spontaneous reformation of 4 disulfide bonds| P3[Native Active Ribonuclease A] P3 -->|Enzymatic activity restored| P4[CONCLUSION
Primary sequence determines all higher structures
Anfinsen Nobel 1972] P0 -.Reagents.-> R1[Urea disrupts H bonds] P0 -.Reagents.-> R2[Beta ME reduces S-S bonds] R1 --> P1 R2 --> P1 ``` ## 4.4 Stabilizing Forces Across Structural Levels (Mermaid Mind Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD F[Stabilizing Forces in Proteins] --> F1[Covalent Forces] F --> F2[Non Covalent Forces] F1 --> F1a[Peptide bonds
Primary structure] F1 --> F1b[Disulfide bridges S-S
Tertiary or Quaternary] F2 --> F2a[Hydrogen bonds
Secondary and Tertiary] F2 --> F2b[Ionic salt bridges
Tertiary] F2 --> F2c[Hydrophobic interactions
Tertiary core] F2 --> F2d[Van der Waals forces
Tertiary packing] F2 --> F2e[Metal coordination
Some tertiary motifs] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the primary structure of a protein. Mention the type of bond involved. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) The **primary structure** of a protein is the **linear, covalent sequence of amino acid residues** joined end-to-end, written from the N-terminal amino acid to the C-terminal amino acid. - (1 mark) The amino acids are linked by **peptide (amide) bonds** formed between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of the next, with the elimination of a water molecule (condensation reaction). - (1 mark) The primary structure is genetically determined and is **unique** for every protein; even a single amino acid substitution (e.g., in sickle cell anaemia) can drastically alter protein function. --- ### Question 2: Distinguish between α-helix and β-pleated sheet secondary structures. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) An **α-helix** is a **right-handed spiral** with **3.6 residues per turn** and a pitch of 5.4 Å. H-bonds form between the C=O of residue *i* and the N–H of residue *i + 4*, running **parallel** to the helix axis. - (1 mark) A **β-pleated sheet** is formed by aligning two or more **β-strands** side by side (parallel or antiparallel), with H-bonds running **perpendicular** to the strand axis, giving a zig-zag "pleated" appearance. - (1 mark) In α-helix, R-groups project **outward**; in β-sheets, R-groups **alternate above and below** the sheet plane. Antiparallel β-sheets are more stable than parallel ones. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A: Discuss in detail the four levels of protein structure with suitable examples and diagrams. Add a note on the forces stabilizing each level. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted to 2024 Scheme]` #### (a) Explain the primary, secondary, and tertiary structures of proteins. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Primary Structure (2 marks):** The primary structure is the linear sequence of amino acids linked by **covalent peptide bonds** (formed by condensation between –COOH and –NH₂ groups with loss of H₂O). It is genetically encoded. Example: **Insulin** has 51 amino acids in two chains (A-chain of 21, B-chain of 30) connected by disulfide bonds. The sequence is read from N-terminus to C-terminus. **Secondary Structure (2.5 marks):** Regular local folding patterns stabilized by **backbone hydrogen bonds** between C=O and N–H groups. - **α-helix:** right-handed, 3.6 residues/turn, H-bond between residue *i* and *i+4*, pitch 5.4 Å, rise 1.5 Å. Example: keratin, myosin, haemoglobin α-chains. - **β-pleated sheet:** parallel or antiparallel strands, H-bonds perpendicular to strand. Example: **silk fibroin** (antiparallel β-sheets), flavodoxin. - **β-turns:** reverse turns of 4 residues, often with proline and glycine. **Tertiary Structure (2.5 marks):** The overall 3D shape of a single polypeptide chain, stabilized by: - **Hydrophobic interactions** (R-groups cluster inside, away from water — major driving force). - **Hydrogen bonds** between polar R-groups. - **Ionic (salt) bridges** between oppositely charged R-groups (e.g., Lys⁺ ⋯ Asp⁻). - **Disulfide bridges** (covalent S–S between two cysteines) — strongest. - **Van der Waals** interactions in the tightly packed core. - Example: **myoglobin** — a globular protein with 8 α-helical segments and a heme group; the hydrophobic interior holds the heme. #### (b) What is quaternary structure? Explain with two examples. Discuss denaturation. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Quaternary Structure (3 marks):** The spatial arrangement of **two or more polypeptide subunits (protomers)** in a functional protein. Subunits may be identical (**homomeric**) or different (**heteromeric**). Stabilized by the same forces as tertiary structure, plus the subunit interface contacts. **Examples:** - **Haemoglobin (Hb):** heterotetramer α₂β₂, MW ≈ 64,500 Da, contains 4 heme groups. Exhibits **cooperative O₂ binding** (sigmoidal curve). - **Immunoglobulin G (IgG):** heterotetramer of 2 heavy + 2 light chains joined by disulfide bonds. - Other examples: DNA polymerase (multi-subunit), collagen (triple helix), viral capsids. **Denaturation (4 marks):** **Denaturation** is the disruption of native secondary, tertiary, and (sometimes) quaternary structures, with the primary structure remaining intact. - **Agents:** - **Heat:** disrupts H-bonds and hydrophobic interactions. - **pH extremes:** disrupt ionic bonds. - **Urea / Guanidinium HCl:** disrupt H-bonds, solubilize hydrophobic core. - **β-mercaptoethanol / DTT:** reduce disulfide bonds. - **Detergents (SDS):** solubilize hydrophobic regions. - **Heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺):** react with –SH groups. - **Reversible vs Irreversible:** - *Reversible* (e.g., RNase A urea denaturation) — function returns. - *Irreversible* (e.g., egg white cooking — ovalbumin) — function lost permanently. - **Biological relevance:** Protein misfolding diseases — Alzheimer's (β-amyloid plaques), Parkinson's (α-synuclein), prion diseases (PrP^Sc). --- ### Question B: What is protein denaturation? Explain the various agents causing denaturation. Discuss Anfinsen's experiment and its significance. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Adapted to 2024 Scheme]` #### (a) Describe Anfinsen's ribonuclease A experiment in detail. What conclusions were drawn? (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Background (1 mark):** In the 1950s, **Christian B. Anfinsen** studied the **bovine pancreatic ribonuclease A (RNase A)** — a 124-amino-acid enzyme with **4 disulfide bonds** and full enzymatic activity. **Experimental Steps (4 marks):** 1. **Native RNase A** was treated with **8 M urea** (denatures by disrupting H-bonds and hydrophobic interactions) AND **β-mercaptoethanol** (reduces –S–S– bonds to –SH groups). 2. The result was a **completely unfolded, inactive random coil** — disulfide bonds broken, H-bonds broken, tertiary and secondary structures lost. **Primary structure (peptide bonds) remained intact.** 3. The denaturants were then **removed by dialysis** (urea dialyses out; β-ME is washed away). 4. Spontaneously, the **polypeptide refolded** and the **4 disulfide bonds reformed in the correct pairs** (out of 105 possible pairings!), restoring **full enzymatic activity**. **Conclusions Drawn (2 marks):** - The **primary structure (amino acid sequence)** contains **all the information** required for the protein to fold into its native 3D structure. - The native state is the **thermodynamically most stable** conformation under physiological conditions. - Disulfide bonds, although covalent, form according to the dictated fold — they are not templated. - Anfinsen received the **1972 Nobel Prize in Chemistry** for this work — the **"thermodynamic hypothesis"** of protein folding. #### (b) Classify the various denaturing agents. Give one example for each. What is the difference between denaturation and coagulation? (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Classification of Denaturing Agents (5 marks):** | Category | Agent | Mechanism | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Physical | Heat | Disrupts H-bonds, increases kinetic energy | Cooking egg (ovalbumin) | | Physical | High pressure | Disrupts hydrophobic core | Pasteurization | | Physical | Radiation (UV, X-ray) | Damages aromatic R-groups, breaks bonds | Sterilization | | Chemical | Strong acids/bases (pH extremes) | Disrupt ionic bonds, protonate/deprotonate R-groups | Adding lemon juice to milk (curdles casein) | | Chemical | Organic solvents (ethanol, acetone) | Disrupt hydrophobic interactions | Alcohol precipitation of proteins | | Chemical | Urea / Guanidinium HCl | Disrupt H-bonds, solubilize hydrophobic core | RNase A denaturation | | Chemical | β-Mercaptoethanol / DTT | Reduce disulfide (S–S) bonds | Reduction in SDS-PAGE | | Chemical | Detergents (SDS) | Solubilize hydrophobic regions, give uniform charge | SDS-PAGE sample prep | | Chemical | Heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺) | React with –SH groups of cysteine | Mercury poisoning | | Biological | Proteases (trypsin, chymotrypsin) | Cleave peptide bonds (this is hydrolysis, irreversible) | Digestion in gut | **Denaturation vs Coagulation (2 marks):** - **Denaturation** is the disruption of secondary/tertiary/quaternary structure with primary structure intact — may be reversible. - **Coagulation** is the *irreversible* aggregation of denatured protein molecules into an insoluble mass (e.g., curdled milk, cooked egg white). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in Protein Structure questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. **Confusing levels:** Writing "disulfide bonds are part of secondary structure" — **WRONG**. Disulfide bonds stabilize **tertiary (or quaternary)** structure, not secondary. > 2. **Bond direction in α-helix:** Students often write "H-bond from residue *i* to *i+3*" — correct answer is **i → i+4** for the standard α-helix (i+3 is for 3₁₀ helix, a different motif). > 3. **Forgetting water in peptide bond math:** When asked "how many peptide bonds in a 300-residue protein?", the answer is **n − 1 = 299**, not 300. > 4. **Denaturation definition:** Always clarify that the **primary structure is preserved** in denaturation. If peptide bonds are broken, it is **hydrolysis**, not denaturation. > 5. **Sequence direction:** Always write protein sequences from **N-terminus → C-terminus** (left to right). > 6. **Quaternary requires ≥ 2 subunits:** A single polypeptide chain has **no quaternary structure**, even if well-folded. Don't credit quaternary structure for monomeric proteins. > 7. **Anfinsen experiment:** Many students miss writing the **role of β-mercaptoethanol** (disulfide reduction) — it is the **second** denaturant, not optional. > 8. **Marks allocation:** A 7-mark sub-part typically expects 4-6 key points, not a one-line answer. Use **headings and sub-points** to ensure the examiner sees each point. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Revision Notes — Protein Structure (KTU 2024 Scheme)** - ☐ Protein = **polymer of L-α-amino acids** linked by **peptide bonds** (condensation, –H₂O). - ☐ **20 standard amino acids** are genetically encoded. - ☐ Average residue weight = **110 Da**; sequence MW ≈ 110 × *n*. - ☐ **Peptide bond is planar (partial double bond character, 1.32 Å)** and **trans** (except Pro). - ☐ **Primary** = sequence (peptide bonds, covalent). - ☐ **Secondary** = α-helix (**3.6 res/turn, 1.5 Å rise, 5.4 Å pitch, i→i+4 H-bond**) and β-sheet (parallel/antiparallel, H-bonds perpendicular to strand). Stabilized by **backbone H-bonds**. - ☐ **Tertiary** = 3D fold of one chain; stabilized by hydrophobic interactions, H-bonds, ionic bridges, **S–S disulfide bonds** (strongest), van der Waals, metal coordination. - ☐ **Quaternary** = assembly of ≥ 2 subunits (haemoglobin α₂β₂, IgG, collagen, DNA pol). - ☐ **Denaturation** = loss of 2°/3°/4° structure, primary intact. Agents: heat, pH, urea, β-ME, SDS, heavy metals, organics. - ☐ **Denaturation can be reversible** (RNase A) or **irreversible** (egg white coagulation). - ☐ **Anfinsen's experiment (RNase A, 1972 Nobel)** proved **primary structure dictates native fold**; native state is the thermodynamic minimum. - ☐ **Levinthal's paradox** shows folding cannot be random — proteins use **directed pathways / folding funnels / chaperones**. - ☐ **Coagulation** is *irreversible aggregation* of denatured proteins. - ☐ **Sickle cell anaemia** = classic example of primary-structure mutation (Glu→Val at position 6 of β-globin) altering tertiary structure and function. - ☐ **Proline** breaks α-helix (no N–H for H-bonding; rigid ring); **Glycine** is flexible and common in β-turns. - ☐ Misfolding diseases: **Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Prion (CJD)** — relevant for biochemical engineering of biotherapeutics.

topics.title: Nucleotides, RNA and DNA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nucleotides, RNA and DNA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Nucleotide

A **nucleotide** is the fundamental monomeric building block (structural unit) of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA). Chemically, a nucleotide is a phosphorylated nucleoside, composed of three covalently linked components:

1. **A nitrogenous (nitrogen-containing) base** — a purine or a pyrimidine derivative.
2. **A pentose (five-carbon) sugar** — either **β-D-ribose** (in RNA) or **2′-deoxy-β-D-ribose** (in DNA).
3. **A phosphate group** — attached to the 5′-carbon of the sugar through a **phosphoester bond**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The covalent linkage between the base and the sugar is called the **N-glycosidic bond** (between N9 of purines / N1 of pyrimidines and C1′ of the sugar). The sugar-phosphate linkage is the **phosphoester bond** (between the 5′-OH of the sugar and the phosphate group). Together, sugar + base = **nucleoside**; nucleoside + phosphate = **nucleotide**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Lego of Life"

Think of a nucleotide as a **3-piece Lego brick**:
- The **base** is the *picture printed on the brick* (it carries the genetic "code letters" — A, T, G, C, U).
- The **sugar** is the *body of the brick* (it provides the structural shape and connectivity).
- The **phosphate** is the *stud-and-tube connector* on top and bottom (it allows one brick to lock firmly to the next, building long chains).

Just as you can string thousands of Lego bricks together to build a castle, living cells string thousands of nucleotides together to build **DNA** (the master blueprint storage) and **RNA** (the working copy and messenger of that blueprint).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The average molecular weight of a single **deoxyribonucleotide monophosphate (dNMP)** is approximately **330 Da (g/mol)**, while a **ribonucleotide monophosphate (NMP)** averages **340 Da**. The human genome (~3.1 × 10⁹ bp) therefore has a total DNA mass of roughly **1 × 10¹² Da per haploid cell**.

## 1.3 The Two Types of Nitrogenous Bases

Nitrogenous bases are flat, heterocyclic aromatic molecules. They are classified into two families:

| Family | Bases Found | Structure |
|--------|------------|-----------|
| **Purines** | **Adenine (A)** and **Guanine (G)** | Double-ring (9-membered fused ring: pyrimidine + imidazole) |
| **Pyrimidines** | **Cytosine (C)**, **Thymine (T)**, **Uracil (U)** | Single six-membered ring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** DNA contains **A, T, G, C**, while RNA contains **A, U, G, C** — RNA uses **Uracil** in place of Thymine. A useful mnemonic: **"DNA has Thymine, RNA has Uracil — T is in DNA; U is in RNA."**

## 1.4 The Two Pentose Sugars

- **β-D-Ribose** — found in **RNA**; carries hydroxyl (–OH) groups on both 2′ and 3′ carbons.
- **2′-Deoxy-β-D-ribose** — found in **DNA**; carries –OH only on the 3′ carbon (the 2′ position has only –H, hence "de-oxy").

> [!NOTE]
> This single difference (presence or absence of the 2′-OH) is what gives **DNA its chemical stability** (no 2′-OH to attack the phosphodiester backbone) and **RNA its catalytic flexibility** (2′-OH allows RNA to fold into complex 3D shapes and act as enzymes — *ribozymes*).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Nucleotide (Step-by-Step)

A nucleotide is assembled in a precise biochemical sequence:

1. **Base + Sugar → Nucleoside**: The C1′ of the pentose sugar forms an **N-glycosidic bond** with N9 of a purine or N1 of a pyrimidine. This reaction is catalyzed by *glycosyltransferases* and releases water (condensation).
2. **Nucleoside + Phosphate → Nucleotide**: The phosphate group (from ATP, typically) is transferred to the 5′-CH₂OH of the sugar, forming a **5′-phosphoester bond** and producing a **nucleoside 5′-monophosphate (NMP or dNMP)**.
3. **Polymerization → Nucleic Acid**: The 3′-OH of one nucleotide attacks the α-phosphate of the next nucleotide's triphosphate, releasing **pyrophosphate (PPᵢ)** and forming the **3′→5′ phosphodiester bond**. This creates the sugar-phosphate *backbone*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The directionality of the resulting polynucleotide chain is therefore written as **5′ → 3′** (the 5′-phosphate end is at the "head" and the 3′-OH end is at the "tail"). This directionality is **fundamental** to all KTU problems on replication, transcription, and translation.

## 2.2 DNA — The Double Helix

DNA is a **double-stranded (duplex)**, right-handed helical polymer proposed by **Watson and Crick (1953)** based on **Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction data**.

**Key structural features (the "B-DNA" form — the biologically common form):**

- **Two antiparallel strands** — one runs 5′→3′, the other 3′→5′.
- **Sugar-phosphate backbone** is on the *outside*; **bases** are on the *inside* (stacked like rungs of a ladder).
- **Base pairing** is highly specific and follows the **Watson-Crick rule**:
  - **Adenine (A) pairs with Thymine (T)** via **2 hydrogen bonds**.
  - **Guanine (G) pairs with Cytosine (C)** via **3 hydrogen bonds**.
- **Helix dimensions:** 1 turn = **10.5 base pairs**, pitch = **3.4 nm**, diameter ≈ **2 nm**.

## 2.3 RNA — The Single-Stranded Workhorse

RNA is typically **single-stranded**, contains **ribose** (2′-OH present), and uses **Uracil** instead of Thymine. Although single-stranded, RNA folds back on itself via intra-strand base pairing to form complex 3D shapes (hairpins, loops, pseudoknots).

**Three principal functional types of cellular RNA:**

| Type | Full Name | Function | Approx. % of total RNA |
|------|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| **mRNA** | Messenger RNA | Carries the genetic code from DNA to the ribosome for protein synthesis | ~3–5% |
| **tRNA** | Transfer RNA | Adapter molecule that delivers the correct amino acid to the growing polypeptide chain (anticodon-codon pairing) | ~10–15% |
| **rRNA** | Ribosomal RNA | Catalytic and structural core of the ribosome (peptidyl transferase activity) | ~80% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Additional (non-coding) RNAs important in KTU context:** **snRNA** (small nuclear RNA — splicing), **miRNA / siRNA** (gene silencing), **ribozymes** (catalytic RNA).

## 2.4 Chargaff's Rule — The Quantitative Foundation

Erwin Chargaff observed that in double-stranded DNA:

$$\%A = \%T \quad \text{and} \quad \%G = \%C$$

Equivalently, the **purine : pyrimidine ratio = 1 : 1**, which is a direct consequence of strict Watson-Crick base pairing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Chargaff's rule holds strictly only for **double-stranded DNA**. In **single-stranded DNA or RNA**, the rule is **not** applicable.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression | Use / Engineering Application |
|---|----------------|------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Chargaff's Rule | $\%A = \%T$ and $\%G = \%C$ | Predicting base composition; verifying dsDNA identity |
| 2 | Base Pair Identity | $A \equiv T$ (2 H-bonds); $G \equiv C$ (3 H-bonds) | Calculating melting temperature (Tm) of DNA |
| 3 | M.W. of ssDNA | $MW \approx (n \times 330) - 330$ (for *n* nt) | Primer/probe design in PCR; oligonucleotide synthesis |
| 4 | M.W. of dsDNA | $MW \approx (n \times 660)$ (for *n* bp) | Estimating genome size; gel electrophoresis |
| 5 | A₂₆₀ / A₂₈₀ purity | Pure DNA: $\approx 1.8$; Pure RNA: $\approx 2.0$ | Quality check during nucleic acid extraction |
| 6 | DNA length (turn) | 1 turn = 10.5 bp = 3.4 nm | Biophysical modeling of chromatin |
| 7 | Genome size conversion | $1 \text{ pg DNA} = 978 \text{ Mbp}$ | Quantifying DNA yield (fluorometry) |
| 8 | Tm (Wallace rule) | $T_m = 2^\circ C \times (A+T) + 4^\circ C \times (G+C)$ | Designing PCR primers (short oligos < 20 nt) |
| 9 | Tm (Marmur–Doty) | $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41\%GC - \frac{600}{L}$ | Long-DNA hybridization / annealing |
| 10 | Absorbance (1 A₂₆₀) | $50 \mu g/mL$ dsDNA, $40 \mu g/mL$ RNA, $33 \mu g/mL$ ssDNA | Spectrophotometric quantification |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **bioprocess and biochemical engineering**, nucleotide chemistry underlies:
- **PCR / qPCR / RT-PCR** for pathogen detection in fermentation broths.
- **Recombinant DNA technology** — designing expression vectors (pET, pUC) for biopharmaceutical production (insulin, monoclonal antibodies).
- **mRNA vaccines** (e.g., COVID-19 vaccines) — synthesis of modified mRNA with **N1-methylpseudouridine** in place of uridine.
- **DNA microarrays & NGS** for strain identification in industrial microbiology.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating the Molecular Weight of a DNA Duplex

**Problem Statement:** A linear double-stranded DNA fragment contains **N = 1,200 base pairs**. Estimate its molecular weight in Daltons and its physical length in micrometres.

### Step 1 — Identify the molecular weight per base pair

The average molecular weight contributed by one **base pair (bp)** in a hydrated, salt-free dsDNA is taken as the sum of two deoxyribonucleotide monophosphates minus one water molecule (lost during phosphodiester formation, plus one extra phosphate for the duplex's 5′ ends). The accepted standard is:

$$MW_{\text{per bp}} \approx 660 \text{ Da/bp}$$

This value is derived as:

$$MW_{\text{per bp}} = MW_{\text{per dNMP}} \times 2 - MW_{H_2O} \approx 330 \times 2 - 18 = 642 \text{ Da}$$

Adjusting for the average salt counterion and end phosphate, the commonly cited experimental value rounds to **~650–660 Da/bp**.

### Step 2 — Multiply by the number of base pairs

$$MW_{\text{dsDNA}} = N \times MW_{\text{per bp}}$$

Substituting N = 1,200 bp and MW per bp = 660 Da:

$$\begin{aligned}
MW_{\text{dsDNA}} &= 1200 \text{ bp} \times 660 \text{ Da/bp} \\
&= 792{,}000 \text{ Da} \\
&= 7.92 \times 10^{5} \text{ Da}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to physical length

The linear length of a B-DNA duplex is **3.4 nm per 10.5 bp**, equivalent to **~0.34 nm per bp** (rise per base pair).

$$L = N \times 0.34 \text{ nm/bp}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 1200 \times 0.34 \text{ nm} \\
&= 408 \text{ nm} \\
&= 0.408 \text{ μm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Da to grams per mole

Since 1 Da = 1 g/mol:

$$MW = 7.92 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol}$$

For a typical sample concentration of 50 µg/mL dsDNA, the molarity of this fragment is:

$$\begin{aligned}
c_{\text{molar}} &= \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g/mL}}{7.92 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol}} \\
&= 6.31 \times 10^{-11} \text{ mol/mL} \\
&= 63.1 \text{ nM}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Chargaff's Rule from Watson-Crick Pairing

**Given:** A dsDNA sample is reported to contain **28% Adenine**. Predict the percentage of every other base.

### Step 1 — Apply strict base pairing

In a double-stranded molecule, every A on one strand is paired with T on the other strand. Therefore:

$$\%A = \%T = 28\%$$

### Step 2 — Use the conservation of all bases

The total must sum to 100%:

$$\%A + \%T + \%G + \%C = 100\%$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the remaining fraction

$$\begin{aligned}
\%G + \%C &= 100\% - (\%A + \%T) \\
&= 100\% - (28\% + 28\%) \\
&= 44\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Apply G ≡ C pairing

By Watson-Crick base pairing, G and C occur in equal proportions:

$$\%G = \%C = \frac{44\%}{2} = 22\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** %A = 28, %T = 28, %G = 22, %C = 22. Verify: (28+28+22+22) = 100 ✓.

## 3.3 Derivation: Absorbance-based DNA Quantification

**Given:** A DNA sample diluted 1:50 gives an A₂₆₀ = 0.325. Calculate the original (undiluted) concentration in µg/mL.

### Step 1 — Use the conversion factor

For dsDNA: 1 A₂₆₀ unit = 50 µg/mL.

### Step 2 — Calculate the concentration in the diluted sample

$$C_{\text{diluted}} = 0.325 \times 50 = 16.25 \text{ µg/mL}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by the dilution factor

$$C_{\text{original}} = 16.25 \times 50 = 812.5 \text{ µg/mL} = 0.8125 \text{ mg/mL}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DNA vs RNA

| Parameter | DNA | RNA |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Sugar | 2′-deoxyribose | Ribose |
| Bases | A, T, G, C | A, U, G, C |
| Strands | Double-stranded (typically) | Single-stranded (typically) |
| Helix | B-form right-handed double helix | Various secondary structures (hairpins, stems) |
| Stability | Highly stable (no 2′-OH) | Labile (2′-OH susceptible to alkaline hydrolysis) |
| Location | Nucleus (eukaryotes) | Nucleus + cytoplasm |
| Function | Long-term genetic storage | Coding, decoding, regulation, catalysis |
| Base pairing rule | A=T, G≡C (Chargaff) | A=U, G≡C (intra-strand allowed) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Nucleotide

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Phosphate Group 5 prime] --> B[Pentose Sugar Ribose or Deoxyribose]
    B --> C[Nitrogenous Base Purine or Pyrimidine]
    B1[5 prime Phosphate] --> B
    B --> B2[3 prime OH Tail]
    B3[1 prime C links to Base via N glycosidic bond] -.-> C
    C --> C1[Purine A or G double ring]
    C --> C2[Pyrimidine C T or U single ring]
    B2 --> D[Polymerization 3 prime OH attacks alpha phosphate of next NTP]
    A --> D
    D --> E[Polynucleotide Chain 5 prime to 3 prime]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Central Dogma of Molecular Biology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DNA1[DNA Nucleus] -->|Transcription by RNA Pol| RNA1[Pre mRNA]
    RNA1 -->|Splicing 5 cap and Poly A tail| mRNA2[Mature mRNA]
    mRNA2 -->|Export from nucleus| Cyto[Cytoplasm]
    Cyto -->|Translation on Ribosome| Prot[Protein Polypeptide]
    DNA1 -->|Replication by DNA Pol| DNA2[Daughter DNA]
    tR[tRNA charged with amino acid] -->|Decodes codon| Prot
    rR[rRNA Ribosome] -->|Catalyzes peptide bond| Prot
```

## 4.3 DNA Double Helix — Schematic Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Strand 1 runs 5 prime to 3 prime] --> P1[Phosphate Sugar Backbone Outside]
    S2[Strand 2 runs 3 prime to 5 prime] --> P2[Phosphate Sugar Backbone Outside]
    P1 --> BP[Base Pairs Inside]
    P2 --> BP
    BP --> R1[A pairs with T via 2 H bonds]
    BP --> R2[G pairs with C via 3 H bonds]
    H1[Helix Geometry 10.5 bp per turn 3.4 nm pitch 2 nm diameter] -.-> BP
```

## 4.4 RNA Types — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RNA[Total Cellular RNA] --> mRNA1[mRNA 3 to 5 percent Messenger of genetic code]
    RNA --> tRNA1[tRNA 10 to 15 percent Amino acid adapter Anticodon loop]
    RNA --> rRNA1[rRNA 80 percent Ribosomal scaffold and Peptidyl transferase]
    RNA --> ncRNA[Non coding RNA snRNA miRNA siRNA Ribozyme]
    tRNA1 --> AA1[Carries activated amino acid to ribosome]
    rRNA1 --> RIB[Combines with proteins to form 60S and 40S subunits]
    mRNA1 --> RIB
```
