> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT521 Module 2):**
> Industrial enzymes are the workhorses of the modern bioeconomy. They are biological macromolecules (mostly globular proteins, occasionally ribozymes) that lower the **activation energy ($E_a$)** of a specific biochemical reaction without being consumed. Unlike inorganic catalysts, enzymes operate under ambient temperature, atmospheric pressure, and near-neutral pH — making them inherently "green" catalysts.

Mathematically, an enzyme ($E$) catalyzes a substrate ($S$) to product ($P$) via the canonical scheme:

$$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \underset{k_{-2}}{\overset{k_{2}}{\rightleftharpoons}} E + P$$

where $k_1$, $k_{-1}$, and $k_2$ are the forward, reverse, and catalytic rate constants respectively. The enzyme is regenerated after each catalytic cycle, so a small mass of enzyme can process a large mass of substrate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Molecular Scissors & Glue" Factory:**
> Think of an industrial enzyme as a **highly specialized, reusable factory tool**. Imagine a textile worker who cuts millions of meters of denim every day using a single pair of fabric scissors. The scissors (the enzyme) are not consumed; they simply speed up the cutting. Different scissors are designed for different fabrics (cotton, silk, polyester) — this is the **specificity** of enzymes. Now imagine a tool that only works at 25 °C, in water, and breaks if you heat it above 60 °C — that fragility is the enzyme's **denaturation threshold**, a critical engineering constraint.

## 1.3 The Industrial Enzyme Market — A Snapshot

| Metric | Value (Approx.) |
|---|---|
| Global Industrial Enzyme Market Size (2024) | **USD 8 – 10 Billion** |
| Projected CAGR (2024–2030) | **~6.5 %** |
| Largest Application Segment | **Detergents (~30 %)** |
| Largest Source Segment | **Microbial (~90 %)** |
| Operating Temperature Range | **20 °C – 80 °C** (typical) |
| Operating pH Range | **3.0 – 11.0** (extremophiles extend this) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Numerical questions on enzyme units, activity, yield, and productivity are common in Part B. Remember the SI unit: **1 Katal = 1 mol·s⁻¹ of substrate converted**, although industry typically uses **1 Enzyme Unit (U) = 1 μmol·min⁻¹**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Industrial Enzymes

Industrial enzymes are systematically classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### A. By Reaction Catalyzed — IUBMB Classification (EC Numbers)

| EC Class | Reaction Type | Industrial Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **EC 1** | Oxidoreductases | Glucose oxidase, Catalase, Laccase |
| **EC 2** | Transferases | Glucose isomerase, Transglutaminase |
| **EC 3** | **Hydrolases** (most industrially important) | **Protease, Amylase, Lipase, Pectinase, Cellulase, Lactase** |
| **EC 4** | Lyases | Pectate lyase, Aspartate ammonia-lyase |
| **EC 5** | Isomerases | Glucose isomerase (also listed under transferases in some texts) |
| **EC 6** | Ligases | Not widely used industrially |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Hydrolases (EC 3) account for **> 70 %** of the global industrial enzyme market. Memorize the EC class of each major enzyme — a frequent 3-mark question.

### B. By Source Organism

| Source | Examples | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Microbial (Fungal)** | *Aspergillus niger, A. oryzae, Rhizopus* | Amylases, pectinases, lipases |
| **Microbial (Bacterial)** | *Bacillus subtilis, B. licheniformis* | Proteases, α-amylases |
| **Plant** | Papain (papaya), Bromelain (pineapple) | Meat tenderizers, pharmaceuticals |
| **Animal** | Rennet (calf stomach), Trypsin, Pepsin | Cheese making, leather |
| **Recombinant (GMOs)** | *E. coli*, *Pichia pastoris* hosts | Insulin, L-asparaginase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Microbes Dominate:** Microbial enzymes are preferred industrially because of: (i) short generation time, (ii) easy scale-up via fermentation, (iii) genetic manipulability, (iv) consistent quality, and (v) no seasonal dependence.

## 2.2 Desirable Properties of an Industrial Enzyme

For an enzyme to succeed commercially, it must satisfy a stringent set of engineering criteria:

1. **High catalytic activity** ($V_{max}$ large) → less enzyme needed per kg of product.
2. **High specificity** → minimum side products.
3. **Thermostability** → withstands process temperatures (often 50–80 °C in starch processing).
4. **pH stability** → performs at process pH (e.g., alkaline proteases in detergents at pH 9–11).
5. **Long shelf life** → low proteolytic self-degradation.
6. **Low $K_m$** for substrate → high affinity, efficient at low [S].
7. **Tolerates inhibitors / heavy metals** (Ca²⁺ often stabilizes α-amylase).
8. **GRAS status** (Generally Recognized As Safe) for food applications.
9. **Cost-effective production and downstream processing**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Michaelis–Menten Rate | $v = \dfrac{V_{max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Predict reactor productivity |
| 2 | Specific Activity | $SA = \dfrac{\text{Total Activity (U)}}{\text{Total Protein (mg)}}$ | U·mg⁻¹ | Purity indicator during DSP |
| 3 | Volumetric Productivity | $Q_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{final}} - P_{\text{initial}}}{t}$ | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ | Compare bioreactor performance |
| 4 | Enzyme Yield ($Y_{P/S}$) | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\text{Enzyme produced (g)}}{\text{Substrate consumed (g)}}$ | g·g⁻¹ | Fermentation economics |
| 5 | Turnover Number ($k_{cat}$) | $k_{cat} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{[E]_T}$ | s⁻¹ | Catalytic efficiency |
| 6 | Catalytic Efficiency | $\dfrac{k_{cat}}{K_m}$ | M⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Compares enzymes at low [S] |
| 7 | Half-life of enzyme | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_d}$ | h, min, s | Operational stability |
| 8 | Activity Recovery | $\%\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{\text{Total Activity}_{\text{after}}}{\text{Total Activity}_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | % | DSP efficiency |
| 9 | Purification Fold | $\text{Fold} = \dfrac{SA_{\text{after}}}{SA_{\text{before}}}$ | dimensionless | Track purification |
| 10 | Temperature Quotient ($Q_{10}$) | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | dimensionless | Predict activity vs. T |
| 11 | Arrhenius for enzyme denaturation | $\ln k_d = \ln A - \dfrac{E_d}{RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Stability design |
| 12 | Immobilized enzyme effectiveness | $\eta = \dfrac{v_{\text{immob}}}{v_{\text{free}}}$ | dimensionless | Reactor selection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Confusing **Activity (U)** with **Specific Activity (U/mg)**. Activity is a rate (μmol/min). Specific Activity is a purity index. The Fold-purification is a ratio, not a percentage.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

The theoretical framework above is applied in:
- **Detergent industry**: Alkaline proteases from *Bacillus* replace phosphates.
- **Starch-to-HFCS**: α-amylase (liquefaction) + glucoamylase (saccharification) + **glucose isomerase** (isomerization).
- **Bioethanol**: Cellulase cocktail + β-glucosidase hydrolyzes lignocellulose.
- **Leather**: Protease + lipase dehairing replaces toxic sodium sulfide.
- **Dairy**: **Lactase** hydrolyzes lactose → low-lactose milk; rennet (chymosin) clots casein.
- **Pharma**: **L-asparaginase** treats ALL leukemia; **Penicillin acylase** produces 6-APA.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Michaelis–Menten Equation (Quick Recall, KTU-Favorite)

Starting from the rapid-equilibrium assumption:

$$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \xrightarrow{k_2} E + P$$

**Step 1:** Rate of product formation:

$$v = k_2 [ES]$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$k_1 [E][S] = (k_{-1} + k_2)[ES]$$

**Step 3:** Define the Michaelis constant:

$$K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging the steady-state balance:

$$[ES] = \dfrac{[E][S]}{K_m}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting total enzyme concentration $[E]_T = [E] + [ES]$ and eliminating $[E]$:

$$[ES] = \dfrac{[E]_T [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Step 6:** Maximum velocity occurs when $[S] \gg K_m$, so $[ES] \approx [E]_T$:

$$V_{max} = k_2 [E]_T$$

**Step 7:** Combine Steps 1, 5, 6:

$$\boxed{\, v = \dfrac{V_{max}\,[S]}{K_m + [S]} \,}$$

**Conversion logic:** The equation converts the microscopic rate constants $k_1, k_{-1}, k_2$ into two macroscopic, measurable parameters ($V_{max}$, $K_m$) that engineers can plot via the **Lineweaver–Burk linearization**:

$$\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Specific Activity and Purification Fold

**Problem Setup:** During downstream processing of a protease, the following data were recorded at two stages:

| Stage | Volume (mL) | Activity (U·mL⁻¹) | Protein (mg·mL⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crude extract | **500** | **120** | **2.0** |
| After ion-exchange | **80** | **450** | **1.5** |

**Step 1: Total Activity at each stage**

$$A_{\text{crude}} = 500 \times 120 = 60{,}000 \text{ U}$$

$$A_{\text{after}} = 80 \times 450 = 36{,}000 \text{ U}$$

**Step 2: Specific Activity (SA)**

$$SA_{\text{crude}} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{500 \times 2.0} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{1000} = 60.0 \text{ U·mg}^{-1}$$

$$SA_{\text{after}} = \dfrac{36{,}000}{80 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{36{,}000}{120} = 300 \text{ U·mg}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Purification Fold**

$$\text{Fold} = \dfrac{SA_{\text{after}}}{SA_{\text{crude}}} = \dfrac{300}{60} = 5.0$$

**Step 4: % Activity Recovery**

$$\%\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{36{,}000}{60{,}000} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**Engineering interpretation:** We recovered 60 % of the enzyme activity but achieved a 5-fold increase in purity — a **typical acceptable trade-off** in industrial DSP, since the lost 40 % activity is more than offset by the higher specific activity (less enzyme mass needed downstream).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Major Industrial Enzymes at a Glance

| Enzyme | EC No. | Source (industrial) | Reaction Catalyzed | Major Industry | Optimum pH | Optimum Temp (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **α-Amylase** | 3.2.1.1 | *Bacillus licheniformis* | Starch → maltose, dextrins | Starch, brewing, textile | 6.0 – 7.0 | 70 – 90 |
| **Glucoamylase** | 3.2.1.3 | *Aspergillus niger* | Dextrins → glucose | HFCS, brewing | 4.0 – 5.0 | 55 – 60 |
| **Glucose Isomerase** | 5.3.1.5 | *Streptomyces* (immob.) | Glucose → Fructose | HFCS | 7.0 – 8.0 | 60 – 70 |
| **Alkaline Protease** | 3.4.21.62 | *Bacillus licheniformis* | Protein → peptides/AA | Detergents, leather | 9.0 – 11.0 | 50 – 60 |
| **Lipase** | 3.1.1.3 | *Candida, Rhizomucor* | Triglyceride → glycerol + FFA | Detergents, dairy, oils | 7.0 – 9.0 | 30 – 50 |
| **Pectinase** | 3.2.1.15 | *Aspergillus niger* | Pectin → galacturonic acid | Juice, wine | 3.5 – 4.5 | 40 – 55 |
| **Cellulase** | 3.2.1.4 | *Trichoderma reesei* | Cellulose → glucose | Bioethanol, textile, paper | 4.5 – 5.5 | 45 – 65 |
| **Lactase (β-galactosidase)** | 3.2.1.23 | *Kluyveromyces lactis* | Lactose → glucose + galactose | Dairy | 6.5 – 7.0 | 30 – 40 |
| **Catalase** | 1.11.1.6 | *Aspergillus niger* | H₂O₂ → H₂O + O₂ | Food, textile | 6.0 – 8.0 | 20 – 40 |
| **L-Asparaginase** | 3.5.1.1 | *E. coli* (recombinant) | L-Asn → L-Asp + NH₃ | Pharmaceuticals (cancer) | 7.0 – 8.0 | 37 |
| **Penicillin Acylase** | 3.5.1.11 | *E. coli* | Penicillin → 6-APA | Antibiotic semisynthesis | 7.5 – 8.0 | 35 – 40 |
| **Rennet (Chymosin)** | 3.4.23.4 | Calf stomach / *Aspergillus* (recombinant) | κ-Casein cleavage | Cheese making | 6.0 – 6.5 | 30 – 40 |

## 3.4 Numerical — Enzyme Productivity in a Bioreactor

A **1000 L** stirred-tank bioreactor produces an industrial protease via *Bacillus licheniformis* in submerged fermentation. After **48 h**, the final enzyme concentration is **2.5 g·L⁻¹**. The initial substrate (molasses + soybean meal) load was **120 kg**. Calculate:

**(a) Volumetric productivity** $Q_p$
**(b) Enzyme yield on substrate** $Y_{P/S}$

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Total enzyme produced:

$$P_{\text{total}} = 2.5 \text{ g·L}^{-1} \times 1000 \text{ L} = 2500 \text{ g} = 2.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Volumetric productivity:

$$Q_p = \dfrac{2.5 \text{ g·L}^{-1}}{48 \text{ h}} = 0.0521 \text{ g·L}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Enzyme yield:

$$Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{2500 \text{ g}}{120{,}000 \text{ g}} = 0.0208 \text{ g·g}^{-1} \approx 0.021 \text{ g·g}^{-1}$$

**Engineering takeaway:** $Q_p \approx 0.05$ g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ is consistent with industrial protease titres; $Y_{P/S} \approx 2$ % reflects the fact that most carbon flows to biomass, CO₂, and by-products, not to the enzyme itself — a key driver for high-cell-density cultivation and recombinant over-expression strategies.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]
**List any six desirable properties of an industrial enzyme. Why is microbial source preferred over plant/animal sources?**

**Model Answer:**

Six desirable properties of an industrial enzyme:
1. **High catalytic activity** ($V_{max}$) → less enzyme mass per kg product.
2. **High substrate specificity** → minimal side reactions.
3. **Thermostability** → withstands elevated process temperatures (50–80 °C).
4. **pH stability** across the operational range of the process.
5. **Long shelf life** and resistance to self-proteolysis.
6. **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** status — mandatory for food-grade enzymes.

**Why microbial sources are preferred:**
- Short generation time and high growth rate → rapid scale-up.
- Independent of climate, season, or geography.
- Easy genetic manipulation (mutagenesis, recombinant DNA).
- Consistent, reproducible quality batch-to-batch.
- Cheap raw substrates (molasses, corn steep liquor, soy meal).
- Wide diversity — extremophiles yield thermostable / pH-tolerant variants.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Heuristic:** Award ½ mark per property (6 × 0.5 = 3). For the "why microbial" part, mention at least **three** valid reasons; this typically carries 1 mark.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Sample, Module 2]
**Classify industrial enzymes based on the IUBMB system. Give one industrial application of each class.**

**Model Answer:**

| EC Class | Reaction Type | Example Enzyme | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| EC 1 | Oxidoreductases | Glucose oxidase | Food preservation, biosensors |
| EC 2 | Transferases | Transglutaminase | Meat binding, dairy |
| **EC 3** | **Hydrolases** | **Protease / Amylase** | **Detergents, starch processing** |
| EC 4 | Lyases | Pectate lyase | Fruit juice clarification |
| EC 5 | Isomerases | Glucose isomerase | HFCS production |
| EC 6 | Ligases | DNA ligase | Molecular biology (not bulk industrial) |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect you to state the *reaction type* (oxidation, hydrolysis, etc.) and *one* example each. EC 3 (Hydrolases) is the most important — worth 1 mark by itself.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule:** Part B questions may carry internal choice. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as independent alternatives; the student answers **one** of the two.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 2]
**(a)** Discuss in detail the major industrial enzymes — **proteases, amylases, lipases, cellulases, and pectinases** — covering source organism, reaction catalyzed, and industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **production of industrial enzymes by submerged fermentation (SmF) and solid-state fermentation (SSF)** with a comparative flow diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**1. Proteases (EC 3.4):**
- **Source:** *Bacillus licheniformis* (alkaline), *Aspergillus oryzae* (acidic/neutral).
- **Reaction:** Hydrolyze peptide bonds in proteins → peptides + amino acids.
- **Applications:**
  - **Detergents** (60 % of global protease market) — remove protein stains.
  - **Leather industry** — bating, dehairing (replaces toxic Na₂S).
  - **Food** — meat tenderization, cheese ripening.
  - **Pharma** — digestive aids, wound debridement.

**2. Amylases (EC 3.2.1):**
- **Source:** *Bacillus licheniformis* (α-amylase), *Aspergillus niger* (glucoamylase).
- **Reaction:** α-Amylase cleaves α-1,4 glycosidic bonds in starch → maltose, dextrins. Glucoamylase releases glucose from non-reducing ends.
- **Applications:**
  - **Starch processing** — liquefaction, saccharification.
  - **HFCS production** — combined with glucose isomerase.
  - **Brewing** — adjunct liquefaction.
  - **Textile desizing** — removes starch from woven fabrics.
  - **Bakery** — improves bread softness and shelf life.

**3. Lipases (EC 3.1.1.3):**
- **Source:** *Candida rugosa*, *Rhizomucor miehei*, *Burkholderia cepacia*.
- **Reaction:** Hydrolyze triglycerides → glycerol + free fatty acids.
- **Applications:**
  - **Detergents** — fat stain removal.
  - **Dairy** — cheese flavor enhancement, interesterification of butter.
  - **Oleochemistry** — biodiesel production via transesterification.
  - **Pharmaceuticals** — resolution of chiral drugs.

**4. Cellulases (EC 3.2.1.4):**
- **Source:** *Trichoderma reesei* (industry standard), *Aspergillus niger*.
- **Reaction:** Hydrolyze β-1,4 glycosidic bonds in cellulose → cellobiose → glucose.
- **Three components:** Endoglucanase, Exoglucanase (cellobiohydrolase), β-Glucosidase.
- **Applications:**
  - **Bioethanol** — saccharification of lignocellulosic biomass.
  - **Textile** — biopolishing of cotton (reduces pilling), stonewashing of denim.
  - **Pulp & paper** — biobleaching, de-inking.
  - **Animal feed** — improves digestibility.

**5. Pectinases (EC 3.2.1.15):**
- **Source:** *Aspergillus niger*.
- **Reaction:** Hydrolyze pectin (α-1,4 D-galacturonic acid backbone) → galacturonic acid.
- **Applications:**
  - **Fruit juice** — clarification, viscosity reduction, yield improvement.
  - **Wine** — improves filtration and color extraction.
  - **Tea / coffee** — fermentation enhancement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split Hint (7 marks):** ~1.4 marks per enzyme (0.4 source + 0.4 reaction + 0.6 application). Covering all 5 enzymes fully is the path to 7/7.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Submerged Fermentation (SmF):**
- Free-flowing liquid broth containing dissolved nutrients.
- **Aeration** via spargers; **agitation** via Rushton-type impellers.
- Operates in stirred-tank reactors (STR) or airlift reactors.
- Easy control of pH, DO, temperature, foam.
- **Examples:** Most industrial enzymes — α-amylase, protease, lipase.

**Solid-State Fermentation (SSF):**
- Moist solid substrate (no free water); water activity $a_w = 0.4$–0.98.
- Substrates: wheat bran, rice husk, sugarcane bagasse, soybean meal.
- Microbes grow as a thin film on the solid surface.
- Better suited for fungi (*Aspergillus*, *Rhizopus*).
- **Examples:** Pectinase, glucoamylase, tannase.

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | SmF | SSF |
|---|---|---|
| Water content | High (free liquid) | Low (no free water) |
| Reactor type | Stirred tank, airlift | Tray, packed-bed, rotary drum |
| Heat removal | Easy (jacketed vessel) | Difficult (low thermal conductivity) |
| pH / DO control | Precise | Limited |
| Microbial preference | Bacteria, yeasts | Filamentous fungi |
| Downstream processing | Easier (filter broth) | Harder (extract from solid) |
| Product concentration | Lower | Higher (10–50×) |
| Scale-up | Well-established | Challenging |
| Industrial examples | α-Amylase, protease | Pectinase, fungal glucoamylase |
| Capital cost | Higher (stainless steel) | Lower (trays / drums) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Do **not** write "SSF is always better than SmF" or vice-versa. The correct framing is that each is suited to specific **enzyme-microbe** combinations. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for one-sided answers.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 2]
**(a)** Explain the **classification, mechanism, and applications of enzymes used in the detergent, starch, and dairy industries**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **enzyme immobilization**? Describe the four major methods of immobilization with suitable examples. State two advantages and two limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Detergent Industry:**
- **Alkaline proteases** (e.g., subtilisin from *Bacillus lentus*) — hydrolyze protein stains (blood, sweat, egg).
- **Lipases** (e.g., *Humicola lanuginosa* lipase) — hydrolyze triglyceride stains.
- **Amylases** — remove starch-based stains (sauces, chocolate).
- **Cellulases** — color brightening, anti-pilling, softness.
- All must be active at pH 9–11 and 20–60 °C, and tolerate surfactants and oxidants (e.g., perborate).

**Starch Industry — Three-Step Conversion to HFCS:**
1. **Liquefaction** — α-amylase at 95–105 °C, pH 6.0–6.5, 5–10 min → maltodextrins.
2. **Saccharification** — Glucoamylase at 55–60 °C, pH 4.5, 48–72 h → glucose syrup.
3. **Isomerization** — **Glucose isomerase** at 60 °C, pH 7.5–8.0 → fructose (HFCS-42, HFCS-55).

**Dairy Industry:**
- **Lactase (β-galactosidase)** from *Kluyveromyces lactis* or *Aspergillus oryzae*:
  - Hydrolyzes lactose → glucose + galactose.
  - **Lactose-free milk**, ice cream (prevents crystallization), whey utilization.
- **Rennet (chymosin, EC 3.4.23.4):**
  - Cleaves κ-casein at Phe105-Met106 → para-κ-casein + macropeptide.
  - **Cheese making** — causes milk coagulation.
  - Recombinant chymosin (FPC, Fermentation-Produced Chymosin) now dominates.

**Mechanism (Common to all):** Enzyme binds substrate at the **active site** via **lock-and-key** and **induced-fit** mechanisms, lowers the activation energy by stabilizing the transition state, releases product, and is regenerated.

**Applications summary table:**

| Industry | Key Enzyme | Function | Final Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detergent | Alkaline protease | Hydrolyze protein stains | Clean laundry |
| Starch | α-Amylase + glucoamylase + glucose isomerase | Starch → HFCS | Sweetener |
| Dairy | Lactase + rennet | Lactose hydrolysis + casein clotting | Lactose-free milk, cheese |

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Definition:** *Enzyme immobilization* is the technique of physically confining or localizing enzyme molecules on a solid support (carrier) or within a restricted matrix, while retaining their catalytic activity and enabling reuse, continuous operation, and easy separation from the product.

**The Four Major Methods:**

**1. Adsorption:**
- Enzyme binds to a water-insoluble carrier via weak forces (van der Waals, ionic, hydrogen bonds).
- **Carriers:** Activated charcoal, alumina, ion-exchange resins, DEAE-cellulose.
- **Example:** Glucose isomerase adsorbed on DEAE-cellulose.
- *Pros:* Simple, mild conditions, cheap.
- *Cons:* Enzyme may leach (desorb) with pH/ionic-strength changes.

**2. Covalent Bonding:**
- Enzyme is covalently attached to a functionalized support (e.g., CNBr-activated Sepharose, glutaraldehyde-activated matrix).
- **Example:** Penicillin acylase on CNBr-Sepharose for 6-APA production.
- *Pros:* Strong, no leaching.
- *Cons:* Harsh chemistry may inactivate enzyme; difficult regeneration.

**3. Entrapment:**
- Enzyme is trapped within a polymer matrix (gel lattice) without covalent attachment.
- **Matrices:** Calcium alginate beads, polyacrylamide gel, κ-carrageenan, PVA.
- **Example:** Lactase in alginate beads for continuous lactose hydrolysis.
- *Pros:* Mild conditions; enzyme does not leak.
- *Cons:* Diffusion limitations; matrix may be damaged by microbial attack.

**4. Cross-Linking (CLEA — Cross-Linked Enzyme Aggregates):**
- Enzyme molecules are cross-linked to each other using a bifunctional reagent (e.g., glutaraldehyde), forming insoluble aggregates.
- No carrier required.
- **Example:** CLEAs of lipase for biodiesel production.
- *Pros:* High enzyme loading, stable.
- *Cons:* Diffusion barriers; brittleness.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Reusability** — enzyme can be used over many reaction cycles, reducing cost.
2. **Easy product separation** — immobilized enzyme is filtered/centrifuged off, giving enzyme-free product (critical for pharmaceuticals/food).
3. *Bonus:* Continuous operation in packed-bed reactors, better stability.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Diffusion limitations** — internal and external mass-transfer resistance reduces observed rate.
2. **Loss of activity** during immobilization (covalent, cross-linking) due to active-site modification.
3. *Bonus:* Cost of carrier materials; mechanical fragility of some matrices.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing entrapment with encapsulation** — Encapsulation has a defined membrane (microcapsule); entrapment does not.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention leaching** as a drawback of adsorption.
> 3. **Listing CLEC (crystallization) as a method** — not in the four-major-methods syllabus.
> 4. **Writing "immobilization increases enzyme activity"** — it does **not**; it may actually **decrease** it. It increases **operational stability** and **reusability**, not specific activity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print & Pin:**

- ☐ **Industrial enzymes = biological catalysts**, mostly proteins, working at mild T & pH.
- ☐ **EC 3 (Hydrolases) = 70 % of market** — proteases, amylases, lipases, cellulases, pectinases.
- ☐ **Microbial sources preferred** — fast growth, easy scale-up, no seasonal dependence.
- ☐ **Six desirable properties**: high $V_{max}$, specificity, thermostability, pH-stability, long shelf life, GRAS.
- ☐ **Major enzymes & applications**:
  - Protease → detergent, leather
  - α-Amylase → starch liquefaction
  - Glucoamylase → saccharification
  - Glucose isomerase → HFCS
  - Lipase → detergent, biodiesel
  - Cellulase → bioethanol, textile
  - Pectinase → juice, wine
  - Lactase → dairy
  - Rennet → cheese
  - L-Asparaginase → leukemia drug
- ☐ **SmF vs SSF**: SmF = free liquid, bacteria; SSF = no free water, fungi.
- ☐ **Immobilization methods**: Adsorption, Covalent, Entrapment, Cross-linking (memorize one example each).
- ☐ **Activity vs Specific Activity**: Activity = total rate; SA = purity index (U·mg⁻¹).
- ☐ **1 Katal = 1 mol·s⁻¹**; 1 U = 1 μmol·min⁻¹ (more common in industry).
- ☐ **Turnover number $k_{cat}$ = $V_{max} / [E]_T$** — units s⁻¹.
- ☐ **Catalytic efficiency** = $k_{cat} / K_m$ — compared at low substrate.
- ☐ **HFCS production** = 3 enzymes in series (α-amylase → glucoamylase → glucose isomerase).
- ☐ **Lactose-free milk** = lactase pretreatment.
- ☐ **Recombinant chymosin (FPC)** has replaced calf rennet in 80 %+ of cheese production.
- ☐ **Alkaline proteases** dominate the **detergent enzyme** segment.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:**
> - A 14-mark question = ~7 sub-points × 2 marks each, or ~3 sub-points × 4–5 marks each.
> - Always **draw at least one flow diagram or table** — it secures 1–2 "presentation marks."
> - Conclude long answers with a **comparative table** — examiners love structured summaries.
> - For numericals, **write units at every step** and **box the final answer**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Immobilized Enzyme Technology: Enzyme Immobilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — IMMOBILIZED ENZYME TECHNOLOGY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enzyme Immobilization** is the technique of confining or anchoring biologically active enzyme molecules onto or within a distinct physical support (carrier matrix) or into a defined region of space, while retaining their catalytic activity and allowing their separation from the reaction medium for repeated or continuous use.

In strict biochemical engineering terms, an **immobilized enzyme** is a *physically localized* or *spatially confined* enzyme preparation that:

- Retains its full **catalytic activity**.
- Can be **recovered** from the reaction mixture.
- Can be **reused** in batch processes.
- Can be deployed in **continuous flow reactors** (PBR, CSTR, packed bed).
- Permits **easy separation** of the biocatalyst from the product stream.

The 1971 Enzyme Engineering Conference defined immobilization as: *"Enzymes whose physical state permits their separation from the substrate and product formed."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Committed Worker on a Conveyor Belt"**
> 
> Imagine a free-roaming chef (the soluble enzyme) cooking dishes in a large open kitchen. Every time a dish is prepared, the chef walks around, gets mixed up with the plates, and you have to chase him through the crowd to retrieve your finished food. Now, instead, imagine the **chef is bolted to a fixed station on a conveyor belt** — ingredients pass over him, he catalyzes the reaction, and the finished product moves on while the chef remains at his post, ready for the next meal. That bolted-down chef is your **immobilized enzyme**. The conveyor belt is your substrate stream. The kitchen boundary is your **support matrix** (carrier). You can now run 24/7 without losing the chef — and his salary (enzyme cost) is paid only ONCE.

This analogy maps directly to the engineering advantages:
- **Reusability** → chef stays bolted
- **Continuous operation** → conveyor belt
- **Easy product separation** → finished dish moves on
- **Operational stability** → chef works longer without fatigue

---

### 1.3 Why Immobilize? — Core Justifications

| # | Justification | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Reusability** | One enzyme charge used for thousands of cycles |
| 2 | **Product purity** | No enzyme in effluent → simpler downstream processing |
| 3 | **Continuous operation** | Plug into CSTR/PFR/fluidized bed reactors |
| 4 | **Operational stability** | Often enhanced thermal, pH, and storage stability |
| 5 | **Process economics** | Reduced enzyme cost per kg product (the metric is **enzyme productivity, g product / g enzyme**) |
| 6 | **Reaction control** | Local microenvironment can be tuned by carrier chemistry |
| 7 | **Multi-enzyme systems** | Co-immobilization enables sequential cascade reactions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Benchmark Metric:** The performance of an immobilization method is judged by:
> 1. **Immobilization yield (%)** = (Activity recovered on support / Initial activity loaded) × 100
> 2. **Activity retention (%)** = (Specific activity of immobilized enzyme / Specific activity of free enzyme) × 100
> 3. **Operational half-life (t₁/₂)** = time for activity to drop to 50 % of original.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Immobilization Methods (High-Level)

The five canonical KTU-recognized techniques are:

1. **Adsorption** (physical/ionic) — onto carriers like activated carbon, ion-exchange resins.
2. **Covalent bonding** — to functionalized supports via glutaraldehyde, carbodiimide, etc.
3. **Entrapment** — within gel matrices (Ca-alginate, polyacrylamide, κ-carrageenan).
4. **Encapsulation (Microencapsulation)** — within semi-permeable membranes.
5. **Cross-linking** — using bifunctional reagents (e.g., glutaraldehyde) to form insoluble aggregates (CLEA = Cross-Linked Enzyme Aggregates).

Detailed treatment follows in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Immobilization Methods — Detailed Mechanics

#### 2.1.1 Adsorption (Physical / Ionic Adsorption)

**Mechanism:** Weak, reversible non-covalent interactions — **van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, ionic (electrostatic) bonds** between the enzyme surface and an insoluble carrier.

- **Physical adsorption**: Enzyme binds to the surface of porous carriers (activated carbon, porous glass, silica).
- **Ionic adsorption**: Enzyme binds via salt linkages to ion-exchange resins (DEAE-cellulose, CM-cellulose, Dowex).

**Why it works:** Enzymes are amphoteric proteins with charged residues (–NH₃⁺, –COO⁻) that interact with oppositely charged supports.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, mild conditions, no chemical modification → retains activity.
- Cheap carriers.
- Reversible → carrier can be regenerated.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Leaching** — desorption by pH, ionic strength, temperature changes.
- Non-specific binding; less stable.
- Loading often limited by surface area.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Fact:** The first industrial immobilized enzyme — **aminoacylase on DEAE-Sephadex** (Chibata, Tanabe Seiyaku, 1969) for L-amino acid production — used *ionic adsorption*. This is a classic 14-mark answer topic.

---

#### 2.1.2 Covalent Bonding (Chemical Attachment)

**Mechanism:** Formation of stable covalent bonds between **functional groups on the enzyme** (–NH₂ of lysine, –COOH of aspartate/glutamate, –SH of cysteine, –OH of serine, imidazole of histidine) and **activated groups on a pre-functionalized support**.

**Common Reactions (KTU-mandatory):**

1. **Schiff's base / Imine formation** with **glutaraldehyde** (CHO–(CH₂)₃–CHO):
   - Carrier–NH₂ + OHC–R–CHO + Enzyme–NH₂ → Carrier–N=CH–R–CH=N–Enzyme
2. **Carbodiimide activation** of carboxyl groups (using EDC/NHS).
3. **Cyanogen bromide (CNBr) activation** of Sepharose/agarose:
   - Sepharose–OH + CNBr → Sepharose–O–C(=NH)– → Couples to enzyme –NH₂.
4. **Diazo coupling** (carrier with aryl-azo groups couples to enzyme tyrosyl/histidyl residues).
5. **Epoxide coupling** (e.g., Eupergit C — oxirane groups on polymethacrylate).

**Advantages:**
- Strong, irreversible bond → **no leaching**.
- High thermal and operational stability.
- Suitable for harsh reactor conditions.

**Disadvantages:**
- Chemical modification may **inactivate the active site** if the active-site residue is involved.
- Harsh reagents can denature the enzyme.
- Carrier preparation is laborious and costly.
- Activity yield is often **low (10–30 %)**.

---

#### 2.1.3 Entrapment (Lattice/Gel Entrapment)

**Mechanism:** Enzyme is **occluded within the pores of a cross-linked polymer matrix** (gel) such that the substrate and product can diffuse in/out, but the enzyme is physically trapped.

- **Polymers used:** Polyacrylamide, calcium alginate, κ-carrageenan, agar, gelatin, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyethersulfone.
- **Process:** Enzyme is mixed with monomer/crosslinker + initiator → polymerization traps enzyme in 3D lattice.

**Advantages:**
- No chemical bonding → enzyme retains native conformation.
- Can entrap cells, organelles, or multi-enzyme systems.
- High enzyme loadings possible.
- Mild conditions.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Diffusion limitations** — substrate transport through matrix → apparent K_m increases.
- Possible enzyme **leakage** if pore size > enzyme diameter.
- Mechanical weakness of gels (especially alginate in phosphate buffers — Ca²⁺ is chelated).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Specific:** Alginate beads dissolve in **sodium phosphate / citrate buffers** because these chelate Ca²⁺. Use HEPES or Tris-HCl instead. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

---

#### 2.1.4 Microencapsulation (Membrane Confinement)

**Mechanism:** Enzyme is enclosed within **semipermeable polymer membranes** (microcapsules, liposomes, hollow fibers, ultrafiltration membranes). Substrates/products (< 1–2 nm) pass through; enzyme (> 5 nm) is retained.

- **Interfacial polymerization** (nylon membranes).
- **Lipid vesicle entrapment** (liposomes).
- **Hollow-fiber / membrane reactor** configurations.

**Advantages:**
- Very high surface area-to-volume ratio.
- Can co-immobilize multiple enzymes for cascade reactions.
- Excellent product separation.

**Disadvantages:**
- Diffusion barrier may be severe.
- Membrane fragility.
- Difficult scale-up of uniform microcapsules.

---

#### 2.1.5 Cross-Linking (Carrier-Free Immobilization)

**Mechanism:** Enzyme molecules are joined to each other using **bifunctional crosslinkers** (glutaraldehyde, bis-diazobenzidine-2,2′-disulfonic acid), forming insoluble aggregates.

- **Cryogels, CLEAs (Cross-Linked Enzyme Aggregates), CLEs (Cross-Linked Enzymes)** are modern variants.
- Precipitation (e.g., using ammonium sulfate, acetone) followed by cross-linking yields CLEAs with **>75 % activity retention**.

**Advantages:**
- No carrier → **high specific activity** per unit mass.
- Very stable.
- Low cost (no carrier purchase).

**Disadvantages:**
- Poor mechanical properties — soft, gelatinous.
- Diffusion limitations in the pellet.
- Limited operational reusability in some cases.

---

### 2.2 Effects of Immobilization on Enzyme Kinetics — The Critical KTU Topic

Immobilization alters the **apparent kinetic constants** of an enzyme compared to its soluble form. The two dominant effects are:

#### Effect A: **Partition (or Solvation) Effect**

When an enzyme is placed in a charged or hydrophobic support, the local concentrations of H⁺, substrate, and product **inside the carrier differ** from those in the bulk solution.

For a charged carrier, **Boltzmann's distribution** governs the partitioning:

$$[\text{S}]_{\text{local}} = [\text{S}]_{\text{bulk}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{z_s F \psi}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $z_s$ = charge on the substrate,
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C mol⁻¹**,
- $\psi$ = electrostatic potential of the carrier (V),
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**,
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

Hence, the **apparent Michaelis constant** observed in the bulk solution is:

$$K_{m,\text{app}} = K_m \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{z_s F \psi}{RT}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - If the support and substrate are **oppositely charged** ($z_s \psi < 0$): $K_{m,\text{app}} < K_m$ → apparent affinity increases.
> - If similarly charged ($z_s \psi > 0$): $K_{m,\text{app}} > K_m$ → apparent affinity decreases.

#### Effect B: **Diffusion (Mass Transfer) Effect**

Two layers of resistance exist:

1. **External film diffusion** — from bulk to support surface.
2. **Internal pore diffusion** — within the porous matrix to the enzyme site.

If diffusion is **rate-limiting**, the observed rate is lower than the intrinsic rate, and the Lineweaver-Burk plot shows **deviation from linearity** at high substrate concentrations.

For spherical porous particles of radius $R_p$, the **effectiveness factor** $\eta$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{actual rate}}{\text{rate if no diffusion limitation}} = \frac{3}{\phi}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tanh \phi} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right)$$

where the **Thiele modulus** $\phi$ is:

$$\phi = R_p \sqrt{\frac{V_{max,\text{int}}}{K_m \cdot D_{e,S}}}$$

- $D_{e,S}$ = effective diffusivity of substrate within the particle (m²/s).
- For $\phi < 0.5$: kinetics controls (no diffusion limitation, $\eta \to 1$).
- For $\phi > 3$: diffusion controls severely ($\eta \approx 3/\phi$).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Michaelis-Menten (free) | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | $v$ = rate (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹); $V_{max}$ (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹); $K_m$ (mol L⁻¹) |
| 2 | Lineweaver-Burk | $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ | Linear form, y-intercept = $1/V_{max}$, x-intercept = $-1/K_m$ |
| 3 | Partition effect | $[S]_{\text{local}} = [S]_{\text{bulk}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{z_s F \psi}{RT}\right)$ | $z_s$ = substrate charge; $\psi$ = potential (V) |
| 4 | Apparent $K_m$ shift | $K_{m,\text{app}} = K_m \exp\!\left(\dfrac{z_s F \psi}{RT}\right)$ | Use for charged support |
| 5 | Thiele modulus | $\phi = R_p \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{max,\text{int}}}{K_m D_{e,S}}}$ | $R_p$ = particle radius (m); $D_{e,S}$ (m² s⁻¹) |
| 6 | Effectiveness factor | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\tanh \phi} - \dfrac{1}{\phi}\right)$ | $\eta = 1$ for $\phi \to 0$ |
| 7 | Damköhler (Da) for internal diffusion | $\text{Da}_I = \dfrac{V_{max,\text{int}} \cdot R_p^2}{K_m \cdot D_{e,S} \cdot [S_b]} = \phi^2 / \beta$ | $\beta = [S_b]/K_m$ |
| 8 | Observed rate (immobilized) | $v_{\text{obs}} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V_{max,\text{int}}[S_b]}{K_m + [S_b]}$ | Includes diffusion |
| 9 | Immobilization yield | $Y_{\text{imm}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{bound}}}{A_{\text{loaded}}} \times 100\,\%$ | $A$ = activity (U) |
| 10 | Activity retention | $R_A = \dfrac{a_{\text{imm}}}{a_{\text{free}}} \times 100\,\%$ | $a$ = specific activity (U/mg) |
| 11 | Operational half-life | $t_{1/2}$ such that $A(t_{1/2}) = A_0/2$ | Days or hours |
| 12 | External film MTC | $k_L$ from $Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$ | $Sh$ Sherwood; $Re$ Reynolds; $Sc$ Schmidt |
| 13 | Enzyme loading | $\text{Loading} = \dfrac{\text{mg enzyme}}{\text{g dry support}}$ | mg/g |
| 14 | Space velocity (packed bed) | $\text{SV} = Q/V_{\text{bed}}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 15 | Turnover number | $k_{\text{cat}} = V_{max}/[E]_T$ | s⁻¹ |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceuticals:** Penicillin G acylase immobilized on Sephadex/Eupergit for synthesis of 6-APA (semi-synthetic penicillin precursors).
- **Food industry:** Invertase on porous glass for sucrose → glucose+fructose; lactase on alginate for lactose-free milk.
- **Biosensors:** Glucose oxidase covalently immobilized on oxygen/peroxidase electrode for diabetes monitoring.
- **Biofuel:** Lipase on hydrophobic carriers (Accurel, Sepabeads) for biodiesel via transesterification.
- **Amino acids:** Aspartase in *E. coli* cells immobilized in κ-carrageenan (Tanabe process) for L-aspartate from fumarate.
- **High-fructose corn syrup (HFCS):** Glucose isomerase immobilized on cellulosic carriers — a multi-billion dollar process.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Apparent K_m in a Charged Support (Partition Effect)

We start from the **Boltzmann distribution** governing ionic species across a potential gradient.

Consider a porous support carrying a surface potential $\psi$ (V) measured relative to the bulk. A substrate ion of valence $z_s$ experiences an electrostatic energy $z_s F \psi$ at the surface.

**Step 1: Energy of a charged ion in the electrostatic field.**

The Gibbs free energy of transferring 1 mole of substrate ion from bulk to the local microenvironment is:

$$\Delta G = z_s F \psi$$

**Step 2: Boltzmann equilibrium between bulk and local concentrations.**

At equilibrium, the chemical potentials balance:

$$\mu_{\text{bulk}} = \mu_{\text{local}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad [S]_{\text{local}} = [S]_{\text{bulk}} \cdot e^{-\Delta G / (RT)}$$

Substituting $\Delta G$:

$$[S]_{\text{local}} = [S]_{\text{bulk}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{z_s F \psi}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3: Intrinsic rate based on local concentration.**

For a Michaelis-Menten enzyme operating at the local concentration:

$$v_{\text{int}} = \frac{V_{max,\text{int}} \, [S]_{\text{local}}}{K_m + [S]_{\text{local}}}$$

**Step 4: Observed rate as if it followed bulk concentration.**

Define an apparent constant such that the rate equation, when written in terms of the *bulk* concentration $[S]_{\text{bulk}}$, takes the form:

$$v_{\text{obs}} = \frac{V_{max,\text{int}} \, [S]_{\text{bulk}}}{K_{m,\text{app}} + [S]_{\text{bulk}}}$$

**Step 5: Equating the two rate expressions.**

$$\frac{V_{max,\text{int}} \, [S]_{\text{local}}}{K_m + [S]_{\text{local}}} = \frac{V_{max,\text{int}} \, [S]_{\text{bulk}}}{K_{m,\text{app}} + [S]_{\text{bulk}}}$$

Substitute $[S]_{\text{local}} = [S]_{\text{bulk}} \cdot e^{-z_s F \psi / (RT)}$ and denote $P = e^{-z_s F \psi / (RT)}$:

$$\frac{P[S]_b}{K_m + P[S]_b} = \frac{[S]_b}{K_{m,\text{app}} + [S]_b}$$

Invert both sides:

$$\frac{K_m}{P[S]_b} + 1 = \frac{K_{m,\text{app}}}{[S]_b} + 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{K_m}{P} = K_{m,\text{app}}$$

Therefore:

$$K_{m,\text{app}} = \frac{K_m}{P} = K_m \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{z_s F \psi}{RT}\right)$$

This is the **Master Result for Partition Effect**, which the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce in full.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Effectiveness Factor for a Spherical Immobilized Particle (Michaelis-Menten Kinetics, Steady State)

Consider a spherical porous particle of radius $R_p$ containing uniformly distributed enzyme. Let $S(r)$ denote substrate concentration at radial position $r$.

**Step 1: Steady-state diffusion-reaction equation (radial, spherical symmetry).**

$$\frac{D_{e,S}}{r^2} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2 \frac{dS}{dr}\right) = \frac{V_{max,\text{int}} \, S}{K_m + S}$$

**Step 2: Boundary conditions.**

- Symmetry at the center: $\left.\dfrac{dS}{dr}\right|_{r=0} = 0$
- External bulk concentration at the surface: $S(R_p) = S_b$ (assuming no external film resistance here for simplicity)

**Step 3: Non-dimensionalization.**

Define dimensionless variables:

$$\bar{S} = S/S_b, \quad \bar{r} = r/R_p$$

Substituting and using the chain rule (with $\dfrac{d}{dr} = \dfrac{1}{R_p}\dfrac{d}{d\bar{r}}$):

$$\frac{D_{e,S}}{R_p^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\bar{r}^2} \frac{d}{d\bar{r}}\!\left(\bar{r}^2 \frac{d\bar{S}}{d\bar{r}}\right) = \frac{V_{max,\text{int}} \, S_b \bar{S}}{K_m + S_b \bar{S}}$$

**Step 4: Introduce the Thiele modulus and $\beta$.**

Define the **Thiele modulus**:

$$\phi^2 = \frac{R_p^2 \, V_{max,\text{int}}}{D_{e,S} \, K_m}$$

And the dimensionless saturation parameter:

$$\beta = \frac{S_b}{K_m}$$

The governing equation becomes:

$$\frac{1}{\bar{r}^2} \frac{d}{d\bar{r}}\!\left(\bar{r}^2 \frac{d\bar{S}}{d\bar{r}}\right) = \phi^2 \cdot \frac{\bar{S}}{1 + \beta \bar{S}}$$

**Step 5: Definition of the effectiveness factor.**

The actual rate per particle is the flux integrated at the surface:

$$\text{Actual rate} = 4\pi R_p^2 \, D_{e,S} \left.\frac{dS}{dr}\right|_{r=R_p}$$

The rate if there were no diffusion limitation (i.e., $S = S_b$ throughout):

$$\text{Intrinsic rate} = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R_p^3 \cdot \frac{V_{max,\text{int}} S_b}{K_m + S_b}$$

Hence, the **effectiveness factor** is:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{actual rate}}{\text{intrinsic rate}} = \frac{3}{\phi^2 \beta}\left.\frac{d\bar{S}}{d\bar{r}}\right|_{\bar{r}=1}\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1}\right)$$

**Step 6: First-order limit (low $S_b$, $\beta \ll 1$).**

When $S_b \ll K_m$ (first-order regime), the reaction rate becomes $V_{max,\text{int}} S / K_m$, and the differential equation becomes linear:

$$\frac{1}{\bar{r}^2} \frac{d}{d\bar{r}}\!\left(\bar{r}^2 \frac{d\bar{S}}{d\bar{r}}\right) = \phi^2 \bar{S}$$

The solution is $\bar{S} = A \dfrac{\sinh(\phi \bar{r})}{\bar{r}}$, and applying $S(R_p) = S_b$ gives:

$$\bar{S}(\bar{r}) = \frac{\sinh(\phi \bar{r})}{\bar{r} \sinh(\phi)}$$

Differentiating and substituting at $\bar{r}=1$:

$$\eta = \frac{3}{\phi}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tanh \phi} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right)$$

This is the celebrated expression used universally for **first-order diffusion-reaction in spheres**.

**Step 7: Limiting cases.**

- **Reaction-limited** ($\phi \to 0$): $\tanh \phi \approx \phi - \phi^3/3$, so:

$$\eta \to \frac{3}{\phi}\!\left(\frac{1}{\phi - \phi^3/3} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right) = \frac{3}{\phi}\!\left(\frac{1}{\phi}(1 + \phi^2/3 + \cdots) - \frac{1}{\phi}\right) \to 1$$

- **Diffusion-limited** ($\phi \to \infty$): $\tanh \phi \to 1$, so:

$$\eta \to \frac{3}{\phi}\!\left(1 - 0\right) = \frac{3}{\phi}$$

**Step 8: Observed rate in packed-bed operation.**

$$v_{\text{obs}} = \eta \cdot \frac{V_{max,\text{int}} \, S_b}{K_m + S_b}$$

This is the working design equation for industrial immobilized enzyme reactors.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Each Immobilization Method Affects Kinetics

| Method | Effect on $V_{max,\text{app}}$ | Effect on $K_{m,\text{app}}$ | Dominant Limitation | Stability |
|--------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Adsorption (ionic) | Slight decrease | Decrease if support and substrate oppositely charged; increase if same charge | Leaching | Low–Moderate |
| Covalent bonding | Decrease (active site may be blocked) | Slight increase or no change | Chemical inactivation | High |
| Entrapment | Significant decrease | Increase (diffusion barrier) | Internal pore diffusion | Moderate |
| Microencapsulation | Decrease | Increase | Membrane diffusion | Moderate |
| Cross-linking (CLEA) | Variable | Variable | Mechanical weakness of pellet | High |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Illustration (Worked-Out Calculation)

**Problem:** Glucose oxidase is immobilized in alginate beads of radius $R_p = 1.5\,\text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$. The effective diffusivity of glucose in the bead is $D_{e,S} = 6.0 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Intrinsic kinetic constants: $V_{max,\text{int}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$, $K_m = 0.30\,\text{mM} = 0.30 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol L}^{-1}$. Bulk glucose $S_b = 0.10\,\text{mM}$. Determine the Thiele modulus, effectiveness factor, and observed rate.

**Step 1: Convert units.** Use $V_{max,\text{int}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$ (since 1 L = $10^{-3}$ m³, $V_{max}$ per m³ = $V_{max}$ per L × 1000).

$$V_{max,\text{int}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^3 = 2.0\,\text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$K_m = 0.30 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^3 = 0.30\,\text{mol m}^{-3}$$

**Step 2: Compute Thiele modulus.**

$$\phi^2 = \frac{R_p^2 \, V_{max,\text{int}}}{D_{e,S} \, K_m} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 2.0}{(6.0 \times 10^{-10}) \times 0.30}$$

$$= \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.0}{1.8 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{4.50 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.50 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\phi = \sqrt{2.50 \times 10^4} = 158.1$$

**Step 3: Effectiveness factor (high $\phi$ regime).**

Since $\phi \gg 1$, diffusion controls:

$$\eta \approx \frac{3}{\phi} = \frac{3}{158.1} = 0.01897 \approx 0.019$$

This means only ~1.9 % of the intrinsic rate is actually observed — severe diffusion limitation.

**Step 4: Intrinsic (no-diffusion) rate at $S_b = 0.10\,\text{mM}$.**

$$v_{\text{int}} = \frac{V_{max,\text{int}} S_b}{K_m + S_b} = \frac{2.0 \times 0.10}{0.30 + 0.10} = \frac{0.20}{0.40} = 0.50\,\text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Observed rate.**

$$v_{\text{obs}} = \eta \cdot v_{\text{int}} = 0.019 \times 0.50 = 9.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol m}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Conclusion:** Reducing $R_p$ would lower $\phi$ (since $\phi \propto R_p$) and dramatically improve $\eta$. For example, halving $R_p$ to $0.75\,\text{mm}$ would quarter $\phi^2$ → $\phi = 79$ → $\eta \approx 0.038$, doubling the observed rate. This is the **engineering lever** designers use to overcome internal diffusion.
