$$\text{6-Phosphogluconate} + \text{NADP}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Ribulose-5-P} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{NADPH}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Oxidative Phase Stoichiometry (per 1 G6P):**
> $$\text{G6P} + 2\,\text{NADP}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Ru5P} + 2\,\text{NADPH} + 2\,\text{H}^+ + \text{CO}_2$$
> Note: **2 NADPH**, **1 CO₂**, **0 ATP** (substrate-level), and the carbon count drops from **6C → 5C**.

---

#### **B. ISOMERIZATION / EPIMERIZATION STEP (Pre Non-Oxidative)**

Before entering the non-oxidative phase, Ru5P is shunted into one of two products:

**Step 4a — Phosphopentose Isomerase (PPI) [EC 5.3.1.6]**
$$\text{Ribulose-5-P (Ru5P)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ribose-5-P (R5P)}$$
- Converts a **ketose** (Ru5P) into an **aldose** (R5P). Required for nucleotide biosynthesis.

**Step 4b — Phosphopentose Epimerase (PPE) [EC 5.1.3.1]**
$$\text{Ribulose-5-P (Ru5P)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Xylulose-5-P (Xu5P)}$$
- Epimerization at **C3** (epimeric difference at C3 carbon). Xu5P is the substrate for **transketolase**.

---

#### **C. NON-OXIDATIVE PHASE (Steps 5–8)** — *The "Flexible Scheduler"*

This phase **rearranges carbon skeletons** between 3C, 4C, 5C, 6C, and 7C sugars without any net oxidation/reduction (no NADPH/NADH consumed). The two key enzymes are:

**Transketolase (TK) [EC 2.2.1.1]** — Transfers a **2C unit (C1–C2 of a ketose)**.
- Cofactor: **Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP)** + Mg²⁺.
- Mechanism: Forms a carbanion/enamine intermediate with TPP at the C2 carbon of the ketose donor.

**Transaldolase (TA) [EC 2.2.1.2]** — Transfers a **3C unit (dihydroxyacetone, DHA)**.
- Cofactor: Schiff-base (lysine) intermediate; no TPP required.

**Step 5 — Transketolase Reaction #1 (TK1):**
$$\text{Xu5P (5C)} + \text{R5P (5C)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Sedoheptulose-7-P (S7P, 7C)} + \text{Glyceraldehyde-3-P (G3P, 3C)}$$
- Xu5P donates 2C (C1–C2) to R5P.

**Step 6 — Transaldolase Reaction (TA):**
$$\text{S7P (7C)} + \text{G3P (3C)} \rightleftharpoons \text{Erythrose-4-P (E4P, 4C)} + \text{Fructose-6-P (F6P, 6C)}$$
- S7P donates 3C (DHA moiety) to G3P.

**Step 7 — Transketolase Reaction #2 (TK2):**
$$\text{Xu5P (5C)} + \text{E4P (4C)} \rightleftharpoons \text{F6P (6C)} + \text{G3P (3C)}$$
- Xu5P donates 2C to E4P.

**Step 8 (Implicit regeneration):**
F6P and G3P are **glycolytic intermediates** that can re-enter glycolysis (or be reconverted to G6P via gluconeogenesis/phosphoglucose isomerase) — completing the **cycle**.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these values — they appear in nearly every KTU Part-B question on this topic.**

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NADPH per G6P (oxidative) | **2** | mol NADPH / mol G6P |
| 2 | CO₂ per G6P (oxidative) | **1** | mol CO₂ / mol G6P |
| 3 | ATP yield from non-oxidative end products (2 F6P + 1 G3P) | 2×(2 ATP glycolytic to pyruvate, F6P→2 pyruvate) + 1×(2 ATP from G3P) = **6 ATP** | mol ATP / 3 mol G6P (via PPP-glycolysis) |
| 4 | Net reaction (3 G6P basis) | $3 \text{ G6P} + 6 \text{ NADP}^+ + 3 \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \text{ F6P} + \text{ G3P} + 6 \text{ NADPH} + 6 \text{ H}^+ + 3 \text{ CO}_2$ | Standard textbook stoichiometry |
| 5 | Rate-limiting enzyme | **G6PDH** (G6P dehydrogenase) | Irreversible, NADP⁺-specific |
| 6 | Cofactor of transketolase | **Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) + Mg²⁺** | 2C unit transfer |
| 7 | Cofactor of transaldolase | **Schiff base (Lys ε-NH₂)** | 3C unit transfer |
| 8 | NADPH/NADPH+H⁺ ratio (cellular) | High ratio **inhibits** G6PDH | Allosteric inhibition |
| 9 | Carbon balance check | 3 × 6C = 18C → 2 × 6C + 1 × 3C + 3 × 1C = 12 + 3 + 3 = 18C ✓ | Mass balance verification |
| 10 | C-atom shuttled per TK step | **2C** | C1–C2 of ketose |
| 11 | C-atom shuttled per TA step | **3C** | DHA unit |
| 12 | Location of pathway | **Cytoplasm** | Unlike TCA (mitochondrial) |
| 13 | Pathway's energy status (own) | **No ATP made or used** in PPP proper | Contrast glycolysis |
| 14 | Yield of ribose-5-P from PPP alone | **1 mol R5P per mol G6P** (when R5P is the desired product) | When oxidative phase is short-circuited at step 3 with epimerase/isomerase |

> **CRITICAL LATEX NOTE:** All vertical bars `|` in expressions such as "$\vert x\vert$" have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The PPP is a **metabolic engineering hot-spot** in industrial biotechnology:
> - **Overexpressing *zwf1* (G6PDH gene)** in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* increases NADPH supply → boosts **lipid biosynthesis** (biodiesel feedstocks).
> - **Knocking out *zwf1*** in *E. coli* engineered for aromatics production forces carbon through glycolysis → higher **shikimate pathway flux** (e.g., tryptophan, phenyalanine).
> - **G6PDH deficiency** (most common human enzyme defect; ~400 million people affected) is a clinical model tested in KTU biomedical engineering electives.
> - **NADPH availability** is rate-limiting in whole-cell biocatalysis of oxidoreductase-driven reactions; PPP modulation is a standard strategy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Net Stoichiometric Equation of the PPP

We start with **3 moles of glucose-6-phosphate** entering the oxidative phase, the textbook assumption to achieve a **whole-number stoichiometry** with all intermediates canceling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 3 G6P?**
> Because the smallest set of reactions in the non-oxidative phase that yields whole-number products (with no remaining pentose) requires **2 Xu5P + 1 R5P** as inputs, which in turn requires **3 Ru5P** upstream, which in turn requires **3 G6P** at the start.

---

#### **Step A — Oxidative Phase (3× G6P basis)**

For each G6P, the oxidative phase yields **1 Ru5P + 2 NADPH + 1 CO₂** (per SECTION 2).

For **3 G6P**:
$$3 \text{ G6P} + 6 \text{ NADP}^+ + 3 \text{ H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 3 \text{ Ru5P} + 6 \text{ NADPH} + 6 \text{ H}^+ + 3 \text{ CO}_2$$

---

#### **Step B — Isomerization / Epimerization (3× Ru5P basis)**

By the cellular need ratio, we need **2 Xu5P + 1 R5P** from the 3 Ru5P. Therefore:

$$1 \text{ Ru5P} \xrightarrow{\text{PPI}} 1 \text{ R5P}$$

$$2 \text{ Ru5P} \xrightarrow{\text{PPE}} 2 \text{ Xu5P}$$

**Subtotal entering non-oxidative phase**: **2 Xu5P + 1 R5P**

---

#### **Step C — Non-Oxidative Phase**

**Transketolase #1** (uses 1 Xu5P + 1 R5P):
$$1 \text{ Xu5P} + 1 \text{ R5P} \longrightarrow 1 \text{ S7P} + 1 \text{ G3P}$$

**Transaldolase** (uses 1 S7P + 1 G3P):
$$1 \text{ S7P} + 1 \text{ G3P} \longrightarrow 1 \text{ E4P} + 1 \text{ F6P}$$

**Transketolase #2** (uses the **second** Xu5P + the 1 E4P):
$$1 \text{ Xu5P} + 1 \text{ E4P} \longrightarrow 1 \text{ F6P} + 1 \text{ G3P}$$

**Subtotal produced by non-oxidative phase**: **2 F6P + 2 G3P**

But 1 G3P was consumed in TA, so net is **2 F6P + 1 G3P**.

---

#### **Step D — Summation of All Phases**

Adding the four subtotals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oxidative:}\quad & 3\,\text{G6P} + 6\,\text{NADP}^+ + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\,\text{Ru5P} + 6\,\text{NADPH} + 6\,\text{H}^+ + 3\,\text{CO}_2 \\
\text{Isomerization/Epimerization:}\quad & 3\,\text{Ru5P} \rightarrow 1\,\text{R5P} + 2\,\text{Xu5P} \\
\text{TK1:}\quad & 1\,\text{Xu5P} + 1\,\text{R5P} \rightarrow 1\,\text{S7P} + 1\,\text{G3P} \\
\text{TA:}\quad & 1\,\text{S7P} + 1\,\text{G3P} \rightarrow 1\,\text{E4P} + 1\,\text{F6P} \\
\text{TK2:}\quad & 1\,\text{Xu5P} + 1\,\text{E4P} \rightarrow 1\,\text{F6P} + 1\,\text{G3P} \\
\hline
\text{Net:}\quad & 3\,\text{G6P} + 6\,\text{NADP}^+ + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{F6P} + 1\,\text{G3P} + 6\,\text{NADPH} + 6\,\text{H}^+ + 3\,\text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Step E — Carbon and Mass Balance Verification**

| Side | Species | Carbon atoms | Total C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactant | 3 G6P | 3 × 6 = **18 C** | 18 |
| Product | 2 F6P | 2 × 6 = 12 C | 12 |
| Product | 1 G3P | 1 × 3 = 3 C | 3 |
| Product | 3 CO₂ | 3 × 1 = 3 C | 3 |
| **Total Product C** | | | **18 C** ✓ |

Mass balance is satisfied.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-style)

> **Problem (Model):** A bioreactor contains *S. cerevisiae* metabolizing glucose exclusively via the Pentose Phosphate Cycle to maximize NADPH for lipid biosynthesis. If the cells consume **15 mM glucose-6-phosphate** per minute, calculate:
> (i) The rate of **NADPH generation** (mM/min).
> (ii) The rate of **CO₂ release** (mM/min).
> (iii) The rate of **F6P + G3P production** (mM/min).

**Solution:**

**(i) NADPH rate:**
NADPH yield = 2 mol per mol G6P (oxidative phase).
$$\text{Rate}_{\text{NADPH}} = 2 \times 15 = 30 \text{ mM/min}$$

**(ii) CO₂ rate:**
CO₂ yield = 1 mol per mol G6P.
$$\text{Rate}_{\text{CO}_2} = 1 \times 15 = 15 \text{ mM/min}$$

**(iii) F6P + G3P rate:**
Per **3 G6P** → 2 F6P + 1 G3P (net non-oxidative). So per G6P, average production = 2/3 F6P + 1/3 G3P = 1 F6P-equivalent + 1/3 G3P.
$$\text{Rate}_{\text{F6P+G3P}} = 15 \times \frac{(2+1)}{3} = 15 \times 1 = 15 \text{ mM/min (total sugar)}$$

(Or: F6P alone = 10 mM/min, G3P alone = 5 mM/min.)

---

### 3.3 ATP Equivalent Comparison (PPP vs. Glycolysis)

If the F6P and G3P produced by the PPP re-enter glycolysis:

| Pathway (3 G6P basis) | NADPH | ATP (net) | CO₂ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Glycolysis → TCA** (3 G6P) | 0 | ~30 ATP (full oxidation) | 18 CO₂ |
| **Pure PPP (oxidative only)** | 6 | 0 | 3 CO₂ |
| **PPP + glycolysis of end products** | 6 | **6** (≈ 2 ATP per F6P, ≈ 2 ATP per G3P, but accounting for entry cost) | 3 + 3 = 6 CO₂ |
| **PPP for biosynthesis** (end products diverted) | 6 | 0 | 3 |

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Diverting G6P through the PPP sacrifices ATP yield in exchange for NADPH — a textbook **metabolic trade-off**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Pathway Map (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Glucose 6 Phosphate G6P 6C"] --> B["G6PDH Reaction Step 1"]
    B --> C["6 Phosphoglucono delta lactone"]
    C --> D["6PGL Hydrolase Step 2"]
    D --> E["6 Phosphogluconate 6C"]
    E --> F["6PGD Dehydrogenase Step 3"]
    F --> G["Ribulose 5 Phosphate Ru5P 5C"]
    F -.-> H["CO2 released"]
    F -.-> I["NADPH generated x2"]
    G --> J["PPI Isomerase"]
    G --> K["PPE Epimerase"]
    J --> L["Ribose 5 Phosphate R5P 5C"]
    K --> M["Xylulose 5 Phosphate Xu5P 5C"]
    L --> N["TK1 Transketolase Step 5"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["Sedoheptulose 7 Phosphate S7P 7C"]
    N --> P["Glyceraldehyde 3 Phosphate G3P 3C"]
    O --> Q["TA Transaldolase Step 6"]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R["Erythrose 4 Phosphate E4P 4C"]
    Q --> S["Fructose 6 Phosphate F6P 6C"]
    M --> T["TK2 Transketolase Step 7"]
    R --> T
    T --> S
    T --> P
    S --> U["Re enter Glycolysis or Gluconeogenesis"]
    P --> U
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Oxidative vs. Non-Oxidative Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OX["OXIDATIVE PHASE IRREVERSIBLE"]
        OX1["G6P"] --> OX2["G6PDH Rate Limiting"]
        OX2 --> OX3["Lactone Hydrolase"]
        OX3 --> OX4["6PGD Decarboxylation"]
        OX4 --> OX5["Ru5P + 2 NADPH + CO2"]
    end
    subgraph NOX["NON OXIDATIVE PHASE REVERSIBLE"]
        NOX1["Ru5P"] --> NOX2["PPI and PPE"]
        NOX2 --> NOX3["R5P and Xu5P"]
        NOX3 --> NOX4["Transketolase x2 + Transaldolase"]
        NOX4 --> NOX5["F6P and G3P Glycolytic Feed"]
    end
    OX5 --> NOX1
    NOX5 --> GLY["Re entry to Glycolysis or Biosynthesis"]
```

---

### 4.3 Carbon-Shuttle Diagram (Transketolase vs. Transaldolase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R5P_node["R5P 5C"] --> TK1_node["Transketolase 1 - 2C transfer - TPP cofactor"]
    Xu5P_node["Xu5P 5C"] --> TK1_node
    TK1_node --> S7P_node["S7P 7C"]
    TK1_node --> G3P_node["G3P 3C"]
    S7P_node --> TA_node["Transaldolase - 3C transfer - Schiff base"]
    G3P_node --> TA_node
    TA_node --> E4P_node["E4P 4C"]
    TA_node --> F6P_node1["F6P 6C"]
    Xu5P_node2["Xu5P 5C second molecule"] --> TK2_node["Transketolase 2 - 2C transfer - TPP cofactor"]
    E4P_node --> TK2_node
    TK2_node --> F6P_node2["F6P 6C"]
    TK2_node --> G3P_node2["G3P 3C"]
```

---

### 4.4 Regulation Schematic (KTU 2024 Focus)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NADPH_H["High NADPH cellular pool"] --> INH["Allosteric Inhibition"]
    INH --> G6PDH["G6PDH Rate Limiting Enzyme"]
    INS["Insulin Hormone Signal"] --> ACT["Transcriptional Activation"]
    ACT --> G6PDH
    G6PDH --> F6P_out["F6P product feeds glycolysis"]
    G6PDH --> R5P_out["R5P feeds nucleotide synthesis"]
    G6PDH --> NADPH_out["NADPH feeds reductive biosynthesis"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Pentose Phosphate Pathway. Mention its location in the cell and the two cofactors required by the transketolase and transaldolase enzymes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Pentose Phosphate Pathway (PPP), also called the Hexose Monophosphate Shunt, is a **cytoplasmic, amphibolic route** parallel to glycolysis that oxidizes glucose-6-phosphate to produce **NADPH, ribose-5-phosphate, and CO₂**.

- **Location**: Cytosol (1 mark)
- **Transketolase cofactor**: Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) + Mg²⁺; transfers 2C units (1 mark)
- **Transaldolase cofactor**: Schiff-base intermediate (active-site lysine); transfers 3C units (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the net reaction of the oxidative phase of the Pentose Phosphate Pathway starting from glucose-6-phosphate.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\text{G6P} + 2\,\text{NADP}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Ribulose-5-P} + 2\,\text{NADPH} + 2\,\text{H}^+ + \text{CO}_2$$

- Substrate and cofactor identification (1 mark)
- Products listed correctly (1 mark)
- Correct stoichiometry and CO₂ release (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

## 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** Describe the **non-oxidative phase** of the Pentose Phosphate Pathway with all enzyme reactions and intermediates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from **6 moles of glucose-6-phosphate**, derive the net stoichiometric equation of the PPP and verify the **carbon balance**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### (a) Non-Oxidative Phase Description (7 marks)

The non-oxidative phase **reversibly interconverts** 3-, 4-, 5-, 6-, and 7-carbon sugars **without net oxidation/reduction**. It uses two key enzymes:

**1. Transketolase (TK) — 2C unit transfer (TPP + Mg²⁺ dependent):**
- Reaction 1: Xu5P (5C) + R5P (5C) → S7P (7C) + G3P (3C)
- Reaction 2: Xu5P (5C) + E4P (4C) → F6P (6C) + G3P (3C)

**2. Transaldolase (TA) — 3C unit transfer (Schiff-base intermediate):**
- Reaction: S7P (7C) + G3P (3C) → E4P (4C) + F6P (6C)

**3. Isomerization/Epimerization at start:**
- Ru5P → R5P (Phosphopentose isomerase)
- Ru5P → Xu5P (Phosphopentose epimerase)

**Net carbon shuffle (per 3 G6P):** 2 F6P + 1 G3P, both of which can re-enter glycolysis. (2 marks for all reactions, 2 marks for enzyme mechanism, 2 marks for carbon flow, 1 mark for re-entry to glycolysis)

---

#### (b) Net Stoichiometric Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Oxidative phase × 6:**
$$6\,\text{G6P} + 12\,\text{NADP}^+ + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 6\,\text{Ru5P} + 12\,\text{NADPH} + 12\,\text{H}^+ + 6\,\text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2 — Convert Ru5P (use ratio 4 R5P : 8 Xu5P? No — for 6 Ru5P, use 2 R5P + 4 Xu5P for two cycles):**

Per 3 G6P, 1 R5P + 2 Xu5P. So for 6 G6P, double: **2 R5P + 4 Xu5P**.

**Step 3 — Run two cycles of TK1 + TA + TK2:**

Cycle 1: 1 Xu5P + 1 R5P → 1 S7P + 1 G3P → 1 E4P + 1 F6P (after TA)
Cycle 2: 1 Xu5P + 1 E4P → 1 F6P + 1 G3P

Repeat for the second set: 1 R5P + 1 Xu5P → 1 S7P + 1 G3P → 1 E4P + 1 F6P; 1 Xu5P + 1 E4P → 1 F6P + 1 G3P.

**Step 4 — Sum:**
- Total F6P = 4
- Total G3P = 2
- Net (per 3 G6P): 2 F6P + 1 G3P
- For 6 G6P: **4 F6P + 2 G3P**

**Final Net Equation:**
$$6\,\text{G6P} + 12\,\text{NADP}^+ + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\,\text{F6P} + 2\,\text{G3P} + 12\,\text{NADPH} + 12\,\text{H}^+ + 6\,\text{CO}_2$$

**Carbon balance:**
- LHS: 6 × 6 = 36 C
- RHS: (4 × 6) + (2 × 3) + (6 × 1) = 24 + 6 + 6 = 36 C ✓

(2 marks for derivation logic, 2 marks for final equation, 2 marks for carbon balance, 1 mark for stoichiometric ratio)

---

## 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **oxidative phase** of the Pentose Phosphate Pathway with the enzymes involved, the cofactors required, and the products formed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **biological significance** of the PPP and explain how it is **regulated** in the cell. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### (a) Oxidative Phase (7 marks)

The oxidative phase comprises **three irreversible reactions** that convert **glucose-6-phosphate (6C)** to **ribulose-5-phosphate (5C)**, generating **2 NADPH** and **1 CO₂**.

| Step | Enzyme | EC | Cofactor | Substrate → Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PDH) | 1.1.1.49 | NADP⁺ | G6P → 6-P-Glucono-δ-lactone |
| 2 | 6-Phosphogluconolactonase (6PGL) | 3.1.1.31 | H₂O, Mg²⁺ | Lactone → 6-Phosphogluconate |
| 3 | 6-Phosphogluconate dehydrogenase (6PGD) | 1.1.1.44 | NADP⁺ | 6-PG → Ru5P + CO₂ |

**Key Features:**
- G6PDH is the **rate-limiting**, NADP⁺-specific enzyme.
- The third step is an **oxidative decarboxylation**.
- The pathway is **independent of ATP** (no substrate-level phosphorylation).
- Strictly **cytoplasmic** localization.

(3 marks for all three reactions, 2 marks for cofactors, 2 marks for the net equation: G6P + 2 NADP⁺ + H₂O → Ru5P + 2 NADPH + 2 H⁺ + CO₂)

---

#### (b) Biological Significance and Regulation (7 marks)

**Significance (4 marks):**
1. **NADPH supply** for reductive biosynthesis (fatty acids, cholesterol, steroids).
2. **Ribose-5-phosphate** generation for **purine/pyrimidine** biosynthesis (nucleic acids, NAD⁺, FAD, CoA).
3. **Antioxidant defense**: maintains reduced glutathione (GSH) pool, protecting RBCs from oxidative damage.
4. **Metabolic flexibility**: non-oxidative phase redistributes carbon skeletons to glycolysis or gluconeogenesis based on cellular need.
5. **Industrial relevance**: NADPH-intensive bioprocesses (e.g., lipid synthesis in engineered yeasts) rely on PPP flux.

**Regulation (3 marks):**
- **Allosteric inhibition** of G6PDH by high **NADPH/NADP⁺ ratio** — feedback inhibition.
- **Transcriptional activation** by **insulin** (increases G6PDH expression in liver/adipose).
- **Substrate availability**: cytoplasmic [G6P] modulates flux.
- **Hormonal control**: glucagon **decreases** G6PDH transcription in fasting state.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing cofactors of TK vs TA** — Transketolase needs **TPP**; Transaldolase does NOT. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Writing "2 NADH" instead of "2 NADPH"** — the PPP is strictly NADP⁺-specific. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the CO₂ in the net equation** — the oxidative decarboxylation step is often missed. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Citing "ATP = 0" for the entire pathway** — this is only for the **PPP proper**; if end products re-enter glycolysis, ATP is generated downstream.
> 5. **Carbon balance errors** — students often write 3 F6P + 1 G3P from 3 G6P, which violates C-conservation (3×6=18 ≠ 3×6+1×3=21). Correct is 2 F6P + 1 G3P + 3 CO₂.
> 6. **Mixing up isomerase vs epimerase** — PPI makes an **aldose (R5P)** from the ketose Ru5P; PPE makes a **C3 epimer (Xu5P)**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ PPP = Hexose Monophosphate Shunt = Phosphogluconate Pathway — three names, one pathway.
- ✅ **Location**: **Cytosol** (not mitochondria).
- ✅ **Phases**: **Oxidative** (irreversible, 2 NADPH, 1 CO₂) + **Non-Oxidative** (reversible, sugar shuffle).
- ✅ **Rate-limiting enzyme**: **G6PDH** (NADP⁺-specific).
- ✅ **Cofactor for Transketolase**: **TPP + Mg²⁺** (2C transfer).
- ✅ **Cofactor for Transaldolase**: **Schiff base (lysine)** (3C transfer).
- ✅ **Net equation (3 G6P basis)**: 3 G6P + 6 NADP⁺ + 3 H₂O → 2 F6P + 1 G3P + 6 NADPH + 6 H⁺ + 3 CO₂.
- ✅ **NADPH per G6P** = **2**; **CO₂ per G6P** = **1**.
- ✅ **Ribose-5-P** is the precursor for **purine and pyrimidine** synthesis.
- ✅ **No ATP** is consumed or produced **within the PPP itself**.
- ✅ **NADPH/NADP⁺ ratio** controls the flux (feedback inhibition on G6PDH).
- ✅ **Insulin** upregulates G6PDH; **glucagon** downregulates it.
- ✅ **Carbon balance**: Always check 6C × n(G6P) = 6C × n(F6P) + 3C × n(G3P) + 1C × n(CO₂).
- ✅ **Clinical correlation**: G6PDH deficiency → hemolytic anemia (fava bean sensitivity).
- ✅ **Engineering application**: Overexpressing *zwf1* in yeast boosts lipid yield via NADPH supply.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Respiration - TCA Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Respiration - TCA Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Tricarboxylic Acid (TCA) Cycle**, also designated as the **Krebs Cycle** or **Citric Acid Cycle**, is the final common oxidative pathway for the catabolism of carbohydrates, fatty acids, and amino acids. It is a series of **eight enzyme-catalyzed reactions** occurring in the **mitochondrial matrix** of eukaryotes (and cytoplasm of prokaryotes), in which the acetyl group of **Acetyl-CoA** is completely oxidized to two molecules of **CO₂**, with concomitant generation of high-energy electron carriers (**NADH** and **FADH₂**) and one substrate-level phosphorylation (**GTP**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The cycle is named *Tricarboxylic* because its first stable intermediate, **citric acid**, contains three carboxyl groups. The cycle is **amphibolic** — it functions in both catabolism (energy generation) and anabolism (biosynthetic precursor supply).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Furnace"

Imagine a **paper-recycling furnace** in a large factory:
- **Input (Acetyl-CoA):** A new piece of paper (2-carbon unit) is thrown into the furnace.
- **Catalyst Wall (OAA):** A rotating wall binds the new paper.
- **Burning Chamber (8 Reactions):** The paper is completely burned in 8 stages, releasing heat and CO₂ (smoke).
- **Outputs:** Heat (ATP via electron transport), smoke (CO₂), and the wall reappears (OAA regenerated) to accept the next paper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The wall (OAA) is *catalytic* — it is not consumed. It is regenerated at the end of every turn, which is why the cycle is self-sustaining as long as Acetyl-CoA is supplied.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cycle Location | **Mitochondrial Matrix** | Compartment for aerobic respiration |
| Cycle Discoverer | **Sir Hans Adolf Krebs (1937)** | Nobel Prize, 1953 |
| O₂ Requirement | **Strictly Aerobic** | Terminal electron acceptor required |
| ΔG°′ (per Acetyl-CoA) | **−20 kcal/mol (−83.6 kJ/mol)** | Highly exergonic |
| pH Optimum (in vitro) | **~7.4** | Physiological cytosolic/matrix pH |
| Turnover Number | **~3–5 molecules/sec/enzyme** | Mitochondrial flux rate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 8 Sequential Reactions of the TCA Cycle

The cycle operates through the following well-orchestrated enzymatic sequence:

**Step 1 — Condensation (Irreversible, Committed Step)**
- *Enzyme:* **Citrate Synthase**
- *Reaction:* Acetyl-CoA + Oxaloacetate + H₂O → Citrate + CoA-SH
- *Energy yield:* None (uses hydrolysis of thioester bond)

**Step 2 — Isomerization (Dehydration + Rehydration)**
- *Enzyme:* **Aconitase** (contains Fe-S cluster, *Iron-Sulfur Protein*)
- *Reaction:* Citrate ⇌ cis-Aconitate + H₂O ⇌ Isocitrate
- *Note:* A stereospecific conversion; only the pro-S arm of citrate is acted upon.

**Step 3 — Oxidative Decarboxylation I (Rate-Limiting)**
- *Enzyme:* **Isocitrate Dehydrogenase (ICDH)**
- *Reaction:* Isocitrate + NAD⁺ → α-Ketoglutarate + CO₂ + NADH
- *Energy yield:* **1 NADH** (≈ 2.5 ATP)

**Step 4 — Oxidative Decarboxylation II**
- *Enzyme:* **α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase Complex** (analogous to PDH; uses TPP, lipoate, FAD, CoA)
- *Reaction:* α-KG + NAD⁺ + CoA-SH → Succinyl-CoA + CO₂ + NADH
- *Energy yield:* **1 NADH** (≈ 2.5 ATP)

**Step 5 — Substrate-Level Phosphorylation**
- *Enzyme:* **Succinyl-CoA Synthetase (Succinate Thiokinase)**
- *Reaction:* Succinyl-CoA + GDP + Pᵢ ⇌ Succinate + GTP + CoA-SH
- *Energy yield:* **1 GTP** (≈ 1 ATP)

**Step 6 — Oxidation**
- *Enzyme:* **Succinate Dehydrogenase (SDH)** — *only TCA enzyme embedded in inner mitochondrial membrane (Complex II of ETC)*
- *Reaction:* Succinate + FAD → Fumarate + FADH₂
- *Energy yield:* **1 FADH₂** (≈ 1.5 ATP)

**Step 7 — Hydration**
- *Enzyme:* **Fumarase (Fumarate Hydratase)**
- *Reaction:* Fumarate + H₂O ⇌ L-Malate

**Step 8 — Oxidation & OAA Regeneration**
- *Enzyme:* **Malate Dehydrogenase**
- *Reaction:* L-Malate + NAD⁺ ⇌ Oxaloacetate + NADH + H⁺
- *Energy yield:* **1 NADH** (≈ 2.5 ATP)

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the master equation and the energy stoichiometry** — these are the most frequently asked derivations in KTU End Semester Examinations.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables | Standard Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $1 \text{ NADH} \rightarrow 2.5 \text{ ATP}$ | P/O ratio for Complex I | mol ATP / mol NADH |
| 2 | $1 \text{ FADH}_2 \rightarrow 1.5 \text{ ATP}$ | P/O ratio for Complex II | mol ATP / mol FADH₂ |
| 3 | $1 \text{ GTP} \equiv 1 \text{ ATP}$ | Nucleoside diphosphate kinase | mol ATP / mol GTP |
| 4 | $\text{Net ATP} = 10(2.5) + 2(1.5) + 4$ | Glucose oxidation | mol ATP / mol Glucose |
| 5 | $\text{Net ATP} = 25 + 3 + 4 = \mathbf{32}$ | Glucose oxidation (aerobic) | mol ATP / mol Glucose |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{n_{\text{ATP}} \cdot \Delta G_{\text{ATP}}^{\circ\prime}}{\Delta G_{\text{glucose}}^{\circ\prime}} \times 100\%$ | Metabolic efficiency | % |
| 7 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{ATP}} = -7.3 \text{ kcal/mol}$ | Standard free energy of ATP hydrolysis | kcal/mol |
| 8 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{glucose}} = -686 \text{ kcal/mol}$ | Combustion energy of glucose | kcal/mol |
| 9 | $\text{P/O} = \dfrac{\text{ATP formed}}{\dfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \text{ reduced}}$ | P/O ratio (theoretical max) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution on Pipe Symbol:** The vertical bar in P/O ratios above is represented with forward slashes, and absolute-value expressions will be written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking KTU's official markdown renderer.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **bioprocess engineering**, the TCA cycle is a critical design parameter for:
- **Industrial citric acid production** using *Aspergillus niger* (overproduces citrate due to disrupted aconitase regulation).
- **Amino acid fermentation** (Glutamate, Aspartate) — both TCA intermediates are direct precursors.
- **Bioprocess yield coefficient ($Y_{X/S}$)** calculations for aerobic cultures.
- **Single-cell protein (SCP)** production from hydrocarbon substrates where the TCA cycle drives energy metabolism.
- **Effluent BOD/COD modeling** — understanding the cycle allows accurate prediction of oxygen demand in wastewater biotreatment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Net ATP Yield from One Mole of Glucose (Standard KTU Problem)

#### Step 1: Glycolysis (Cytoplasm)

The complete oxidative breakdown of glucose begins with glycolysis, yielding 2 pyruvate, 2 NADH, and 2 ATP via substrate-level phosphorylation.

$$\text{Glucose} + 2\text{NAD}^+ + 2\text{ADP} + 2\text{P}_i \longrightarrow 2\text{Pyruvate} + 2\text{NADH} + 2\text{ATP} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{H}^+$$

*Logic:* One 6-carbon glucose is split into two 3-carbon pyruvates. Two ATP are invested in the early phase (hexokinase, PFK-1) and four are produced later, netting **+2 ATP**.

#### Step 2: Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex (Mitochondrial Matrix)

Each pyruvate is decarboxylated and oxidized to form Acetyl-CoA. Since there are two pyruvates, all stoichiometric coefficients double.

$$2\text{Pyruvate} + 2\text{NAD}^+ + 2\text{CoA-SH} \longrightarrow 2\text{Acetyl-CoA} + 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{NADH}$$

*Logic:* Five cofactors (TPP, lipoamide, FAD, CoA, NAD⁺) participate; the net result is one NADH and one CO₂ per pyruvate.

#### Step 3: TCA Cycle (Per Glucose, i.e., 2 Turns)

For every 2 Acetyl-CoA entering, the cycle turns **twice**. Summing both turns:

$$2\text{Acetyl-CoA} + 6\text{NAD}^+ + 2\text{FAD} + 2\text{GDP} + 2\text{P}_i + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 4\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{NADH} + 2\text{FADH}_2 + 2\text{GTP} + 2\text{CoA} + 6\text{H}^+$$

*Logic:* Eight steps × 2 turns = 16 reactions; only 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, and 1 GTP are produced per turn. **Carbon balance:** 2 carbons (Acetyl-CoA) × 2 turns = 4 CO₂ released per glucose.

#### Step 4: Total Energy Carrier Inventory

Adding the NADH, FADH₂, and substrate-level ATP produced across all three stages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total NADH} &= 2_{\text{(glycolysis)}} + 2_{\text{(PDH)}} + 6_{\text{(TCA)}} = 10 \text{ NADH} \\
\text{Total FADH}_2 &= 0 + 0 + 2_{\text{(TCA)}} = 2 \text{ FADH}_2 \\
\text{Total ATP/GTP (substrate-level)} &= 2_{\text{(glycolysis)}} + 0 + 2_{\text{(TCA)}} = 4 \text{ ATP}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Oxidative Phosphorylation (Electron Transport Chain)

Each NADH drives the synthesis of **2.5 ATP** at Complex I; each FADH₂ drives **1.5 ATP** at Complex II (enters at ubiquinone).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ATP from NADH} &= 10 \times 2.5 = 25 \text{ ATP} \\
\text{ATP from FADH}_2 &= 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \text{ ATP} \\
\text{ATP from substrate-level} &= 4 \text{ ATP} \\
\hline
\text{Net ATP} &= 25 + 3 + 4 = \boxed{32 \text{ ATP / mole glucose}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Older textbooks report 36 or 38 ATP. The modern P/O ratio of 2.5/1.5 (revised by Hinkle, 1991) yields **30–32 ATP** per glucose. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to use the **modern stoichiometry**.

#### Step 6: Metabolic Efficiency Calculation

The efficiency of energy conversion is the ratio of energy stored in ATP to the total combustion energy of glucose:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{n_{\text{ATP}} \times \Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{ATP}}}{|\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{glucose}}|} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{32 \text{ mol} \times 7.3 \text{ kcal/mol}}{686 \text{ kcal/mol}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{233.6}{686} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 34.05\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Roughly **34%** of glucose's chemical energy is captured as ATP; the remaining 66% is dissipated as heat. This approaches the theoretical efficiency of a heat engine at biological temperatures — a remarkable evolutionary outcome.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Regulation of Key TCA Enzymes

| Enzyme | Activator(s) | Inhibitor(s) | Type of Regulation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Citrate Synthase | ADP, Ca²⁺ | ATP, Citrate, Succinyl-CoA | Allosteric |
| Isocitrate Dehydrogenase | ADP, Ca²⁺ | ATP, NADH | Allosteric (Rate-limiting) |
| α-KG Dehydrogenase | Ca²⁺ | NADH, Succinyl-CoA | Allosteric |
| Succinyl-CoA Synthetase | — | — | Substrate-driven |
| Succinate Dehydrogenase | — | Malonate (competitive) | Competitive inhibition |
| Fumarase | — | — | Substrate-driven |
| Malate Dehydrogenase | — | NADH (product inhibition) | Allosteric |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Functional Flow Diagram of the TCA Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACC[Acetyl-CoA] --> R1
    OAA1[Oxaloacetate] --> R1
    R1["Step 1: Citrate Synthase"] --> CIT[Citrate]
    CIT --> R2["Step 2: Aconitase"]
    R2 --> ISC[Isocitrate]
    ISC --> R3["Step 3: Isocitrate DH"]
    R3 --> AKG[alpha-Ketoglutarate]
    R3 -.->|NADH + CO2| POOL[Energy Carrier Pool]
    AKG --> R4["Step 4: alpha-KG DH Complex"]
    R4 --> SCOA[Succinyl-CoA]
    R4 -.->|NADH + CO2| POOL
    SCOA --> R5["Step 5: Succinyl-CoA Synthetase"]
    R5 --> SUC[Succinate]
    R5 -.->|GTP| POOL
    SUC --> R6["Step 6: Succinate DH Complex II"]
    R6 --> FUM[Fumarate]
    R6 -.->|FADH2| POOL
    FUM --> R7["Step 7: Fumarase"]
    R7 --> MAL[Malate]
    MAL --> R8["Step 8: Malate DH"]
    R8 --> OAA1
    R8 -.->|NADH| POOL
    POOL --> ETC[Electron Transport Chain]
    ETC --> ATP[Oxidative Phosphorylation: 10 ATP per turn]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Energy Integration Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph CYTOPLASM
        GLC[Glucose 6C] -->|Glycolysis -2 ATP| PYR[2 Pyruvate 3C]
        PYR -->|2 NADH via GAPDH| NADH1[NADH Cytoplasmic]
    end
    NADH1 -->|Glycerol-3-Phosphate Shuttle -1.5 ATP| ETC
    subgraph MITOCHONDRIAL_MATRIX
        PYR -->|PDH Complex| ACC[2 Acetyl-CoA 2C]
        ACC -->|TCA Cycle x2| CO2_FINAL[4 CO2 Released]
        ACC -->|TCA Cycle| NADH2[6 NADH]
        ACC -->|TCA Cycle| FADH2[2 FADH2]
        ACC -->|TCA Cycle| GTP[2 GTP]
    end
    NADH2 --> ETC
    FADH2 --> ETC
    GTP --> ATP_DIRECT[2 ATP Direct]
    ETC -->|32 ATP Total| ATP_FINAL[Net Energy Yield]
    ATP_DIRECT --> ATP_FINAL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
Define the TCA cycle. Why is it also called the **amphibolic pathway**?

**Model Answer:**
The TCA cycle (Krebs/Citric Acid cycle) is a series of eight enzyme-catalyzed reactions in the mitochondrial matrix that oxidizes the acetyl group of Acetyl-CoA to 2 CO₂, generating NADH, FADH₂, and GTP. It is called **amphibolic** because it functions in both **catabolism** (oxidation of Acetyl-CoA for energy) and **anabolism** (provides precursors like α-ketoglutarate, oxaloacetate, succinyl-CoA for biosynthesis of amino acids, heme, porphyrins, and glucose via gluconeogenesis). *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Name the four dehydrogenase enzymes of the TCA cycle and the cofactors they use.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Isocitrate Dehydrogenase** — NAD⁺
2. **α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase** — NAD⁺
3. **Succinate Dehydrogenase** — FAD
4. **Malate Dehydrogenase** — NAD⁺

Three use **NAD⁺**; one (Succinate DH) uses **FAD**, which is why it is the only membrane-bound enzyme (Complex II). *(3 marks)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU ESE Standard):** *Answer any ONE full question. Each question carries 14 marks and is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **eight sequential reactions** of the TCA cycle. In your answer, name the enzyme, substrate, and product for each step, and identify the energy-yielding steps. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Step | Substrate → Product | Enzyme | Energy Yield |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Acetyl-CoA + OAA + H₂O → Citrate + CoA | Citrate Synthase | — |
| 2 | Citrate → cis-Aconitate → Isocitrate | Aconitase | — |
| 3 | Isocitrate + NAD⁺ → α-KG + CO₂ + NADH | Isocitrate DH | **1 NADH** |
| 4 | α-KG + NAD⁺ + CoA → Succinyl-CoA + CO₂ + NADH | α-KG DH Complex | **1 NADH** |
| 5 | Succinyl-CoA + GDP + Pᵢ → Succinate + GTP + CoA | Succinyl-CoA Synthetase | **1 GTP** |
| 6 | Succinate + FAD → Fumarate + FADH₂ | Succinate DH | **1 FADH₂** |
| 7 | Fumarate + H₂O → Malate | Fumarase | — |
| 8 | Malate + NAD⁺ → OAA + NADH | Malate DH | **1 NADH** |

**Energy tally per turn:** 3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP. The **regeneration of OAA** in Step 8 makes the cycle catalytic. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b)** Calculate the **total ATP yield** from the complete aerobic oxidation of one mole of glucose. Use the modern P/O ratio of 2.5 for NADH and 1.5 for FADH₂. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Energy carriers produced:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glycolysis:} &\quad 2 \text{ NADH} + 2 \text{ ATP (net)} \\
\text{PDH Complex:} &\quad 2 \text{ NADH} \\
\text{TCA Cycle (2 turns):} &\quad 6 \text{ NADH} + 2 \text{ FADH}_2 + 2 \text{ GTP}
\end{aligned}
$$

ATP equivalents:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From NADH} &= (2 + 2 + 6) \times 2.5 = 10 \times 2.5 = 25 \text{ ATP} \\
\text{From FADH}_2 &= 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \text{ ATP} \\
\text{Substrate-level (ATP + GTP)} &= 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ ATP} \\
\hline
\text{Net Total} &= 25 + 3 + 4 = \boxed{32 \text{ ATP per mole of glucose}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **regulation of the TCA cycle** with reference to its three key regulatory enzymes and the effect of energy charge on the cycle. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The TCA cycle is primarily regulated at three **irreversible, exergonic** steps:

1. **Citrate Synthase (Step 1):**
   - *Activated by:* ADP (low energy signal)
   - *Inhibited by:* Citrate (product inhibition), ATP, Succinyl-CoA
   - Function: Prevents over-accumulation of citrate when downstream steps are saturated.

2. **Isocitrate Dehydrogenase (Step 3) — Major Rate-Limiting Step:**
   - *Activated by:* ADP, Ca²⁺ (especially in muscle during contraction)
   - *Inhibited by:* ATP and NADH (high energy charge)
   - This is the **primary control point** linking TCA flux to cellular energy demand.

3. **α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase (Step 4):**
   - *Activated by:* Ca²⁺
   - *Inhibited by:* NADH and Succinyl-CoA (product inhibition)

**Energy Charge Logic:** When [ATP] is high and [ADP] is low, the cell does not need more ATP — the cycle is **downregulated**. When [ATP] is low and [ADP] is high, the cycle is **upregulated** to replenish ATP via oxidative phosphorylation. Ca²⁺ signaling (e.g., in contracting muscle) overrides inhibition to allow rapid ATP generation. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b)** Write short notes on: **(i) Anaplerotic reactions** and **(ii) The glyoxylate cycle** in microorganisms. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Anaplerotic Reactions (3.5 marks):**
These are "filling-up" reactions that replenish TCA intermediates that have been diverted into biosynthetic pathways. Key examples:
- **Pyruvate Carboxylase:** Pyruvate + CO₂ + ATP → Oxaloacetate (most important anaplerotic enzyme in liver and kidney).
- **PEP Carboxylase:** PEP + CO₂ → Oxaloacetate (in bacteria and plants).
- **Transamination:** Glutamate + Pyruvate ⇌ α-KG + Alanine (refills α-KG pool).

Without anaplerosis, the cycle would stall due to depletion of OAA.

**(ii) The Glyoxylate Cycle (3.5 marks):**
A variant of the TCA cycle found in **bacteria, fungi, and plants** (NOT in animals) that allows net synthesis of carbohydrates from fatty acids or acetate. It **bypasses the two CO₂-releasing steps** (Steps 3 and 4 of TCA) using two unique enzymes:
- **Isocitrate Lyase:** Isocitrate → Succinate + Glyoxylate
- **Malate Synthase:** Glyoxylate + Acetyl-CoA → Malate

**Net result:** 2 Acetyl-CoA + NAD⁺ + 2 FAD → Succinate + NADH + FADH₂. The succinate is converted to oxaloacetate, then to PEP, then to glucose via gluconeogenesis — enabling microorganisms to grow on **two-carbon substrates** like acetate or ethanol.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Stoichiometry Slip:** Students often forget to *multiply TCA cycle products by 2* (since one glucose yields 2 Acetyl-CoA). Always state "per 2 turns" or "per glucose."
> 2. **P/O Ratio Trap:** Using outdated values (3 ATP/NADH and 2 ATP/FADH₂) gives the obsolete 38 ATP answer. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires the **modern 2.5/1.5** values.
> 3. **Enzyme Naming:** *α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase* is a multi-enzyme complex (TPP, lipoate, FAD, CoA, NAD⁺) — do not call it a single enzyme.
> 4. **Confusing Citrate vs. Isocitrate:** Aconitase dehydrates citrate to aconitate, then rehydrates to **isocitrate** — not directly to isocitrate. State the cis-aconitate intermediate.
> 5. **Glyoxylate Cycle Trap:** This cycle is **absent in animals** — citing it in mammalian context is a guaranteed mark-loss.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The TCA cycle is **aerobic**, **mitochondrial** (matrix), and **amphibolic**.
- ✅ One glucose = **2 Acetyl-CoA** = **2 turns of TCA** = **4 CO₂ released**.
- ✅ Per turn: **3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP**; per glucose: **6 NADH + 2 FADH₂ + 2 GTP**.
- ✅ Total net ATP per glucose (modern P/O) = **32 ATP**.
- ✅ Metabolic efficiency ≈ **34%** (energy captured as ATP).
- ✅ The **first step (Citrate Synthase)** is the committed, condensation step.
- ✅ **Isocitrate Dehydrogenase** = primary rate-limiting enzyme; activated by **ADP and Ca²⁺**.
- ✅ **Succinate Dehydrogenase** is the only membrane-bound TCA enzyme = **Complex II of ETC**.
- ✅ Malonate is a classical **competitive inhibitor** of Succinate DH.
- ✅ **Fluoroacetate** inhibits Aconitase (via fluorocitrate accumulation) — classic KTU poisoning question.
- ✅ **Anaplerotic reactions** (Pyruvate Carboxylase) refill cycle intermediates; without them, the cycle stalls.
- ✅ The **Glyoxylate shunt** (Isocitrate Lyase + Malate Synthase) bypasses the 2 CO₂ steps in microorganisms and plants — **not in animals**.
- ✅ Modern P/O ratios: **NADH → 2.5 ATP**; **FADH₂ → 1.5 ATP** (NOT 3 and 2).
- ✅ ΔG°′ per Acetyl-CoA oxidized = **−20 kcal/mol**; ΔG°′ of ATP hydrolysis = **−7.3 kcal/mol**.
- ✅ Five cofactors of α-KG DH and PDH: **TPP, Lipoamide, FAD, CoA, NAD⁺** (mnemonic: **"Tender Loving Care For Nancy"**).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport Across Cell Membranes - Passive Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Transport Across Cell Membranes - Passive Transport # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Passive Transport** is the net movement of molecular species (ions, small metabolites, gases, water) across a biological membrane **down the electrochemical potential gradient** (i.e., from a region of higher chemical/electrochemical potential to a region of lower potential) **without the direct expenditure of metabolic energy (ATP hydrolysis)**. The driving force is purely thermodynamic — a gradient in concentration, electrical potential, or hydrostatic pressure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):** Passive transport is a thermodynamically spontaneous process (ΔG < 0) governed by the Second Law of Thermodynamics. It includes three principal sub-mechanisms: > 1. **Simple Diffusion** (passage directly through the lipid bilayer) > 2. **Facilitated Diffusion** (carrier proteins / channel proteins) > 3. **Osmosis** (net water movement across a semipermeable membrane) The mathematical statement of spontaneity is captured by the Gibbs free energy change per mole of solute transported: $$\Delta G = RT \ln\left(\frac{C_2}{C_1}\right) + zF\Delta\psi$$ For passive transport, $\Delta G < 0$, meaning transport proceeds spontaneously from side 1 (high) to side 2 (low). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Rolling Hill":** > Imagine a ball placed on a smooth hill. The ball rolls **downhill** by itself without anyone pushing it. The height difference is the **driving force**; the smooth surface represents the **membrane**; and gravity is the analogue of the **chemical potential gradient**. > - The **membrane** is the hill surface — some surfaces are slick (lipid-soluble molecules slide easily = simple diffusion), while others have grooves or tunnels (proteins = facilitated diffusion). > - **Passive transport** = ball rolling downhill (ΔG < 0, no ATP needed). > - **Active transport** = pushing the ball **uphill** (requires ATP / energy). A second analogy: A crowded room with people wanting to exit through a door. If the door is open and wide, people stream out rapidly (**simple diffusion** through lipid bilayer of small nonpolar molecules). If the door is narrow, only some people fit and the flow slows (**size/charge selectivity**). If the door has a security guard checking IDs, the rate is now controlled by the guard's speed — saturable kinetics (**facilitated diffusion via carriers**). ## 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Constants for KTU 2024:** > > | Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit | > |--------|----------|--------------| > | $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** | > | $F$ | Faraday Constant | **96,485 C·mol⁻¹** (≈ 9.649 × 10⁴) | > | $T$ | Absolute Temperature | **310 K** (physiological, 37 °C) | > | $z$ | Valency of ion | dimensionless integer | > | $K$ | Partition Coefficient | dimensionless (oil/water ratio) | > | $P$ | Permeability Coefficient | **cm·s⁻¹** (or m·s⁻¹) | > | $D$ | Diffusion Coefficient | **cm²·s⁻¹** (≈ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁶ for small solutes in water) | ## 1.4 Why Passive Transport Matters in Biochemical Engineering In bioreactor design, downstream processing, and bioseparations, the rate at which **nutrients (O₂, glucose, amino acids) enter cells** and **metabolic wastes (CO₂, lactate) leave cells** is governed by passive transport. Oxygen delivery to large-scale cell cultures is **diffusion-limited** (the critical parameter in scaling up mammalian cell bioreactors). Membrane transport kinetics directly determine: - **Oxygen transfer rate (OTR)** in aerated fermenters - **Substrate uptake rates** in microbial kinetics - **Drug delivery design** (liposomal carriers, transdermal patches) - **Dialysis and ultrafiltration** in artificial kidneys - **Permeability selection** in membrane bioreactors --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Universal Pillars of Passive Transport ### Pillar I — The Driving Force (Why transport occurs) The driving force is the **gradient in electrochemical potential** $\tilde{\mu}$. For an uncharged solute: $$\Delta\mu = RT \ln(C_2/C_1)$$ For a charged species (ion) with valence $z$: $$\Delta\tilde{\mu} = RT \ln(C_2/C_1) + zF(\psi_2 - \psi_1)$$ **Key Insight:** Transport continues until $\Delta\tilde{\mu} = 0$ (equilibrium). No more net flux thereafter. ### Pillar II — The Resistance (How fast transport occurs) The resistance is the **permeability** of the membrane, which depends on: 1. The **diffusion coefficient** of the solute inside the membrane ($D_m$) 2. The **partition coefficient** between membrane and aqueous phase ($K$) 3. The **membrane thickness** ($\delta$) Combined, the **permeability coefficient** is: $$P = \frac{K \cdot D_m}{\delta}$$ This single expression ties together the three key membrane properties. ## 2.2 Detailed Mechanism Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Simple Diffusion (Lipid Bilayer Pathway) **Step-by-step operational logic:** 1. **Partitioning (Entry):** Solute dissolves from the bulk aqueous phase into the hydrophobic interior of the lipid bilayer. Equilibrium governed by partition coefficient $K = C_{m}/C_{aq}$. 2. **Diffusion (Transit):** Solute diffuses across the bilayer thickness $\delta$ via random molecular motion. Flux given by Fick's First Law. 3. **Desorption (Exit):** Solute partitions back out into the receiving aqueous phase. > [!NOTE] > **Membrane Crossing (Overton's Rule):** Small, nonpolar, hydrophobic molecules (O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones, small alcohols) cross fastest. The permeability correlates with the **oil-water partition coefficient** — the higher the lipid solubility, the higher the permeability. ### 2.2.2 Facilitated Diffusion (Protein-Mediated Pathway) Uses transmembrane proteins: - **Channel proteins** (e.g., aquaporins for water, voltage-gated Na⁺ channels): form continuous aqueous pores; **do not** bind the substrate tightly; show **non-saturable, fast** kinetics. - **Carrier proteins** (e.g., GLUT1 glucose transporter): undergo conformational change to shuttle substrate; **bind** substrate with finite affinity; show **saturable Michaelis-Menten-like kinetics**. **Michaelis-Menten (Carrier-Mediated) Flux Equation:** $$J = \frac{J_{max} \cdot C}{K_m + C}$$ where: - $J_{max}$ = maximum flux (when all carriers saturated) - $K_m$ = substrate concentration at half-maximal flux (analogous to enzyme $K_m$) - $C$ = external substrate concentration ### 2.2.3 Osmosis (Water-Specific Passive Transport) Net diffusion of **water** across a **semipermeable membrane** from a region of high water chemical potential (low solute) to low water chemical potential (high solute). **Van't Hoff Equation (for dilute solutions):** $$\Pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$$ where $\Pi$ is the osmotic pressure, $C$ is the solute molar concentration, and $i$ is the van't Hoff factor (number of dissociated particles per formula unit). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table — these are the equations that appear in 90% of KTU ESE questions on this module.** | # | Equation | Application | Key Variables | Units | |---|----------|-------------|---------------|-------| | 1 | $J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law (diffusive flux) | $J$: flux, $D$: diffusivity, $C$: concentration | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹, m²·s⁻¹, mol·m⁻³, m | | 2 | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law (unsteady state) | time-dependent concentration field | s, m²·s⁻¹, m | | 3 | $J = P \cdot \Delta C$ | Steady-state membrane flux | $P = KD_m/\delta$, $\Delta C$: conc. difference | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹, m·s⁻¹, mol·m⁻³ | | 4 | $P = \dfrac{K D_m}{\delta}$ | Permeability-coefficient definition | $K$: partition, $D_m$: in-membrane diffusivity, $\delta$: thickness | m·s⁻¹ | | 5 | $\Pi = iCRT$ | Van't Hoff osmotic pressure | $i$: van't Hoff factor | Pa (= J·m⁻³) | | 6 | $\Delta G = RT\ln(C_2/C_1) + zF\Delta\psi$ | Gibbs free energy of transport | spontaneity criterion | J·mol⁻¹ | | 7 | $J = \dfrac{J_{max} C}{K_m + C}$ | Carrier-mediated (facilitated) flux | saturable kinetics | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹, M, M | | 8 | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6\pi \mu r}$ | Stokes–Einstein diffusivity | $k_B$: Boltzmann const, $\mu$: viscosity, $r$: hydrodynamic radius | m²·s⁻¹ | | 9 | $P_{app} = \dfrac{dQ/dt}{A \cdot C_0}$ | Apparent permeability (drug delivery) | $dQ/dt$: cumulative permeated rate | cm·s⁻¹ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **NEVER** use the vertical bar `|` inside these tables. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value (e.g., $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}\vert_{x=0}$). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Transdermal drug delivery and oral absorption are governed by the permeability coefficient $P$ across the intestinal epithelium. The Biopharmaceutics Classification System (BCS) categorizes drugs by $P$ and solubility. - **Bioreactor Scaling:** The **critical oxygen delivery distance** $L_c = \sqrt{D \cdot C^* / q_{O_2}}$ determines the maximum cell-clump radius before the core becomes anoxic. - **Hemodialysis:** Solute clearance from blood follows Fickian diffusion across a synthetic membrane; flux $J = P \cdot (C_{blood} - C_{dialysate})$. - **Wastewater Treatment:** In MBRs (membrane bioreactors), the membrane acts as a selective barrier — small ions pass passively, larger molecules (proteins, polysaccharides) are rejected. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fick's First Law from Thermodynamic Principles ### Goal Derive the steady-state diffusive flux equation: $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ ### Starting Premise (Fick's 1855 Analogy to Fourier's Heat Law) Fick postulated that the amount of substance diffusing per unit area per unit time (flux $J$) is **proportional to the concentration gradient** normal to the cross-section. **Step 1 — State the proportionality:** Assume linearity between flux and gradient (justified empirically for dilute systems): $$J \propto -\frac{dC}{dx}$$ **Step 2 — Introduce the proportionality constant $D$ (diffusivity):** $$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$ The negative sign encodes the physical fact that solute moves from **high to low** concentration (gradient is negative in the direction of flow). **Step 3 — Derive from random walk (Einstein's derivation):** Consider $N$ particles, each executing 1-D random walk with step length $\lambda$ and time step $\tau$. After $n$ steps: $$\langle x^2 \rangle = n\lambda^2$$ **Step 4 — Relate to mean square displacement:** Time $t = n\tau \Rightarrow n = t/\tau$, so: $$\langle x^2 \rangle = \frac{\lambda^2}{\tau} t$$ **Step 5 — Define $D$:** $$D = \frac{\lambda^2}{2\tau}$$ (This factor of 2 arises from 1-D; in 3-D, $\langle r^2 \rangle = 6Dt$.) **Step 6 — Build the Gaussian concentration profile:** The probability distribution for a particle released at origin after time $t$: $$P(x,t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Differentiate twice to recover Fick's Second Law:** $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial x^2}$$ Thus Fick's Second Law is a direct consequence of the central limit theorem applied to molecular randomness. **Fick's First Law** is its steady-state form (set $\partial C / \partial t = 0$). $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} \quad \text{and} \quad J = -D\frac{dC}{dx}\;}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Permeability Coefficient from Partition–Diffusion Model ### Goal Show that $P = \dfrac{K D_m}{\delta}$ ### Step 1 — Solute enters the membrane at $x = 0$ from bulk aqueous phase of concentration $C_1$. At the membrane surface, the concentration **inside** the membrane is $C_{m,1} = K \cdot C_1$ (partition equilibrium). **Step 2 — Solute exits at $x = \delta$ into aqueous phase of concentration $C_2$.** Inside concentration at exit: $C_{m,2} = K \cdot C_2$. **Step 3 — Apply Fick's First Law inside the membrane (assume linear gradient in steady state):** $$J = -D_m \frac{dC_m}{dx} \approx -D_m \frac{C_{m,2} - C_{m,1}}{\delta - 0} = D_m \frac{C_{m,1} - C_{m,2}}{\delta}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute partition relation:** $$J = D_m \frac{K C_1 - K C_2}{\delta} = \frac{K D_m}{\delta} (C_1 - C_2)$$ **Step 5 — Define permeability coefficient $P$:** $$\boxed{\;P = \frac{K D_m}{\delta} \quad \text{so that} \quad J = P \cdot (C_1 - C_2)\;}$$ **Physical meaning:** Permeability is high when the molecule is **lipid-soluble** (high $K$), moves **fast inside** the membrane (high $D_m$), and the membrane is **thin** (small $\delta$). ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Unsteady Diffusion into a Membrane (Transient Solution) ### Goal Solve $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ with initial/boundary conditions to obtain time-lag $\theta_L$. ### Initial and Boundary Conditions $$C(x,0) = 0, \quad C(0,t) = C_0, \quad C(\delta,t) = 0 \quad \text{(sink)}$$ ### Step 1 — Steady-state solution (large $t$): Linear profile: $C_{ss}(x) = C_0(1 - x/\delta)$, giving $J_{ss} = D C_0/\delta$. ### Step 2 — Define cumulative amount permeated: $$Q(t) = \int_0^t J(0,t')\,dt'$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Laplace transform $\tilde{C}(x,s) = \int_0^\infty C(x,t)e^{-st}dt$: $$D \frac{d^2 \tilde{C}}{dx^2} = s \tilde{C} - C(x,0) = s\tilde{C}$$ $$\Rightarrow \frac{d^2 \tilde{C}}{dx^2} = \frac{s}{D}\tilde{C}$$ General solution: $\tilde{C}(x,s) = A e^{x\sqrt{s/D}} + B e^{-x\sqrt{s/D}}$ **Step 4 — Apply BCs:** - $C(0,t) = C_0 \Rightarrow \tilde{C}(0,s) = C_0/s$ - $C(\delta,t) = 0 \Rightarrow \tilde{C}(\delta,s) = 0$ Solving: $$A = -\frac{C_0}{s}\frac{e^{-\delta\sqrt{s/D}}}{e^{\delta\sqrt{s/D}} - e^{-\delta\sqrt{s/D}}} = -\frac{C_0 e^{-\delta\sqrt{s/D}}}{2s\sinh(\delta\sqrt{s/D})}$$ $$B = \frac{C_0}{2s\sinh(\delta\sqrt{s/D})}$$ ### Step 5 — Flux at $x=0$: $$J(0,t) = -D\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} \Rightarrow \tilde{J}(0,s) = \frac{D C_0}{\delta}\cdot \frac{1}{s}\cdot \frac{\delta\sqrt{s/D}}{\sinh(\delta\sqrt{s/D})}$$ ### Step 6 — Invert Laplace transform in the long-time limit (use expansion $1/\sinh(u) \approx 1/u - u/6$): $$J(0,t) \approx \frac{D C_0}{\delta}\left[1 + 2\sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1)^n \exp\!\left(-\frac{n^2 \pi^2 D t}{\delta^2}\right)\right]$$ As $t \to \infty$, all exponentials vanish: $$J_{ss} = \frac{D C_0}{\delta}$$ ### Step 7 — Extrapolate $Q(t)$ to zero to obtain the **time-lag $\theta_L$:** $$Q(t) = J_{ss}\!\left(t - \frac{\delta^2}{6D}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\;\theta_L = \frac{\delta^2}{6D}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance of $\theta_L$:** Experimentally measuring the time-lag (by plotting cumulative amount permeated vs. time and extrapolating to $Q = 0$) gives access to the **diffusion coefficient inside the membrane $D_m$** — a quantity otherwise very hard to measure. This is the principle behind the **time-lag method** for membrane characterization. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Passive Transport Modes | Feature | Simple Diffusion | Facilitated Diffusion (Carrier) | Facilitated Diffusion (Channel) | Osmosis | |---------|-----------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|---------| | **Energy Required (ATP)** | No | No | No | No | | **Direction** | Down gradient | Down gradient | Down gradient | Down water-potential gradient | | **Membrane component** | Lipid bilayer directly | Carrier protein | Channel protein | Aquaporins / lipid bilayer | | **Kinetics** | Linear ($J = P\Delta C$) | Saturable (Michaelis-Menten) | Non-saturable (linear at low C) | Linear at low $\Pi$ | | **Saturation ($J_{max}$)?** | No | **Yes** | No (saturates only with channels) | No | | **Specificity** | Low (Overton rule) | **High** (stereospecific) | High | Low (water + some small solutes) | | **Examples** | O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones, ethanol | Glucose (GLUT1), amino acids | Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻ channels | Water across RBC membrane | | **Temperature dependence** | Modest ($E_a \sim 10$–30 kJ/mol) | **High** ($E_a \sim 50$–100 kJ/mol) | Moderate | Modest | | **Inhibitors** | N/A | Competitive, non-competitive | Channel blockers (e.g., tetrodotoxin) | Hg²⁺ blocks aquaporins | | **Effect of membrane fluidity** | Strong | Moderate | Weak | Moderate | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Passive Transport ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Passive Transport
No ATP required] --> B{Does solute
dissolve in lipid?} B -- Yes, no protein needed --> C[Simple Diffusion
Overton's Rule] B -- No, needs protein help --> D[Facilitated Diffusion] C --> C1[O2, CO2, N2, steroid hormones, ethanol] C --> C2[Permeability P = KD_m / delta] C --> C3[Linear kinetics: J = P * dC] D --> D1{Type of
transmembrane protein} D1 -- Carrier protein --> E[Carrier-Mediated Diffusion] D1 -- Channel protein --> F[Channel-Mediated Diffusion] E --> E1[Conformational change] E --> E2[Saturable Michaelis-Menten] E --> E3[Glucose GLUT1, amino acids] F --> F1[Continuous aqueous pore] F --> F2[Ion flux, fast, non-saturable] F --> F3[Na, K, Ca, Cl channels] A --> G[Osmosis] G --> G1[Water only] G --> G2[Driven by water chemical potential] G --> G3[Van't Hoff: Pi = iCRT] G --> G4[Aquaporins accelerate] classDef main fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef simple fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef carrier fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef channel fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef osmosis fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class A main class C,C1,C2,C3 simple class E,E1,E2,E3 carrier class F,F1,F2,F3 channel class G,G1,G2,G3,G4 osmosis ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: Solute Crossing a Membrane by Simple Diffusion ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Bulk Phase 1
Concentration C1 high] -- Partition K --> B[Membrane Surface x=0
Cm1 = K*C1] B -- Diffuse across thickness delta
Fick: J = -D dC/dx --> C[Membrane Surface x=delta
Cm2 = K*C2] C -- Desorption --> D[Bulk Phase 2
Concentration C2 low] style A fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style D fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd ``` ## 4.3 Carrier Protein Conformational Cycle (Facilitated Diffusion) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> OpenToOutside OpenToOutside --> SubstrateBound : S binds at high C SubstrateBound --> Occluded : Conformational change Occluded --> OpenToInside : Carrier flips OpenToInside --> SubstrateReleased : S dissociates at low C SubstrateReleased --> OpenToOutside : Reset note right of SubstrateBound Saturable kinetics: J = Jmax * C / Km + C end note ``` ## 4.4 Concentration Profile Across Membrane (Steady State) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bulk1 [Bulk Phase 1: C1 high] A1[C1] --> A2[Boundary layer 1] end subgraph Membrane [Lipid Bilayer: thickness delta] B1[Cm1 = K*C1] --> B2[Linear gradient inside] --> B3[Cm2 = K*C2] end subgraph Bulk2 [Bulk Phase 2: C2 low] C1[Boundary layer 2] --> C2[C2] end A2 --> B1 B3 --> C1 style Membrane fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The two **plateau regions** on either side of the membrane are the bulk aqueous phases; the **steep drop** inside the membrane reflects the partition coefficient $K$ and the small thickness $\delta$ (typically ~5 nm). The slope inside the membrane is $\Delta C_m / \delta = K \Delta C_{bulk} / \delta$. --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define passive transport. List the three major types of passive transport across biological membranes with one example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Passive transport (1 mark):** Passive transport is the spontaneous movement of solutes across a biological membrane **down their electrochemical potential gradient** without the expenditure of metabolic energy (ATP). The Gibbs free energy change for the process is negative ($\Delta G < 0$). **Three types (1.5 marks):** 1. **Simple diffusion** — direct passage through the lipid bilayer; example: O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones. 2. **Facilitated diffusion** — carrier or channel protein-mediated; example: glucose uptake via GLUT1 transporter. 3. **Osmosis** — net water movement across a semipermeable membrane; example: water reabsorption in kidney tubules. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** All three modes are non-ATP-requiring and thermodynamically spontaneous. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Define each term with units.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$ - **Statement (1 mark):** The diffusive flux of a species is proportional to the concentration gradient in the direction of transport, with the proportionality constant being the diffusion coefficient. - **Term definitions (1.5 marks):** - $J$ = molar flux [mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹] - $D$ = diffusion coefficient [m²·s⁻¹] (typical solutes in water: $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s) - $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ = concentration gradient [mol·m⁻⁴] - **Negative sign (0.5 mark):** Indicates flux flows from high to low concentration (down-gradient). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) Derive Fick's First Law of Diffusion from the kinetic theory of random molecular motion. Show all steps clearly. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark) — Postulate:** In an isotropic medium, the net flux of molecules in the +x direction is the difference between the flux crossing a plane at $x=0$ from the left and from the right. **Step 2 (1 mark) — Define local concentration at distance $\lambda$ (mean free path):** Let $C(x)$ be the local concentration. The number of molecules per unit volume crossing a unit area at $x=0$ from the left per unit time is $\frac{1}{6}n \bar{v}$ for 3-D (factor $1/6$ from averaging directions). **Step 3 (2 marks) — Net flux:** $$J_x = \frac{1}{6} \bar{v}\left[C(-\lambda) - C(+\lambda)\right]$$ **Step 4 (1 mark) — Taylor expand** $C(\pm \lambda) \approx C(0) \pm \lambda \dfrac{dC}{dx}\bigg|_0$: $$J_x = \frac{1}{6}\bar{v}\left[-2\lambda \frac{dC}{dx}\right] = -\frac{\bar{v}\lambda}{3}\frac{dC}{dx}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark) — Identify $D$:** The mean-free-path theory gives $D = \dfrac{1}{3}\bar{v}\lambda$, hence: $$\boxed{\;J = -D\frac{dC}{dx}\;}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark) — Limitation:** Valid only for **dilute, ideal, isothermal systems**; breaks down for concentrated solutions, polymers, or reactive solutes. --- **(b) Explain the partition–diffusion model for solute permeability across a lipid bilayer. Derive the expression $P = KD_m / \delta$. Compute the permeability coefficient for a drug with $K = 10$, $D_m = 5\times10^{-8}$ cm²/s, and $\delta = 5$ nm. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Model description:** According to the partition–diffusion model, transport across a lipid bilayer occurs in three sequential steps: 1. **Partitioning** of solute from bulk water into the membrane surface (governed by $K$) 2. **Diffusion** across the membrane thickness $\delta$ (governed by $D_m$) 3. **Desorption** into the receiving aqueous phase **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Boundary concentrations inside membrane:** $C_{m,1} = K C_1$ at $x=0$ and $C_{m,2} = K C_2$ at $x=\delta$. **Step 3 (1 mark) — Apply Fick's First Law inside membrane** (assume linear steady-state profile): $$J = -D_m \frac{dC_m}{dx} = D_m \frac{C_{m,1} - C_{m,2}}{\delta} = \frac{K D_m}{\delta}(C_1 - C_2)$$ **Step 4 (1 mark) — Define permeability:** $$\boxed{\;P = \frac{K D_m}{\delta}\;}$$ **Step 5 (2 marks) — Numerical calculation:** Convert units consistently: - $K = 10$ (dimensionless) - $D_m = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ cm²/s - $\delta = 5$ nm $= 5 \times 10^{-7}$ cm $$P = \frac{(10)(5 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s})}{5 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 1\,\text{cm/s}$$ **Result:** $P = 1$ cm/s — a typical value for a moderately lipophilic drug. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) Explain the Michaelis-Menten kinetics of carrier-mediated (facilitated) diffusion across a cell membrane. Sketch a plot of flux $J$ versus substrate concentration $C$ and identify $J_{max}$ and $K_m$. Compare this with simple diffusion. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Carrier mechanism:** Carrier proteins (e.g., GLUT1 for glucose) bind the substrate on one side of the membrane, undergo a conformational change, and release it on the other side. Because binding is reversible and finite in capacity, the carrier can become **saturated** at high substrate concentrations. **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Derive flux equation:** Let $n_T$ = total number of carrier sites per unit membrane area. At steady state, applying the quasi-steady-state assumption (analogous to enzyme kinetics): $$J = \frac{k_{cat} \, n_T \, C}{K_m + C} = \frac{J_{max}\, C}{K_m + C}$$ where $J_{max} = k_{cat} n_T$ and $K_m = (k_{-1} + k_{cat})/k_1$. **Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Sketch of plot:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[At low C: linear region
J approx Jmax*C/Km] --> B[At C = Km: J = Jmax/2] --> C[At high C: plateau at Jmax
Saturated] style A fill:#d1e7dd style B fill:#fff3cd style C fill:#f8d7da ``` **Key feature:** The curve is **hyperbolic** (Michaelis-Menten type), with $J_{max}$ being the asymptotic plateau and $K_m$ being the concentration at half-saturation. **Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Comparison with simple diffusion:** | Feature | Simple Diffusion | Facilitated (Carrier) | |---|---|---| | Equation | $J = P \cdot \Delta C$ | $J = J_{max} C / (K_m + C)$ | | Curve shape | **Linear** | **Hyperbolic** | | Saturation | **No** | **Yes** | | Specificity | Low (Overton) | High (stereospecific) | | Inhibitors | None | Competitive/non-competitive | | Activation energy | Low (~10–30 kJ/mol) | High (~50–100 kJ/mol) | | Temperature effect | Modest | Pronounced (protein dependent) | **Step 5 (1 mark) — Conclusion:** Carrier-mediated transport adds **specificity, saturability, and regulation** at the cost of higher sensitivity to temperature and inhibitors. --- **(b) An industrial fermentation broth contains glucose at 0.05 M inside the cell and 0.005 M outside. The membrane permeability for glucose is $P = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ cm/s. Calculate: (i) the diffusive flux out of the cell, and (ii) the Gibbs free energy change per mole for glucose transport. Assume $T = 310$ K and $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark) — Given data:** - $C_{in} = 0.05$ M - $C_{out} = 0.005$ M - $P = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ cm/s - $T = 310$ K, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ - Glucose is uncharged ($z = 0$) **Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute flux (part i):** Convert permeability to SI: $P = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$ m/s. $$J = P \cdot (C_{in} - C_{out}) = (1.5 \times 10^{-8})(0.05 - 0.005)\,\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ $$J = (1.5 \times 10^{-8})(0.045) = 6.75 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ Or in CGS: $J = (1.5 \times 10^{-6})(0.045) = 6.75 \times 10^{-8}$ mol·cm⁻²·s⁻¹. **Result:** $\boxed{J = 6.75 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}}$ **Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute $\Delta G$ (part ii):** $$\Delta G = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$$ $$\Delta G = (8.314)(310) \ln\!\left(\frac{0.005}{0.05}\right) = 2577.34 \times \ln(0.1) = 2577.34 \times (-2.3026)$$ $$\boxed{\Delta G = -5936\,\text{J/mol} \approx -5.94\,\text{kJ/mol}}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark) — Interpretation:** Since $\Delta G < 0$, glucose transport **out of the cell is spontaneous** (i.e., cell would lose glucose unless actively pumped in). **Step 5 (1 mark) — Bioengineering remark:** In a fed-batch bioreactor, if intracellular glucose is higher than extracellular, glucose passively leaks out — wasting the carbon source. This motivates the use of **active transport systems** or **permeabilized-cell mutants** in industrial strain engineering. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes (losing 2–4 marks each):** > > 1. **Sign error in Fick's Law:** Writing $J = +D(dC/dx)$ instead of $J = -D(dC/dx)$. The negative sign is **essential** — without it, the law would predict flux up the gradient, which is **impossible** for passive transport. > > 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing cm and m in the same calculation. **Always** convert all lengths and concentrations to a single unit system (SI or CGS) before substituting. For $P$, common units are **cm/s** and **m/s**; for $D$, **cm²/s** and **m²/s**. > > 3. **Confusing $J_{max}$ with $V_{max}$:** In carrier-mediated transport, $J_{max}$ has units of flux (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹), not concentration. Forgetting to multiply by membrane area $A$ when computing total transport rate. > > 4. **Misapplying Van't Hoff to concentrated solutions:** $\Pi = iCRT$ is valid only for **dilute, ideal** solutions. For concentrated or electrolytic solutions, use the full osmotic virial expansion or Pitzer model. > > 5. **Forgetting the partition coefficient in membrane transport:** Setting $K = 1$ (assuming equal solubility in membrane and water) is wrong for most drugs. Always check $K$ from $\log P$ tables. > > 6. **Mixing up osmosis and diffusion:** Osmosis is **water movement** driven by a *water potential* gradient, not a solute concentration gradient. The two are related but thermodynamically distinct. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Card — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:** - **Passive transport** = movement down gradient; **no ATP**; **ΔG < 0**; entropy-driven. - **Three modes:** Simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion (carrier + channel), osmosis. - **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ (steady state). - **Fick's Second Law:** $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ (unsteady state). - **Permeability coefficient:** $P = \dfrac{K D_m}{\delta}$ — three membrane properties in one. - **Membrane flux:** $J = P \cdot \Delta C$ at steady state. - **Carrier-mediated kinetics:** $J = \dfrac{J_{max} C}{K_m + C}$ — Michaelis-Menten, **saturable**. - **Channel kinetics:** approximately linear, non-saturable (until channel density limits). - **Stokes-Einstein:** $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6\pi \mu r}$ — bigger molecules diffuse slower; higher T → higher D. - **Van't Hoff osmotic pressure:** $\Pi = iCRT$ — valid for dilute solutions only. - **Time-lag method:** $\theta_L = \dfrac{\delta^2}{6D}$ — experimentally measures $D_m$ inside membrane. - **Overton's rule:** Permeability correlates with **lipid solubility** (octanol-water partition coefficient $\log P$). - **Direction of flux:** high chemical potential → low chemical potential (down-gradient). - **Charged species:** Use Nernst-Planck equation when both concentration and electric gradients act: $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx} - \dfrac{zFD}{RT} C \dfrac{d\psi}{dx}$. - **Equilibrium condition:** $\Delta \tilde{\mu} = 0$, i.e., $C_2/C_1 = e^{-zF\Delta\psi/RT}$ (Nernst equation for ions). - **SI vs CGS units:** $1$ cm/s $= 10^{-2}$ m/s; $1$ cm²/s $= 10^{-4}$ m²/s. - **Typical $D$ values:** small solutes in water $\sim 10^{-5}$ cm²/s; in lipid bilayer $\sim 10^{-8}$ cm²/s. - **Typical $P$ values:** O₂ $\sim 30$ cm/s; glucose $\sim 10^{-6}$ cm/s; ions $\sim 10^{-10}$ cm/s. - **Remember:** ΔG < 0 means **spontaneous**; ΔG > 0 means **non-spontaneous** (would require active transport). ---

topics.title: Active Transport and Facilitated Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Active Transport and Facilitated Diffusion ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Active Transport — Formal KTU Definition **Active Transport** is a mode of transmembrane solute movement in which a membrane-bound molecular machine (pump, translocase, or ATPase) moves a substrate **against its electrochemical potential gradient** using an exogenous energy source. The energy currency is either: - **Primary Active Transport:** Free energy released by ATP hydrolysis ($\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$). - **Secondary Active Transport:** Free energy stored in a pre-existing ion gradient (e.g., $\text{H}^+$ or $\text{Na}^+$ symport/antiport). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Active transport is *non-equilibrium thermodynamics in action* — it maintains a steady-state dis-equilibrium critical for nerve signaling, nutrient accumulation, and pH homeostasis. In bioreactor design, ignoring active uptake can underestimate microbial substrate consumption rates by **3–10×**. ### 1.2 Facilitated Diffusion — Formal KTU Definition **Facilitated Diffusion** is a carrier-mediated (or channel-mediated) transmembrane transport mechanism in which specific integral membrane proteins enhance the rate of solute movement **down its electrochemical gradient** without consuming metabolic energy. It exhibits: - **Saturation kinetics** (Michaelis–Menten behavior). - **Stereospecificity** (only certain isomers are transported). - **Competitive inhibition** by structural analogues. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat Ferry vs. Cargo Tug" Model > [!NOTE] > **Analogy for the Student:** > Imagine a river separating two towns. > > - **Simple Diffusion** = a person swimming across the river — slow, passive, follows the current. > - **Facilitated Diffusion** = a **passenger ferry** (boat with a captain). The captain (carrier protein) recognizes ticket-holders (specific molecules) and ferries them across, *but only in the direction of the river flow* (down gradient). The ferry has a *limited number of seats* → that's why the rate saturates. > - **Active Transport** = a **fuel-powered cargo tug** that hauls goods *upstream*, against the current, using diesel (ATP). It works against the natural flow because it's externally powered. | Property | Simple Diffusion | Facilitated Diffusion | Active Transport | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Energy Required | No | No | **Yes (ATP or ion gradient)** | | Direction | Down gradient | Down gradient | **Up gradient** | | Carrier Protein | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | | Saturation Kinetics | No | **Yes ($K_m$, $V_{max}$)** | Yes (pump kinetics) | | Specificity | Low | **High** | **High** | | Inhibitable | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | | Temperature Dependence | Low | High ($Q_{10} \approx 2$–$3$) | High ($Q_{10} \approx 2$–$4$) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism #### 2.1.1 Facilitated Diffusion via Carrier Proteins 1. **Recognition Step:** Substrate $S$ binds to the *exofacial* (outside-facing) binding site of the carrier protein with dissociation constant $K_m$. 2. **Conformational Change:** The carrier undergoes an allosteric shift (analogous to the enzyme in Michaelis–Menten kinetics). 3. **Translocation:** The binding pocket is exposed to the cytoplasmic side. 4. **Release Step:** Substrate dissociates into the cytoplasm (driven by the inside concentration being lower). 5. **Reset:** Carrier returns to its original conformation (no net energy cost; reset is *exergonic*). > [!NOTE] > **Why $K_m$ matters in Bioreactor Design:** The $K_m$ of a glucose transporter (e.g., *S. cerevisiae* Hxt1) determines the **monod half-saturation constant** when microbes uptake limiting carbon. Mistaking facilitated diffusion for active transport can collapse Monod kinetics in your mass-balance equations. #### 2.1.2 Primary Active Transport (P-type ATPase Example) 1. **Binding:** 3 $\text{Na}^+$ bind to the cytoplasmic domain of $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase. 2. **Phosphorylation:** ATP $\rightarrow$ ADP + $\text{P}_i$; $\text{P}_i$ attaches to an Asp residue of the pump. 3. **E1 → E2 Transition:** Conformational shift exposes the binding sites to the *extracellular* face. 4. **Release:** 3 $\text{Na}^+$ are expelled against a $\sim 140$ mM gradient. 5. **Counter-binding:** 2 $\text{K}^+$ bind from outside. 6. **Dephosphorylation:** Hydrolysis of phosphoenzyme releases $K^+$ inside. 7. **Reset:** E2 → E1, cycle restarts (3:2 stoichiometry per ATP). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The 3:2 charge asymmetry makes the pump *electrogenic* — it directly contributes to the **resting membrane potential** ($V_m \approx -70$ mV in neurons). In bioelectrochemical systems (microbial fuel cells), this electrogenicity is exploited to harvest microbial energy. #### 2.1.3 Secondary Active Transport - **Symport:** Substrate and ion move in the *same* direction (e.g., $\text{Na}^+$-glucose cotransporter SGLT1). - **Antiport:** Substrate and ion move in *opposite* directions (e.g., $\text{Na}^+/\text{Ca}^{2+}$ exchanger NCX).
