The driving force is the **electrochemical potential** of the coupling ion, not ATP directly. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Engineering Use | | :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law of Diffusion | Flux across membrane in absence of protein | | 2 | $J = \dfrac{J_{max}\,[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Michaelis–Menten for carriers | Predicts saturation of nutrient uptake | | 3 | $\Delta G = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{C_1}\right)$ | Free energy of concentration transfer | Minimum work for active transport | | 4 | $\Delta G = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{C_1}\right) + zF\Delta \psi$ | Electrochemical potential (Nernst) | Ion pumps, membrane potential | | 5 | $E_{Nernst} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[C]_{out}}{[C]_{in}}\right)$ | Nernst Equation | Equilibrium potential of single ion | | 6 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) | Resting membrane potential with multiple ions | | 7 | $\Delta G_{ATP} = -30.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | Standard free energy of ATP hydrolysis | Energy budget of active transport | | 8 | $J_{pump} = k_{cat}\,[E_{tot}] \cdot \dfrac{[S]}{K_{0.5}^S + [S]}$ | Hill/pump kinetics | Rate of primary active transport | | 9 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$ | Temperature coefficient | Distinguishes active vs. passive | | 10 | $\eta_{coupling} = \dfrac{\text{ions transported per ATP}}{\text{theoretical max}}$ | Coupling efficiency | Bioreactor energy audit | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Board Error:** Students frequently write Fick's Law inside the carrier kinetics table. **Do not** — Fick's law applies to *free* diffusion only. Carriers follow Michaelis–Menten. Mixing them = **2-mark penalty** in the ESE valuation key. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR):** Carrier-mediated uptake governs substrate flux into immobilized cells. The $K_m$ of the limiting transporter dictates the *critical dilution rate* $D_c$ to avoid washout. - **Bioprocess Scale-Up:** Active transport of metal ions (e.g., $\text{Fe}^{3+}$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$) by siderophores must be modeled in high-cell-density fermentation. - **Drug Delivery:** Liposomal drug carriers exploit facilitated diffusion of weak acids/bases through phospholipid bilayers. - **Microbial Fuel Cells:** Electrogenic active transport (e.g., in *Geobacter* and *Shewanella*) generates current densities of 0.5–1.2 mA/cm². - **Biosensors:** Glucose oxidase-based sensors depend on facilitated diffusion of $\text{O}_2$ to the enzyme layer. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Michaelis–Menten Kinetics for a Facilitated Diffusion Carrier Consider a carrier protein $C$ in a membrane of area $A$. Substrate $S$ binds on the outside, the complex $CS$ reorients, and $S$ is released inside. **Step 1 — Binding equilibrium (outside):** $$S_{out} + C \;\underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; CS$$ **Step 2 — Translocation rate (this is the rate-limiting step):** $$CS \;\xrightarrow{k_{2}}\; C + S_{in}$$ **Step 3 — Steady-state approximation on $[CS]$:** $$\dfrac{d[CS]}{dt} = k_1[S_{out}][C] - (k_{-1} + k_2)[CS] = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $[CS]$:** $$[CS] = \dfrac{k_1[S_{out}][C]}{k_{-1} + k_2} = \dfrac{[S_{out}][C]}{K_m}$$ where $K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$ is the **Michaelis constant** of the carrier (units: M or mol/L). **Step 5 — Mass conservation of carrier:** $$[C]_T = [C] + [CS]$$ Substitute and solve for $[CS]$: $$[CS] = \dfrac{[C]_T\,[S_{out}]}{K_m + [S_{out}]}$$ **Step 6 — Flux per unit membrane area:** $$J = k_2\,[CS] = \underbrace{k_2\,[C]_T}_{J_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{[S_{out}]}{K_m + [S_{out}]}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\,J = J_{max}\,\dfrac{[S]}{K_m + [S]}\,}$$ **Conversion Logic Check:** This equation is dimensionally consistent: $[J] = \text{mol}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s})$, $J_{max}$ carries the same units, and the bracketed term is dimensionless. ### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Energy Required for Active Transport We wish to transport a solute from concentration $C_1$ (in) to $C_2$ (out) against the gradient. **Step 1 — Free energy of ideal isothermal transfer (constant T, P):** $$\Delta G = \mu_2 - \mu_1$$ **Step 2 — Recall chemical potential definition:** $$\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT\ln C_i$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$\Delta G = \left(\mu_2^{\circ} + RT\ln C_2\right) - \left(\mu_1^{\circ} + RT\ln C_1\right)$$ **Step 4 — At standard state $\mu_1^{\circ} = \mu_2^{\circ}$ (same species):** $$\boxed{\,\Delta G = RT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{C_1}\right)\,}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical example (Glucose in *E. coli*):** Suppose $[G]_{in} = 1\ \text{mM}$, $[G]_{out} = 1\ \mu\text{M}$, $T = 310\ \text{K}$. Compute $C_2/C_1 = 10^{-3}$, so: $$\Delta G = (8.314)(310)\ln(10^{-3}) = 2577.34 \times (-6.9078) = -17{,}805\ \text{J/mol} \approx -17.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 6 — Realistic energy source:** ATP hydrolysis provides $-30.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$, more than sufficient. The **coupling efficiency** is: $$\eta = \dfrac{17.8}{30.5} \approx 58.4\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step Interpretation:** $\Delta G < 0$ means the transfer is *exergonic* (favorable, like diffusion). For active transport we run it in *reverse* — the cell spends $\geq 17.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$ to push glucose inward against a 1000-fold gradient. ### 3.3 Derivation: Electrochemical Work for Ion Pumps (Nernst Component) For a charged ion, the work term includes the membrane potential $\Delta \psi$: $$\Delta \tilde{\mu} = RT\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) + zF\Delta \psi$$ For the $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$ pump transporting 3 $\text{Na}^+$ out and 2 $\text{K}^+$ in: $$\Delta G_{pump} = RT\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[Na^+]_{out}^3}{[Na^+]_{in}^3}\right) + RT\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[K^+]_{in}^2}{[K^+]_{out}^2}\right) + F\Delta\psi(3 - 2)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The final term $+F\Delta\psi$ represents the *electrogenic* cost — the pump performs extra electrical work, which is exactly what makes the cell interior electronegative. This is the molecular basis of *excitability* in neurons and cardiomyocytes. ### 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix | Transport Mode | Energy Source | Equation | Sign of $\Delta G$ | Typical Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Simple Diffusion | Thermal motion | $J = -D\,\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | $<0$ | $\text{O}_2$ in alveoli | | Facilitated Diffusion | Thermal motion | $J = J_{max}\dfrac{[S]}{K_m+[S]}$ | $<0$ | GLUT1 (glucose in RBC) | | Primary Active | ATP hydrolysis | $\Delta G_{ATP} = -30.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$ | Coupled | $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase | | Secondary Active | Ion gradient | $\Delta \tilde{\mu}_{ion}$ drives flux | Coupled | SGLT1, NCX | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Classification Tree — Membrane Transport Mechanisms ```mermaid graph TD A[TRANSPORT ACROSS MEMBRANE] --> B[Passive Transport] A --> C[Active Transport] B --> B1[Simple Diffusion] B --> B2[Facilitated Diffusion] B --> B3[Osmosis] B2 --> B2a[Channel Proteins - Aquaporins, Ion Channels] B2 --> B2b[Carrier Proteins - GLUT, LacY] C --> C1[Primary Active Transport] C --> C2[Secondary Active Transport] C1 --> C1a[P-type ATPase - Na K pump, Ca ATPase] C1 --> C1b[ABC Transporters - MDR, CFTR] C1 --> C1c[V-type ATPase - Lysosomal H pump] C2 --> C2a[Symport - SGLT1 glucose Na] C2b[Antiport - Na Ca exchanger NCX] C2c[Uniport - rare in active class] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Ext[Extracellular Space 140 mM Na, 5 mM K] E1[3 Na ions bind] end subgraph Mem[Plasma Membrane] P1[ATP binds to pump] P2[Phosphorylation by ATP] P3[Conformational change E1 to E2] P4[3 Na released outside] P5[2 K bind from outside] P6[Dephosphorylation] P7[2 K released inside] end subgraph Int[Intracellular Space 10 mM Na, 140 mM K] I1[K ions accumulate in cell] end E1 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7 P7 --> I1 I1 -.->|resets carrier| P1 ``` ### 4.3 Energy Landscape — Why Active Transport is "Uphill" ```mermaid flowchart LR A[High External S] -->|Facilitated Diffusion
DOWN gradient| B[Low Internal S] A -->|Active Transport
UP gradient
uses ATP| C[High Internal S] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745 style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box is a "state" of the cell (or pump). The arrows represent *transitions*. Energy input is required *only* for the transition A → C (active transport). A → B occurs spontaneously because the system moves to lower free energy. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** **Differentiate between facilitated diffusion and active transport. List two specific examples of membrane proteins involved in each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Criterion | Facilitated Diffusion | Active Transport | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Energy | Not required | ATP or ion gradient | | Direction | Down gradient | Against gradient | | Saturation | Yes | Yes (pump kinetics) | | Examples | GLUT1 (glucose transporter), aquaporin (water) | $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase, SGLT1 (secondary) | *Mark Split:* Definition 1M + Comparison table 1M + Examples 1M. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** **State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Why does facilitated diffusion not obey Fick's Law at high substrate concentrations?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Fick's First Law: $J = -D\,\dfrac{dC}{dx}$, valid for *free* diffusion in the linear regime. Facilitated diffusion depends on a finite pool of carrier proteins. At high $[S]$, all carriers become saturated, so $J \to J_{max}$ (constant), **not** proportional to $[S]$. Thus the linear Fickian regime breaks down. The correct kinetic description is the Michaelis–Menten equation: $J = J_{max}[S]/(K_m + [S])$. *Mark Split:* Statement 1M + Reason 1M + Correct equation 1M. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **KTU Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]`** **(a)** Derive the Michaelis–Menten equation for a membrane carrier protein involved in facilitated diffusion, clearly stating the steady-state assumption. Discuss how the parameters $J_{max}$ and $K_m$ are interpreted in the context of bioreactor design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A glucose carrier in yeast has $J_{max} = 2.5\ \mu\text{mol}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s})$ and $K_m = 1.2\ \text{mM}$. Calculate the flux when external glucose is (i) $0.1\ \text{mM}$, (ii) $1.2\ \text{mM}$, and (iii) $10\ \text{mM}$. Comment on the saturation behavior and its implication on fed-batch glucose feeding strategies. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** 1. Carrier binding equilibrium — 1M 2. Steady-state assumption on $[CS]$ — 1M 3. Mass balance $[C]_T = [C] + [CS]$ — 1M 4. Final flux equation $J = J_{max}[S]/(K_m + [S])$ — 1M 5. Interpretation: $J_{max}$ is *maximum uptake capacity*; $K_m$ is the substrate concentration at half-saturation (analogous to Monod $K_s$) — 2M 6. Engineering implication: at $[S] \gg K_m$, the cell operates at $J \approx J_{max}$, so increasing glucose has *no effect* on uptake → fed-batch should hold $[G] \approx K_m$ for cost-effective operation — 1M **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** The flux equation is $J = J_{max}\cdot[S]/(K_m + [S])$ with $J_{max} = 2.5$, $K_m = 1.2$. (i) At $[S] = 0.1\ \text{mM}$: $$J_1 = 2.5 \times \dfrac{0.1}{1.2 + 0.1} = 2.5 \times \dfrac{0.1}{1.3} = 2.5 \times 0.0769 = 0.192\ \mu\text{mol}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s})$$ (ii) At $[S] = 1.2\ \text{mM} = K_m$: $$J_2 = 2.5 \times \dfrac{1.2}{1.2 + 1.2} = 2.5 \times 0.5 = 1.25\ \mu\text{mol}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s})$$ (iii) At $[S] = 10\ \text{mM}$: $$J_3 = 2.5 \times \dfrac{10}{1.2 + 10} = 2.5 \times 0.8929 = 2.232\ \mu\text{mol}/(\text{m}^2\cdot\text{s})$$ **Comment (1M):** Notice $J$ does *not* scale linearly with $[S]$. From 0.1 → 1.2 mM (12× increase in $[S]$), $J$ rises 6.5×; from 1.2 → 10 mM (8.3× increase), $J$ rises only 1.78×. This is **saturation behavior**. **Fed-batch implication (1M):** Maintain $[G]$ near $K_m$ (1.2 mM) — beyond this, additional glucose is wasted (respires to ethanol in *Crabtree-positive* yeasts) with negligible flux gain. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]`** **(a)** Explain the mechanism of the $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase as a primary active transport system. Include the stoichiometry, the role of phosphorylation, and the electrogenic consequence. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the minimum free energy required to transport $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ from a cytosolic concentration of $100\ \text{nM}$ to an extracellular concentration of $1.2\ \text{mM}$ at $T = 310\ \text{K}$. Compare this to the free energy available from one mole of ATP hydrolysis. State how many $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ ions can be transported per ATP molecule assuming $100\%$ coupling efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Mechanism (7 marks):** 1. Pump architecture: P-type ATPase with $\alpha$- and $\beta$-subunits — 1M 2. 3 $\text{Na}^+$ bind on cytoplasmic side — 0.5M 3. ATP $\rightarrow$ ADP + $\text{P}_i$, phosphorylation of Asp-369 — 1M 4. E1 → E2 conformational change, exposes binding sites outside — 1M 5. 3 $\text{Na}^+$ released, 2 $\text{K}^+$ bound — 1M 6. Dephosphorylation, E2 → E1, 2 $\text{K}^+$ released inside — 1M 7. Stoichiometry 3:2 per ATP; net transfer of +1 charge out per cycle = **electrogenic**, contributing to $V_m \approx -70$ mV — 1.5M **(b) Energy Calculation (7 marks):** Using $\Delta G = RT \ln(C_{out}/C_{in})$ for an uncharged equivalent (charge handled separately). **Step 1 — Concentration ratio:** $$\dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 12{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Chemical work term:** $$\Delta G = (8.314)(310)\ln(12{,}000) = 2577.34 \times 9.3927 = 24{,}205\ \text{J/mol} = +24.21\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3 — ATP free energy available:** $$\Delta G_{ATP} = -30.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ So one ATP can transport at most 1 $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ (since $24.21 < 30.5$). **Step 4 — Number of $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ per ATP (100% coupling):** $$N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{30.5}{24.21} \right\rfloor = 1\ \text{ion per ATP}$$ (Real PMCA pump transports 1 $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ per ATP, with $\sim 80\%$ efficiency, so 1 is realistic.) **Comparison (1M):** The ATP provides a 6.3 kJ/mol surplus, which is dissipated as heat — useful for keeping the pump operating *away* from thermodynamic equilibrium and ensuring *robustness* against fluctuations in $\Delta G$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting units in $K_m$ and $J_{max}$:** Always state M (or mM) and $\mu\text{mol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$. Missing units = **−0.5 to −1 mark**. > 2. **Confusing "down gradient" with "negative $\Delta G$":** This is true for *spontaneous* diffusion, but the cell *expends* the opposite energy for active transport. State explicitly: $\Delta G_{transport} = +17.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$, while $\Delta G_{ATP} = -30.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$ **drives** it. > 3. **Mixing $K_m$ (carrier) with $K_s$ (Monod):** They are *analogous* but not identical. $K_m$ is a carrier binding constant; $K_s$ is an empirical growth constant. Examiners look for this distinction. > 4. **Omitting the "steady-state" assumption** in Michaelis–Menten derivations = **−1 mark minimum**. > 5. **Calculating the wrong stoichiometry of the Na/K pump:** It is 3 Na out / 2 K in — *not* 1:1, *not* 2:3. > 6. **Sign errors in Nernst/Goldman equations:** Use $\ln$ consistently; do *not* mix $\ln$ and $\log_{10}$ without conversion. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-day exam prep):** - ✅ **Facilitated Diffusion** = carrier-mediated, *down* gradient, **no ATP**, Michaelis–Menten kinetics, **saturable**. - ✅ **Active Transport** = *against* gradient, **ATP** (primary) or **ion gradient** (secondary), maintains disequilibrium. - ✅ **Primary Active** examples: $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase (3:2), $\text{Ca}^{2+}$-ATPase (SERCA), $\text{H}^+$-ATPase (V-type in lysosomes). - ✅ **Secondary Active** types: **Symport** (same direction, e.g., SGLT1), **Antiport** (opposite, e.g., NCX). - ✅ **Carrier Kinetics:** $J = J_{max}[S]/(K_m + [S])$. - ✅ **Energy of Transport:** $\Delta G = RT\ln(C_2/C_1) + zF\Delta\psi$. - ✅ **ATP Hydrolysis Free Energy:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5\ \text{kJ/mol} = -7.3\ \text{kcal/mol}$ (biochemical standard state). - ✅ **GHK Equation** computes resting $V_m$ with multiple ions and permeabilities. - ✅ **$Q_{10} \approx 2$–$3$** indicates protein-mediated transport; $Q_{10} \approx 1$–$1.5$ indicates simple diffusion. - ✅ **Engineering relevance:** Bioreactor substrate uptake, MBR design, microbial fuel cells, drug delivery, biosensors. - ✅ **Number to memorize:** 3 $\text{Na}^+$ out, 2 $\text{K}^+$ in, 1 ATP, $V_m \approx -70\ \text{mV}$. **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** > *"FADS-CAMP"* > **F**acilitated, **A**TP, **D**own, **S**aturable, **C**arrier, **A**gainst, **M**embrane, **P**ump.

topics.title: Transport Phenomena in Bioprocess System - Gas-Liquid Mass Transfer in Cellular System - Basic Mass Transfer Concepts - Rates of Metabolic Oxygen Utilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transport Phenomena in Bioprocess System - Gas-Liquid Mass Transfer in Cellular System - Basic Mass Transfer Concepts - Rates of Metabolic Oxygen Utilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transport Phenomena in Bioprocess Systems

**Transport phenomena** is the unified study of the transfer of **mass**, **momentum**, and **energy** in a bioprocess system. In biochemical engineering, the most critical transport process is **mass transfer**, which governs how substrates (like oxygen, glucose) reach the microbial cells and how products/metabolites are removed.

In a bioprocess (fermenter/bioreactor), transport phenomena are studied across different scales:
- **Microscale (Molecular level):** Molecular diffusion
- **Mesoscale (Cellular level):** Diffusion through cell membrane and cytoplasm
- **Macroscale (Reactor level):** Bulk transport, mixing, gas-liquid transfer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes the coupling of **reaction kinetics** with **mass transfer rates**. A bioprocess can never operate faster than the *slowest* of: (1) microbial reaction rate, (2) mass transfer rate of substrate, (3) heat removal rate.

## 1.2 Gas-Liquid Mass Transfer in Cellular Systems

**Gas-liquid mass transfer** in a cellular system refers to the transfer of a gaseous substrate (typically **O₂** or **CO₂**) from the gas phase (air bubbles) into the liquid phase (broth), and ultimately into the microbial cell where it is metabolized.

The oxygen pathway in a typical aerobic fermentation follows this **resistance-in-series** model:

1. Transfer from **bulk gas** to **gas-liquid interface**
2. Diffusion across the **gas film**
3. Crossing the **gas-liquid interface**
4. Diffusion through the **liquid film** (rate-limiting step)
5. Transport through the **bulk liquid**
6. Diffusion through the **liquid film around the cell**
7. Transport across the **cell membrane**
8. Intracellular diffusion and metabolic consumption

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In most aerobic fermentations, the **liquid film resistance** (steps 3–5) is the dominant resistance. Gas-phase resistance is generally negligible because oxygen diffuses ~10,000 times faster in gases than in liquids.

## 1.3 Basic Mass Transfer Concepts

### Molecular Diffusion (Fick's Law)

**Molecular diffusion** is the spontaneous movement of molecules from a region of high concentration to low concentration due to random molecular motion.

**Fick's First Law of Diffusion:**

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $J_A$ is the molar flux of A (kmol/m²·s), $D_{AB}$ is the diffusivity of A in B (m²/s), and $dC_A/dz$ is the concentration gradient.

### Convective Mass Transfer

When bulk fluid motion is present (e.g., agitation, bubbling), mass transfer is enhanced. The flux is given by:

$$N_A = k_C (C_A^* - C_{A,b})$$

where $k_C$ is the convective mass transfer coefficient (m/s), $C_A^*$ is the equilibrium (saturation) concentration, and $C_{A,b}$ is the bulk concentration.

### Conceptual Analogy 🍬

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The Sugar Cube Analogy):** Imagine dropping a sugar cube into unstirred tea. Initially, sugar dissolves slowly because molecules can only move by **molecular diffusion** (Fick's law). Now stir the tea — sugar distributes almost instantly due to **convective mass transfer** (eddy diffusion). In a fermenter, the impeller does the same job — it converts slow molecular diffusion into fast convective transport. However, no matter how vigorously you stir, there is always a thin **stagnant liquid film** around the gas bubble and around each cell where only molecular diffusion operates. This film is the **bottleneck** of gas-liquid mass transfer.

## 1.4 Rates of Metabolic Oxygen Utilization

**Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)** — also called the **respiration rate** or **specific oxygen uptake rate (q_{O₂})** — is the rate at which microbial cells consume dissolved oxygen for **aerobic metabolism** (oxidative phosphorylation to generate ATP).

- **OUR (Overall):** $r_{O_2} = q_{O_2} \cdot C_X$ (mmol O₂/L·h)
- **q_{O₂} (Specific):** mmol O₂ consumed per g dry cell weight per hour (mmol/g·h)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Oxygen Concentration (C_{crit}):** Below this dissolved oxygen level, the OUR becomes oxygen-limited and cell metabolism shifts from aerobic to fermentative (e.g., ethanol production in yeast, lactate in mammalian cells). Typical values: bacteria ~ 0.5–2 mg/L, yeast ~ 1–3 mg/L, animal cells ~ 0.1–1 mg/L.

**Why oxygen is special:** O₂ has very low solubility in aqueous broths (≈ 7–8 mg/L at 25°C, 1 atm air). It is therefore the **rate-limiting substrate** in most industrial aerobic fermentations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Film Theory (Lewis-Whitman, 1924)

The **two-film theory** is the cornerstone of gas-liquid mass transfer analysis. It postulates two stagnant films — one on the gas side and one on the liquid side — separated by a well-defined interface.

### Key Assumptions:
- Steady state
- Interface is at thermodynamic equilibrium
- Bulk phases are perfectly mixed
- All resistance is concentrated in the two films
- Transport through films is by molecular diffusion only

### Why is the Liquid Film Controlling?

The ratio of gas to liquid film resistance is:

$$\frac{R_G}{R_L} = \frac{k_L}{k_G \cdot H}$$

where $H$ is the **Henry's law constant**. For oxygen (poorly soluble gas), $H$ is very large, so $R_G \ll R_L$. Hence, **~90–95% of the resistance lies in the liquid film** for oxygen transfer.

## 2.2 Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient (K_L)

The overall driving force is expressed using either gas or liquid phase concentrations:

$$N_A = K_L (C_A^* - C_{A,L}) = K_G (p_A^G - p_A^{G,*})$$

The two overall coefficients are related by:

$$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{H \cdot k_G}$$

For oxygen, since $H$ is very large, $1/(H k_G) \to 0$, simplifying to $K_L \approx k_L$.

## 2.3 Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient (k_La)

In a fermenter, total transfer rate depends on the **total interfacial area** between gas and liquid, not just the local flux. Therefore, engineers use the lumped parameter **k_La** (s⁻¹):

$$OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$$

where $a$ is the specific interfacial area (m² gas / m³ liquid). The term $k_L a$ combines the mass transfer coefficient and the interfacial area, and it is the most important design parameter for aerobic bioreactors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** k_La is **measurable experimentally** (dynamic gassing-out method, sulfite oxidation method) and **correlatable** with operating variables (impeller speed N, gas flow rate Q, geometry). It cannot be split into k_L and a individually by simple experiments.

## 2.4 Oxygen Balance in a Fermenter

At steady state, the **rate of oxygen transfer from gas** must equal the **rate of oxygen consumption by cells**:

$$OTR = OUR$$

$$k_L a (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} C_X$$

Solving for the operating dissolved oxygen concentration $C_L$:

$$C_L = C^* - \frac{q_{O_2} C_X}{k_L a}$$

This equation is the **design heart** of aerobic bioprocesses — it tells engineers whether their reactor can supply enough oxygen.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Fick's First Law | kmol/m²·s |
| 2 | $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | Fick's Second Law | kg/m³·s |
| 3 | $N_A = k_C (C_A^* - C_{A,b})$ | Convective flux | kmol/m²·s |
| 4 | $p_A = H \cdot C_A^*$ | Henry's Law | atm·m³/kmol |
| 5 | $N_A = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | OTR in fermenter | mmol/L·h |
| 6 | $k_L a (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} C_X$ | OTR = OUR balance | mmol/L·h |
| 7 | $OUR = q_{O_2} C_X$ | Oxygen uptake rate | mmol/L·h |
| 8 | $\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{H k_G}$ | Resistances in series | s/m |
| 9 | $C_{crit}$ | Critical DO below which metabolism shifts | mg/L |
| 10 | $Sh = \frac{k_L d}{D_{AB}} = 2 + 0.6 Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$ | Frössling/Ranz-Marshall correlation | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Scale-up of fermenters:** k_La must be maintained on scale-up; correlations like Van't Riet ($k_La = K \cdot (P/V)^{0.4} v_s^{0.5}$) are used to scale agitator power and aeration rate.
- **Reactor sizing:** Determines aeration capacity, agitator horsepower, and aspect ratio.
- **Process control:** DO probes provide feedback to manipulate agitation speed or air flow to maintain $C_L > C_{crit}$.
- **Industrial examples:** Antibiotic (penicillin), recombinant protein, single-cell protein, and beer production all depend critically on oxygen supply.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Two-Film Theory and Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient

### Step 1 — Set up the resistance framework

The total flux across the gas-liquid interface must be the same on both sides (steady state, no accumulation):

$$N_A = k_G (p_A^{bulk} - p_A^{interface}) = k_L (C_A^{interface} - C_A^{bulk})$$

### Step 2 — Eliminate the interface concentration using Henry's Law

At the interface, gas and liquid are in equilibrium:

$$p_A^{interface} = H \cdot C_A^{interface}$$

Rearranging the gas-phase equation:

$$p_A^{bulk} - p_A^{interface} = \frac{N_A}{k_G}$$

$$p_A^{interface} = p_A^{bulk} - \frac{N_A}{k_G}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the equilibrium condition

$$p_A^{bulk} - \frac{N_A}{k_G} = H \left( C_A^{interface} \right)$$

$$C_A^{interface} = \frac{p_A^{bulk}}{H} - \frac{N_A}{H k_G} = C_A^* - \frac{N_A}{H k_G}$$

### Step 4 — Use the liquid-phase flux expression

From the liquid side:

$$N_A = k_L \left( C_A^{interface} - C_A^{bulk} \right)$$

$$N_A = k_L \left( C_A^* - \frac{N_A}{H k_G} - C_A^{bulk} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Collect the N_A terms

$$N_A = k_L (C_A^* - C_A^{bulk}) - \frac{k_L N_A}{H k_G}$$

$$N_A + \frac{k_L N_A}{H k_G} = k_L (C_A^* - C_A^{bulk})$$

$$N_A \left( 1 + \frac{k_L}{H k_G} \right) = k_L (C_A^* - C_A^{bulk})$$

### Step 6 — Final form of overall flux

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{k_L}{\left( 1 + \frac{k_L}{H k_G} \right)} (C_A^* - C_A^{bulk}) = K_L (C_A^* - C_A^{bulk})}$$

This gives the **overall mass transfer coefficient**:

$$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{H k_G}$$

This is the **additivity of resistances** for mass transfer — directly analogous to electrical resistances in series. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship between k_La, OUR, and Dissolved Oxygen

Consider a well-mixed fermenter of volume $V_L$ (L of liquid) operating at steady state. A mole balance on dissolved oxygen gives:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Transfer} - \text{Consumption}$$

At steady state, accumulation = 0; with no DO in feed, and assuming perfectly mixed bulk:

$$0 = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} C_X$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{C_L = C^* - \frac{q_{O_2} C_X}{k_L a}}$$

This single equation reveals the **design trade-off**:
- Increase $k_L a$ (more agitation / aeration) → $C_L$ rises → more aerobic metabolism.
- Increase $C_X$ (cell density) → $C_L$ drops → risk of anoxia.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 10 m³ fermenter is used for an antibiotic fermentation. Data: $C^* = 8 \text{ mg/L}$ (saturation DO), $k_L a = 250 \text{ h}^{-1}$, $q_{O_2} = 1.5 \text{ mg O}_2/\text{g DCW·h}$, $C_X = 5 \text{ g DCW/L}$.
> **(a)** Calculate OUR.
> **(b)** Calculate steady-state dissolved oxygen $C_L$.
> **(c)** Will the cells be oxygen-limited? (Given $C_{crit} = 1.0 \text{ mg/L}$).

### (a) Oxygen Uptake Rate

$$OUR = q_{O_2} \cdot C_X = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5 \text{ mg/L·h}$$

Total oxygen demand: $7.5 \text{ mg/L·h} \times 10{,}000 \text{ L} = 75{,}000 \text{ mg/h} = 75 \text{ g/h}$.

### (b) Steady-State Dissolved Oxygen

Using the oxygen balance:

$$C_L = C^* - \frac{OUR}{k_L a} = 8 - \frac{7.5}{250} = 8 - 0.03 = 7.97 \text{ mg/L}$$

### (c) Check for Oxygen Limitation

Since $C_L = 7.97 \text{ mg/L} \gg C_{crit} = 1.0 \text{ mg/L}$, the cells are **not oxygen-limited**. The fermenter has significant spare oxygen transfer capacity.

### What if cell density doubled?

If $C_X = 10$ g/L, then $OUR = 15$ mg/L·h:

$$C_L = 8 - \frac{15}{250} = 8 - 0.06 = 7.94 \text{ mg/L}$$

Still safe. But at very high cell densities (e.g., $C_X = 200$ g/L, as in industrial fed-batch), $OUR = 300$ mg/L·h:

$$C_L = 8 - \frac{300}{250} = 8 - 1.2 = 6.8 \text{ mg/L} \quad \text{(still OK)}$$

At $C_X = 1500$ g/L (theoretical):

$$C_L = 8 - \frac{2250}{250} = -1 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Physically impossible** → the system is **mass transfer limited** and the cells will become oxygen-starved, regardless of how much agitation is applied (with the current k_La).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Industrial high-cell-density fermentations (e.g., recombinant *E. coli* at OD > 100) often need **pure O₂ sparging** or **pressurized vessels** to raise $C^*$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How k_La is Influenced by Operating Variables

| Variable | Effect on $k_L$ | Effect on $a$ | Net Effect on $k_L a$ | Typical Exponent |
|----------|------------------|----------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Impeller speed (N) ↑ | ↑↑ (via turbulence) | ↑ (smaller bubbles) | Strongly ↑ | $N^{0.7}$ to $N^{1.0}$ |
| Air flow rate (Q) ↑ | Small ↑ | ↑↑ (more bubbles) | ↑ | $Q^{0.3}$ to $Q^{0.5}$ |
| Power input (P/V) ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | $(P/V)^{0.4}$ |
| Superficial gas velocity (v_s) ↑ | — | ↑ | ↑ | $v_s^{0.5}$ |
| Viscosity (μ) ↑ | ↓↓ (poor mixing) | ↓ (coalescence) | ↓↓ | μ⁻⁰·⁵ to μ⁻¹ |
| Temperature ↑ | ↑ (D increases) | ↓ (coalescence) | Variable | Complex |
| Surfactant/antifoam | ↓ | ↑ (smaller bubbles) | Ambiguous | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gas-Liquid Mass Transfer Pathway (Bubble-to-Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air / O2 Gas Bulk] -->|Step 1: Gas film diffusion| B[Gas Liquid Interface]
    B -->|Step 2: Liquid film diffusion - rate limiting| C[Liquid Bulk Broth]
    C -->|Step 3: Liquid film around cell| D[Cell Membrane]
    D -->|Step 4: Intracellular diffusion| E[Mitochondria - Site of Consumption]
    E -->|O2 consumed| F[ATP Energy + H2O + CO2]
    
    R1[/R_g gas film resistance/]:::resistor
    R2[/R_L liquid film resistance - DOMINANT/]:::resistor
    R3[/R_LC liquid film around cell/]:::resistor
    R4[/R_M membrane resistance/]:::resistor
    R5[/R_INT intracellular resistance/]:::resistor
    
    A --- R1
    R1 --- B
    B --- R2
    R2 --- C
    C --- R3
    R3 --- D
    D --- R4
    R4 --- E
    E --- R5
    R5 --- F
    
    classDef resistor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Aerobic Bioreactor Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AIR[Compressed Air Supply] --> FILTER[Sterile Air Filter]
    FILTER --> SPARGER[Sparger below Impeller]
    SPARGER --> BROTH[Fermenter Broth with Cells]
    
    IMPELLER[Mechanical Impeller Motor] -->|Agitation| BROTH
    
    BROTH -->|O2 transfer| CELLS[Microbial Cells]
    CELLS -->|Consume O2| DO[DO Probe Sensor]
    DO -->|4-20 mA signal| CONTROLLER[PID Controller]
    CONTROLLER -->|Modulates| IMPELLER
    CONTROLLER -->|Modulates| AIRC[Air Flow Control Valve]
    AIRC --> AIR
    
    CELLS -->|Produce| PRODUCT[Biomass and Product]
    CELLS -->|Release| HEAT[Heat of Metabolism]
    HEAT -->|Removed by| JACKET[Cooling Jacket]
    
    style CONTROLLER fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style DO fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style CELLS fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Resistance-in-Series Electrical Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[p_A_bulk] --> R1[R_G = 1 slash H k_G]
    R1 --> P2[p_A_interface = H C_A_interface]
    P2 --> R2[R_L = 1 slash k_L]
    R2 --> P3[p_A_bulk_equivalent]
    P3 --> R3[R_LC around cell]
    R3 --> P4[p_A at membrane]
    P4 --> R4[R_M membrane]
    R4 --> P5[p_A at mitochondria]
    
    style R1 fill:#FFDAB9
    style R2 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFFFFF
    style R3 fill:#FFDAB9
    style R4 fill:#FFDAB9
    style R5 fill:#FFDAB9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The largest "brick" (R_L) is the **liquid film** — that is the bottleneck. All other resistances are relatively small. This is why process engineers focus on increasing $k_L a$ through better **bubble dispersion** and **turbulence**.

## 4.4 Dissolved Oxygen Dynamics During Batch Fermentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T0: Inoculation] -->|Lag phase| T1[T1: Exponential growth]
    T1 -->|DO drops as cells multiply| T2[T2: Critical point C_L approaches C_crit]
    T2 -->|Controller increases N or Q| T3[T3: Steady DO above C_crit]
    T3 -->|Cells enter stationary phase| T4[T4: DO rises - substrate exhausted]
    T4 --> T5[T5: Death phase]
    
    style T2 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFFFFF
    style T3 fill:#90EE90
    style T4 fill:#87CEEB
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient (k_La)" and explain its significance in the design of aerobic bioreactors.**

**Model Answer:**
The **volumetric mass transfer coefficient (k_La)** is defined as the product of the **liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (k_L)** and the **specific gas-liquid interfacial area (a)** per unit volume of liquid. It has units of **s⁻¹** (or h⁻¹) and represents the overall capacity of a fermenter to transfer oxygen (or any sparingly soluble gas) from the gas phase to the liquid phase.

$$OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$$

**Significance in bioreactor design:**
1. It is the **key design parameter** governing oxygen supply in aerobic fermentations.
2. It is **experimentally measurable** (dynamic gassing-out or sulfite oxidation methods).
3. It is **correlatable** with operating variables like impeller speed (N), aeration rate (Q), and power input (P/V) — e.g., $k_L a = K (P/V)^{0.4} v_s^{0.5}$.
4. It directly determines whether the **oxygen demand of cells (OUR = q_{O₂}·C_X)** can be met; if k_La(C* − C_L) < OUR, the system is oxygen-limited.
5. It is used as the **scale-up criterion** in industrial fermentation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is "Critical Dissolved Oxygen Concentration (C_crit)"? Why is it important in industrial fermentations?**

**Model Answer:**
**Critical Dissolved Oxygen Concentration (C_crit)** is defined as the **minimum dissolved oxygen (DO) concentration in the fermentation broth** below which the **specific oxygen uptake rate (q_{O₂})** of the microorganisms becomes limited by oxygen availability. Above C_crit, the OUR remains essentially constant and independent of DO (zero-order with respect to O₂); below C_crit, OUR drops sharply.

**Importance in industrial fermentations:**
1. **Process control target:** Operators maintain $C_L > C_{crit}$ to ensure **fully aerobic metabolism** — preventing shift to undesirable fermentative pathways (e.g., ethanol in yeast, lactate in mammalian cells).
2. **Reactor design:** Aeration and agitation systems must be designed so that the steady-state $C_L = C^* - (q_{O_2} C_X)/k_L a \geq C_{crit}$ for the entire fermentation.
3. **Product quality:** Anaerobic shift can lead to by-product formation, lower product yield, and altered product quality (e.g., mixed acids in *E. coli*).
4. **Typical values:** Bacteria: 0.5–2 mg/L; Yeast: 1–3 mg/L; Animal cells: 0.1–1 mg/L.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified]
**(a)** Derive the expression for the **overall mass transfer coefficient (K_L)** in a gas-liquid system based on the **two-film theory**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 m³ stirred-tank fermenter is used for the production of penicillin by *Penicillium chrysogenum*. Operating conditions are:
- $C^* = 7.5$ mg/L (saturation DO at operating T and P)
- $k_L a = 300$ h⁻¹
- Biomass concentration: $C_X = 12$ g DCW/L
- Specific oxygen uptake rate: $q_{O_2} = 1.2$ mg O₂/g DCW·h
- Critical DO: $C_{crit} = 1.5$ mg/L

Calculate: **(i)** OUR, **(ii)** steady-state $C_L$, **(iii)** comment on whether the cells will be oxygen-limited, **(iv)** the maximum permissible biomass concentration that can be supported without falling below $C_{crit}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### (a) Derivation of K_L (Two-Film Theory)

**Assumptions of the two-film theory:**
1. Steady-state transfer.
2. A well-defined interface exists with no accumulation.
3. Two stagnant films exist — one gas-side, one liquid-side.
4. Bulk gas and bulk liquid are perfectly mixed.
5. At the interface, gas and liquid are in thermodynamic equilibrium (Henry's law applies).
6. Mass transfer across each film occurs by **molecular diffusion** (Fick's law).

**Derivation:**

At steady state, the molar flux through both films must be equal:

$$N_A = k_G (p_{A,G} - p_{A,i}) = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,L})$$

At the interface, Henry's law applies:

$$p_{A,i} = H \cdot C_{A,i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{A,i} = \frac{p_{A,i}}{H}$$

From the gas-phase equation:

$$p_{A,i} = p_{A,G} - \frac{N_A}{k_G}$$

Substituting:

$$C_{A,i} = \frac{p_{A,G}}{H} - \frac{N_A}{H k_G} = C_A^* - \frac{N_A}{H k_G}$$

where $C_A^* = p_{A,G}/H$ is the equilibrium (saturation) concentration.

From the liquid-phase equation:

$$N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,L}) = k_L \left( C_A^* - \frac{N_A}{H k_G} - C_{A,L} \right)$$

$$N_A \left( 1 + \frac{k_L}{H k_G} \right) = k_L (C_A^* - C_{A,L})$$

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{k_L}{1 + \dfrac{k_L}{H k_G}} (C_A^* - C_{A,L}) = K_L (C_A^* - C_{A,L})}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{H k_G}}$$

This represents **two resistances in series**: the liquid-film resistance ($1/k_L$) and the gas-film resistance ($1/Hk_G$, expressed on liquid-concentration basis). ✔

##### (b) Numerical Problem

**(i) Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR):**

$$OUR = q_{O_2} \times C_X = 1.2 \text{ mg/g·h} \times 12 \text{ g/L} = 14.4 \text{ mg/L·h}$$

**(ii) Steady-state Dissolved Oxygen:**

Using the OTR = OUR balance:

$$C_L = C^* - \frac{OUR}{k_L a} = 7.5 - \frac{14.4}{300} = 7.5 - 0.048 = 7.452 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(iii) Oxygen-limitation check:**

Since $C_L = 7.452$ mg/L $\gg C_{crit} = 1.5$ mg/L, the cells are **NOT oxygen-limited**. There is substantial margin (≈ 5.95 mg/L) above the critical level.

**(iv) Maximum permissible biomass concentration:**

Setting $C_L = C_{crit}$ in the OTR-OUR balance:

$$C_{crit} = C^* - \frac{q_{O_2} \cdot C_X^{max}}{k_L a}$$

Solving for $C_X^{max}$:

$$C_X^{max} = \frac{(C^* - C_{crit}) \cdot k_L a}{q_{O_2}} = \frac{(7.5 - 1.5) \times 300}{1.2} = \frac{6.0 \times 300}{1.2} = 1500 \text{ g DCW/L}$$

The fermenter can theoretically support up to **1500 g DCW/L** of biomass before becoming oxygen-limited — a value far beyond the typical industrial range (30–50 g/L), confirming this reactor is over-designed for oxygen transfer.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]
**(a)** Explain the **basic mechanisms of mass transfer** (molecular diffusion and convective mass transfer) with relevant equations. Discuss the **role of the liquid film** in gas-liquid mass transfer in aerobic fermentations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained from a dynamic gassing-out experiment in a 5 L laboratory fermenter:

| Time (s) | DO (mg/L) |
|----------|-----------|
| 0 | 0.0 |
| 30 | 2.5 |
| 60 | 4.2 |
| 90 | 5.4 |
| 120 | 6.2 |
| 150 | 6.7 |
| 180 | 7.0 |
| 210 | 7.2 |
| 240 | 7.3 |

Saturation DO $C^*$ = 7.5 mg/L. Using the **slope method** (assuming negligible OUR during the brief re-oxygenation), determine the value of $k_L a$ in s⁻¹. Also explain why a high $k_L a$ is required in **high-cell-density industrial fermentations**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### (a) Basic Mechanisms of Mass Transfer

**1. Molecular Diffusion (Fick's First Law):**
Spontaneous movement of molecules down a concentration gradient by random thermal motion.

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $D_{AB}$ is the molecular diffusivity (m²/s), $J_A$ is the molar flux (kmol/m²·s), and $dC_A/dz$ is the concentration gradient driving the transfer. Fick's Second Law extends this to transient systems:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

**2. Convective Mass Transfer:**
When bulk fluid motion (agitation, sparging) is present, mass transfer is greatly enhanced. The flux is expressed using a **convective mass transfer coefficient (k_C)**:

$$N_A = k_C (C_A^* - C_{A,b})$$

**3. Role of the Liquid Film:**
In an agitated fermenter, even though bulk mixing is intense, a thin **stagnant liquid film** persists at:
- The gas-liquid interface (around each bubble)
- The cell surface (around each microbe)

Within these films, fluid motion vanishes and only **molecular diffusion** operates. The **liquid film resistance** is the dominant resistance for oxygen transfer because:
- $D_{AB}$ in liquids (~10⁻⁹ m²/s) is ~10,000× smaller than in gases.
- Oxygen's low solubility (high $H$) makes the gas-side resistance $1/(Hk_G) \to 0$.

Hence, in practice, the **overall mass transfer coefficient $K_L \approx k_L$** and the **rate-limiting step is diffusion of O₂ through the liquid film** surrounding gas bubbles.

##### (b) Slope-Method Determination of k_La

During dynamic re-oxygenation (with cells actively respiring), the DO balance is:

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} C_X$$

Rearranging:

$$C^* - C_L = \frac{1}{k_L a} \left( \frac{dC_L}{dt} + q_{O_2} C_X \right)$$

If OUR is small and approximately constant during the test, plotting $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ should give a straight line of slope $-k_L a$.

**Step 1: Compute (C* − C_L) and ln(C* − C_L):**

| $t$ (s) | $C_L$ (mg/L) | $C^* - C_L$ | $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ |
|---------|--------------|-------------|------------------|
| 0 | 0.0 | 7.5 | 2.015 |
| 30 | 2.5 | 5.0 | 1.609 |
| 60 | 4.2 | 3.3 | 1.194 |
| 90 | 5.4 | 2.1 | 0.742 |
| 120 | 6.2 | 1.3 | 0.262 |
| 150 | 6.7 | 0.8 | -0.223 |
| 180 | 7.0 | 0.5 | -0.693 |
| 210 | 7.2 | 0.3 | -1.204 |
| 240 | 7.3 | 0.2 | -1.609 |

**Step 2: Linear regression of ln(C* − C_L) vs. t**

A best-fit straight line through these points gives a slope of approximately:

$$\text{slope} \approx \frac{(-1.609) - (2.015)}{240 - 0} = \frac{-3.624}{240} \approx -0.0151 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Determine k_La**

Since slope = $-k_L a$:

$$\boxed{k_L a \approx 0.015 \text{ s}^{-1} = 54 \text{ h}^{-1}}$$

**Why a high k_La is required in high-cell-density industrial fermentations:**

In industrial fermentations (e.g., recombinant *E. coli*, *Pichia pastoris*, mammalian cell culture), cell densities often exceed 50–200 g DCW/L. Since $OUR = q_{O_2} \cdot C_X$, the volumetric oxygen demand rises proportionally with $C_X$. The only way to keep $C_L \gg C_{crit}$ is to either:
- **Increase $k_L a$** (via more intense agitation, higher aeration rate, use of pure O₂ instead of air, membrane spargers, or pressurized vessels), or
- **Increase $C^*$** (by enriching the inlet gas with O₂ or operating under elevated pressure, since $C^* \propto p_{O_2}$).

Failing this, the broth becomes oxygen-starved, leading to anaerobic metabolism, by-product accumulation (ethanol, acetate, lactate), reduced product yield, and cell death.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and lose marks for — in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Writing "$k_L a$" as a single coefficient and then splitting it incorrectly into $k_L$ and $a$** — these are NOT independently measurable by a single experiment. Do not derive them separately in derivations.
>
> 2. **Confusing OTR with OUR.** OTR is the *supply* rate (depends on $k_L a$, $C^*$, $C_L$); OUR is the *demand* rate (depends on $q_{O_2}$ and $C_X$). At steady state, they are equal, but they have **different driving forces**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to state assumptions** of the two-film theory in derivations. KTU examiners specifically test assumption-writing (1 mark reserved).
>
> 4. **Mixing up Henry's law direction.** Some students write $C_A^* = H \cdot p_A$ while others use $p_A = H \cdot C_A^*$. Always **state the form** of Henry's law you are using.
>
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** $k_L a$ in s⁻¹ vs. h⁻¹; flux in mmol vs. mol; volume in m³ vs. L. Always convert and write units in **every step** of a numerical problem.
>
> 6. **Plotting $C_L$ vs. $t$** instead of $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ in the dynamic gassing-out method. The first plot is a curve, the second is linear. KTU expects the **linear form** with slope = $-k_L a$.
>
> 7. **Ignoring the OUR term** when the OUR is non-negligible. The complete dynamic equation is $\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_L a(C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} C_X$. A correct advanced analysis accounts for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Gas-Liquid Mass Transfer**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Transport phenomena** = mass + momentum + energy transfer; in bioprocess, mass transfer is usually rate-limiting.
- ☐ **Gas-liquid mass transfer** follows a **resistance-in-series** pathway from gas bubble → liquid film (rate-limiting) → bulk liquid → cell membrane → metabolism.
- ☐ **Fick's First Law:** $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ — molecular diffusion.
- ☐ **Convective mass transfer:** $N_A = k_C (C_A^* - C_{A,b})$.
- ☐ **Two-film theory:** stagnant gas and liquid films, equilibrium at interface, all resistance in the films.

### Key Equations
- ☐ Overall mass transfer: $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{H k_G}$ (resistances in series).
- ☐ For oxygen (high $H$): $K_L \approx k_L$ (liquid-film controlling).
- ☐ OTR = $k_L a (C^* - C_L)$.
- ☐ OUR = $q_{O_2} \cdot C_X$.
- ☐ Steady-state balance: $k_L a (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} C_X$.
- ☐ Critical DO: $C_{crit}$ — below this, aerobic metabolism fails.

### Engineering Heuristics
- ☐ Typical k_La values: lab fermenter 100–500 h⁻¹; industrial 200–1000 h⁻¹.
- ☐ k_La increases with $N$, $Q$, $P/V$; decreases with viscosity.
- ☐ Van't Riet correlation: $k_L a = K (P/V)^{0.4} v_s^{0.5}$.
- ☐ O₂ solubility in water: ~7–8 mg/L at 25°C, 1 atm air (very low).
- ☐ Henry's constant for O₂ at 25°C: $H \approx 4.3 \times 10^4$ atm (large → gas-side resistance negligible).

### Measurement Methods
- ☐ **Sulfite oxidation method** (chemical, no cells needed) → $k_L a$.
- ☐ **Dynamic gassing-out method** (most common; uses DO probe and re-aeration) → $k_L a$ from $\ln(C^* - C_L)$ vs. $t$ slope.
- ☐ **Oxygen balance / off-gas analysis** → OUR in real time.

### Scale-up Reminder
- ☐ Maintain $k_L a$ on scale-up → adjust $N$ (impeller speed), $P/V$ (power), $v_s$ (gas velocity) using empirical correlations.
- ☐ High-cell-density cultures require **pure O₂ sparging** or **elevated pressure** to raise $C^*$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biochemical Reactors - Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biochemical Reactors - Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biochemical Reactor:** A **biochemical reactor** (also called a **bioreactor** or **fermenter**) is a closed, sterile, and precisely instrumented vessel designed to provide an optimal, controlled environment for the growth of microorganisms, animal cells, or plant cells, or for the catalytic action of enzymes, in order to manufacture bioproducts through defined biological transformations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a **biochemical reactor specification** refers to the exhaustive set of design, mechanical, operational, and control parameters that uniquely define the geometry, working volume, agitation system, aeration system, heat-transfer system, material of construction, sterilization strategy, instrumentation, and control logic of the vessel. The specifications are the **engineering contract** between the process biochemist (who defines *what* must happen biologically) and the chemical engineer (who defines *how* the vessel must be built to make it happen).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECHT521 — Module 4):** Specifications cover **working volume, aspect ratio, impeller type and number, sparger design, baffles, material of construction (SS-316L), sterilization-in-place (SIP) / cleaning-in-place (CIP) provisions, instrumentation ports (pH, DO, temperature, foam, level, OD), and the control philosophy** required to operate the vessel aseptically under the desired cultivation mode (batch, fed-batch, or continuous).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microbial Chef's Kitchen"

Imagine a high-end commercial kitchen where the *chef* is a microscopic organism (say, *E. coli* or *S. cerevisiae*). The kitchen must:

| Kitchen Element | Bioreactor Equivalent | Why It Matters |
|-----------------|-----------------------|----------------|
| Room size & height | **Working volume & aspect ratio (H/D)** | Determines mixing time & oxygen transfer |
| Stove (heat source) | **Heat-transfer jacket / coil** | Removes metabolic heat (ΔT rise ~ 2–5 °C) |
| Exhaust hood | **Vent filter (0.2 µm PTFE)** | Maintains sterility while releasing CO₂ |
| Chef's whisk | **Impeller (Rushton / pitched-blade / marine)** | Homogenizes nutrients, O₂, cells |
| Gas stove burner | **Sparger (ring / porous / jet)** | Delivers sterile air/oxygen |
| Faucet & sink | **Feed / harvest ports, drain valve** | Adds substrate, removes product |
| Air-conditioner | **Cooling water circulation loop** | Controls exothermic reactions |
| Kitchen timer & oven thermometer | **pH, DO, T, foam, OD probes + PLC** | Real-time process monitoring |

> [!TIP]
> Just as a chef refuses to cook in a dirty kitchen, a microbial cell refuses to perform in a contaminated reactor. **Asepsis** is the single non-negotiable specification.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Operational Constants

- **Sterilization filter rating:** **0.2 µm (absolute) or 0.1 µm** (PTFE or PVDF membrane).
- **Standard material of construction:** **SS-316L** (low-carbon stainless steel, ≤ 0.03 % C) for corrosion resistance and weldability.
- **Standard surface finish (internal):** **Ra ≤ 0.4 µm** (electropolished) to prevent microbial adhesion and ease CIP.
- **Working volume (V_w) range:** **1 L (lab) → 200 m³ (industrial)**; typical fill fraction **60–80 %**.
- **Sterilization temperature:** **121 °C @ 1.03 bar (g) for 20–30 min** (saturated steam).
- **Design pressure (vessel):** **Full vacuum to 2.5 bar (g)** per ASME BPVC.
- **Typical agitation speed:** **50–500 rpm**; specific power input **0.5–5 W/L** for bacteria, **0.1–0.5 W/L** for animal cells.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Biochemical Reactor — A Structured Walk-Through

A biochemical reactor is not a "tank with a stirrer" — it is a **layered system** of concentric engineering considerations. From the outside to the inside, we have:

1. **Outer Shell / Jacket**
   - Cylindrical body with **dish-end (torispherical)** or **hemispherical** heads.
   - Surrounded by a **limpet coil** or **full jacket** carrying cooling water / steam.
   - **Insulation** (mineral wool, 50–100 mm) on the outermost layer to reduce heat loss to ~<2 % of metabolic heat.

2. **Baffles (Internal)**
   - **4 baffles**, each **T/12** wide (T = tank diameter), mounted **off-wall by ~10 mm**.
   - Convert swirling flow into **axial + radial turbulence** → suppresses vortex → improves gas-liquid mass transfer.
   - For shear-sensitive cultures (animal cells), baffles are **replaced by a sparger ring placed near the bottom** to avoid mechanical damage.

3. **Agitation System (Shaft + Impellers)**
   - Driven by a **top-driven** (preferred for sterility) or **bottom-driven** magnetic-coupled motor.
   - **Mechanical seal** (single/double) or **magnetic coupling** to prevent contamination.
   - Impeller options:
     - **Rushton turbine (RT)** — 6 flat blades, D/T = 1/3, high shear, used for **bacteria/yeast**.
     - **Pitched-blade (PBT)** — 4 blades at 45°, lower shear, used for **fungi/filamentous cultures**.
     - **Marine propeller (MP)** — 3 blades, axial flow, used for **low-viscosity, low-shear (animal cells)**.
   - Multi-impeller shafts use **spacing = 1.0–1.5 × D**.

4. **Aeration / Sparger System**
   - **Ring sparger** (most common) — perforated tube, hole size **1–5 mm**, mounted **0.5–1.0 D** off bottom.
   - **Porous (frit) sparger** — for fine bubbles (1–3 mm), used when **high k_La** is needed.
   - **Jet sparger** — for viscous, non-Newtonian broths.
   - Air is filtered through **inlet vent filter (0.2 µm)** and **exhaust vent filter (0.2 µm)**.

5. **Instrumentation & Control Ports**
   - **Top-head ports** for: pH probe, DO (dissolved oxygen) probe, temperature probe (Pt-100), foam sensor, level sensor, optical density (OD) probe, sampling port, addition ports (acid/base/anti-foam/feed).
   - **Bottom port** for harvest/drain valve (typically **pneumatically actuated, steam-sterilizable diaphragm valve**).

6. **Sterilization & Cleaning Infrastructure**
   - **SIP (Sterilization-In-Place):** Steam at **121 °C / 30 min** through jacket and all process lines.
   - **CIP (Cleaning-In-Place):** Caustic (1–2 % NaOH, 80 °C) → acid rinse (1 % HNO₃) → sterile water rinse.
   - All lines are **steam-blocked** or **double-block-and-bleed** to prevent cross-contamination.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Specification

| Specification | Engineering "Why" | Biological "How" |
|---------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Aspect ratio H/T = 1–2 (mostly 1.5)** | Maximizes interfacial area for gas-liquid mass transfer | Provides sufficient liquid height for gas residence time |
| **Multiple Rushton impellers** | High power input → high gas dispersion → high k_La | Sustains aerobic metabolism for high cell densities |
| **0.2 µm vent filter** | Particle retention of *Brevundimonas diminuta* (industry standard) | Maintains monoseptic environment |
| **SS-316L with EP finish** | Resists Cl⁻-induced pitting, prevents biofilm | Avoids toxic metal leaching (Cr, Ni) into broth |
| **V/V_max = 0.7–0.8** | Allows headspace for foam disengagement and off-gas analysis | Prevents foam carry-over into exhaust filter (which causes filter wet-out and pressure drop) |
| **Baffles off-wall** | Prevents dead zones, allows CIP flow behind baffles | Ensures uniform nutrient distribution |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU University Exam — Biochemical Reactor Specifications**

| # | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units | Boundary / Application |
|---|----------|-------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5}$ | $N_p$ = power number (–), $P$ = power, $\rho$ = broth density, $N$ = impeller speed, $D$ = impeller diameter | – | $Re \geq 10^4$ (turbulent): $N_p \approx 5$ (RT) |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | – | $Re < 10$ laminar, $>10^4$ turbulent |
| 3 | $Na = \dfrac{Q_g}{N \cdot D^3}$ | Aeration number | – | Aerated power = un-aerated × $f(N_a, Fr)$ |
| 4 | $P_g = P_u \cdot \left[1 - C \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_g}{N \cdot V}\right)^{a}\right]$ | $P_g$ = gassed power, $C \approx 0.85$, $a \approx 0.45$ | W | Hughmark correlation |
| 5 | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.475} \cdot (v_s)^{0.4}$ | Van't Riet (1979) for **non-ionic** solutions; $v_s$ = superficial gas velocity, $K \approx 0.0026$ | s⁻¹ | Coalescing broths |
| 6 | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.7} \cdot (v_s)^{0.2}$ | Van't Riet (1979) for **ionic** (electrolyte) solutions; $K \approx 0.01$ | s⁻¹ | Non-coalescing broths |
| 7 | $OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen transfer rate | mol O₂/L·h | $C^*$ = saturation, $C_L$ = dissolved |
| 8 | $\nabla_{total} = \nabla_{medium} + \nabla_{cooling}$ | Del factor for batch sterilization | – | Design target: $\nabla \geq 7$ for media |
| 9 | $\nabla = k \cdot \int_0^t \exp\left(-\dfrac{E}{R \cdot T(t)}\right) dt$ | Arrhenius-integrated lethality | – | Batch sterilization |
| 10 | $\nabla_{cont} = k \cdot D \cdot \ln\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{\theta}{\tau}\right)\left(e^{E/RT_1} - 1\right)\right]$ | Continuous sterilization (holding tube + cooling) | – | $\theta$ = residence time, $\tau$ = thermal death time at $T_1$ |
| 11 | $q_{heat} = r_{O_2} \cdot V \cdot \Delta H_{O_2}$ | Metabolic heat generation | W | $\Delta H_{O_2} \approx 460$ kJ/mol O₂ |
| 12 | $t_{mix} = 5.9 \cdot T^{1.5} \cdot \dfrac{H}{N \cdot D^{2.5}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{N_p}\right)^{1/3}$ | Mixing time correlation (Rushton) | s | $T < 1$ m, $H/T < 2$ |
| 13 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{F} = \dfrac{1}{D}$ | Residence time (CSTR / PFR) | h | Continuous mode |
| 14 | $OUR = q_{O_2} \cdot C_x$ | Oxygen uptake rate | mmol/L·h | $q_{O_2}$ specific uptake, $C_x$ biomass |
| 15 | $\dfrac{dC_x}{dt} = \mu \cdot C_x - k_d \cdot C_x$ | Biomass balance (batch) | g/L·h | $\mu$ = specific growth rate |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry (mAbs, vaccines):** Specification of **single-use bioreactors (SUB)** with pre-sterilized bags eliminates SIP/CIP validation cost.
- **Industrial enzymes (Novozymes, DuPont):** Specifications drive **500 m³** stirred-tank reactors with **multi-Rushton** impellers, $P/V \approx 3$ kW/m³.
- **Wastewater treatment:** Specification of **airlift / bubble-column** reactors — no mechanical agitation, low energy.
- **Tissue engineering:** Specification of **perfusion bioreactors** with **hollow-fiber membranes** to retain shear-sensitive cells.
- **Biofuel (ethanol) industry:** Specification of **continuous stirred-tank reactor cascades (CSTR-in-series)** with residence time **6–12 h**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Gassed Power Requirement (Hughmark's Correlation)

**Problem Statement:** A 5 m³ working-volume fermenter (T = 1.8 m) uses two Rushton turbines (D = 0.6 m, spaced 0.9 m apart). The broth density ρ = 1050 kg/m³, viscosity μ = 0.002 Pa·s. Aeration rate Q_g = 0.5 vvm (volume per volume per minute) at N = 200 rpm. Calculate the **gassed power input** and the **specific power input P_g/V**.

**Given:**

$$V_w = 5 \text{ m}^3, \quad T = 1.8 \text{ m}, \quad D = 0.6 \text{ m}, \quad \rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\mu = 0.002 \text{ Pa·s}, \quad N = 200 \text{ rpm} = 3.333 \text{ rps}, \quad Q_g = 0.5 \text{ vvm}$$

**Step 1 — Convert aeration rate to volumetric flow:**

$$Q_g = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 0.04167 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Reynolds number (to confirm turbulent regime):**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu} = \dfrac{1050 \times 3.333 \times 0.6^2}{0.002}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{1050 \times 3.333 \times 0.36}{0.002} = \dfrac{1259.94}{0.002} = 6.30 \times 10^5$$

**Logic check:** $Re = 6.30 \times 10^5 \gg 10^4$ → **Turbulent regime** is fully valid.

**Step 3 — Un-gassed power (using N_p for Rushton turbine, turbulent = 5):**

$$P_u = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

$$P_u = 5 \times 1050 \times (3.333)^3 \times (0.6)^5$$

$$P_u = 5 \times 1050 \times 37.037 \times 0.07776$$

$$P_u = 5 \times 1050 \times 2.880 = 15{,}120 \text{ W} \approx 15.12 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Aeration number Na:**

$$N_a = \dfrac{Q_g}{N \cdot D^3} = \dfrac{0.04167}{3.333 \times 0.6^3} = \dfrac{0.04167}{3.333 \times 0.216} = \dfrac{0.04167}{0.7200} = 0.0579$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hughmark's correlation (C = 0.85, a = 0.45, valid for N_a < 0.1):**

$$P_g = P_u \cdot \left[1 - 0.85 \cdot (0.0579)^{0.45}\right]$$

$$(0.0579)^{0.45} = e^{0.45 \cdot \ln(0.0579)} = e^{0.45 \cdot (-2.849)} = e^{-1.282} = 0.2776$$

$$P_g = 15{,}120 \times (1 - 0.85 \times 0.2776)$$

$$P_g = 15{,}120 \times (1 - 0.2360) = 15{,}120 \times 0.7640$$

$$\boxed{P_g \approx 11{,}552 \text{ W} \approx 11.55 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6 — Specific power input (a critical specification!):**

$$\dfrac{P_g}{V} = \dfrac{11{,}552}{5} = 2310 \text{ W/m}^3 \approx 2.31 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A $P_g/V$ of **2.3 kW/m³** lies in the optimal range (**1–5 kW/m³**) for bacterial cultures, ensuring high k_La without excessive shear.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient (k_La)

**Problem Statement:** For the fermenter above, estimate the **volumetric oxygen transfer coefficient k_La** for a **non-coalescing broth** (e.g., electrolyte-rich medium) and the resulting **C** (steady-state DO) given an OTR demand of 250 mmol O₂/L·h and saturation C* = 0.21 mmol/L.

**Given:** $P_g/V = 2310$ W/m³, $v_s$ (superficial gas velocity) to be calculated.

**Step 1 — Superficial gas velocity:**

$$v_s = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_{cross}} = \dfrac{0.04167}{\pi \cdot (1.8)^2 / 4} = \dfrac{0.04167}{2.545} = 0.01637 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Van't Riet correlation for non-ionic / coalescing (K = 0.0026):**

$$k_L a = 0.0026 \cdot (2310)^{0.475} \cdot (0.01637)^{0.4}$$

$$(2310)^{0.475} = e^{0.475 \cdot \ln(2310)} = e^{0.475 \times 7.745} = e^{3.679} = 39.59$$

$$(0.01637)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \cdot \ln(0.01637)} = e^{0.4 \times (-4.111)} = e^{-1.644} = 0.1931$$

$$k_L a = 0.0026 \times 39.59 \times 0.1931 = 0.01988 \text{ s}^{-1} \approx 71.6 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Van't Riet correlation for ionic / non-coalescing (K = 0.01):**

$$k_L a = 0.01 \cdot (2310)^{0.7} \cdot (0.01637)^{0.2}$$

$$(2310)^{0.7} = e^{0.7 \times 7.745} = e^{5.421} = 226.5$$

$$(0.01637)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times (-4.111)} = e^{-0.8222} = 0.4395$$

$$k_L a = 0.01 \times 226.5 \times 0.4395 = 0.9955 \text{ s}^{-1} \approx 3584 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The two values differ by **~50×**! In a KTU exam, **always state which regime** (coalescing vs. non-coalescing) you are using. The **ionic (non-coalescing) broth gives k_La ~ 1000 h⁻¹** which is the typical high-performance range; the coalescing value is too low for high-density cultures.

**Step 4 — Steady-state dissolved oxygen C_L:**

$$\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = \text{OUR}$$

$$C_L = C^* - \dfrac{\text{OUR}}{k_L a}$$

Using ionic regime:

$$C_L = 0.21 - \dfrac{0.250}{0.9955 \times 3600} \text{ (unit conversion)}$$

$$\text{OUR} = 0.250 \text{ mmol/L·h} = 6.944 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mmol/L·s}$$

$$C_L = 0.21 - \dfrac{6.944 \times 10^{-5}}{0.9955} = 0.21 - 6.97 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.21 \text{ mmol/L}$$

**Logic check:** $C_L \approx C^*$ means the **oxygen supply comfortably meets the demand** — a healthy sign of over-designed aeration.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sterilization Time (Del Factor Approach)

**Problem Statement:** A 10 m³ medium contains an initial spore load $N_0 = 10^5$ spores/mL. We need to achieve a final contamination probability of 1 in 1000 batches, i.e., $N_f = 1$ spore in the whole 10⁷ mL medium. Calculate the required **Del factor**, and from that the **sterilization time at 121 °C**, given $k_{121} = 1.0 \text{ min}^{-1}$ (typical for *B. stearothermophilus* spores).

**Step 1 — Total initial spores in the medium:**

$$N_{0,\text{total}} = 10^5 \text{ spores/mL} \times 10^7 \text{ mL} = 10^{12} \text{ spores}$$

**Step 2 — Required Del factor (decimal reduction equivalent):**

$$\nabla = \ln\left(\dfrac{N_{0,\text{total}}}{N_{f,\text{total}}}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{10^{12}}{1}\right) = 12 \cdot \ln(10) = 27.63$$

$$\boxed{\nabla \approx 27.6}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU-accepted "safe" Del factor for media sterilization is **∇ ≥ 7** (i.e., 10⁷ fold reduction). Our calculation of **27.6** is a **conservative industrial value** (3.95× the minimum) — common in **pharmaceutical media prep**.

**Step 3 — Sterilization time at 121 °C (assuming isothermal, since ∇ is dominated by the holding phase):**

At $T = 121$ °C = 394 K, $k = 1.0$ min⁻¹, and since the reference temperature for $k_{121}$ IS 121 °C:

$$\nabla = k_{121} \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \dfrac{\nabla}{k_{121}} = \dfrac{27.63}{1.0}$$

$$\boxed{t = 27.63 \text{ min} \approx 28 \text{ min at 121 °C}}$$

**Step 4 — Realistic design with heating + cooling contributions:**

We apportion $\nabla_{medium} = 25$ to holding phase and $\nabla_{heating} + \nabla_{cooling} = 2.6$. For batch sterilization with **non-isothermal profile**, the **integral form** must be used:

$$\nabla = k_0 \int_0^t \exp\left[-\dfrac{E}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T(t)} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right] dt$$

For *B. stearothermophilus*: $E = 283$ kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T_{ref} = 394$ K.

If a typical heating time is **45 min** from 100 → 121 °C (linear):

$$\bar{T} = \dfrac{100 + 121}{2} = 110.5 \text{ °C} = 383.65 \text{ K}$$

$$k_{110.5} = 1.0 \cdot \exp\left[-\dfrac{283{,}000}{8.314} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{383.65} - \dfrac{1}{394}\right)\right]$$

$$\dfrac{1}{383.65} - \dfrac{1}{394} = 0.002607 - 0.002538 = 6.86 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$k_{110.5} = 1.0 \cdot \exp\left(-34040 \times 6.86 \times 10^{-5}\right) = \exp(-2.335) = 0.0967 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

$$\nabla_{heating} = 0.0967 \times 45 = 4.35$$

This single-phase contribution is already significant! In **industrial practice**, the heating and cooling phases cannot be ignored; they often contribute 20–50 % of total ∇.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Mixing Time (Specification for Nutrient Homogeneity)

**Problem Statement:** For the same 5 m³ fermenter (T = 1.8 m, H ≈ 2.5 m, D = 0.6 m, N = 3.333 rps, N_p = 5), calculate the **mixing time** $t_m$.

**Step 1 — Apply Rushton's mixing time correlation:**

$$t_m = 5.9 \cdot T^{1.5} \cdot \dfrac{H}{N \cdot D^{2.5}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{N_p}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$T^{1.5} = (1.8)^{1.5} = 2.414$$

$$D^{2.5} = (0.6)^{2.5} = 0.6^2 \times 0.6^{0.5} = 0.36 \times 0.7746 = 0.2789$$

$$N_p^{1/3} = 5^{1/3} = 1.710$$

**Step 3 — Plug in:**

$$t_m = 5.9 \times 2.414 \times \dfrac{2.5}{3.333 \times 0.2789} \times \dfrac{1}{1.710}$$

$$t_m = 14.24 \times \dfrac{2.5}{0.9295} \times 0.5848$$

$$t_m = 14.24 \times 2.690 \times 0.5848 = 22.4 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \approx 22 \text{ s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Specification acceptance criterion:** For most industrial fermentations, $t_m \leq 30$ s ensures that **pH, temperature, and substrate gradients** are negligible — meeting the KTU "homogeneous broth" specification.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Reactor Type vs. Specification

| Specification | Stirred Tank (STR) | Airlift (ALR) | Bubble Column (BC) | Packed Bed (PBR) | Membrane (MBR) |
|---------------|-------------------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Aspect ratio H/T | 1.0 – 2.0 | 4.0 – 8.0 | 4.0 – 6.0 | 2.0 – 5.0 | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| $P/V$ (kW/m³) | 1 – 5 | 0.2 – 1 | 0.05 – 0.3 | 0.1 – 1 | 0.05 – 0.3 |
| $k_L a$ (h⁻¹) | 200 – 2000 | 50 – 500 | 20 – 200 | 50 – 300 | 30 – 200 |
| Shear level | High | Low | Very low | Low | Very low |
| Cell-type fit | Bacteria, Yeast | Fungi, Algae | Yeast, Microalgae | Immobilized cells, Enzymes | Animal, Plant |
| Sterilization | Steam-SIP | Steam-SIP | Steam-SIP | In-situ | CIP + module replacement |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Low | High | Very high |
| Scale-up ease | Moderate | Easy | Easiest | Hard | Hard |
| KTU exam weight | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ |
