## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*List any six essential mechanical specifications of an industrial-scale biochemical reactor used for aerobic bacterial fermentation.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Material of construction:** SS-316L with internal electropolished finish (Ra ≤ 0.4 µm) to prevent corrosion and microbial adhesion.
2. **Working volume:** Typically **60–80 % of total volume (V_max)**; e.g., 5 m³ working in a 7 m³ vessel to allow headspace for foam disengagement.
3. **Aspect ratio (H/T):** 1.0–2.0 (commonly ~1.5) to optimize mixing and gas-liquid mass transfer.
4. **Agitation system:** 2 Rushton turbines (D/T = 1/3) with mechanical seal; N_p ≈ 5; speed 100–300 rpm.
5. **Baffles:** 4 standard baffles, each T/12 wide, mounted off-wall by ~10 mm to prevent vortex and improve CIP.
6. **Aeration system:** Ring sparger with 1–5 mm holes located 0.5–1.0 D off the bottom; air through 0.2 µm inlet filter.
7. **Sterilization:** SIP capability at 121 °C / 30 min with steam-locked lines.
8. **Instrumentation ports:** pH, DO, temperature, foam, level, and OD probes on the top head.

> [!TIP]
> A 3-mark question expects **6 short points × ½ mark each = 3 marks**. Use bullet format and engineering units!

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Distinguish between power number (N_p), Reynolds number (Re), and aeration number (N_a) with their significance in biochemical reactor scale-up.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Significance |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Power number, $N_p$** | $N_p = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$ | Dimensionless measure of impeller power consumption; constant in turbulent regime (N_p ≈ 5 for RT) — used to compute $P$ for scale-up. |
| **Reynolds number, $Re$** | $Re = \rho N D^2 / \mu$ | Indicates flow regime: $Re < 10$ laminar, $> 10^4$ turbulent; essential to identify which $N_p$ to use. |
| **Aeration number, $N_a$** | $N_a = Q_g / (N D^3)$ | Quantifies the effect of gas inflow on impeller power; high $N_a$ (gassed) reduces $P$ via Hughmark correlation; governs k_La. |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, **scale-up is judged on constant P/V** (oxygen transfer), **constant N·D** (shear), or **constant tip speed** — each tied to one of these dimensionless groups.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
**(A) Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **critical mechanical specifications** of a **sterilizable-in-place (SIP) stirred-tank bioreactor** for the production of a recombinant protein in *E. coli*, including geometry, material, agitation, aeration, baffling, and instrumentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 m³ working volume fermenter (T = 1.8 m) is equipped with two Rushton turbines (D = 0.6 m). The broth has ρ = 1050 kg/m³ and μ = 2 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. The impeller runs at 200 rpm with an aeration rate of 0.5 vvm. Calculate:
   (i) Reynolds number and confirm the flow regime. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) Un-gassed and gassed power input. **(3 marks)**
   (iii) Specific power input $P_g/V$ and comment on its suitability for high-cell-density *E. coli* cultivation. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Mechanical Specifications (7 marks)**

**(1) Geometry:**
- Cylindrical, vertical vessel; dish-end (torispherical) or hemispherical heads.
- **Aspect ratio H/T = 1.0–2.0**; fill fraction = 0.7–0.8; headspace = 20–30 %.
- Standard sizes from 1 L (lab) to 250 m³ (industrial); for *E. coli* recombinant protein, 10–100 m³ is common.

**(2) Material of Construction:**
- **SS-316L** (low carbon ≤ 0.03 %, 16–18 % Cr, 10–14 % Ni, 2–3 % Mo) for Cl⁻ corrosion resistance.
- Internal surface **electropolished to Ra ≤ 0.4 µm**.
- All welds **ground flush, crevice-free**, then passivated with HNO₃.

**(3) Agitation System:**
- **Top-driven motor** with **mechanical double seal** (or magnetic coupling for sterility).
- **2 × Rushton disc turbines**, D/T = 0.33, spaced 1.0–1.5 D apart.
- Operating speed: 100–400 rpm; specific power input target: **2–5 kW/m³** (for high-cell-density *E. coli*).

**(4) Baffles:**
- 4 standard baffles, T/12 wide, **off-wall** by 10 mm.
- Allow CIP flow behind; convert swirling to axial-radial turbulence.

**(5) Aeration System:**
- **Ring sparger** with 1–3 mm holes, mounted 0.5 D off bottom.
- **0.2 µm PTFE inlet vent filter** on the air line; sparger designed for **0.3–1.0 vvm**.
- Exhaust vent filter (0.2 µm, large area to handle foam) + condenser for off-gas.

**(6) Heat-Transfer:**
- **Half-pipe limpet coil** (preferred) or full jacket on side wall.
- Cooling capacity: peak metabolic heat = 0.4 × OUR × V × 460 kJ/mol O₂.
- Insulated externally with 50–80 mm mineral wool.

**(7) Instrumentation & Control Ports:**
- **Top-head ports:** pH probe, DO (polarographic), Pt-100 RTD, foam sensor (conductivity), load cell, OD (NIR), sampling, and 4–6 addition ports (acid, base, anti-foam, feed, inducer).
- **Bottom port:** Pneumatic diaphragm drain valve (steam-sterilizable).
- **PLC/DCS with PID control** for pH, DO, T, foam, level; **SCADA/HMI** for operator interface.

**(8) Sterilization (SIP) Provisions:**
- All process lines **steam-locked** (positive steam pressure) when not in use.
- Sterilization cycle: 121 °C / 30 min saturated steam in vessel + lines.
- **Double-block-and-bleed valves** at critical junctions to prevent cross-contamination.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu} = \dfrac{1050 \times 3.333 \times 0.6^2}{0.002} = 6.30 \times 10^5$$

Since $Re = 6.30 \times 10^5 \gg 10^4$, the flow is **fully turbulent**. (N_p = 5 is valid for a single Rushton turbine.) **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Un-gassed power:**

$$P_u = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5 = 5 \times 1050 \times (3.333)^3 \times (0.6)^5 = 15{,}120 \text{ W} \approx 15.12 \text{ kW}$$

Aeration number:

$$N_a = \dfrac{Q_g}{N \cdot D^3} = \dfrac{0.04167}{3.333 \times 0.216} = 0.0579$$

Hughmark's gassed power:

$$P_g = P_u \cdot [1 - 0.85 \cdot N_a^{0.45}] = 15{,}120 \times [1 - 0.85 \times (0.0579)^{0.45}]$$

$$(0.0579)^{0.45} = 0.2776$$

$$P_g = 15{,}120 \times (1 - 0.2360) = 15{,}120 \times 0.7640 \approx 11{,}552 \text{ W} \approx 11.55 \text{ kW}$$

**(3 marks)**

**(iii) Specific power input:**

$$\dfrac{P_g}{V} = \dfrac{11{,}552 \text{ W}}{5 \text{ m}^3} = 2310 \text{ W/m}^3 = 2.31 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

**Comment:** For high-cell-density *E. coli* (which can reach 50–100 g DCW/L in fed-batch), the specific oxygen demand is **25–40 mmol O₂/g DCW·h**. A $P_g/V$ of **2.3 kW/m³** lies in the recommended industrial range (**1–5 kW/m³**) and can sustain a k_La in the order of **500–1000 h⁻¹**, which is **adequate for fed-batch *E. coli* processes**. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Part B — Alternative Question Choice

**(B) Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **material of construction, surface finish, and sterilization philosophy** for an industrial bioreactor. Why is SS-316L preferred over SS-304? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 m³ batch of medium at 10⁵ spores/mL is to be sterilized to achieve a final contamination probability of 1 in 10³ batches. The reference death rate constant at 121 °C is $k_{121} = 1.0$ min⁻¹ (using *B. stearothermophilus* data, $E = 283$ kJ/mol).
   (i) Compute the required **Del factor**. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) Calculate the **holding time at 121 °C**. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) If the heating phase is non-isothermal (T rises linearly from 100 → 121 °C over 30 min), estimate the **lethality contributed by the heating phase** and state its significance. **(3 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Material of Construction (7 marks)**

**Why SS-316L over SS-304:**

| Property | SS-304 | SS-316L | Why it matters |
|----------|--------|---------|----------------|
| Cr content | 18 % | 16–18 % | Passive film (Cr₂O₃) — corrosion resistance |
| Ni content | 8 % | 10–14 % | Austenitic stability, ductility |
| **Mo content** | 0 % | **2–3 %** | **Resistance to pitting by Cl⁻ ions** (critical for media salts) |
| C content | ≤ 0.08 % | **≤ 0.03 %** | **Prevents carbide precipitation at welds** → eliminates intergranular corrosion |
| Cost | Lower | ~25 % higher | Justified by longevity and validation cost |

**Surface Finish:**
- Mechanical polishing (180–320 grit) followed by **electropolishing** → Ra ≤ 0.4 µm.
- Removes micro-crevices that harbor spores and biofilms; facilitates CIP coverage.
- **Passivation** with 20 % HNO₃ for 30 min at 50 °C to restore the Cr-rich oxide layer.

**Sterilization Philosophy:**
- **SIP (Sterilization-In-Place):** All wetted parts reach 121 °C / 30 min with saturated steam.
- Steam purity: condensate conductivity < 1 µS/cm; no amines or boiler chemicals.
- **SIP cycle validation:** 3 consecutive successful runs with each point reaching F₀ ≥ 30 min (integrated lethality).
- **Double-block-and-bleed** at junctions; **steam-locks** on idle lines.
- **Sterile boundary** maintained by **0.2 µm vent filters** at all air/steam inlets and exhausts.

**Part (b) — Sterilization Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Required Del factor:**

Total volume = 10 m³ = 10⁷ mL. Initial spore count:

$$N_0 = 10^5 \text{ spores/mL} \times 10^7 \text{ mL} = 10^{12} \text{ spores}$$

Final: 1 in 10³ batches → 10⁻³ contamination events/batch, i.e., we need:

$$N_f \cdot (\text{batches}) = 10^{-3} \Rightarrow N_f = 10^{-3} \text{ spores per batch (acceptable)}$$

$$\nabla = \ln\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N_f}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{10^{12}}{10^{-3}}\right) = \ln(10^{15}) = 15 \cdot \ln 10 = 34.54$$

$$\boxed{\nabla \approx 34.5}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Holding time at 121 °C (isothermal):**

$$t = \dfrac{\nabla}{k_{121}} = \dfrac{34.5}{1.0} = 34.5 \text{ min}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Lethality of heating phase:**

Average temperature: $\bar{T} = (100 + 121)/2 = 110.5$ °C = 383.65 K

$$k_{110.5} = k_{121} \cdot \exp\left[-\dfrac{E}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{\bar{T}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right]$$

$$= 1.0 \cdot \exp\left[-\dfrac{283{,}000}{8.314} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{383.65} - \dfrac{1}{394}\right)\right]$$

$$= \exp\left[-34{,}040 \times 6.86 \times 10^{-5}\right] = \exp(-2.335) = 0.0967 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

$$\nabla_{heating} = k_{110.5} \cdot t_{heat} = 0.0967 \times 30 = 2.90$$ **(2 marks)**

**Significance (1 mark):**
> The heating phase alone contributes **∇_heating = 2.90**, which is **~8.4 %** of the total required ∇ = 34.5. This is significant! Therefore, when designing sterilization cycles, the **non-isothermal heating ramp** must be included in the integrated lethality calculation. Ignoring it (assuming all ∇ comes from the holding phase) would underestimate the true sterilization and could lead to **failure of the SIP validation** in GMP audits. The cooling phase similarly adds lethality.

---

### 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Aeration rate in vvm must be converted to **m³/s** for power calculations. rpm must be converted to **rps** ($N$ in rps = rpm/60). This alone costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong N_p for non-Rushton impellers:** Pitched-blade (4-blade 45°) has $N_p \approx 1.3$ in turbulent regime — **not 5**. Propeller has $N_p \approx 0.32$. Always state which impeller.
> 3. **Sterilization arithmetic error:** Forgetting $\ln(10) \approx 2.303$ and writing $\nabla = N_0 / N_f$ (ratio) instead of $\ln(N_0/N_f)$. This is a **fatal 1-mark error** in 3-mark questions.
> 4. **k_La correlation confusion:** Mixing up coalescing vs. non-coalescing correlations → 50× error. Always **state the regime** and cite the correct $K$ value.
> 5. **Listing specifications without units:** A specification like "high power input" carries no marks. Always give $P/V$ in **W/m³** or **kW/m³**, $k_L a$ in **s⁻¹ or h⁻¹**, $N_p$, $Re$ as dimensionless numbers.
> 6. **Not mentioning SS-316L specifically:** SS-304 is **NOT** accepted as a bioreactor material for pharmaceutical processes — it fails Cl⁻ pitting tests.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Biochemical reactor = sterile, instrumented, controlled vessel** for biological transformations.
- **Six core specification groups:** Geometry, Material, Agitation, Aeration, Instrumentation, Sterilization.
- **Standard material:** **SS-316L, electropolished to Ra ≤ 0.4 µm**.
- **Aspect ratio:** $H/T = 1.0$ – $2.0$ (mostly 1.5).
- **Fill fraction:** $V_w / V_{max} = 0.7$ – $0.8$.
- **Agitation:** Rushton turbine ($N_p \approx 5$, turbulent), D/T = 1/3, multi-impeller.
- **Baffles:** 4 off-wall baffles, T/12 wide.
- **Sparger:** Ring type, off-bottom, holes 1–5 mm.
- **Vent filter:** 0.2 µm PTFE, both inlet and exhaust.
- **Specific power input:** $P_g/V$ = **1–5 kW/m³** for bacteria/yeast; **0.05–0.3 kW/m³** for animal cells.
- **k_La correlations (Van't Riet, 1979):**
  - Coalescing: $k_L a = 0.0026 \cdot (P_g/V)^{0.475} \cdot v_s^{0.4}$
  - Non-coalescing: $k_L a = 0.01 \cdot (P_g/V)^{0.7} \cdot v_s^{0.2}$
- **Sterilization target:** $\nabla \geq 7$ (media) and $\nabla \geq 20$ (heat-stable sugars/pharmaceutical grade).
- **Del factor:** $\nabla = \ln(N_0/N_f) = \int k(T) \cdot dt$.
- **SIP cycle:** **121 °C / 30 min** (saturated steam).
- **Mixing time target:** $t_m \leq 30$ s for homogeneous broth.
- **Heat removal:** $q = r_{O_2} \cdot V \cdot \Delta H_{O_2}$ with $\Delta H_{O_2} \approx 460$ kJ/mol O₂.
- **Scale-up criteria:** Constant $P/V$ (mass transfer), constant $N \cdot D$ (shear), constant tip speed.
- **Reactor-type selection:** STR (most versatile), ALR (low shear, fungi), BC (cheapest), PBR (immobilized enzymes), MBR (animal cells).
- **Continuous sterilization:** $\nabla = k \cdot D \cdot \ln[1 + (\theta/\tau)(e^{E/RT_1} - 1)]$.
- **GMP mantra for specifications:** "**Documented, Validated, Repeatable**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Stirred Tank Bioreactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Continuous Stirred Tank Bioreactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Continuous Stirred Tank Bioreactor (CSTB)**, also known as a **Chemostat** when operating under steady-state microbial growth conditions, is a perfectly mixed biological reactor in which sterile nutrient medium is continuously fed into the vessel at a constant volumetric flow rate ($F$), while an equal volume of well-mixed reaction broth (containing cells, unused substrate, and products) is simultaneously withdrawn. The contents are maintained in a homogeneous state by mechanical agitation, ensuring that the concentration of every component inside the reactor equals the concentration in the exit stream.

Mathematically, the **space time ($\tau$)** or its reciprocal, the **dilution rate ($D$)**, characterizes the reactor:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{F} \quad \text{and} \quad D = \frac{F}{V} = \frac{1}{\tau}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A CSTB operates as an **open system** in steady state, with constant volume ($V$ = constant), uniform composition (perfect mixing), and continuous inflow equal to outflow ($F_{in} = F_{out}$). The bioreactor is the most fundamental model in biochemical engineering for studying microbial growth kinetics and continuous bioprocesses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Fish Tank with Filter**
> Imagine a rectangular fish tank with an inlet pipe constantly dripping fresh, clean water from the top, and a drain at the bottom removing water (along with fish waste, leftover food, and even some fish) at exactly the same rate. A small pump keeps the water swirling so well that the water quality is identical everywhere — near the inlet, in the middle, and at the outlet. If the inflow rate is too fast, fish are washed out faster than they can reproduce and the tank becomes empty. If the inflow is perfectly tuned, the fish population stabilizes at an equilibrium. This is exactly how a **Chemostat / CSTB** behaves with **microorganisms, nutrients, and product**.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Features of a CSTB

- **Steady-state operation:** Once equilibrium is reached, all concentrations remain time-invariant.
- **Independent control of dilution rate ($D$):** The growth rate $\mu$ of cells is forced to equal $D$, making it possible to set the cellular growth rate experimentally.
- **Self-regulating nutrient limitation:** By choosing a limiting substrate, the steady-state cell density is uniquely determined.
- **No spatial gradients:** Mixing eliminates concentration and temperature gradients.

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor volume | $V$ | **1 L – 1000 m³** |
| Volumetric flow rate | $F$ | **L·h⁻¹** or **m³·s⁻¹** |
| Dilution rate | $D$ | **0.01 – 1.0 h⁻¹** |
| Maximum specific growth rate | $\mu_{max}$ | **0.1 – 1.0 h⁻¹** |
| Substrate saturation constant | $K_s$ | **mg·L⁻¹** |
| Cell yield coefficient | $Y_{X/S}$ | **0.4 – 0.6 g/g** |
| Feed substrate concentration | $S_0$ | **5 – 50 g·L⁻¹** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind the Equations

A CSTR is governed by the **general mass balance equation**:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} \pm \text{Generation/Consumption}$$

When applied to a CSTB for a substrate $S$:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} \cdot V = F \cdot S_0 - F \cdot S + V \cdot r_s$$

where $r_s$ is the net rate of substrate consumption (negative value, g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Why mixing matters):** Because the reactor is *perfectly mixed*, the concentration of substrate *inside* the reactor equals the concentration *in the outlet stream*. This single assumption collapses a 3-D fluid mechanics problem into 1 algebraic equation — making the chemostat the most analytically tractable bioreactor in the syllabus.

## 2.2 Steady-State Assumption

At **steady state** (denoted by overbar or subscript $ss$), all time derivatives vanish:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{dX}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{dP}{dt} = 0$$

This transforms differential equations into algebraic equations, which is the power of continuous operation for design and control.

## 2.3 Monod Kinetics in a CSTB

The **Monod equation** describes microbial specific growth rate:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$$

**Why Monod is used:** It captures two extreme behaviors — at low $S$, $\mu$ is first-order in $S$ (substrate-limited); at high $S$, $\mu \to \mu_{max}$ (zero-order, enzyme-saturated). This mirrors Michaelis–Menten enzyme kinetics.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it contains 90 % of the marks awarded in CSTB problems.**

| # | Equation | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = F / V = 1/\tau$ | Dilution rate definition | h⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\mu = \mu_{max} S / (K_s + S)$ | Monod specific growth rate | h⁻¹ |
| 3 | $D = \mu_{ss}$ | Chemostat steady-state identity (sterile feed) | h⁻¹ |
| 4 | $S_{ss} = K_s D / (\mu_{max} - D)$ | Steady-state residual substrate | g·L⁻¹ |
| 5 | $X_{ss} = Y_{X/S} (S_0 - S_{ss})$ | Steady-state biomass concentration | g·L⁻¹ |
| 6 | $D_c = \mu_{max} S_0 / (K_s + S_0)$ | **Critical dilution rate** (max operable $D$) | h⁻¹ |
| 7 | $D_{max} = \mu_{max}$ | **Washout dilution rate** (when $S_0 \gg K_s$) | h⁻¹ |
| 8 | $X_{max} = Y_{X/S}[S_0 + K_s - \sqrt{K_s^2 + S_0 K_s}]$ | Maximum cell concentration (occurs at $D \to 0$) | g·L⁻¹ |
| 9 | $D \cdot X_{ss}$ | Volumetric productivity of cells | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 10 | $D(S_0 - S_{ss})$ | Volumetric productivity of substrate consumption | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 11 | $P_{ss} = Y_{P/S}(S_0 - S_{ss})$ | Product concentration (if growth-associated) | g·L⁻¹ |
| 12 | $r_X = D X_{ss}$ | Rate of biomass production per unit volume | g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 13 | $\tau_{min} = 1/D_{max} = 1/\mu_{max}$ | Minimum residence time to avoid washout | h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - Valid only when **$D < D_c$** (otherwise washout occurs).
> - Valid when feed is **sterile** ($X_{in} = 0$).
> - Valid when **substrate is the limiting nutrient** (one substrate controls growth).
> - For intracellular / non-growth-associated product (e.g., secondary metabolite), use Luedeking–Piret kinetics: $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CSTBs are the workhorses of:
- **Wastewater treatment** (activated sludge process — a giant aerobic CSTB).
- **Single-cell protein (SCP) production** (e.g., *Methylophilus methylotrophus* for Pruteen).
- **Ethanol fermentation** in industrial breweries running continuous mode.
- **Recombinant protein production** (e.g., monoclonal antibodies in perfusion mode).
- **Production of amino acids, organic acids, and enzymes** at industrial scale.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Substrate Concentration ($S_{ss}$)

We start from the **unsteady-state substrate mass balance** for a sterile-feed CSTB:

$$V \frac{dS}{dt} = F S_0 - F S - V \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

**Step 1 — Divide through by $V$ to convert to intensive form:**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(S_0 - S) - \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $F/V = D$ (dilution rate):**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = D(S_0 - S) - \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

**Step 3 — Write the cell mass balance (sterile feed ⇒ $X_{in} = 0$):**

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = -D X + \mu X = X(\mu - D)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the steady-state condition for cells: $dX/dt = 0$.**

Because $X_{ss} \neq 0$ (we have a viable culture), it follows that:

$$\mu_{ss} = D$$

This is the **famous chemostat identity** — the operating dilution rate *forces* the microbial specific growth rate to match it.

**Step 5 — Substitute $\mu = D$ into the Monod equation and solve for $S_{ss}$:**

$$D = \frac{\mu_{max} S_{ss}}{K_s + S_{ss}}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-multiply:**

$$D(K_s + S_{ss}) = \mu_{max} S_{ss}$$

**Step 7 — Expand the left-hand side:**

$$D \cdot K_s + D \cdot S_{ss} = \mu_{max} S_{ss}$$

**Step 8 — Collect the $S_{ss}$ terms on one side:**

$$D \cdot K_s = \mu_{max} S_{ss} - D \cdot S_{ss} = S_{ss}(\mu_{max} - D)$$

**Step 9 — Solve explicitly for $S_{ss}$:**

$$\boxed{S_{ss} = \frac{K_s \cdot D}{\mu_{max} - D}}$$

**Logical commentary:** As $D \to \mu_{max}$, the denominator shrinks to zero and $S_{ss} \to \infty$ — physically meaning the substrate can no longer be consumed and washout begins. As $D \to 0$, $S_{ss} \to 0$, meaning the cells have enough residence time to consume all substrate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Biomass Concentration ($X_{ss}$)

**Step 1 — Return to the substrate balance and set $dS/dt = 0$:**

$$0 = D(S_0 - S_{ss}) - \frac{\mu_{ss} X_{ss}}{Y_{X/S}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\mu_{ss} = D$ (from derivation 1, step 4):**

$$0 = D(S_0 - S_{ss}) - \frac{D \cdot X_{ss}}{Y_{X/S}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $D$ (assuming $D \neq 0$):**

$$0 = (S_0 - S_{ss}) - \frac{X_{ss}}{Y_{X/S}}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$\frac{X_{ss}}{Y_{X/S}} = S_0 - S_{ss}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply both sides by $Y_{X/S}$:**

$$\boxed{X_{ss} = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S_{ss})}$$

**Logical commentary:** This is identical in form to a simple "substrate-consumed × yield" mass balance, which is intuitive — biomass is produced in proportion to substrate consumed, with yield $Y_{X/S}$ as the proportionality constant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Dilution Rate ($D_c$)

Washout occurs when cells can no longer maintain themselves against dilution. Setting $X_{ss} = 0$:

**Step 1 — Use $X_{ss} = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S_{ss}) = 0$:**

$$\implies S_{ss} = S_0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $S_{ss} = S_0$ into the $S_{ss}$ expression:**

$$S_0 = \frac{K_s D_c}{\mu_{max} - D_c}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$S_0(\mu_{max} - D_c) = K_s D_c$$

**Step 4 — Expand:**

$$S_0 \mu_{max} - S_0 D_c = K_s D_c$$

**Step 5 — Collect the $D_c$ terms:**

$$S_0 \mu_{max} = S_0 D_c + K_s D_c = D_c(S_0 + K_s)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $D_c$:**

$$\boxed{D_c = \frac{\mu_{max} S_0}{K_s + S_0}}$$

**Special case (most often tested):** When $S_0 \gg K_s$ (typical industrial case, since $K_s$ is very small, often < 10 mg/L), then $K_s + S_0 \approx S_0$ and:

$$D_c \approx \mu_{max}$$

This is why **washout dilution rate ≈ $\mu_{max}$** is the universal textbook statement.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Volumetric Productivity

Volumetric productivity $P_r$ of biomass is the mass of cells produced per unit reactor volume per unit time:

$$P_r = D \cdot X_{ss} = D \cdot Y_{X/S}\left(S_0 - \frac{K_s D}{\mu_{max} - D}\right)$$

**Maximization:** Setting $dP_r/dD = 0$ and solving (a routine KTU exam sub-question), we get the optimal dilution rate:

$$D_{opt} = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{K_s + S_0}}\right)$$

and the corresponding maximum productivity:

$$P_{r,max} = Y_{X/S} \mu_{max} S_0 \left(\sqrt{\frac{K_s}{K_s + S_0}}\right) \left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{K_s + S_0}}\right)^2$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of $D$ on System Variables

| Dilution rate $D$ | $S_{ss}$ | $X_{ss}$ | $\mu$ | $D \cdot X$ (Productivity) | Operating regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D \to 0$ | $\to 0$ | $\to Y_{X/S} S_0$ | $\to 0$ | $\to 0$ | All substrate consumed; cells "starved" |
| $0 < D < D_{opt}$ | Small | Near max | $< D_{opt}$ | Increasing | Substrate-limited |
| $D = D_{opt}$ | Moderate | Moderate | $D_{opt}$ | **Maximum** | **Optimal productivity** |
| $D_{opt} < D < D_c$ | Increasing | Decreasing | $< D$ | Decreasing | Substrate-stressed |
| $D = D_c$ | $\to S_0$ | $\to 0$ | $\to \mu_{max}$ | $\to 0$ | **Washout threshold** |
| $D > D_c$ | $S_0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | **Washed out** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** The maximum cell concentration $X_{max}$ occurs as $D \to 0$ (long residence time, near-complete substrate consumption), but maximum **productivity** $D \cdot X$ occurs at an *intermediate* $D$ (typically $D_{opt} \approx 0.5 D_c$). Engineers always optimize productivity, not cell density.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Continuous Stirred Tank Bioreactor

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block depicts a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the mass and information flows in a Chemostat. It adheres to the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, raw uppercase labels, no markdown inside quoted labels).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[STERILE FEED RESERVOIR] -->|Flow F Substrate S0| A2[INFLOW LINE]
    A2 --> A3[REACTOR VESSEL V]
    A3 <-->|MECHANICAL IMPELLER MIXING| A3
    A3 -->|SUBSTRATE S CONSUMED| A4[BIOMASS X GROWS]
    A3 -->|PRODUCT P FORMED| A5[PRODUCT ACCUMULATION]
    A3 -->|Flow F| A6[OUTFLOW LINE]
    A6 --> A7[EFFLUENT STREAM]
    A7 -->|CENTRIFUGE OR FILTER| A8[CELL SEPARATION]
    A8 -->|BIOMASS HARVEST| A9[PRODUCT PURIFICATION]
    A8 -->|SUPERNATANT| A10[WASTE TREATMENT]
    A3 -.->|SAMPLING PORT| A11[ONLINE SENSOR ARRAY]
    A11 -.->|FEEDBACK SIGNAL| A12[CONTROL LOOP PLC]
    A12 -.->|ADJUST F| A1
```

## 4.2 Steady-State Logic Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Start CSTB with Inoculum X0] --> B2{Culture grows batch mode}
    B2 --> B3[Switch on Feed Pump and Harvest]
    B3 --> B4{D has been set}
    B4 --> B5[Calculate mu = D from chemostat identity]
    B5 --> B6[Calculate Sss from Monod equation]
    B6 --> B7[Calculate Xss from yield balance]
    B7 --> B8{D greater than Dc question}
    B8 -->|Yes| B9[WASHOUT all cells lost reset system]
    B8 -->|No| B10[STABLE STEADY STATE achieved]
    B10 --> B11[Measure Productivity D times Xss]
    B11 --> B12{Productivity acceptable question}
    B12 -->|No| B13[Tune D and repeat]
    B12 -->|Yes| B14[Continuous production for weeks or months]
```

## 4.3 Sub-System Schematic of a Real Industrial CSTB

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Medium Sterilization Skid] -->|Steam 121 C| C2[Cooling Heat Exchanger]
    C2 --> C3[Feed Tank S0 g per L]
    C3 -->|Peristaltic Pump| C4[Inlet Port at Top of Vessel]
    C4 --> C5[Jacketed Stainless Steel Vessel]
    C5 --> C6[Stirred by Rushton Impeller]
    C5 --> C7[Sparger for Aeration if Aerobic]
    C7 --> C8[Filter Sterilized Air Inlet]
    C5 --> C9[Harvest Port Overflow]
    C9 --> C10[Continuous Centrifuge]
    C10 --> C11[Cell Paste Stream]
    C10 --> C12[Supernatant to Downstream]
    C5 -.-> C13[DO Probe pH Probe Temperature Probe]
    C13 -.-> C14[Distributed Control System DCS]
    C14 -.->|PID Control| C3
    C14 -.->|PID Control| C8
    C14 -.->|Variable Frequency Drive| C6
```

## 4.4 Mass-Balance Interaction Map (Generic CSTB)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[F S0] -->|Input| D2[Reactor Accumulation term dS per dt times V]
    D3[F S] -->|Output| D2
    D2 --> D4[Reaction term V times rs]
    D4 --> D5[S balance closed]
    D6[F zero sterile feed] -->|Input| D7[Reactor Accumulation term dX per dt times V]
    D8[F Xss] -->|Output| D7
    D7 --> D9[Growth term V times mu times X]
    D9 --> D10[X balance closed]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 model]**

### **Q1. Define a Continuous Stirred Tank Bioreactor. State any two assumptions used in its analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Continuous Stirred Tank Bioreactor (CSTB)** is a well-mixed biological reactor in which sterile nutrient feed enters continuously at a constant flow rate $F$ and an equal volume of culture broth leaves the reactor continuously, maintaining constant volume $V$ and uniform composition.

**Two key assumptions:**
1. The contents are **perfectly mixed**, so the concentration inside the vessel equals the concentration in the exit stream.
2. The feed is **sterile** ($X_{in} = 0$), and the system is at **steady state** ($dX/dt = dS/dt = 0$).
3. (any other valid assumption: single limiting substrate, constant $\mu_{max}$, constant $Y_{X/S}$, isothermal operation — write any 2 of these)

---

### **Q2. What is washout in a chemostat? Write the condition under which washout occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Washout** is the operating condition in a continuous bioreactor in which the dilution rate $D$ exceeds the cells' maximum ability to reproduce, causing the biomass concentration $X$ to fall to zero and the cells to be flushed out of the reactor faster than they can grow.

**Condition for washout:**

$$D \geq D_c = \frac{\mu_{max} S_0}{K_s + S_0}$$

For $S_0 \gg K_s$, this simplifies to:

$$D \geq \mu_{max}$$

Once washout occurs, $X_{ss} = 0$ and $S_{ss} = S_0$; the reactor behaves as an empty, sterile vessel and the culture is lost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Starting from the general mass balance equation, derive the steady-state expressions for substrate concentration ($S_{ss}$) and biomass concentration ($X_{ss}$) in a chemostat operating with sterile feed and Monod kinetics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemostat operates with a feed substrate concentration $S_0 = 20$ g/L. The kinetic parameters are $\mu_{max} = 0.5$ h⁻¹, $K_s = 0.2$ g/L, $Y_{X/S} = 0.5$ g/g. Calculate the critical dilution rate $D_c$ and the steady-state values of $S_{ss}$ and $X_{ss}$ when the operating dilution rate is $D = 0.2$ h⁻¹. Comment on whether washout will occur. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

Write substrate mass balance over the reactor:

$$V \frac{dS}{dt} = F S_0 - F S - V \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

Divide by $V$:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = D(S_0 - S) - \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

Cell mass balance (sterile feed):

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = -D X + \mu X = X(\mu - D)$$

At steady state $dX/dt = 0$ and $X_{ss} \neq 0$, so $\mu_{ss} = D$. Substituting into Monod:

$$D = \frac{\mu_{max} S_{ss}}{K_s + S_{ss}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{S_{ss} = \frac{K_s D}{\mu_{max} - D}}$$

From substrate balance at steady state with $\mu = D$:

$$X_{ss} = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S_{ss})$$

> (Examiner's note: 1 mark for each correct general equation, 1 mark for steady-state simplification, 2 marks for solving $S_{ss}$, 1 mark for $X_{ss}$, 1 mark for stating assumptions.)

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $S_0 = 20$ g/L, $\mu_{max} = 0.5$ h⁻¹, $K_s = 0.2$ g/L, $Y_{X/S} = 0.5$ g/g, $D = 0.2$ h⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Critical dilution rate:**

$$D_c = \frac{\mu_{max} S_0}{K_s + S_0} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{0.2 + 20} = \frac{10}{20.2} = 0.4950 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state substrate concentration:**

$$S_{ss} = \frac{K_s D}{\mu_{max} - D} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.2}{0.5 - 0.2} = \frac{0.04}{0.3} = 0.1333 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state biomass:**

$$X_{ss} = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S_{ss}) = 0.5 \times (20 - 0.1333) = 0.5 \times 19.8667 = 9.9333 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 4 — Washout comment:**

Since $D = 0.2$ h⁻¹ $< D_c = 0.495$ h⁻¹, the operating point is **safe** — no washout will occur. The system runs at $\sim 40\%$ of the critical dilution rate.

> (Examiner's note: 1 mark for $D_c$, 1 mark for $S_{ss}$, 1 mark for $X_{ss}$, 2 marks for the washout comparison, 2 marks for proper units and logical commentary.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** What is a chemostat? With the help of a neat schematic, explain the steady-state operation of a continuous stirred tank bioreactor. Mention the role of the dilution rate $D$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **volumetric productivity** of biomass in a chemostat. Show that the productivity is maximum at an optimal dilution rate $D_{opt}$ and obtain an expression for $D_{opt}$. Given $\mu_{max} = 0.4$ h⁻¹, $K_s = 0.1$ g/L, $S_0 = 25$ g/L, find the value of $D_{opt}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) (7 marks)

A **chemostat** is a CSTB operating at steady state with continuous sterile feed. The vessel is well-mixed by an impeller; inlet flow $F$ (substrate concentration $S_0$) enters and outlet flow $F$ (substrate $S$, biomass $X$, product $P$) leaves. The volume $V$ remains constant.

Schematic: see **Section 4.3** of these notes.

**Role of $D$:** $D = F/V$ sets the *forced* specific growth rate of the cells because $\mu_{ss} = D$. It is the operator's single most important control knob. Higher $D$ → higher growth rate → less substrate consumption → lower biomass. The reactor fails (washout) when $D \geq D_c$.

---

#### Part (b) — Productivity Derivation and Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Volumetric productivity definition:**

$$P_r = D \cdot X_{ss} = D \cdot Y_{X/S}\left(S_0 - \frac{K_s D}{\mu_{max} - D}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Maximize $P_r$ by setting $dP_r/dD = 0$.** Differentiating and simplifying (routine algebra, full expansion given below):

$$\frac{dP_r}{dD} = Y_{X/S}\left[S_0 - \frac{K_s D}{\mu_{max} - D} - D \cdot \frac{K_s(\mu_{max} - D) - K_s D \cdot (-1)}{(\mu_{max} - D)^2}\right]$$

After cancellation, the condition $dP_r/dD = 0$ reduces to:

$$S_0 K_s = (\mu_{max} - D)^2 / D \cdot \text{constant term}$$

The well-known closed-form result is:

$$\boxed{D_{opt} = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{K_s + S_0}}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical calculation:**

Given $\mu_{max} = 0.4$ h⁻¹, $K_s = 0.1$ g/L, $S_0 = 25$ g/L:

$$\sqrt{\frac{K_s}{K_s + S_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{0.1 + 25}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{25.1}} = \sqrt{0.003984} = 0.06312$$

$$D_{opt} = 0.4 \times (1 - 0.06312) = 0.4 \times 0.93688 = 0.3748 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{D_{opt} \approx 0.375 \text{ h}^{-1}}$$

> (Examiner's note: 2 marks for productivity definition, 3 marks for the derivation steps (or for correctly stating and applying the standard result), 2 marks for the numerical calculation with correct units.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $D < D_c$:** In Part (b) numerical questions, students compute $S_{ss}$ and $X_{ss}$ without checking whether their $D$ is below the critical value. If $D \geq D_c$, the entire calculation is invalid and the student loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sign errors in $r_s$:** The substrate consumption rate is *negative* (substrate disappears). Students often drop the minus sign, leading to a $S_{ss}$ that is greater than $S_0$ — an obvious red flag for the examiner.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** $\mu_{max}$ and $D$ must both be in h⁻¹ (or both in day⁻¹). Mixing units is the #1 mark-loser.
> 4. **Conflating $D_c$ and $D_{max}$:** $D_c$ depends on $S_0$ and $K_s$; $D_{max} = \mu_{max}$ is its asymptotic value. Writing $D_{max} = \mu_{max} S_0 / (K_s + S_0)$ is a half-mark deduction.
> 5. **Missing the productivity maximization step:** Several questions in the 2022–2024 papers ask for the *optimal* $D$, not just steady-state values. If you are only given $S_0$, $\mu_{max}$, $K_s$, $Y_{X/S}$, expect to find $D_{opt}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this aloud 3 times before the exam.**

- **CSTB = Chemostat** when sterile feed, perfect mixing, single limiting substrate.
- **Operating variable:** dilution rate $D = F/V$ (h⁻¹).
- **Key identity:** $\mu_{ss} = D$ (sterile feed + steady state).
- **Substrate balance:** $S_{ss} = K_s D / (\mu_{max} - D)$.
- **Biomass balance:** $X_{ss} = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S_{ss})$.
- **Critical dilution rate:** $D_c = \mu_{max} S_0 / (K_s + S_0)$.
- **Washout condition:** $D \geq D_c$ ⇒ $X = 0$, $S = S_0$.
- **Maximum $X$** at $D \to 0$ → $X_{max} = Y_{X/S}(S_0 + K_s - \sqrt{K_s^2 + K_s S_0})$.
- **Maximum productivity** at $D_{opt} = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \sqrt{K_s/(K_s + S_0)}\right)$.
- **Mass balance template (substrate):** $V dS/dt = F S_0 - F S - V \mu X / Y_{X/S}$.
- **Mass balance template (biomass):** $V dX/dt = -F X + V \mu X$ (sterile feed).
- **Assumptions checklist:** perfect mixing, sterile feed, single limiting substrate, steady state, constant volume, constant $T$, constant pH, constant $Y_{X/S}$.
- **For non-growth-associated products:** add Luedeking–Piret term $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$.
- **Industrial examples:** activated sludge, ethanol fermentation, SCP (Pruteen), amino acids, monoclonal antibodies (perfusion mode).
- **Numerical tip:** always carry **4 significant figures** and check the magnitude of the answer (e.g., $X_{ss}$ should be between $0$ and $Y_{X/S} S_0$).
- **Examiner mantra:** *"Show the mass balance, apply steady state, use $\mu = D$."* This sequence alone secures 60–70% of the marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bubble Column Bioreactor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bubble Column Bioreactor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 : BUBBLE COLUMN BIOREACTOR

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bubble Column Bioreactor (BCB)** is a cylindrical, mechanically agitated-free (or pneumatically agitated) pressure vessel in which a gas phase (typically air or oxygen) is sparged uniformly through a distributor (sparger) located at the bottom of a continuous liquid phase containing suspended microorganisms, cells, or catalysts. The rising swarm of gas bubbles provides the necessary oxygen transfer, liquid-phase mixing, and heat removal required to sustain aerobic biochemical reactions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the bubble column belongs to the family of **Pneumatically Agitated Bioreactors (PABR)** and is formally classified as a **Tower Bioreactor** with the gas phase as the discontinuous (dispersed) phase and the liquid (or liquid–solid suspension) as the continuous phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT521 – Module 4):** The bubble column is studied under "Non-Mechanically Agitated Bioreactors" with focus on constructional features, design equations (gas hold-up, kLa, power input), and scale-up criteria. It is a 14-mark and 3-mark favourite topic in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Key Constructional Components

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylindrical Shell** | Houses the reaction broth | SS-304 / SS-316 |
| **Gas Sparger** | Disperses inlet gas into fine bubbles | Perforated plate / Ring / Sintered metal |
| **Baffle Plates (optional)** | Prevents liquid swirl & large bubble coalescence | SS plates |
| **Heat Exchanger Coil** | Removes metabolic heat | SS-316 helical coil |
| **Gas Inlet & Outlet** | Sterile air entry & vent | Filtered lines |
| **Drain & Sampling Ports** | Harvesting & process monitoring | Aseptic valves |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Effervescent Fish Tank"

Imagine a deep cylindrical fish tank where an air-stone at the bottom pumps a continuous stream of tiny bubbles. The bubbles rise, dragging water upwards in the centre and pulling fresh water down along the walls — this natural, gentle, *circular motion* of water is exactly how a bubble column works. The bubbles act like thousands of tiny "elevators" carrying dissolved oxygen to microorganisms while simultaneously mixing the broth — **all without any mechanical impeller**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no impeller?** Because the gas itself does the work of mixing (pneumatic agitation). This eliminates shaft seals, reduces contamination risk, and lowers capital cost — a major advantage for sterile fermentations.

### 1.4 Types of Bubble Column Bioreactors

1. **Simple (Plain) Bubble Column** – straight cylindrical shell, internal sparger only.
2. **Internal-Loop Airlift Bioreactor (IL-ALR)** – draft tube inserted centrally to direct circulation.
3. **External-Loop Airlift Bioreactor (EL-ALR)** – riser and downcomer connected by external pipes.
4. **Multi-Stage Bubble Column** – perforated trays split the column into compartments for plug-flow behaviour.

### 1.5 Operating Ranges (Industrial Standards)

- **Height-to-Diameter Ratio (H/D):** 3 : 1 to 10 : 1 (typical)
- **Superficial Gas Velocity (Ug):** **0.01 – 0.30 m/s**
- **Working Volume:** 0.05 m³ (lab) → 500 m³ (industrial)
- **Operating Pressure:** 1 – 5 bar (gauge)
- **Temperature:** 25 °C – 45 °C (mesophilic range)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The working of a bubble column is governed by **three coupled transport phenomena** that occur simultaneously:

1. **Interphase Mass Transfer (Gas → Liquid)** — Oxygen dissolves into the broth across the bubble interface.
2. **Momentum Transfer (Bubble → Liquid)** — Rising bubbles drag liquid, generating axial liquid velocity and circulation.
3. **Heat Transfer (Liquid → Wall/Coil)** — Metabolic heat is removed to maintain isothermal conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** Whenever you see a bubble column question, immediately think of three variables: **εg (gas hold-up), kLa (mass transfer), Pg (power input)**. These three govern 90 % of the design.

### 2.2 Hydrodynamic Flow Regimes

The flow regime dictates the design equation to be used:

| Regime | Bubble Behaviour | Ug Range | Visual Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Homogeneous (Bubbly)** | Uniform small bubbles, uniform dispersion | **< 0.05 m/s** | "Champagne flow" |
| **Heterogeneous (Churn-turbulent)** | Coalescence + large bubbles, churns | **0.05 – 0.30 m/s** | "Bubbling cauldron" |
| **Slug Flow** | Taylor bubbles fill cross-section | Ug high + small D | "Pistons rising" |

> [!NOTE]
> Most industrial aerobic fermentations operate in the **Heterogeneous (Churn-turbulent)** regime, where the Akita–Yoshida and Hikita correlations for kLa are valid.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Superficial Gas Velocity | $U_g = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_c}$ | m/s | All |
| 2 | Superficial Liquid Velocity | $U_l = \dfrac{Q_l}{A_c}$ | m/s | All |
| 3 | Drift Flux | $J_{gl} = U_g \,\bigl(1 - \varepsilon_g\bigr) + U_l\,\varepsilon_g$ | m/s | All |
| 4 | Gas Hold-up (Hughmark) | $\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{U_g}{0.30 + U_g}$ | — | Heterogeneous |
| 5 | Gas Hold-up (Akita–Yoshida) | $\dfrac{\varepsilon_g}{\bigl(1-\varepsilon_g\bigr)^4} = C \cdot \dfrac{g\,D^{2}\,\rho_l}{\sigma_L} \cdot \bigl(\dfrac{g\,D^{3}\,\rho_l^{2}}{\mu_l^{2}}\bigr)^{-0.1} \cdot \bigl(\dfrac{U_g^{2}}{g\,D}\bigr)^{0.2}$ | — | Heterogeneous |
| 6 | Expanded Bed Height | $H = \dfrac{H_0}{1 - \varepsilon_g}$ | m | All |
| 7 | Sauter Mean Bubble Diameter | $d_{32} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^{3}}{\sum n_i d_i^{2}}$ | m | All |
| 8 | kLa – Hikita Correlation | $\dfrac{k_L a\,D^{2}}{D_L} = 14.9 \bigl(\dfrac{g D^{2} \rho_l}{\sigma_L}\bigr)^{0.5} \bigl(\dfrac{g D^{3} \rho_l^{2}}{\mu_l^{2}}\bigr)^{0.32} \bigl(\dfrac{\mu_l}{\rho_l D_L}\bigr)^{0.39} U_g^{0.51}$ | s⁻¹ | Heterogeneous |
| 9 | kLa – Akita–Yoshida | $\dfrac{k_L a\,D^{2}}{D_L} = 0.6 \bigl(\dfrac{\mu_l}{\rho_l D_L}\bigr)^{0.5} \bigl(\dfrac{g D^{2} \rho_l}{\sigma_L}\bigr)^{0.62} \bigl(\dfrac{g D^{3} \rho_l^{2}}{\mu_l^{2}}\bigr)^{0.31} \varepsilon_g^{1.1}$ | s⁻¹ | Heterogeneous |
| 10 | Isothermal Power Input | $P_g = Q_g \,\rho_g \,g\,H$ | W | All |
| 11 | Adiabatic (Compressed) Power | $P_g = Q_g \,R T \ln\bigl(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\bigr)$ | W | Pressurized BC |
| 12 | Liquid Circulation Velocity (IL-ALR) | $U_{lc} \approx 1.2 \bigl(g\,U_g\,D\bigr)^{1/3}$ | m/s | Internal loop |
| 13 | Mixing Time | $\theta_m = 11 \bigl(\dfrac{H}{D}\bigr)^{2} \bigl(\dfrac{g\,D}{U_g^{2}}\bigr)^{0.33}$ | s | Heterogeneous |
| 14 | Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) | $OTR = k_L a \,\bigl(C^{*}_O - C_L\bigr) \,V_L$ | kg O₂/h | All |
| 15 | Volumetric Oxygen Demand | $Q_{O_2} = \mu \,X \dfrac{1}{Y_{X/O}} \,V_L$ | kg O₂/h | Aerobic design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marker Check:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, examiners expect students to clearly state the **assumptions** (isothermal, ideal gas, no reaction in gas phase) before substituting values into Pg. Skipping assumptions costs 1–2 marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bubble columns are the **workhorse of the bio-industry** for processes demanding high gas–liquid mass transfer at low shear. Notable industrial applications include:

- **Single-Cell Protein (SCP)** production from methanol / n-alkanes (e.g., *Methylococcus capsulatus* – Pruteen, UniBio).
- **Beer Fermentation** in tower fermenters (e.g., APV/Metal Box design).
- **Bakers' Yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae)** propagation in airlift towers.
- **Wastewater Treatment** in deep-shaft bioreactors (ICI Deep Shaft).
- **Bioremediation** of phenolic and hydrocarbon-contaminated water.
- **Animal Cell Culture** where low-shear pneumatic mixing protects shear-sensitive mammalian cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Decision Tree:** Choose a bubble column when (a) the reaction is gas–liquid or gas–liquid–solid, (b) shear sensitivity is high, (c) broth is non-Newtonian, and (d) long residence times are acceptable. For highly viscous broths (Péclet > 1), prefer an airlift or stirred tank.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Gas Hold-up from Drift-Flux Balance

### 3.1.1 Concept

Gas hold-up $\varepsilon_g$ is the fraction of the total dispersion volume occupied by gas. It is the **master hydrodynamic parameter** because it controls interfacial area, kLa, and power input.

### 3.1.2 Assumptions

1. Steady-state, isothermal operation.
2. Bubbles rise at their terminal velocity (relative to liquid).
3. Liquid flow is one-dimensional (plug-flow in vertical direction).
4. Bubbles do not significantly accelerate.

### 3.1.3 Derivation

The superficial gas velocity equals the volumetric gas flux through a cross-section:

$$
U_g = \varepsilon_g \, u_b
$$

where $u_b$ is the absolute bubble rise velocity (m/s).

The relative (slip) velocity between bubble and liquid is:

$$
u_s = u_b - u_l
$$

A widely used closure (Zuber–Hench–Richardson correlation) for churn-turbulent flow is:

$$
u_s = u_\infty \,\bigl(1 - \varepsilon_g\bigr)^{n-1}
$$

For air–water churn-turbulent flow, $n \approx 2$ and the Richardson–Zuber correlation simplifies to:

$$
u_s = u_\infty \bigl(1 - \varepsilon_g\bigr)
$$

Substituting $u_b = u_s + u_l$:

$$
U_g = \varepsilon_g \bigl[ u_s + u_l \bigr] = \varepsilon_g \bigl[ u_\infty (1 - \varepsilon_g) + u_l \bigr]
$$

Expanding:

$$
U_g = \varepsilon_g \, u_\infty (1 - \varepsilon_g) + \varepsilon_g \, u_l
$$

Rearranging to express $u_l$ in terms of $U_g$ and $u_l$ (the true liquid velocity $\neq$ superficial):

$$
\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{U_g}{u_\infty (1 - \varepsilon_g) + u_l}
$$

For a **batch liquid** ($Q_l = 0$, so $U_l = 0$):

$$
U_g = u_\infty \,\varepsilon_g (1 - \varepsilon_g)
$$

Solving the quadratic for $\varepsilon_g$ yields the **Hughmark correlation** (a common KTU result):

$$
\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{-u_\infty + \sqrt{u_\infty^{2} + 4\,u_\infty\,U_g}}{2\,u_\infty}
$$

For large $u_\infty$ and small $U_g$, this reduces to the practical form:

$$
\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{U_g}{u_\infty}
$$

Empirically, for water-like broths, $u_\infty \approx 0.30$ m/s, giving:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{U_g}{0.30 + U_g}\;}
$$

This is the **Hughmark** shortcut, valid for $U_g < 0.15$ m/s.

### 3.1.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A bubble column of diameter $D = 1.0$ m is sparged with air at $Q_g = 0.05$ m³/s. The broth is water-like with $u_\infty = 0.30$ m/s. Calculate the gas hold-up using the Hughmark equation.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Calculate cross-sectional area:

$$
A_c = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.0^{2}}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Step 2 — Calculate superficial gas velocity:

$$
U_g = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_c} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.7854} = 0.0637 \text{ m/s}
$$

Step 3 — Apply Hughmark correlation:

$$
\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{0.0637}{0.30 + 0.0637} = \dfrac{0.0637}{0.3637} = 0.1752
$$

Step 4 — Percentage gas hold-up:

$$
\varepsilon_g \,\bigl(\%\bigr) = 0.1752 \times 100 = 17.52\,\%
$$

Step 5 — Expanded bed height (if $H_0 = 5$ m ungassed):

$$
H = \dfrac{H_0}{1 - \varepsilon_g} = \dfrac{5.0}{1 - 0.1752} = 6.06 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\varepsilon_g \approx 0.175$ and the broth expands from 5 m to 6.06 m — a 21 % increase. Always report both ungassed and gassed heights in KTU answers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Isothermal Gas Expansion Power Input

### 3.2.1 Concept

A gas entering at the bottom of a column expands isothermally as it rises against the hydrostatic pressure of the liquid. The work done by this expansion manifests as **pneumatic power input** into the broth, which is the energy source for mixing.

### 3.2.2 Assumptions

1. Isothermal expansion of an ideal gas.
2. Gas density at sparger $\rho_{g1}$, at top $\rho_{g2}$.
3. No work of bubble formation included (separate term).

### 3.2.3 Derivation

The isothermal work done by gas expanding from pressure $P_1$ to $P_2$ per unit mass:

$$
w = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{\rho_g}
$$

For ideal gas: $\rho_g = \dfrac{P\,M}{R\,T}$ and $dP/\rho_g = RT\,dP/(MP)$.

$$
w = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{R\,T}{M} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{R\,T}{M}\ln\Bigl(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\Bigr)
$$

The mass flow rate of gas is $\dot{m} = Q_g \rho_g$. The total pneumatic power:

$$
P_g = Q_g \rho_g \dfrac{R\,T}{M}\ln\Bigl(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\Bigr)
$$

Using $\dfrac{\rho_g R T}{M} = P$, and $P_1 \approx P_{top} + \rho_l g H \approx \rho_l g H$ for tall columns (since $P_{top}$ is small):

$$
P_g = Q_g \,\rho_l\,g\,H
$$

> This gives the **alternative simplified form** $P_g = Q_g \rho_l g H$, where $\rho_l$ replaces $\rho_g$ because the work is ultimately transferred to the dense liquid.

$$
\boxed{\;P_g = Q_g \,\rho_l \,g\,H\;}
$$

### 3.2.4 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Air at $Q_g = 0.05$ m³/s is sparged into a 1.0 m diameter column with expanded height $H = 6.06$ m. Liquid density $\rho_l = 1000$ kg/m³. Calculate the isothermal power input.

**Solution:**

$$
P_g = 0.05 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 6.06 = 2972.4 \text{ W} \approx 2.97 \text{ kW}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: Linking kLa to OTR (Oxygen Transfer Rate)

### 3.3.1 Concept

The driving force for oxygen transfer is the concentration difference between the equilibrium saturation value (at the bubble–liquid interface) and the dissolved oxygen concentration in the bulk liquid.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

Mass balance for oxygen in the bulk liquid at steady state:

$$
OTR = k_L a \, \bigl(C^{*}_{O_2} - C_{L,O_2}\bigr) \, V_L
$$

Where:
- $OTR$ = Oxygen Transfer Rate (kg O₂/m³·h)
- $k_L$ = Liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $a$ = Specific interfacial area (m² gas / m³ dispersion)
- $C^{*}_{O_2}$ = Saturation dissolved O₂ (≈ 6–8 mg/L at 30 °C, 1 atm)
- $C_{L,O_2}$ = Actual dissolved O₂ in broth (typically 2–4 mg/L)
- $V_L$ = Liquid volume (m³)

Equating OTR to cellular oxygen demand (Pirt's law with maintenance):

$$
Q_{O_2} = \mu\, X \dfrac{1}{Y_{X/O}} V_L + m_O X V_L
$$

Therefore, the required $k_L a$ for a given biomass productivity is:

$$
k_L a = \dfrac{\mu X / Y_{X/O} + m_O X}{C^{*}_{O_2} - C_{L,O_2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;k_L a_{req} = \dfrac{X\bigl(\mu / Y_{X/O} + m_O\bigr)}{C^{*}_{O_2} - C_{L,O_2}}\;}
$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example

**Problem:** A baker's yeast culture requires $X = 5$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.30$ /h, $Y_{X/O} = 1.0$ kg/kg, $m_O = 0.05$ kg O₂/kg cell·h. $C^{*}_{O_2} = 7$ mg/L, $C_L = 2$ mg/L. Calculate $k_L a_{req}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Numerator:

$$
5 \times (0.30 / 1.0 + 0.05) = 5 \times 0.35 = 1.75 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{m}^{3}\cdot\text{h}
$$

Step 2 — Denominator:

$$
7 - 2 = 5 \text{ mg/L} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^{3}
$$

Step 3 — $k_L a_{req}$:

$$
k_L a_{req} = \dfrac{1.75}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 350 \text{ h}^{-1} = 0.0972 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** A required $k_L a$ of 350 h⁻¹ is typical for industrial yeast production. Check the Hikita correlation predicts a value ≥ 350 h⁻¹ at your chosen $U_g$. If not, **increase $U_g$** and re-iterate.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Hot-Spot)

| Feature | Simple Bubble Column | Internal-Loop Airlift | Stirred Tank Reactor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mixing device** | Gas sparging | Gas sparging + draft tube | Impeller |
| **Shear stress** | **Low** | Low | **High** |
| **kLa range (h⁻¹)** | 100 – 600 | 200 – 800 | 200 – 1500 |
| **Power per unit volume (kW/m³)** | 0.1 – 3 | 0.2 – 4 | 1 – 10 |
| **Capital cost** | **Low** | Medium | High |
| **Shear-sensitive cell use** | **Yes** | **Yes** | No |
| **Gas–liquid back-mixing** | High (CSTR-like) | Moderate | High |
| **Scale-up complexity** | Simple | Moderate | Complex |
| **Sterility risk** | **Low** (no shaft seal) | Low | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Logic:** A question that says "compare and justify" almost always awards marks for drawing a **3-column comparison table** with bolded keywords.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic Block Architecture of a Bubble Column Bioreactor System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

## 4.2 Internal-Loop Airlift (Draft-Tube) Variant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ILALR["INTERNAL LOOP AIRLIFT BIOREACTOR"]
        direction TB
        SPARG2[Sparger at Annulus Base]
        ANNU2[Downcomer Annulus]
        DRAFT2[Central Draft Tube Riser]
        TOP2[Top Disengagement Zone]
        BOT2[Bottom Recirculation Zone]
    end

    GAS2[Sparged Air] --> SPARG2
    SPARG2 --> ANNU2
    SPARG2 --> DRAFT2
    ANNU2 --> BOT2
    BOT2 --> DRAFT2
    DRAFT2 --> TOP2
    TOP2 --> ANNU2

    ANNU2 --> PROD2[Cell Suspension Outlet]

    style DRAFT2 fill:#FFE08A
    style ANNU2 fill:#B9E0FF
    style TOP2 fill:#C9F5C0
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** In the riser (central draft tube), bubbles rise rapidly with the liquid, creating a low-density zone. In the downcomer (annular region), bubble-free or bubble-depleted liquid falls, creating a **density-driven circulation loop**. This is the hallmark of all airlift devices.

