# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q:** Define gas hold-up. State the factors affecting gas hold-up in a bubble column bioreactor.

**Model Answer:**

**Gas Hold-up (εg):** It is defined as the volume of gas dispersed in the liquid, expressed as a fraction of the total gas–liquid dispersion volume. Mathematically:

$$
\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{V_g}{V_g + V_l} = \dfrac{\text{Volume of gas phase}}{\text{Total dispersion volume}}
$$

Typical values range from **0.10 to 0.40** (i.e., 10 – 40 %) in industrial bubble columns.

**Factors affecting εg:**

1. **Superficial gas velocity (Ug)** – εg increases with Ug.
2. **Liquid properties** – viscosity (↑ viscosity → ↓ εg); surface tension (↑ σL → smaller bubbles → ↑ εg).
3. **Sparger design** – pore size and number of holes (smaller pores → finer bubbles → ↑ εg).
4. **Column diameter (D)** – εg slightly decreases with increasing D (wall effect diminishes).
5. **Operating pressure** – ↑ pressure → ↓ bubble size → ↑ εg.
6. **Presence of solids / antifoam** – coalescence-promoting agents ↓ εg.
7. **Temperature** – ↑ T → ↓ ρL and ↓ σL → slight ↑ εg.

**(3 marks)** ✓

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q:** Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous flow regimes in a bubble column.

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Homogeneous (Bubbly) | Heterogeneous (Churn-Turbulent) |
|---|---|---|
| **Bubble size** | Uniform, small | Polydisperse, with large bubbles |
| **Ug range** | **< 0.05 m/s** | **0.05 – 0.30 m/s** |
| **Flow pattern** | Uniform dispersion | Churning, coalescence visible |
| **Mixing** | Mild, plug-like | Vigorous, well-mixed |
| **kLa** | Lower | Higher |
| **εg correlation** | Linear with Ug | Non-linear (Hughmark) |
| **Industrial use** | Lab scale, slow reactions | Production fermenters |

**(3 marks)** ✓

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** A pilot-scale bubble column bioreactor is to be designed for the production of baker's yeast.

- Column diameter, $D = 0.6$ m
- Sparged air flow rate, $Q_g = 0.02$ m³/s
- Clear (ungassed) liquid height, $H_0 = 3.0$ m
- Broth: water-like, $u_\infty = 0.30$ m/s, $\rho_l = 1000$ kg/m³
- Operating pressure: 1.2 atm
- Saturation dissolved O₂, $C^{*}_{O_2} = 7$ mg/L

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the gas hold-up, expanded bed height, and the isothermal power input.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** If the Hikita correlation gives $k_L a = 0.12$ s⁻¹, determine the maximum biomass concentration that can be supported (assuming $C_L = 2$ mg/L, $Y_{X/O} = 1.0$, $m_O = 0.05$ kg O₂/kg·h, $\mu = 0.30$ /h).

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_c = \dfrac{\pi (0.6)^{2}}{4} = 0.2827 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Superficial gas velocity:**

$$
U_g = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_c} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.2827} = 0.0707 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Gas hold-up (Hughmark):**

$$
\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{0.0707}{0.30 + 0.0707} = 0.1908 \approx 0.191
$$

**Step 4 — Expanded bed height:**

$$
H = \dfrac{H_0}{1 - \varepsilon_g} = \dfrac{3.0}{1 - 0.191} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.809} = 3.708 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Isothermal power input:**

$$
P_g = Q_g \,\rho_l\, g\, H = 0.02 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.708 = 727.6 \text{ W} \approx 0.728 \text{ kW}
$$

> ✓ **(7 marks)** — Awarded for: 5 clear steps (1.4 each) with units.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Driving force for O₂ transfer:**

$$
\Delta C = C^{*}_{O_2} - C_L = 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ mg/L} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen Transfer Rate per unit volume:**

$$
OTR/V = k_L a \,\Delta C = 0.12 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg O}_2/\text{m}^{3}\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to per hour:**

$$
OTR/V = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 2.16 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{m}^{3}\cdot\text{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Pirt's law to equate to cellular demand:**

$$
\dfrac{OTR}{V} = X \Bigl( \dfrac{\mu}{Y_{X/O}} + m_O \Bigr)
$$

$$
2.16 = X \Bigl( \dfrac{0.30}{1.0} + 0.05 \Bigr) = X \times 0.35
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for X:**

$$
X_{max} = \dfrac{2.16}{0.35} = 6.17 \text{ kg/m}^{3}
$$

> ✓ **(7 marks)** — Awarded for: OTR setup, unit conversions (mg/L → kg/m³, s → h), Pirt's equation application.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2022]

**Q:** Write detailed notes on the constructional features and design equations of a bubble column bioreactor. Include in your answer a derivation of the isothermal gas expansion power and a discussion of the role of the sparger.

#### Model Answer Outline

**1. Constructional Features [4 marks]:** Cylindrical shell, sparger, baffles, heat exchanger, instrumentation ports, vent system. (Refer to Section 1.2 of these notes.)

**2. Isothermal Power Derivation [5 marks]:** (As in Section 3.2 of these notes.)

$$
P_g = Q_g \,\rho_l\, g\, H
$$

with all assumption steps explicitly stated.

**3. Role of the Sparger [5 marks]:**

- **Sparger types:** perforated plate, ring, sintered disc, jet nozzle.
- **Functions:** uniform bubble generation, distribution of gas across full cross-section, prevention of liquid backflow into gas line.
- **Bubble size influence:** $d_b \propto \bigl( \sigma / (\rho_l g) \bigr)^{0.5}$ — smaller pores → smaller bubbles → higher interfacial area → higher kLa.
- **Pressure drop:** $\Delta P_{sparger} = \dfrac{2 \sigma}{r_p} + \rho_l g H$.
- **Material of construction:** SS-316 sintered bronze for sterile duty.

> ✓ **(14 marks)** — Awarded for: 4 + 5 + 5 distribution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** before applying Hughmark / Hikita (loss: 1 mark).
> 2. **Mixing up units** — Ug in m/s vs cm/s; kLa in s⁻¹ vs h⁻¹ (loss: 1–2 marks).
> 3. **Reporting gas hold-up as a percentage** without also giving the decimal (loss: 0.5 mark).
> 4. **Ignoring bed expansion** — writing $H_0$ in Pg equation instead of $H$ (loss: 1 mark).
> 5. **Skipping the free-body / energy-balance logic** in derivation (loss: 2 marks).
> 6. **Writing "power = Qg·Pg"** by mistake — should be $P_g = Q_g \cdot P$ (pressure), not $P_g$ (power) [the same symbol is used — be careful!] (loss: 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Your Wall):**

1. **Definition** — Bubble column = gas–liquid contactor with **no mechanical impeller**; gas sparger is the only energy source.
2. **Three master parameters:** $\varepsilon_g$, $k_L a$, $P_g$ — design any column around these.
3. **Hughmark shortcut:** $\varepsilon_g = \dfrac{U_g}{0.30 + U_g}$ (valid $U_g < 0.15$ m/s).
4. **Bed expansion:** $H = \dfrac{H_0}{1 - \varepsilon_g}$ — always report both heights.
5. **Isothermal power:** $P_g = Q_g \,\rho_l\, g\, H$ (use expanded height!).
6. **Hikita kLa:** $\dfrac{k_L a\,D^{2}}{D_L} = 14.9 \bigl(\dfrac{g D^{2} \rho_l}{\sigma_L}\bigr)^{0.5} \bigl(\dfrac{g D^{3} \rho_l^{2}}{\mu_l^{2}}\bigr)^{0.32} \bigl(\dfrac{\mu_l}{\rho_l D_L}\bigr)^{0.39} U_g^{0.51}$
7. **OTR formula:** $OTR = k_L a \,(C^* - C_L)\,V_L$ — remember the **driving force** is the gradient.
8. **Pirt's law for demand:** $Q_{O_2} = X\bigl(\mu / Y_{X/O} + m_O\bigr)\,V_L$.
9. **Mixing time:** $\theta_m = 11 (H/D)^{2} (g D / U_g^{2})^{0.33}$.
10. **Scale-up strategies:** constant $U_g$ (preferred for kLa), constant $P_g/V$, constant $\theta_m$.
11. **Airlift advantage:** draft tube creates a **directed flow loop** → 20–40 % higher kLa than plain bubble column at same $U_g$.
12. **Industrial classics:** SCP (Pruteen), Tower beer fermenter, ICI Deep-Shaft wastewater plant.
13. **Common H/D ratio:** 3 to 10 — taller → longer gas residence → higher O₂ utilisation.
14. **Maximum kLa ceiling:** ~1000 h⁻¹ for plain bubble columns; >1500 h⁻¹ requires external loop or stirred tank.
15. **KTU favourite numerical:** always involves one of {Ug, εg, kLa, Pg, OTR, Xmax} — practise all six families.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Airlift Bioreactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Airlift Bioreactors ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Airlift Bioreactor (ALR)** is a pneumatically agitated, cylindrical vessel in which fluid circulation is induced and maintained by the difference in bulk densities between two interconnected zones — a **gassed (riser)** section and an **ungassed (downcomer)** section. Unlike mechanically stirred reactors, the energy required for mixing, mass transfer, and heat removal is supplied exclusively by the **isothermal expansion of compressed gas** (typically sterile air or oxygen-enriched air) introduced through a sparger at the base of the riser. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECHT521, Module 4), airlift bioreactors are classified under **pneumatic bioreactors with defined flow patterns** and are contrasted against conventional stirred-tank and bubble-column reactors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** > Airlift bioreactors must be studied under the umbrella of *“design, construction, and operation of selected bioreactors”* with emphasis on **configuration, hydrodynamics, mass-transfer correlations, and scale-up methodology**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Kettle with a Chimney" Imagine a **deep, narrow boiling kettle** fitted with an internal vertical pipe (a "chimney" or **draft tube**) reaching close to the bottom. As you boil water: - Inside the chimney: water + steam bubbles rise → **riser** (low density, fast upward flow) - In the annular space outside the chimney: degassed water descends → **downcomer** (high density, slow downward flow) - The whole body of water gently circulates in one **closed loop**, without any mechanical stirrer. This is exactly how an airlift bioreactor works: **gas does the pumping**, not an impeller. ### 1.3 Classification of Airlift Bioreactors | Configuration | Internal Loop | External Loop | |---|---|---| | **Draft tube position** | Concentric (riser inside, downcomer outside) | Riser and downcomer are separate columns connected top & bottom | | **Flow path** | Inside vessel | Outside vessel | | **Scale-up ease** | Moderate | High (modular columns) | | **KTU exam relevance** | Common in 14-mark problems | Common in design/comparison questions | > [!NOTE] > **Terminology Snapshot (must memorize for KTU):** > - **Riser** = gassed section where bubbles ascend. > - **Downcomer** = degassed section where liquid descends. > - **Gas separator** (top section) = disengages excess gas before liquid re-enters downcomer. > - **Sparger** = porous or orifice plate at base of riser that disperses incoming air into fine bubbles. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Operating Metrics - **Operating superficial gas velocity (U_G):** typically **0.005 – 0.10 m/s** (industrial range). - **Liquid circulation velocity (U_L):** typically **0.2 – 0.6 m/s** in riser. - **Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient (k_La):** typically **100 – 1000 h⁻¹**. - **Gas holdup (ε_G):** typically **0.05 – 0.20 (5–20 %)**. - **Working pressure:** 1 – 3 bar (gauge). - **Aspect ratio (H/D):** 3 – 6 for internal-loop ALRs. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principles — The “Why” Behind the Loop The circulation in an airlift reactor is governed by a **driving force equal to the hydrostatic pressure difference** between riser and downcomer columns. This is *not* pumping by the gas itself — the gas only reduces the *effective density* of the liquid in the riser. **Step-by-step logic:** 1. Compressed sterile air enters through the **sparger** at the bottom of the riser. 2. Bubbles rise, expanding as pressure drops. The two-phase mixture in the riser has a **lower bulk density** than the bubble-free liquid in the downcomer. 3. The **density imbalance (Δρ)** creates a hydrostatic pressure difference at the base, forcing liquid up the riser and down the downcomer. 4. At the top, gas escapes through the **gas-separator** zone; the degassed liquid recirculates back into the downcomer. 5. The flow forms a **closed, well-defined toroidal loop** — without any moving mechanical parts. ### 2.2 Governing Hydrodynamic Equations The driving force for circulation can be expressed as: $$\Delta P_{\text{drive}} = g \, H \, (\rho_{Ld} - \rho_{Lr})$$ where $H$ is the dispersion height, $\rho_{Ld}$ is downcomer liquid density, and $\rho_{Lr}$ is riser (two-phase) density. This is balanced by the **frictional pressure losses** in the loop: $$\Delta P_{\text{drive}} = \Delta P_{F,r} + \Delta P_{F,d} + \Delta P_{F,b}$$ where subscripts $r$, $d$, and $b$ denote riser, downcomer, and base (bottom). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Superficial gas velocity | $U_G = \dfrac{Q_G}{A_r}$ | m/s | $Q_G$ = gas volumetric flowrate, $A_r$ = riser cross-section | | 2 | Superficial liquid velocity in riser | $U_{Lr} = \dfrac{Q_L}{A_r}$ | m/s | — | | 3 | Drift-flux gas holdup (Zuber–Findlay) | $\dfrac{U_G}{\varepsilon_G} = C \, U_{b\infty} + \dfrac{U_{Lr}}{\varepsilon_G}$ | m/s | $C$ = distribution parameter, $U_{b\infty}$ = single bubble rise velocity | | 4 | Mean gas holdup (loop-average) | $\varepsilon_G = \dfrac{A_r \, \varepsilon_{Gr} + A_d \, \varepsilon_{Gd}}{A_r + A_d}$ | dimensionless | $\varepsilon_{Gd} \approx 0$ (downcomer essentially bubble-free) | | 5 | Liquid circulation velocity (Chisti) | $U_{Lr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g \, H \, (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd})}{K_B \, \left(\dfrac{A_r}{A_d}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}} + \dfrac{K_T}{\varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}}}}$ | m/s | $K_B$ = bottom friction-loss coefficient, $K_T$ = top friction-loss coefficient | | 6 | Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient (Kawase–Hashimoto) | $k_La = 0.0216 \, U_G^{0.4} \, \mu_{L}^{-0.6} \, \sigma^{-0.6} \, D^{0.4} \, \rho_{L}^{0.2} \, g^{0.8}$ | s⁻¹ | Empirical, Newtonian fluids | | 7 | Oxygen transfer rate | $OTR = k_La \, (C^{*} - C_L)$ | mol/(m³·s) | $C^{*}$ = saturation DO concentration | | 8 | Power input (isothermal expansion) | $P = Q_G \, \rho_L \, g \, H$ | W | **Crucial KTU result** — power is gas-flow-dependent, not impeller-dependent | | 9 | Mixing time (approximate) | $t_m \approx \dfrac{V}{Q_L} = \dfrac{V}{U_{Lr} \, A_r}$ | s | — | | 10 | Shear rate (approximate) | $\dot{\gamma} \approx \dfrac{U_{Lr}}{D_r}$ | s⁻¹ | Used for shear-sensitive cultures (animal cells) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The KTU board often asks students to derive the **Chisti circulation-velocity equation** or to compare **power input** between a stirred-tank and an airlift reactor. Memorize equation (5) and (8) — they are the highest-weightage. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Single-cell protein (SCP)** production (e.g., *Methylophilus methylotrophus* using ICI’s “Pruteen” process, 1500 m³ external-loop ALR — the world’s largest airlift unit). - **Aerobic wastewater treatment** using external-loop ALRs (deep-shaft reactors). - **Plant-cell culture** (shear-sensitive). - **Monoclonal antibody production** in **mammalian-cell airlift bioreactors** with low-shear flow. - **Beer maturation / fermentation** in brewing industry. ### 2.5 Why Airlift > Stirred-Tank for Many Applications | Property | Airlift | Stirred-Tank | |---|---|---| | Mechanical parts in contact with broth | **None** | Impeller, shaft, bearings | | Sterility risk | Low | High (shaft seal leakage) | | Capital cost | Lower | Higher | | Energy efficiency (kLa per kW) | **High** | Lower | | Shear stress | Low & uniform | High, locally extreme | | Scale-up predictability | **Good** (U_G is invariant) | Poor | | Best for | Shear-sensitive, long-duration, high-volume cultures | Fast-growing bacteria, batch mode | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Chisti Liquid Circulation Velocity This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 4. We start from a mechanical-energy balance on the closed loop. **Step 1 — Define the driving force.** The riser contains a two-phase mixture of average density: $$\rho_{mr} = \rho_L (1 - \varepsilon_{Gr}) + \rho_G \varepsilon_{Gr}$$ Because $\rho_G \ll \rho_L$ at operating pressures (≲ 3 bar), we can drop the second term: $$\rho_{mr} \approx \rho_L (1 - \varepsilon_{Gr})$$ Likewise, the downcomer density is: $$\rho_{md} \approx \rho_L (1 - \varepsilon_{Gd})$$ **Step 2 — Write the hydrostatic pressure difference at the base of the loop.** The liquid column in the downcomer pushes *down*, while the riser column pushes *up*. The net driving pressure is: $$\Delta P_{\text{drive}} = g H (\rho_{md} - \rho_{mr})$$ Substituting the simplified expressions: $$\Delta P_{\text{drive}} = g H \rho_L \left[ (1 - \varepsilon_{Gd}) - (1 - \varepsilon_{Gr}) \right] = g H \rho_L (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd})$$ Since the downcomer is usually almost bubble-free ($\varepsilon_{Gd} \approx 0$ for most designs): $$\Delta P_{\text{drive}} \approx g H \rho_L \, \varepsilon_{Gr}$$ **Step 3 — Write the frictional pressure losses around the loop.** For a steady circulating flow, the energy supplied equals the energy dissipated: $$\Delta P_{\text{drive}} = \Delta P_{F,b} + \Delta P_{F,r} + \Delta P_{F,t}$$ Friction loss in any section is proportional to the square of the superficial liquid velocity and inversely proportional to the cube of the local gas holdup (because slip between phases reduces the effective cross-section): $$\Delta P_{F,b} = K_B \cdot \dfrac{\rho_L \, U_{Lr}^{2}}{2 \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}}$$ $$\Delta P_{F,r} = K_{T,r} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_L \, U_{Lr}^{2}}{2 \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}}$$ $$\Delta P_{F,t} = K_T \cdot \dfrac{\rho_L \, U_{Lr}^{2}}{2 \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}}$$ > **Why is the downcomer loss divided by $\varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}$?** > Because in the downcomer, the *only* liquid flowing carries the drag, and as $\varepsilon_{Gd} \to 0$, the local cross-section for liquid shrinks and the effective velocity (interstitial) becomes very high — producing large losses. **Step 4 — Equate driving force and total losses.** $$g H \rho_L (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd}) = \dfrac{\rho_L \, U_{Lr}^{2}}{2} \left[ \dfrac{K_B + K_{T,r}}{\varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}} + \dfrac{K_T}{\varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}} \right]$$ **Step 5 — Combine the riser friction coefficients into a single effective value.** Defining $K_R = K_B + K_{T,r}$ (combined riser friction constant) and cancelling $\rho_L$: $$g H (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd}) = \dfrac{U_{Lr}^{2}}{2} \left[ \dfrac{K_R}{\varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}} + \dfrac{K_T}{\varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}} \right]$$ **Step 6 — Solve algebraically for $U_{Lr}$.** Rearranging: $$U_{Lr}^{2} = \dfrac{2 g H (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd})}{\dfrac{K_R}{\varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}} + \dfrac{K_T}{\varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}}}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\varepsilon_{Gr}^{2} \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}$: $$U_{Lr}^{2} = \dfrac{2 g H (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd}) \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2} \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}}{K_R \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2} + K_T \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}}$$ Taking the positive square root (physical solution): $$U_{Lr} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 g H (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd}) \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2} \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}}{K_R \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2} + K_T \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}} }$$ This is the **Chisti circulation-velocity equation** used industry-wide. ### 3.2 Derivation of Power Input from Isothermal Gas Expansion The gas does work on the liquid as it expands isothermally from the sparger pressure to the top of the column. **Step 1 — Pressure at sparger (bottom of riser):** $$P_1 = P_{\text{top}} + \rho_{mr} \, g \, H$$ **Step 2 — Pressure at top of riser (just below gas separator):** $$P_2 = P_{\text{top}}$$ **Step 3 — Isothermal work per unit mass of gas:** $$w = \int_{P_2}^{P_1} \dfrac{dP}{\rho_G} = R T \ln \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = R T \ln \dfrac{P_{\text{top}} + \rho_{mr} g H}{P_{\text{top}}}$$ **Step 4 — Mass flow rate of gas:** $$\dot{m}_G = \rho_{G, \text{top}} \, Q_{G, \text{top}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{top}} \, M_G}{R T} \, Q_G$$ **Step 5 — Total power dissipated into the liquid:** $$\boxed{P = Q_G \, \rho_L \, g \, H}$$ This simplified form (from Chisti & Moo-Young) is valid when $P_{\text{top}} \ll \rho_L g H$, i.e., at low overhead pressure — the common industrial case. KTU examiners accept this form as the **canonical airlift power-input equation**. ### 3.3 Numerical Example — Worked-Out KTU-Style Problem **Problem (typical 14-mark KTU question):** > An external-loop airlift bioreactor has a riser height of **8 m**, riser cross-sectional area **0.20 m²**, downcomer cross-sectional area **0.10 m²**, gas holdup in the riser **0.12**, gas holdup in the downcomer **0.01**, friction coefficient in the riser $K_R = 8.0$, friction coefficient in the top section $K_T = 5.0$, and superficial gas velocity $U_G = 0.04$ m/s. > Calculate: (a) the liquid circulation velocity, (b) the power input. **Solution — Part (a):** Using the Chisti equation derived above: $$U_{Lr} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 g H (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd}) \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2} \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}}{K_R \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2} + K_T \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}} }$$ Substituting the numerical values: $$U_{Lr} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times (0.12 - 0.01) \times (0.12)^{2} \times (0.01)^{2}}{8.0 \times (0.01)^{2} + 5.0 \times (0.12)^{2}} }$$ Compute the numerator: $$2 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 0.11 \times 0.0144 \times 0.0001$$ $$= 2 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 0.11 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 2 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 1.584 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$= 2.486 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{(numerator)}$$ Compute the denominator: $$8.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 0.0144 = 0.0008 + 0.0720 = 0.0728$$ Therefore: $$U_{Lr}^{2} = \dfrac{2.486 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0728} = 3.415 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{U_{Lr} = 0.0185 \; \text{m/s}}$$ **Solution — Part (b):** First, find the gas volumetric flow rate: $$Q_G = U_G \times A_r = 0.04 \times 0.20 = 0.008 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$ Then, power input: $$P = Q_G \, \rho_L \, g \, H = 0.008 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 8$$ $$\boxed{P = 627.8 \; \text{W} \approx 0.628 \; \text{kW}}$$ This illustrates the hallmark of airlift reactors: **low power, high efficiency**. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Airlift Bioreactor Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["STERILE AIR INLET
FROM COMPRESSOR + FILTER"] --> node2["SPARGER
RING OR POROUS DISC
AT BASE OF RISER"] node2 --> node3["RISER SECTION
GASSED ZONE
LOW BULK DENSITY
UPRWARD FLOW"] node3 --> node4["GAS SEPARATOR
TOP ZONE
BUBBLE DISENGAGEMENT"] node4 --> node5["DOWNCOMER SECTION
DEGASSED ZONE
HIGH BULK DENSITY
DOWNWARD FLOW"] node5 --> node6["BASE REGION
FLOW REVERSAL
ENTRY TO RISER"] node6 --> node3 node3 --> node7["MASS TRANSFER
O2 FROM GAS TO LIQUID
kLa x C STAR MINUS C"] node3 --> node8["MIXING
DEFINED TOROIDAL LOOP
NO MOVING PARTS"] node3 --> node9["HEAT REMOVAL
INTERNAL COILS OR JACKET
CONTROLLED BY Q_G"] node4 --> node10["EXHAUST GAS
CO2 + O2 + WATER VAPOUR
CONDITIONED AND VENTED"] node1 --> node11["CONTROL SYSTEM
DO PROBE
PH PROBE
TEMP SENSOR"] node11 --> node3 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison of Bioreactor Types ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["BIOREACTOR
FAMILY"] --> nodeB["MECHANICAL
STIRRED TANK"] nodeA --> nodeC["PNEUMATIC
REACTORS"] nodeC --> nodeD["BUBBLE COLUMN
NO INTERNAL DRAFT"] nodeC --> nodeE["AIRLIFT REACTOR
WITH DRAFT TUBE"] nodeE --> nodeF["INTERNAL LOOP
CONCENTRIC DRAFT"] nodeE --> nodeG["EXTERNAL LOOP
SEPARATE COLUMNS"] nodeB -.->|"HIGHER SHEAR"| nodeH["KTU KEY METRIC
kLa PER kW"] nodeF -.->|"LOW SHEAR"| nodeH nodeG -.->|"LOW SHEAR"| nodeH ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Mass-Transfer Pathway in an Airlift ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["GAS PHASE
BUBBLE INTERIOR"] -->|"DIFFUSION
THROUGH FILM"| node2["GAS LIQUID
INTERFACE"] node2 -->|"DIFFUSION
THROUGH LIQUID FILM"| node3["BULK LIQUID
CIRCULATING LOOP"] node3 -->|"CONVECTIVE
TRANSPORT"| node4["CELL OR
ENZYME SITE"] node4 -->|"UPTAKE OR
REACTION"| node5["METABOLITE OR
PRODUCT"] node1 -->|"RISING
BUBBLE"| node6["EXHAUST
VENT"] node3 -->|"DOWN
DOWNCOMER"| node2 ``` ### 4.4 Functional Flow Map — Inputs, Transformations, Outputs ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["INPUTS
SUBSTRATE
STERILE AIR
INOCULUM"] --> node2["AIRLIFT
BIOREACTOR
CONTROLLED LOOP"] node2 --> node3["OUTPUTS
BIOMASS
PRODUCT
EXHAUST GAS
HEAT"] node2 --> node4["CONTROL
VARIABLES
Q_G, H, A_r, A_d"] node4 --> node2 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define an airlift bioreactor. With a neat sketch, differentiate between an **internal-loop** and an **external-loop** airlift reactor.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An airlift bioreactor is a **pneumatically agitated vessel** in which liquid circulation is induced by the **density difference** between a gassed riser and an ungassed downcomer, without any mechanical agitator. - **Internal-loop ALR:** Riser and downcomer are coaxial *inside the same vessel* — typically a concentric draft tube (riser) inside an outer annulus (downcomer). - **External-loop ALR:** Riser and downcomer are *separate columns* connected by horizontal pipes at the top (gas separator) and bottom (base). (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison with sketch.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** *State and explain the **Chisti equation** for liquid circulation velocity in an airlift reactor. Why is the downcomer gas holdup often approximated as zero?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$U_{Lr} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 g H (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd}) \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2} \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}}{K_R \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2} + K_T \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}} }$$ This equation arises from a **mechanical energy balance**: the hydrostatic driving force (due to gas holdup difference) equals the frictional losses in the riser, downcomer, and base. The downcomer gas holdup is approximated as zero because **bubbles entering the downcomer are quickly disengaged** in the top gas-separator zone; only a small fraction of bubbles recirculate, and for engineering accuracy this is neglected (1 mark each for statement, equation, and justification). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]* **(a)** Derive the **Chisti equation** for liquid circulation velocity in an external-loop airlift bioreactor, starting from a hydrostatic pressure balance. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An internal-loop airlift bioreactor of riser height **10 m**, riser cross-section **0.30 m²**, and downcomer cross-section **0.15 m²** is sparged at $U_G$ = **0.05 m/s**. The mean gas holdup in the riser is **0.15**. The riser friction constant $K_R$ = **10.0**, the top friction constant $K_T$ = **6.0**, and the downcomer gas holdup is **0.02**. Calculate: - (i) The **liquid circulation velocity** in the riser. **(4 marks)** - (ii) The **power input** from the gas. **(3 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation** — see **Section 3.1** above. The student must reproduce the five-step derivation of the Chisti equation, including: - Driving force $\Delta P_{\text{drive}} = g H \rho_L (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd})$ - Friction losses in riser, downcomer, and base - Final boxed equation Assumptions to state explicitly: - Steady, isothermal operation - Negligible gas density relative to liquid density - No-slip or specified slip condition - Downcomer is essentially bubble-free (or low holdup) - Negligible kinetic-energy and potential-energy changes at junctions **(b) Numerical solution:** (i) Plug into Chisti equation: $$U_{Lr} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times 10 \times (0.15 - 0.02) \times (0.15)^{2} \times (0.02)^{2}}{10.0 \times (0.02)^{2} + 6.0 \times (0.15)^{2}} }$$ Numerator: $$2 \times 9.81 \times 10 \times 0.13 \times 0.0225 \times 0.0004 = 2.296 \times 10^{-4}$$ Denominator: $$10.0 \times 0.0004 + 6.0 \times 0.0225 = 0.004 + 0.135 = 0.139$$ $$U_{Lr}^{2} = \dfrac{2.296 \times 10^{-4}}{0.139} = 1.652 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{U_{Lr} \approx 0.041 \; \text{m/s}}$$ (ii) Power input: $$Q_G = U_G \times A_r = 0.05 \times 0.30 = 0.015 \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$ $$P = Q_G \rho_L g H = 0.015 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 10 = 1471.5 \; \text{W}$$ $$\boxed{P \approx 1.47 \; \text{kW}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Supplementary]* **(a)** With the help of a **functional block diagram**, explain the construction and working of an **internal-loop airlift bioreactor**. Discuss the role of the **gas separator** and **sparger** in determining the reactor performance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare airlift bioreactors with **conventional stirred-tank bioreactors** under the following heads: (i) energy input and efficiency, (ii) shear stress and cell damage, (iii) scale-up methodology, and (iv) typical industrial applications. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Construction and working (7 marks):** A typical internal-loop ALR consists of: - **Outer cylindrical vessel** with aspect ratio $H/D = 3$–$6$. - **Concentric draft tube** (riser) mounted on a base plate, creating an annular downcomer around it. - **Sparger** at the base of the draft tube — distributes sterile air uniformly. - **Gas-separator section** at the top — allows bubble disengagement before liquid re-enters the downcomer. - **Sensors**: DO probe, pH probe, temperature probe, sampling port. - **Cooling jacket or internal coils** for temperature control. *Working:* Compressed sterile air → sparger → fine bubbles in riser → density reduction in riser → hydrostatic pressure imbalance → upward flow in riser + downward flow in downcomer → defined toroidal circulation → efficient OTR → no mechanical seals → low contamination risk. *Role of gas separator:* Prevents bubbles from being carried into the downcomer; without it, $\varepsilon_{Gd}$ rises and the driving force collapses. Design of the separator (height, baffles) strongly affects $K_T$ and hence $U_{Lr}$. *Role of sparger:* Determines the **initial bubble size**, which controls $\varepsilon_{Gr}$, $k_La$, and the entire hydrodynamic regime (bubbly → churn-turbulent). A fine-pore sparger gives higher holdup and $k_La$ but higher pressure drop. **(b) Comparison (7 marks, 1.75 each):** | Aspect | Airlift | Stirred-Tank | |---|---|---| | (i) Energy input | $P = Q_G \rho_L g H$ (from gas) | $P = N_p \rho N^{3} D_i^{5}$ (from impeller) | | (i) Efficiency | Higher $k_La$/kW due to gas–liquid interfacial area generation along entire riser height | Lower — only at impeller region | | (ii) Shear stress | Low, uniform; suited for animal/plant cells | High, localized; can damage shear-sensitive cells | | (iii) Scale-up | Based on constant $U_G$ or constant $\varepsilon_G$ | Based on constant $P/V$ or constant $k_La$ — empirical | | (iv) Applications | SCP, animal-cell, plant-cell, wastewater | Recombinant *E. coli*, yeast, most bacteria | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-deduction zones in KTU valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in the Chisti derivation → loses 2–3 marks. Always list: steady-state, isothermal, $\rho_G \ll \rho_L$, one-dimensional flow. > 2. **Unit mismatches** in $k_La$ and $U_G$ — convert mm/s → m/s; h⁻¹ → s⁻¹ where required. > 3. **Confusing $U_G$ (superficial) with $U_{b\infty}$ (single-bubble rise velocity)** — they are NOT the same. > 4. **Mixing up the area ratio** $A_r/A_d$ — riser is the gassed side, downcomer is the ungassed side. Students often invert this. > 5. **Forgetting the ½ factor** in the friction-loss term: $\rho_L U^{2} / 2$ is required (kinetic-energy form), not $\rho_L U^{2}$. > 6. **Reporting power input as $Q_G \times P$** (gas × pressure, wrong!) — KTU board expects the isothermal-expansion form $Q_G \rho_L g H$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - An **airlift bioreactor (ALR)** uses **gas-induced density difference** to drive liquid circulation — **no mechanical agitator**. - Two configurations: **internal loop (concentric draft tube)** and **external loop (separate columns)**. - **Riser = gassed (low density, up-flow)**, **downcomer = ungassed (high density, down-flow)**. - **Master equation — Chisti circulation velocity:** $$U_{Lr} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 g H (\varepsilon_{Gr} - \varepsilon_{Gd}) \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2} \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2}}{K_R \, \varepsilon_{Gd}^{2} + K_T \, \varepsilon_{Gr}^{2}} }$$ - **Power input (isothermal expansion):** $P = Q_G \rho_L g H$. - **Mass transfer:** governed by $k_La$ correlations (Kawase–Hashimoto for Newtonian fluids). - **$k_La$** is typically 100–1000 h⁻¹; $U_G$ in the range **0.005–0.10 m/s**. - **Aspect ratio $H/D$** = 3–6 for internal-loop ALRs. - **Advantages over stirred-tank:** no seals, low shear, low contamination, high $k_La$/kW, easy scale-up. - **Disadvantages:** limited to aerobic, gas-side-controlled systems; high gas throughput required for viscous broths. - **Scale-up rule:** maintain **constant $U_G$** and **geometric similarity** (same $A_r/A_d$ ratio). - **Industrial benchmark:** ICI’s 1500 m³ Pruteen loop for SCP — the largest airlift reactor ever built. - **Key derived quantities to compute:** $U_G$, $U_{Lr}$, $\varepsilon_G$, $k_La$, $P$, $t_m$ (mixing time). - **For KTU:** always pair the **Chisti equation with the isothermal power equation** — they are *the* KTU Module-4 airlift pair. - **Memorize the comparison table** — KTU loves 7-mark comparison questions on ALR vs STR.

topics.title: Fluidized Bed Bioreactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluidized Bed Bioreactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (FBR)** is a three-phase or two-phase biochemical reactor in which solid bioparticles (such as immobilized enzymes, immobilized microbial cells, biofilm-support carriers, or inert adsorbent particles) are maintained in a suspended, fluid-like state by the upward flow of a fluidizing medium (gas, liquid, or a gas–liquid mixture). The hydrodynamic condition in which the drag force exerted by the upward fluid exactly balances the net gravitational force (weight minus buoyancy) on the particles is termed the **incipient (minimum) fluidization state**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, FBRs are classified under **multi-phase catalytic bioreactors** where the biocatalyst is either physically trapped in a porous matrix or supported as a biofilm on solid carrier particles. The reactor operates in the regime between the **packed bed** (low velocity) and **pneumatic transport / elutriation** (high velocity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT521, Module 4):**
> Fluidized bed reactors form a sub-class of multi-phase bioreactors in which the solid phase (immobilized biocatalyst / biofilm carrier) is suspended by fluid drag. The key design parameter is the **minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$)**, governed by the balance of pressure drop across the bed and the buoyant weight of the particles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Boiling Sand Bucket"

Imagine a **bucket of dry sand** through which air is blown from the bottom. At low flow rates, air merely trickles through the interstices — the sand is **stationary (fixed bed)**. As you increase the air velocity, at one critical point, the sand grains suddenly start dancing, swirling, and behaving like a thick, bubbling liquid. The bucket appears to "boil." This is exactly what happens inside a fluidized bed bioreactor — only instead of sand, the solid phase is the **biocatalyst**, and instead of air, the fluidizing medium may be **wastewater, nutrient broth, or a gas mixture**.

If the air is shut off, the sand settles back into a packed bed. If the air is increased too much, the sand particles fly out the top — this is called **pneumatic conveying (elutriation)**. The safe operating window lies **between minimum fluidization and entrainment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Phase vs Two-Phase FBR:**
> - **Two-Phase (Liquid–Solid FBR):** A liquid (e.g., wastewater) fluidizes the solid carriers supporting biofilm — common in anaerobic wastewater treatment.
> - **Three-Phase (Gas–Liquid–Solid FBR):** Gas is sparged from the bottom in addition to liquid flow — creates bubbles that enhance mixing and oxygen transfer. Used in aerobic fermentations and biofilm processes.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities & Constants

The following constants and standard ranges are essential for FBR design and frequently appear in KTU numerical problems:

- Particle diameter ($d_p$): typically **0.1 mm to 1.0 mm** for bioparticle supports.
- Bed porosity at minimum fluidization ($\varepsilon_{mf}$): **0.4 to 0.55** (dimensionless).
- Superficial velocity ($U$): **0.001 m/s to 0.10 m/s** in liquid–solid; higher in gas–solid.
- Particle density ($\rho_p$): **1050 to 2500 kg/m³** (for carriers like activated carbon, sand, polymer beads).
- Fluid density ($\rho_f$): **1000 kg/m³** (water), **1.2 kg/m³** (air at STP).
- Bed height-to-diameter ratio (H/D): typically **2 to 10**.
- Viscosity of water at 30 °C: **$\mu \approx 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s**.
- Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81$ m/s²**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Regimes of a Fluidized Bed

As the superficial fluid velocity is progressively increased, the bed passes through the following well-defined regimes. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to identify these regimes from a $U$ vs. $\Delta P$ plot.

| Regime | Fluid Velocity | Bed Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed (Packed) Bed** | $U < U_{mf}$ | Solid stationary; $\Delta P$ increases with $U$ |
| **Incipient Fluidization** | $U = U_{mf}$ | Drag = buoyant weight; $\Delta P$ reaches maximum |
| **Particulate Fluidization** | $U_{mf} < U < U_{mb}$ | Uniform expansion (typically liquid–solid) |
| **Aggregative Fluidization** | $U > U_{mb}$ | Bubbles form (typically gas–solid) |
| **Slugging** | $U$ very high (small D) | Bubbles coalesce into slugs |
| **Pneumatic Transport** | $U > U_{t}$ (terminal velocity) | Particles elutriate out of bed |

> [!NOTE]
> **$U_{mb}$ — Minimum Bubbling Velocity:**
> This is the velocity at which the first gas bubble forms in a gas–solid fluidized bed. For $U_{mf} < U < U_{mb}$, the bed undergoes smooth (particulate) expansion without bubbles.

## 2.2 The Core Physics: Why Fluidization Happens

A particle resting in a fluidized bed is acted upon by:
- **Gravity** (downward, proportional to $\rho_p$).
- **Buoyancy** (upward, proportional to $\rho_f$).
- **Drag force** (upward, due to fluid flow).

The **pressure drop across the bed** is what supports the weight of the particles. As $U$ increases in the fixed-bed regime, $\Delta P$ rises (governed by the Ergun equation) until it equals the buoyant weight per unit area of the bed. At that point, particles begin to lift and the bed fluidizes.

> **Key insight:** Once the bed is fluidized, **$\Delta P$ remains essentially constant** regardless of further increases in $U$, because the extra fluid simply increases the bed porosity ($\varepsilon$) and bed height ($L$) — the bed "opens up" to accommodate it. This is a fundamental feature that distinguishes FBRs from fixed beds and is a high-yield KTU concept.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table lists every equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Fluidized Bed Bioreactors. The pipe symbol has been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon)^2 \mu U}{\varepsilon^3 d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75(1-\varepsilon) \rho_f U^2}{\varepsilon^3 d_p}$ | **Ergun Equation** — pressure drop in packed/fluidized bed | Pa/m |
| 2 | $\Delta P_{bed} = (1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g L_{mf}$ | Pressure drop at incipient fluidization (bed weight) | Pa |
| 3 | $Re_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_f}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number at minimum fluidization | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}$ | Archimedes number (ratio of gravitational to viscous forces) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 27.2$ | **Wen & Yu Correlation** (most commonly used) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $U_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ (Stokes regime, $Re_t < 0.1$) | Terminal (free-fall) velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \left(\dfrac{U}{U_t}\right)^{1/n}$ (Kunii–Levenspiel) | Bed porosity–velocity relationship | dimensionless |
| 8 | $L(1-\varepsilon) = L_{mf}(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) = \text{const}$ | **Mass conservation** of solids during expansion | m |
| 9 | $H_{bed} = L_{mf} \left(\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_{mf}}{1-\varepsilon}\right)$ | Expanded bed height | m |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.586 \, \Phi^{-0.72} \left(\dfrac{\mu^2}{\rho_f g d_p^3}\right)^{0.029} \left(\dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.021}$ | Bed porosity at minimum fluidization (Broadhurst & Becker) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\Delta P_{orifice} = \dfrac{\rho_f U_{or}^2}{2 C_D^2}$ | Pressure drop across distributor (orifice) | Pa |

Where:
- $L$ = bed height (m)
- $\varepsilon$ = bed porosity (void fraction)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = fluid viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\rho_p, \rho_f$ = particle and fluid density (kg/m³)
- $\Phi$ = sphericity of particle
- $C_D$ = discharge coefficient of distributor orifice
- $n$ = Richardson–Zaki index (≈ 2.4–4.6 depending on $Re_t$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:**
> - Equation 1 is used to find $\Delta P$ in both fixed and fluidized regimes.
> - Equation 2 is the **defining condition for $U_{mf}$** — when Ergun $\Delta P$ equals the buoyant weight, we are at incipient fluidization.
> - Equation 5 (Wen & Yu) is the **most popular shortcut** in KTU numerical problems because it directly relates $Re_{mf}$ to $Ar$ without iterative solution.
> - Equation 6 sets the **upper limit** of fluidization — beyond $U_t$, particles are carried away.

## 2.4 Advantages and Disadvantages (High-Yield KTU Theory)

**Advantages:**
- Excellent **heat and mass transfer** due to high solid–fluid interfacial area.
- **No channeling** or dead zones (uniform solids distribution).
- **Continuous catalyst/biofilm renewal** — fresh biocatalyst exposed to substrate.
- Easy **temperature control** (high thermal capacity of solid phase).
- Tolerates **particulate feedstocks** (e.g., suspended solids in wastewater).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Particle attrition** due to inter-particle collisions.
- Complex **hydrodynamics** — difficult to scale up.
- **Backmixing** of phases reduces driving force for reaction.
- Requires **robust distributor design** to avoid gas maldistribution.
- **Catalyst/biofilm loss** via elutriation if $U$ approaches $U_t$.

## 2.5 Industrial Applications

- **Aerobic wastewater treatment** (biological fluidized bed — BFB).
- **Anaerobic digestion** of organic effluents.
- **Ethanol fermentation** with immobilized yeast on alginate beads.
- **Enzyme production** by immobilized *Aspergillus* in tower FBRs.
- **Whole-cell biocatalysis** for steroid transformations.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$) Using the Ergun Equation

### Step 1: State the Condition for Incipient Fluidization

At the point of incipient fluidization, the pressure drop across the bed exactly balances the net buoyant weight of the particles per unit cross-sectional area.

$$
\Delta P_{Ergun} = \Delta P_{bed\,weight}
$$

### Step 2: Write the Ergun Equation at $U = U_{mf}$ and $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{mf}$

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{mf}}{L_{mf}} = \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2 \mu U_{mf}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \rho_f U_{mf}^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p}
$$

### Step 3: Write the Buoyant Weight Term

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{bed}}{L_{mf}} = (1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f)g
$$

### Step 4: Equate Both Expressions

$$
(1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f)g = \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2 \mu U_{mf}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \rho_f U_{mf}^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p}
$$

### Step 5: Divide Both Sides by $(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$ (since $0 < \varepsilon_{mf} < 1$)

$$
(\rho_p - \rho_f)g = \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \mu U_{mf}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75 \, \rho_f U_{mf}^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 d_p}
$$

### Step 6: Multiply Both Sides by $d_p^3 \rho_f / \mu^2$ to Nondimensionalize

$$
\frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2} = \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} \cdot \frac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_f}{\mu} \cdot \frac{d_p \rho_f U_{mf}}{\mu} \cdot \frac{1}{d_p \rho_f U_{mf}/\mu} + \frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} \left(\frac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_f}{\mu}\right)^2
$$

This is best expressed more cleanly. Recognize:

- Left-hand side = $Ar$ (Archimedes number)
- $\frac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_f}{\mu} = Re_{mf}$ (Reynolds number at minimum fluidization)

### Step 7: Define Dimensionless Group $\psi$ (Ergun Dimensionless Group)

$$
\psi = \frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\left(Re_{mf}\right)^2 + \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3}\left(Re_{mf}\right) = Ar
$$

### Step 8: Rearrange into a Quadratic in $Re_{mf}$

$$
1.75 \, Re_{mf}^2 + 150(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) Re_{mf} - Ar \, \varepsilon_{mf}^3 = 0
$$

### Step 9: Solve the Quadratic Equation (Taking the Positive Root)

$$
Re_{mf} = \frac{-150(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) + \sqrt{[150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})]^2 + 4(1.75)(Ar \, \varepsilon_{mf}^3)}}{2(1.75)}
$$

### Step 10: Simplify Using Wen & Yu's Approximation

Wen & Yu (1966) experimentally correlated $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.45$ (for most bioparticle systems) and proposed the following simpler, widely used form:

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 27.2
$$

This is **Equation 5 in the formula sheet** and is the **single most-tested equation on FBRs in KTU exams**.

### Step 11: Recover $U_{mf}$ from $Re_{mf}$

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \, \mu}{d_p \, \rho_f}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Expanded Bed Height (Richardson–Zaki Equation)

The Richardson–Zaki law relates superficial velocity to bed porosity:

### Step 1: Start from the empirical law

$$
U = U_t \, \varepsilon^n
$$

### Step 2: Apply the condition of solid mass conservation

$$
V_{solids} = A \cdot L(1-\varepsilon) = A \cdot L_{mf}(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) = \text{constant}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad L(1-\varepsilon) = L_{mf}(1-\varepsilon_{mf})
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Expanded Bed Height

$$
L = \frac{L_{mf}(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{1-\varepsilon}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute into the operating velocity expression

At $U = U_{mf}$, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{mf}$:

$$
U_{mf} = U_t \, \varepsilon_{mf}^n
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
n = \frac{\ln(U_{mf}/U_t)}{\ln(\varepsilon_{mf})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For $Re_t < 0.1$, $n \approx 4.65$. For $0.1 < Re_t < 1$, $n \approx 4.4 \cdot Re_t^{-0.03}$. KTU often gives $n$ directly as a numerical value (e.g., $n = 2.4$ for high $Re_t$ systems).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem (Model):** A liquid–solid fluidized bed uses water (30 °C) to suspend biocarrier particles of diameter $d_p = 0.5$ mm and density $\rho_p = 1200$ kg/m³. The bed porosity at minimum fluidization is $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.45$. The unexpanded bed height is $L_{mf} = 0.30$ m.

Calculate:
1. The minimum fluidization velocity, $U_{mf}$.
2. The expanded bed height when the superficial velocity is $U = 0.005$ m/s, assuming Richardson–Zaki index $n = 2.5$.
3. The pressure drop across the bed at incipient fluidization.

**Given:**
- $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $d_p = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m

**Solution:**

### Part (a): Calculate $Ar$ and then $Re_{mf}$ (Wen & Yu)

$$
Ar = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})^3 (1000)(1200 - 1000)(9.81)}{(0.8 \times 10^{-3})^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{(1.25 \times 10^{-10})(1000)(200)(9.81)}{6.4 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{(1.25 \times 10^{-10})(1.962 \times 10^{6})}{6.4 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{2.4525 \times 10^{-4}}{6.4 \times 10^{-7}} = 383.2
$$

Apply Wen & Yu:

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{(27.2)^2 + 0.0408 \times 383.2} - 27.2
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{739.84 + 15.63} - 27.2 = \sqrt{755.47} - 27.2 = 27.49 - 27.2 = 0.29
$$

Therefore:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{0.29 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000} = \frac{2.32 \times 10^{-4}}{0.5} = 4.64 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}
$$

### Part (b): Expanded Bed Height

From Richardson–Zaki: $\varepsilon^n = U/U_t$ is not directly given. Instead, we use the operating $U$ to find $\varepsilon$:

Use the form:

$$
\frac{U}{U_{mf}} = \left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_{mf}}\right)^n
$$

$$
\frac{0.005}{4.64 \times 10^{-4}} = \left(\frac{\varepsilon}{0.45}\right)^{2.5}
$$

$$
10.78 = \left(\frac{\varepsilon}{0.45}\right)^{2.5}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{0.45}\right) = 10.78^{0.4} = 2.55
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 0.45 \times 2.55 = 1.15 \quad \text{(Unphysical!)}
$$

This indicates that $U$ chosen (0.005 m/s) is too high — likely above $U_t$ for these particles. **A physically correct $U$ should give $\varepsilon < 1$.** For KTU problems, ensure $U < U_t$.

**Correction / Re-check (Illustrative):** Let us use $U = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s (just above $U_{mf}$):

$$
\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4.64 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.323 = \left(\frac{\varepsilon}{0.45}\right)^{2.5}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{0.45}\right) = 0.323^{0.4} = 0.638
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 0.45 \times 0.638 = 0.287 \quad \text{(Physically valid)}
$$

Expanded bed height:

$$
L = \frac{L_{mf}(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{1-\varepsilon} = \frac{0.30 \times 0.55}{1 - 0.287} = \frac{0.165}{0.713} = 0.231 \text{ m}
$$

### Part (c): Pressure Drop at Incipient Fluidization

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g L_{mf}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = 0.55 \times 200 \times 9.81 \times 0.30 = 323.7 \text{ Pa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical example demonstrates the **complete design calculation sequence** for an FBR — from particle properties to bed height to pressure drop. KTU 14-mark problems routinely test all three sub-parts together.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of a Three-Phase Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture and flow pathways of a typical three-phase FBR used in biochemical engineering applications (e.g., aerobic wastewater treatment with biofilm carriers).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Reservoir - Substrate Solution] --> B1[Feed Pump - Liquid]
    B1 --> C[Liquid Distributor - Perforated Plate]
    D[Compressed Air Supply] --> E[Air Filter and Regulator]
    E --> F[Sparger - Gas Distributor at Bottom]
    C --> G[Reactor Column - Main Shell]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Fluidized Zone - Solid Bioparticles in Suspension]
    H --> I[Freeboard Section - Settling Zone]
    I --> J[Cyclone Separator - Particle Recovery]
    J --> K1[Recycle Line - Return Solids]
    K1 --> G
    J --> L[Product Outlet - Treated Effluent]
    G --> M[Temperature Control Jacket - Heating or Cooling]
    M --> G
    G --> N[Pressure Transducer - Delta P Measurement]
    G --> O[Sampling Port - Online Analysis]
    P[Bioparticles Support Carriers - Biofilm or Immobilized Cells] -.-> H
```

### Architecture Interpretation (Engineering Reading)

| Node | Function | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| A → B1 → C | Liquid phase feed | Controls superficial liquid velocity $U_L$ |
| D → E → F | Gas phase inlet | Controls gas hold-up and oxygen transfer |
| G | Reactor shell | Houses the fluidized bed; designed for H/D = 2–10 |
| H | Fluidized zone | Active reaction volume; solids suspended by drag |
| I → J | Freeboard + cyclone | Prevents bioparticle washout (elutriation) |
| J → K1 → G | Solids recycle | Maintains biofilm carrier inventory |
| M | Jacket | Removes metabolic heat (highly exothermic fermentations) |
| N | $\Delta P$ sensor | Confirms incipient fluidization condition |
| P | Bioparticle supports | Provide surface area for biofilm attachment |

---

## 4.2 Hydrodynamic State Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

This diagram shows the decision logic for identifying the operating regime of an FBR — a high-yield KTU conceptual question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Operate Fluidized Bed Reactor] --> Q1{Compare U and Umf}
    Q1 -- U less than Umf --> R1[Fixed Bed Regime - No Fluidization]
    Q1 -- U equals Umf --> R2[Incipient Fluidization - Threshold Point]
    Q1 -- Umf less than U less than Ut --> R3[Fluidized Bed - Stable Operation]
    R3 --> Q2{Compare U and Ut}
    Q2 -- U approaches Ut --> R4[Approaching Entrainment - Caution]
    Q2 -- U exceeds Ut --> R5[Pneumatic Transport - Particle Elutriation]
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Decision Node 1** decides whether fluidization has commenced.
- **Decision Node 2** decides whether particles are being carried out.
- The safe operating window is the branch **"Fluidized Bed – Stable Operation"** (between $U_{mf}$ and $U_t$).
- The transitions ($R_2$ and $R_4$) are **critical control boundaries** monitored in industrial FBRs.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison: Fixed Bed vs Fluidized Bed (Mermaid Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FB[Fixed Bed Bioreactor]
        A1[Substrate flows over stationary catalyst] --> A2[Channeling and dead zones possible]
        A2 --> A3[Pressure drop increases with flow]
    end
    subgraph FLB[Fluidized Bed Bioreactor]
        B1[Substrate fluidizes solid bioparticles] --> B2[Uniform mixing and contact]
        B2 --> B3[Pressure drop nearly constant above Umf]
    end
    FB -- "Higher U applied" --> FLB
```

This visual emphasises the **fundamental shift in pressure drop behaviour** as the bed transitions from fixed to fluidized — the **single most-tested conceptual difference** in KTU vivas.
