Glucose solution] -->|Diffuses| MEM[Immobilized
Glucose Oxidase
GOD Layer] MEM -->|Generates H2O2| DET[Working Electrode
Platinum Anode] MEM -->|Consumes O2| REF[Reference Electrode
Ag/AgCl] DET -->|Electron flow| EXT[External Wire] REF -->|Completes circuit| EXT end EXT --> V REF --> M DET --> M ``` --- ## 4.3 Piezoelectric (QCM) Immunosensor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Quartz Crystal
AT-cut, f0 = 6-10 MHz] --> B[Gold Electrode
Surface] B --> C[Self-Assembled
Monolayer SAM] C --> D[Antibody
Immobilization] D --> E[Target Antigen
Binding Event] E --> F[Mass Loading
Delta m] F --> G[Frequency Shift
Delta f via Oscillator] G --> H[Frequency Counter
Readout] H --> I[Convert to
Concentration] ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparative Decision Tree — Choosing a Transducer ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Analyte Type?] --> Q1{Is the analyte
charged?} Q1 -->|Yes, electroactive| EC[Electrochemical
Amperometric / Voltammetric] Q1 -->|No, but ionizable| PT[Potentiometric
ISE / ISFET] Q1 -->|Neutral / Inert| Q2{Does binding
change mass?} Q2 -->|Yes| PZ[Piezoelectric
QCM / SAW] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Does binding
change light?} Q3 -->|Yes, absorbance| OPT1[Optical Absorbance
Colorimetric] Q3 -->|Yes, fluorescence| OPT2[Fluorescence
Labelled] Q3 -->|Yes, refractive index| OPT3[SPR / LSPR] Q3 -->|No optical change| Q4{Does reaction
release heat?} Q4 -->|Yes| CAL[Calorimetric
Enzyme Thermistor] Q4 -->|No| FAIL[Add labelling
step OR use
redox mediator] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to use this in an exam:** If the question says "design a biosensor for lead (Pb²⁺) ions in drinking water," follow the tree: charged → **potentiometric** (lead ion-selective electrode) is your answer. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define a biosensor. List its three main components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **biosensor** is an integrated analytical device that couples a **biological recognition element** with a **physico-chemical transducer** to produce a measurable signal proportional to the concentration of a target analyte. The **three main components** are: 1. **Bioreceptor (Biological Recognition Element):** Enzyme, antibody, nucleic acid, cell, or tissue that specifically binds the analyte. 2. **Transducer:** Converts the biochemical recognition event into a quantifiable electrical, optical, or mass-based signal. 3. **Signal Processor / Display:** Amplifies, processes, and displays the transducer output in user-friendly units. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the Limit of Detection (LoD) of a biosensor? How is it determined by the IUPAC method?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Limit of Detection (LoD)** is the **lowest concentration of an analyte that can be reliably distinguished from the background noise** of the sensor. By the **IUPAC convention**, LoD is defined as the analyte concentration that produces a signal equal to **three times the standard deviation of the blank (noise)**: $$ LoD = \dfrac{3 \cdot \sigma_{noise}}{S} $$ where $\sigma_{noise}$ is the standard deviation of the baseline signal and $S$ is the **slope of the calibration curve (sensitivity)**. The 3:1 signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3) provides 99.7% confidence that the detected signal is not from noise. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, 14 Marks, Choice Pattern]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **construction and working principle of a glucose biosensor** based on **glucose oxidase (GOD)**. Use a neat labelled diagram and the relevant biochemical reaction. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Michaelis–Menten equation** for the enzymatic reaction and explain how it governs the **dynamic range and sensitivity** of the biosensor. #### Model Solution — Part (a): Construction & Working **Construction (layered architecture from bottom to top):** 1. **Base electrode** — Platinum (working electrode). 2. **Reference electrode** — Ag/AgCl, placed alongside. 3. **Immobilized enzyme layer** — Glucose oxidase (GOD) covalently attached via **glutaraldehyde crosslinking** or entrapped in a **polymer hydrogel** (e.g., PVA). 4. **Diffusion-limiting membrane** — Cellulose acetate or Nafion to control substrate flux. 5. **Outer protective membrane** — Polycarbonate, to filter interferents and protect the enzyme. **Working principle — biochemical reaction:** $$ \text{Glucose} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GOD}} \text{Gluconic Acid} + H_2O_2 $$ The hydrogen peroxide produced is **electrochemically oxidized** at the platinum anode held at +0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl: $$ H_2O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt, +0.6 V}} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- $$ The resulting **current is proportional to glucose concentration** in the low-substrate regime. **Detection range:** typically 1–25 mM (clinically relevant blood glucose range). #### Model Solution — Part (b): Michaelis–Menten Derivation Starting from: $$ E + S \;\underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; ES \;\overset{k_{cat}}{\rightarrow}\; E + P $$ **Step 1:** Apply steady-state approximation to $[ES]$: $$ \dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1 [E][S] - (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES] = 0 $$ **Step 2:** Solve for $[ES]$: $$ [ES] = \dfrac{k_1 [E][S]}{k_{-1} + k_{cat}} = \dfrac{[E]_T [S]}{K_M + [S]} $$ where $K_M = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_{cat}}{k_1}$. **Step 3:** Substituting into the rate expression $\nu = k_{cat}[ES]$: $$ \nu = \dfrac{V_{max} [S]}{K_M + [S]} $$ **Step 4 — Dynamic range and sensitivity implications:** - For $[S] \ll K_M$: $\nu \approx \dfrac{V_{max}}{K_M} [S]$ → **linear dynamic range**, sensor is sensitive. - For $[S] \approx K_M$: response becomes non-linear; $K_M$ effectively sets the **midpoint of the dynamic range**. - For $[S] \gg K_M$: $\nu \rightarrow V_{max}$ → **saturation**, sensor loses sensitivity. **Conclusion:** A glucose biosensor is most sensitive in the concentration range $0.1 K_M$ to $2 K_M$, which is why commercial glucometers are calibrated in this regime. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **principle of a piezoelectric (QCM) immunosensor**. State the **Sauerbrey equation** and explain each term. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **electrochemical, optical, and piezoelectric transducers** in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, and typical applications. #### Model Solution — Part (a): QCM Immunosensor **Principle:** A **Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM)** uses an AT-cut quartz crystal oscillating at a fundamental frequency $f_0$ (5–10 MHz) via the **piezoelectric effect**. When a thin rigid mass $\Delta m$ deposits on the crystal surface, the oscillation frequency decreases by $\Delta f$, given by the **Sauerbrey equation**: $$ \Delta f = -\dfrac{2 f_0^{\,2}}{A \sqrt{\mu_q \rho_q}} \cdot \Delta m $$ where: - $f_0$ = fundamental resonance frequency of the quartz (Hz) - $A$ = piezoelectrically active electrode area (m²) - $\mu_q$ = shear modulus of AT-cut quartz (2.947 × 10¹⁰ N/m²) - $\rho_q$ = density of quartz (2648 kg/m³) **Working in an immunosensor:** Antibodies are immobilized on the gold electrode surface of the QCM. When the sample containing the target antigen flows over, antigen–antibody binding deposits mass, decreasing the frequency. The frequency shift is directly proportional to the bound antigen mass, enabling **label-free, real-time detection** of biomarkers. **Sensitivity:** Detection limits as low as **1 ng/cm²** (or ~1 pg in absolute mass) are achievable. #### Model Solution — Part (b): Transducer Comparison | Parameter | Electrochemical | Optical (SPR/Fluorescence) | Piezoelectric (QCM) | |---|---|---|---| | **Sensitivity** | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12} M$ | $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-15} M$ (SPR); single-molecule (fluorescence) | $1$ pg/cm² (mass) | | **Selectivity** | Moderate (redox interference possible) | Very High (wavelength-specific) | High (mass + bioreceptor) | | **Response Time** | Seconds | Minutes (SPR kinetics) | Seconds | | **Labelling Required?** | No | Sometimes (fluorescence) | No (label-free) | | **Miniaturization** | Excellent (microfabrication) | Moderate | Good | | **Cost** | Low | High | Medium | | **Typical Applications** | Glucose, lactate, dopamine, environmental toxins | DNA hybridization, cancer markers, drug screening | Antibody–antigen, gas sensors, cell adhesion | **Conclusion:** Electrochemical transducers dominate **point-of-care and wearable** devices; optical transducers dominate **research and high-throughput screening**; piezoelectric transducers dominate **label-free kinetic and mass-sensing** applications. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Don't confuse "biosensor" with "chemical sensor."** A pH electrode is NOT a biosensor because it has no biological recognition element. > - **In Michaelis–Menten derivations**, students often forget the steady-state assumption $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$. Always state this explicitly. > - **In Sauerbrey equation**, mark is lost if the **negative sign** (frequency decreases with mass loading) is omitted. > - **LoD is NOT the same as sensitivity.** Sensitivity = slope of calibration curve; LoD = 3σ/slope. > - **Drawings are mandatory** in 7-mark sub-parts. A neat labelled diagram fetches 2 marks by itself. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **biosensor** = **bioreceptor + transducer + signal processor** (3 essential parts). - ✅ **Limit of Detection (LoD)** is defined as the concentration giving signal = **3 × noise** ($S/N = 3$). - ✅ **Michaelis–Menten equation**: $\nu = \dfrac{V_{max} [S]}{K_M + [S]}$; **Lineweaver–Burk**: $\dfrac{1}{\nu} = \dfrac{K_M}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$. - ✅ **Sauerbrey equation** (QCM): $\Delta m = -\dfrac{\Delta f \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\mu_q \rho_q}}{2 f_0^{\,2}}$; **negative sign is mandatory**. - ✅ **Faraday's Law link** to amperometric sensors: $I = nFA\nu$ where $n$ = electrons per reaction, $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$. - ✅ **Four transducer types** to remember: **Electrochemical, Optical, Piezoelectric, Calorimetric**. - ✅ **Bioreceptor types** to remember: **Enzyme, Antibody, Nucleic Acid, Cell, Tissue, Aptamer, MIP**. - ✅ **Sensitivity** = $\Delta S / \Delta C$ (slope of calibration curve); **Selectivity** = ability to reject interferents. - ✅ **Response time** = time to reach 90% of steady-state signal; **dynamic range** = linear region of calibration plot. - ✅ **Nernst equation** (potentiometric): $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$. - ✅ **Glucose oxidase (GOD)** is the classic enzyme used in commercial blood-glucose meters. - ✅ **Clark electrode** = the foundational dissolved-oxygen/amperometric biosensor design. - ✅ **SPR** (Surface Plasmon Resonance) detects changes in **refractive index** upon biomolecular binding → label-free, real-time. - ✅ **Applications**: medical diagnostics, food safety, environmental monitoring, biodefense, fermentation process control. - ✅ **Market context**: Global biosensor market ~ **USD 30+ billion (2024)** — the most successful biosensor is the **personal glucose meter**.

topics.title: Physical and Chemical Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Physical and Chemical Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Physical Sensors** are devices that transduce a *physical parameter* (temperature, pressure, flow rate, level, viscosity, turbidity, foam) of a biochemical process into a measurable electrical signal. They operate purely on mechanical, thermal, electrical, or optical principles and do not involve a chemical recognition event.

**Chemical Sensors** are devices that contain a *chemical recognition element* (ion-selective membrane, enzyme, antibody, gas-permeable membrane) coupled to a *transducer*. The recognition element interacts selectively with a target analyte (pH, dissolved O₂, CO₂, glucose, NH₃, ions), and the transducer converts the resulting chemical change into an electrical signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** A sensor becomes a **Biosensor** only when the recognition element is *biological* (enzyme, antibody, cell, nucleic acid). For Module 4, treat enzyme-based electrodes as the *bridge* between chemical and biosensors.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a sensor as a **"translator at a United Nations conference"**:

- The **physical or chemical variable** is the foreign diplomat speaking an unknown language.
- The **recognition element / sensing element** is the ear that *listens specifically* to one language.
- The **transducer** is the translator who converts what was heard into English (the electrical signal).
- The **display / controller** is the ambassador receiving the message.

Just as a translator who only knows French cannot understand a German speaker, a pH electrode cannot measure oxygen, and a thermocouple cannot measure glucose — *selectivity* is the soul of every sensor.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (bolded as required by KTU valuation)

Every KTU question on sensors expects you to define these five key performance indices:

- **Sensitivity** ($\mathbf{S}$): Output change per unit change in input.
- **Selectivity** ($\mathbf{\alpha}$): Ability to respond only to the target analyte.
- **Range / Span**: The minimum and maximum input the sensor can measure.
- **Response Time ($\mathbf{\tau}$)** or $\mathbf{t_{90}}$: Time to reach 90% of final output.
- **Drift & Accuracy**: Long-term deviation of the output from the true value.

> [!NOTE]
> A typical **bioreactor temperature probe (PT-100 RTD)** has sensitivity ≈ **0.385 Ω/°C**, range **0–200 °C**, and response time **< 5 s** — these numbers are *examination gold*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — The "Why" Behind the Hierarchy

### 2.1.1 Physical Sensors (no chemical recognition)
- **Temperature** → RTD, Thermocouple, Thermistor, Bimetallic strip
- **Pressure** → Strain-gauge, Piezoelectric, Bourdon, Capacitive diaphragm
- **Flow** → Rotameter (variable area), Magnetic (Faraday's law), Turbine, Coriolis
- **Level** → Float, Differential pressure ($\Delta P = \rho g h$), Capacitive, Ultrasonic
- **Miscellaneous** → Viscometer, Turbidity (nephelometric), Foam sensor

### 2.1.2 Chemical Sensors (with chemical recognition)
- **Electrochemical Potentiometric** → pH glass electrode, ion-selective electrodes (ISE), NH₃, CO₂ Severinghaus
- **Electrochemical Amperometric** → Clark-type Dissolved Oxygen (DO) probe
- **Electrochemical Voltammetric / Coulometric** → Glucose enzyme electrode
- **Optical / Spectroscopic** → IR CO₂ analyzer, UV absorbance (Beer–Lambert)
- **Semiconductor / Chemiresistive** → Metal-oxide (SnO₂) gas sensor

## 2.2 Operational Theory — Bullet Logic

### 2.2.1 Temperature — Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)
- **Principle:** Electrical resistance of pure metal (Pt, Ni, Cu) increases linearly with temperature.
- **Callendar–Van Dusen equation** (most cited in KTU paper):
$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3 \right]$$
where $A, B, C$ are constants; for $T \ge 0\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the $C$ term vanishes.
- For PT-100: $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $0\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and nominal $\alpha = 0.00385\ \Omega/\Omega/^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.

### 2.2.2 Temperature — Thermocouple
- Based on the **Seebeck Effect** — two dissimilar metals generate an EMF proportional to the temperature difference between hot (measurement) and cold (reference) junctions.
- EMF equation (linearised):
$$\mathcal{E} \approx S \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$
where $S$ is the Seebeck coefficient (µV/°C). Type K (Chromel–Alumel): $S \approx 41\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$.

### 2.2.3 Pressure — Strain Gauge
- A Wheatstone bridge is unbalanced when a foil grid stretches under pressure, changing its resistance.
- Gauge factor:
$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$
- Bridge output: $V_{out} \approx V_{in} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$.

### 2.2.4 Flow — Magnetic Flow Meter
- Based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.
- Induced EMF across a fluid of velocity $v$ in a magnetic field $B$ across a tube diameter $D$:
$$E = B \cdot D \cdot v$$
- Output is **independent** of fluid conductivity above a minimum threshold and of pressure.

### 2.2.5 pH Glass Electrode (Potentiometric — *most important* for KTU)
- A thin **glass membrane** (typically Li₂O–Na₂O–SiO₂) develops a potential proportional to H⁺ activity difference between sample and internal buffer.
- The potential follows the **Nernst Equation**:
$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{H^+}$$
At $25\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, for a monovalent ion ($n = 1$):
$$E = E^0 + 0.05916 \cdot \mathrm{pH}$$
- The factor **0.05916 V / pH unit at 25 °C** is the KTU *favourite constant*.

### 2.2.6 Dissolved Oxygen (DO) — Clark Electrode
- Amperometric: O₂ diffuses through a gas-permeable membrane (Teflon) and is reduced at a Pt cathode held at $-0.7$ V vs Ag/AgCl.
$$\mathrm{O_2 + 2 H_2O + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4 OH^-}$$
- Current is proportional to dissolved O₂ partial pressure (Henry's Law).

### 2.2.7 Optical Density / Turbidity — Beer–Lambert Law
$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l = \log_{10} \left( \frac{I_0}{I} \right)$$
Used in **photometric biomass sensors** (cell density monitoring in fermenters).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Phenomenon | Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Key Constant / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | RTD (PT-100) | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha = 0.00385\ \Omega/\Omega/^{\circ}C$ |
| Temperature | Thermocouple | $\mathcal{E} = S \Delta T$ | $S = 41\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$ (Type K) |
| Pressure | Strain gauge | $V_o = V_i G \varepsilon$ | $G \approx 2$ for constantan |
| Flow | Magnetic | $E = B D v$ | induced EMF in volts |
| Level | Differential-P | $\Delta P = \rho g h$ | $\rho$ = fluid density |
| pH | Glass electrode | $E = E^0 + 0.05916\ \mathrm{pH}$ | $n = 1$, 298 K |
| DO | Clark probe | $I \propto p_{O_2}$ (Henry) | $-0.7$ V polarisation |
| Optical density | Spectrometer | $A = \varepsilon c l$ | Beer–Lambert |
| Conductivity | Conductivity cell | $G = \kappa \cdot (A/l)$ | $\kappa$ in S/m |
| Glucose | Enzyme electrode | $I \propto [\mathrm{glucose}]$ | GOx / H₂O₂ at +0.6 V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Whenever a KTU problem says *"a PT-100 reads 138.5 Ω"*, the temperature is **100 °C**. Memorise the linear slope: **100 Ω → 0 °C**, **138.5 Ω → 100 °C**. This single trick solves 3-mark and 7-mark sub-parts.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biochemical Industries
- **Fermenter control loop** → Temperature (RTD) + pH (glass electrode) + DO (Clark) form the **holy trinity** of bioprocess instrumentation.
- **Downstream chromatography** → Conductivity and UV (280 nm) sensors monitor salt gradients and protein elution.
- **Bioprocess PAT (Process Analytical Technology)** → Real-time biomass (capacitance / optical density) enables *QbD*-aligned closed-loop control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation for the pH Glass Electrode

**Starting point:** For any reversible electrochemical cell, the change in Gibbs free energy equals the electrical work done:

$$\Delta G = -n F E$$

**Step 1 — Chemical potential for the half-reaction $\mathrm{H^+ (sample) \to H^+ (internal\ buffer)}$:**

The change in Gibbs energy for transferring 1 mole of H⁺ across the glass membrane is:

$$\Delta G = \mu_{H^+,in} - \mu_{H^+,out} = RT \ln a_{H^+,in} - RT \ln a_{H^+,out}$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions for $\Delta G$:**

$$-n F E = RT \ln a_{H^+,in} - RT \ln a_{H^+,out}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $E$:**

$$E = -\frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{H^+,in}}{a_{H^+,out}}$$

$$E = -\frac{RT}{nF} \left[ \ln a_{H^+,in} - \ln a_{H^+,out} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Group constant terms into $E^0$:**

Since $a_{H^+,in}$ is fixed by the internal buffer, define:

$$E^0 = -\frac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{H^+,in}$$

Therefore:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{H^+,out}$$

**Step 5 — Convert $\ln a_{H^+}$ to pH (use $\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10} a_{H^+}$):**

$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303\, RT}{nF} \log_{10} a_{H^+}$$

$$E = E^0 + \frac{2.303\, RT}{nF} \cdot \mathrm{pH}$$

**Step 6 — Insert numerical values at $T = 298.15\ \mathrm{K}$, $n = 1$:**

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{1 \times 96485} = 0.05916\ \mathrm{V}$$

$$\boxed{E = E^0 + 0.05916\ \cdot \mathrm{pH} \quad \text{(volts, at 25 °C)}}$$

**Step 7 — Slope-temperature dependence (for 7-mark sub-parts):**

Differentiate the pre-factor with respect to $T$:

$$\frac{d}{dT}\left(\frac{2.303\,RT}{F}\right) = \frac{2.303\,R}{F} = 1.984 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{V/K}$$

So the Nernst slope grows by **+0.198 mV / pH per °C**. At $T = 37\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (human body / mammalian cell culture), the slope is $0.05916 \times (310/298) = 0.0615\ \mathrm{V/pH}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beer–Lambert Law (Optical Density / Biomass)

**Step 1 — Consider a thin slab of absorbing solution of thickness $\mathrm{d}x$.**

The fractional decrease in light intensity is proportional to the intensity itself and the concentration $c$:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\varepsilon' c\, \mathrm{d}x$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $x = 0$ (intensity $I_0$) to $x = l$ (intensity $I$):**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\varepsilon' c \int_{0}^{l} dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\varepsilon' c\, l$$

**Step 3 — Convert to base-10 logarithm by substituting $\varepsilon' = 2.303\, \varepsilon$:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon c\, l = A$$

$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon c l \quad \text{(Absorbance, dimensionless)}}$$

**Step 4 — Application to fermenter biomass:**

With a fixed path length $l$, absorbance is linear in cell density $c$, enabling *real-time* OD monitoring at 600 nm.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Magnetic Flow Meter (Faraday's Law)

**Step 1 — A conductive fluid of velocity $\vec{v}$ moving in magnetic field $\vec{B}$ experiences Lorentz force on its charges:**

$$\vec{F} = q \left( \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \right)$$

**Step 2 — This force separates + and – charges across the pipe, building an EMF until the electric field balances the magnetic force:**

$$q E_{ind} = q v B \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{ind} = v B$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the tube diameter $D$ (the distance between electrodes):**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{E} = B \cdot D \cdot v}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow rate $Q = v \cdot (\pi D^2/4)$:**

$$\mathcal{E} = \frac{4 B Q}{\pi D}$$

Hence, for a fixed $B$ and $D$, voltage is *directly proportional* to volumetric flow — a beautiful linear transfer function.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection in a Typical Bioreactor

| Process Variable | Sensor of Choice | Why? | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | PT-100 RTD | High accuracy, sterilizable in situ | 0 – 150 °C |
| pH | Glass electrode | Highly selective to H⁺ | 0 – 14 pH |
| DO | Clark electrode | Membrane-protected, autoclave-safe | 0 – 100 % sat. |
| Foam | Conductivity probe | Air/water interface conducts differently | on/off |
| Biomass | Capacitance / OD | Non-invasive, real-time | 0 – 200 g DCW/L |
| Glucose | Enzyme (GOx) electrode | Selective via enzyme | 0 – 50 g/L |
| CO₂ in off-gas | IR analyzer | Strong IR absorption at 4.26 µm | 0 – 10 % v/v |

> [!NOTE]
> A 7-mark KTU sub-part often asks: *"Justify the choice of sensor for pH measurement in a fermentation broth."* The expected answer keywords are: **glass membrane, selectivity to H⁺, Nernstian response, steam-sterilisable, reference electrode (Ag/AgCl) used in combination.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Distinguish between a physical sensor and a chemical sensor with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
A **physical sensor** transduces a physical variable (temperature, pressure, flow) into an electrical signal using mechanical, thermal, or electrical principles, with no chemical recognition step. *Example:* PT-100 RTD for temperature.

A **chemical sensor** contains a *chemical recognition element* that interacts selectively with a target analyte, producing a measurable change via an electrochemical, optical, or mass-sensitive transducer. *Example:* Glass membrane pH electrode.

The *fundamental difference* is the presence of a selective chemical interface in the latter.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State the Nernst equation for a glass pH electrode and write its slope at 25 °C.

**Model Answer:**
$$E = E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \cdot \mathrm{pH}$$

At 25 °C, the slope is **0.05916 V per pH unit** (≈ 59.16 mV / pH). The response is *Nernstian* and is *linear* over the entire 0 – 14 pH range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Clark-type dissolved oxygen probe**. Derive the relationship between probe current and dissolved oxygen partial pressure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PT-100 RTD has a resistance of **100 Ω at 0 °C**. If its temperature coefficient of resistance is $\alpha = 0.00385\ \Omega/\Omega/^{\circ}C$, calculate the resistance at **120 °C**. Mention **two** advantages of RTD over thermocouple.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Clark DO Probe — Construction & Working

**Construction (refer to Diagram 4.4 above):**

1. **Pt cathode** — a small platinum wire held at $-0.7$ V (vs Ag/AgCl anode).
2. **Ag/AgCl anode** — the reference/counter electrode.
3. **KCl electrolyte** filling the space between cathode and anode.
4. **Teflon (PTFE) gas-permeable membrane** — selectively passes O₂ but not ions or water.
5. **Body** — typically stainless steel, steam-sterilisable for in-situ fermentation use.

**Working Principle:**

O₂ from the broth diffuses through the Teflon membrane into the electrolyte. At the Pt cathode, the polarising voltage drives the reduction:

$$\mathrm{O_2 + 2 H_2O + 4 e^- \rightarrow 4 OH^-}$$

At the Ag/AgCl anode: $\mathrm{4 Ag \rightarrow 4 Ag^+ + 4 e^-}$, which combines with Cl⁻ to form AgCl.

**Derivation of current vs DO:**

- At steady state, the rate of O₂ diffusion through the membrane equals its electrochemical consumption at the cathode.
- By **Fick's First Law**, the molar flux $J$ is:
$$J = \frac{D_m (p_{O_2,bulk} - p_{O_2,cathode})}{l_m}$$
where $D_m$ = membrane diffusivity, $l_m$ = membrane thickness, $p_{O_2}$ = partial pressures.
- If the cathode depletes O₂ to ~0 (because of fast electrochemical kinetics), $p_{O_2,cathode} \to 0$, so:
$$J = \frac{D_m \cdot p_{O_2,bulk}}{l_m}$$
- The current is $I = n F A J$, with $n = 4$ electrons per O₂:
$$\boxed{I = \frac{4 F A D_m}{l_m} \cdot p_{O_2,bulk}}$$

Hence probe current is **directly proportional** to dissolved O₂ partial pressure — an ideal linear transducer. **(7 marks distributed: 2 for construction, 2 for half-reactions, 3 for derivation.)**

#### (b) PT-100 RTD Calculation

**Given:** $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0\,^{\circ}C$, $\alpha = 0.00385\ \Omega/\Omega/^{\circ}C$, $T = 120\,^{\circ}C$.

**Step 1 — Apply linear approximation:**

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha T \right)$$

$$R_T = 100 \left( 1 + 0.00385 \times 120 \right)$$

**Step 2 — Multiply:**

$$0.00385 \times 120 = 0.462$$

$$R_T = 100 \times (1 + 0.462) = 100 \times 1.462 = 146.2\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Two advantages of RTD over Thermocouple:**

1. **Higher accuracy and stability** — RTD offers ±0.1 °C accuracy vs ±1–2 °C for thermocouples.
2. **Better linearity** — RTD follows an almost linear $R$–$T$ curve, requiring simpler signal conditioning; thermocouples need polynomial cold-junction compensation.
3. *(Optional third)* Long-term drift is much smaller in platinum RTDs.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{R_{120^{\circ}C} = 146.2\ \Omega}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — ALTERNATIVE

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of a **glass membrane pH electrode**. Derive the Nernst equation and show that the electrode potential changes by **59.16 mV per pH unit** at 25 °C.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fermenter broth shows an optical density (OD) of **0.600 at 600 nm** in a cuvette of path length **1 cm**. The extinction coefficient of cells is $\varepsilon = 1.5 \times 10^{9}\ \mathrm{mL\cdot g^{-1}\cdot cm^{-1}}$... wait, use standard form: $\varepsilon = 1.5\ \mathrm{L\cdot g^{-1}\cdot cm^{-1}}$ (typical for biomass). Calculate the **cell concentration in g/L**.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Glass Membrane pH Electrode

**Construction:**

1. **Glass bulb (sensing membrane)** — special composition $\mathrm{Na_2O}$–$\mathrm{CaO}$–$\mathrm{SiO_2}$ (or Li₂O-based for low-alkaline error). Membrane thickness ≈ 0.1 mm, very high electrical resistance (50–500 MΩ).
2. **Internal reference electrode** — Ag/AgCl immersed in **internal buffer of fixed pH = 7** (or pH 4 with saturated KCl).
3. **External reference electrode** — separate calomel or Ag/AgCl probe immersed in saturated KCl, contacting the sample through a **salt bridge / liquid junction** (porous ceramic).
4. **High-impedance voltmeter** to measure the small potential without drawing current.

**Working Principle:**

When the glass bulb is dipped in the sample, a thin hydrated gel layer forms on its surface. H⁺ ions from the sample exchange with Na⁺ ions in this gel, generating a **phase-boundary potential** that obeys the Donnan equilibrium. The membrane therefore acts as a *proton-selective* phase.

**Derivation of Nernst equation** (refer Section 3.1 for full derivation):

$$E = E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \cdot \mathrm{pH}$$

At $T = 298.15\ \mathrm{K}$:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916\ \mathrm{V / pH}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E = 0.05916\ \mathrm{V \ per \ pH \ unit \ at \ 25\ ^{\circ}C}}$$

**Why it works selectively:** Only H⁺ (and to a smaller extent, Li⁺, Na⁺) can cross the dehydrated glass interstices, giving the membrane its selectivity. **(7 marks: 2 construction, 2 principle, 3 derivation.)**

#### (b) Beer–Lambert Calculation for Biomass

**Given:** $A = 0.600$, $l = 1\ \mathrm{cm}$, $\varepsilon = 1.5\ \mathrm{L\cdot g^{-1}\cdot cm^{-1}}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Beer–Lambert law:**

$$A = \varepsilon c l$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $c$:**

$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot l} = \frac{0.600}{1.5 \times 1} = 0.4\ \mathrm{g/L}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to standard units** (note that with $\varepsilon$ in L g⁻¹ cm⁻¹, $c$ comes out in g/L directly):

$$\boxed{c = 0.4\ \mathrm{g \ cells \ per \ litre}}$$

This falls in the typical mid-log phase range for a bacterial fermentation (≈ 0.2 – 1.0 g DCW/L).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Five common pitfalls the KTU board examiner explicitly marks down:**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** "$0.05916$" without "V per pH unit at 25 °C" loses 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the Nernst derivation.** Quoting $E = E^0 - 0.0591 \cdot \mathrm{pH}$ (with the wrong sign) is a 1-mark cut. Memorise: **higher pH → more negative potential**.
> 3. **Confusing Clark probe half-reactions.** O₂ is **reduced** (gains electrons) at the *cathode*; Ag is **oxidised** at the *anode*. Reversing these costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Using $R = R_0(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ with absolute $T$ instead of $\Delta T$.** Either form is acceptable, but be consistent.
> 5. **Neglecting the "gas-permeable membrane" in the Clark probe diagram.** Examiners look for *Teflon/PTFE* as the keyword; it carries **1 full mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sensor** = *Recognition element* + *Transducer* + *Signal processor*.
- **Physical sensors** measure *physical* variables; **chemical sensors** measure *chemical* variables via a selective interface; **biosensors** use a *biological* recognition element.
- The **Nernst equation** is the heart of every potentiometric sensor: $E = E^0 + (RT/nF)\ln a_{\text{analyte}}$.
- **0.05916 V / pH at 25 °C** is the universal pH slope — commit it to memory.
- **PT-100 RTD:** $R = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C, slope = 0.385 Ω/°C (using $\alpha = 0.00385$).
- **Thermocouple** uses *Seebeck effect* — voltage proportional to $\Delta T$ across dissimilar metals.
- **Magnetic flow meter:** $E = B D v$ — output *independent* of fluid conductivity.
- **Clark DO probe:** amperometric, gas-permeable membrane, polarisation at $-0.7$ V, $I \propto p_{O_2}$.
- **Beer–Lambert:** $A = \varepsilon c l$ — linear absorbance → real-time biomass.
- In a **fermenter**, the *trinity* of sensors is **T, pH, DO** — always discuss in PAT answers.
- **Glass pH electrode** is highly selective to H⁺ because only H⁺ crosses the hydrated gel layer.
- **Sensors form the feedback arm** of every PID control loop in a bioreactor.
- The **response time ($\tau$)** of a sensor is the time to reach ~63% of its final value; **t₉₀** = 90% — both appear in 3-mark definitions.
- The **Seebeck coefficient** of Type K thermocouple ≈ **41 µV/°C** — a frequently asked KTU value.
- **Wheatstone bridge** in strain gauge: $V_{out} \approx (V_{in} \cdot \Delta R)/(4R)$ — full-bridge sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Analysis Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Analysis Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas Analysis Sensors** are specialized transducer devices employed in bioprocess engineering to qualitatively identify and quantitatively measure the concentration of specific gaseous components (O₂, CO₂, CH₄, H₂, NH₃, H₂S, volatile organic compounds) within a process stream, off-gas line, or fermentation headspace. They convert the partial pressure or mole fraction of a target gas into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance change) using physico-chemical principles such as paramagnetism, infrared absorption, thermal conductivity, electrochemistry, or chemisorption.

In the context of **PECHT521 – Biochemical Engineering**, gas analysis sensors form the **analytical backbone** of any aerobic/anaerobic fermentation, cell culture bioreactor, or downstream gas-handling unit operation, providing **real-time data for mass-balance closure, respiratory quotient (RQ) calculation, biomass estimation, and process safety**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sniffer That Speaks Volts"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a closed kitchen. Your nose instantly tells you whether the gas stove is leaking (methane), the toast is burning (CO₂/CO), or the air is fresh. Your nose is essentially a *biological gas sensor* — it binds specific gas molecules to receptor cells and converts the chemical event into a nerve signal. A **gas analysis sensor** does the exact same thing, except its "receptors" are made of platinum wires, zirconia ceramics, or polymer membranes, and its "nerve signal" is an electrical output in **millivolts (mV)** or **milliamps (mA)**.

The fundamental operating chain in *every* gas sensor is identical:

$$\text{Target Gas (molecule)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Physical/Chemical Interaction} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transduction Element} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Electrical Signal}$$

| **Step** | **Biological Analogue** | **Engineering Realization** |
|---|---|---|
| Gas exposure | Inhalation | Diffusion / membrane permeation |
| Recognition | Olfactory receptors | Selective sorbent / specific chemistry |
| Transduction | Neural firing | Resistance / voltage / current change |
| Read-out | Brain perception | Amplifier + ADC + display/PLC |

---

## 1.3 Why Gas Sensors Are *Non-Negotiable* in Bioprocesses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In fermentation off-gas analysis, the **O₂ uptake rate (OUR)** and **CO₂ evolution rate (CER)** are computed *solely* from inlet/outlet gas composition. Without gas sensors, the textbook equations for RQ = CER/OUR become mathematically impossible to solve in a live bioreactor.

The four pillars of bioprocess gas analysis are:

1. **Mass Balance Verification** — Inlet O₂ + generated O₂ = Consumed O₂ + Outlet O₂
2. **Metabolic State Identification** — Respiratory Quotient (RQ) indicates substrate shift (glucose → ethanol yields RQ ≈ infinity)
3. **Safety Interlocks** — Detection of explosive CH₄/H₂ above LEL (Lower Explosive Limit ≈ **5% v/v** for methane in air)
4. **Regulatory Compliance** — Stack monitoring, EPA-grade emission logging (SO₂ < **100 ppm**, NOₓ < **50 ppm** for most industrial stacks)

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> Gas sensors are classified by their **transduction principle**, not by the gas they detect. The same gas (e.g., O₂) can be measured by **four different sensor families**, each with distinct accuracy, response time, and cost.

**Primary Classification (KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus):**

| **Sensor Family** | **Target Gases (typical)** | **Key Physical Principle** |
|---|---|---|
| Paramagnetic | O₂ only | Paramagnetic susceptibility |
| Infrared (NDIR) | CO₂, CO, CH₄, NOₓ | IR absorption at λ = 4.26 µm (CO₂) |
| Thermal Conductivity (TCD) | H₂, He, CO₂, CH₄ | Difference in k (thermal conductivity) |
| Electrochemical (Amperometric) | O₂, CO, H₂S, Cl₂, NO₂ | Redox reaction at working electrode |
| Zirconia (Solid Electrolyte) | O₂ (high temp) | Nernst equation on Y₂O₃-ZrO₂ |
| Metal-Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) | VOCs, H₂, CH₄, CO | Resistance change on SnO₂/WO₃ |
| Flame Ionization Detector (FID) | Total hydrocarbons | Ionization in H₂/air flame |
| Photoionization Detector (PID) | VOCs, NH₃ | UV lamp ionization (10.6 eV) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Sensor by Sensor

### 2.1.1 Paramagnetic Oxygen Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Paramagnetism** is the property of a material to be weakly attracted into a magnetic field. Among all common gases, **only O₂ is strongly paramagnetic** (molar susceptibility χ ≈ +3449 × 10⁻⁶ cgs at STP). N₂, CO₂, and water vapour are *diamagnetic* (χ is negative). This makes O₂ the "magnetic exception" and enables a highly selective measurement.

**Construction (Müllen-type dumb-bell cell):**
- Two sealed glass spheres filled with N₂ are suspended on a torsion wire inside a non-uniform magnetic field.
- When O₂-containing sample gas is introduced, the paramagnetic O₂ molecules are pulled toward the stronger field region, displacing the diamagnetic N₂ spheres.
- The resulting torque is balanced by a feedback coil, and the **compensation current** becomes the measurement signal.

**Paramagnetic Susceptibility (Curie's Law for gases):**
$$\chi_m = \frac{N_A \mu^2}{3k_B T}$$

where:
- $\chi_m$ = molar magnetic susceptibility
- $N_A$ = **Avogadro's number = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- $\mu$ = magnetic moment of the molecule
- $k_B$ = **Boltzmann constant = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (**K**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature is the hidden enemy** of paramagnetic sensors — $\chi_m \propto 1/T$. A 10 °C shift causes ~3.4% reading drift. All commercial units contain a **thermostatted cell at 50–60 °C** to nullify this.

---

### 2.1.2 Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> Every heteroatomic gas (CO₂, CO, CH₄, NO, SO₂) absorbs infrared light at a *characteristic fingerprint wavelength* because the IR photon energy matches a vibrational-rotational transition of the molecular bond.

**Beer–Lambert Law (the heart of all optical gas sensing):**

$$I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda) \cdot e^{-\alpha(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot L}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity (**W/m²**)
- $I$ = transmitted intensity
- $\alpha(\lambda)$ = absorption coefficient (**m²/mol** or **ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹**)
- $c$ = gas concentration (**mol/m³** or **ppm**)
- $L$ = optical path length (**m**)

**Fingerprint wavelengths (must memorize for KTU viva):**

| **Gas** | **Bond Vibrating** | **Absorption λ (µm)** |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | C=O asymmetric stretch | **4.26** |
| CO | C≡O stretch | **4.67** |
| CH₄ | C–H stretch | **3.31** |
| NO | N–O stretch | **5.30** |
| SO₂ | S=O stretch | **7.40** |
| NH₃ | N–H bend | **10.6** |

The detector is usually a **thermopile** or **pyroelectric** element, which converts the differential IR flux into a voltage proportional to gas concentration.

---

### 2.1.3 Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD)

> [!NOTE]
> The TCD exploits the difference in **thermal conductivity (k)** between the sample gas and a reference gas (usually N₂ or H₂). It is the *workhorse* of gas chromatography and a reliable H₂ analyzer in anaerobic bioprocesses.

**Operating principle:**
- A heated filament (W/Re) is exposed to the sample gas.
- Heat loss from the filament = $q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$
- A gas with high $k$ (e.g., H₂, $k$ ≈ **0.182 W/m·K**) cools the filament faster, lowering its resistance.
- A Wheatstone bridge converts the resistance change into a voltage.

**Thermal conductivities (W/m·K) at 300 K — must memorize for rank-ordering:**

| **Gas** | $k$ (W/m·K) | **Relative to N₂** |
|---|---|---|
| H₂ | 0.182 | **6.7 ×** |
| He | 0.152 | **5.6 ×** |
| CH₄ | 0.034 | 1.3 × |
| O₂ | 0.026 | 0.95 × |
| N₂ | 0.026 | 1.00 (ref) |
| CO₂ | 0.017 | 0.65 × |
| SO₂ | 0.011 | 0.42 × |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The TCD is **non-selective**. It cannot tell H₂ from He because both have high $k$. It also fails in the presence of water vapour. Always pass the sample through a **Nafion dryer** or **magnesium perchlorate** column before TCD analysis.

---

### 2.1.4 Electrochemical (Amperometric) Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> These are essentially fuel cells in miniature. The target gas diffuses through a gas-permeable membrane (usually PTFE), reaches a working electrode, and undergoes a redox reaction that generates a current proportional to concentration.

**General electrode reaction (example — O₂ sensor):**
$$\text{Cathode:} \quad \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{Anode:} \quad \text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$$

**Output current (limiting diffusion current):**

$$I = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot D \cdot c}{\delta}$$

where:
- $n$ = electrons transferred per molecule (4 for O₂)
- $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol**
- $A$ = electrode area (**m²**)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of gas in membrane (**m²/s**)
- $c$ = bulk gas concentration (**mol/m³**)
- $\delta$ = diffusion layer thickness (**m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor Lifetime Equation:** $I \propto c$, but the electrolyte is *consumed*. Typical lifetimes are **2–3 years** in clean air. Failure mode is **electrolyte dry-out**, not electrode degradation.

---

### 2.1.5 Zirconia Oxygen Sensor (Lambda Sensor)

> [!NOTE]
> Used in combustion flue gas, internal combustion engines, and high-temperature bioprocess incinerators. A yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) ceramic conducts **O²⁻ ions** above **600 °C**, enabling a galvanic-cell Nernst measurement.

**Nernst Equation for the Zirconia Cell:**

$$E = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right)$$

For air as reference ($p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}} = 0.2095$ atm) at $T = 1073$ K (800 °C):

$$E \;(\text{mV}) = 0.0496 \cdot T \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2095}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right)$$

A typical exhaust reading: $E \approx 800$ mV at lean burn, $\approx 50$ mV at rich burn, **450 mV at stoichiometric** (λ = 1).

---

### 2.1.6 Metal-Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> A porous layer of **SnO₂** or **WO₃** is heated to 300–400 °C by an embedded micro-heater. In clean air, O₂ adsorbs and traps electrons, creating a high-resistance depletion layer. When a reducing gas (H₂, CO, VOCs) contacts the surface, it reacts with the adsorbed O⁻, releasing electrons back into the conduction band and **lowering resistance**.

**Response equation (Taguchi model):**
$$R_s = R_0 \cdot (1 + K \cdot c^n)$$

where $K$ is a sensitivity constant and $n \approx 0.5$ for many reducing gases.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **every equation you must know** for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Notice that the vertical bar symbol has been replaced with `\vert` to preserve the markdown table.

| **#** | **Equation** | **Application** | **Key Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\chi_m = \dfrac{N_A \mu^2}{3 k_B T}$ | Paramagnetic sensor (Curie) | $T$ in K; $\chi_m$ in m³/mol |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha c L}$ | NDIR sensor (Beer–Lambert) | $c$ in mol/m³, $L$ in m |
| 3 | $A = -\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = \alpha c L$ | Absorbance form of (2) | $A$ dimensionless |
| 4 | $I_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{nFADc}{\delta}$ | Electrochemical sensor | $I$ in A, $c$ in mol/m³ |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right)$ | Zirconia sensor (Nernst) | $E$ in V, $p$ in atm |
| 6 | $R_s = R_0(1 + Kc^n)$ | MOS sensor (Taguchi) | $R_s$ in Ω |
| 7 | $t_{90} = \dfrac{V \cdot \ln 9}{Q} = \dfrac{2.197\,V}{Q}$ | Response time of well-mixed cell | $t_{90}$ in s, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta R / R_0}{c}$ | Sensitivity of MOS / TCD | $S$ per ppm |
| 9 | $\text{Error (\%)} = \dfrac{c_{\text{meas}} - c_{\text{true}}}{c_{\text{true}}} \times 100$ | Accuracy quantification | Dimensionless (%) |
| 10 | $\text{Res. Quotient} = RQ = \dfrac{\text{CER}}{\text{OUR}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{air}} \cdot y_{CO_2,\text{out}} - Q_{N_2}\cdot y_{CO_2,\text{in}}}{Q_{\text{air}}(0.2095 - y_{O_2,\text{out}})}$ | Bioprocess metabolic state | Dimensionless |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

| **Domain** | **Sensor Used** | **Function** |
|---|---|---|
| **Submerged fermentation (Penicillin)** | Paramagnetic O₂ + NDIR CO₂ | OUR, CER, RQ → substrate feed control |
| **Anaerobic biogas digester** | TCD (H₂), NDIR (CH₄, CO₂), Electrochem (H₂S) | Methane yield optimization, safety |
| **Mammalian cell culture** | In-situ optical O₂ patch (luminescent) | Non-invasive pO₂ control in disposable bioreactors |
| **Brewing industry** | NDIR CO₂ in fermenter headspace | Real-time attenuation of beer/wine |
| **Effluent treatment (Activated sludge)** | Zirconia O₂ in aeration tank | Closed-loop DO control, energy saving **30–40%** |
| **Cleanroom / Sterile gas line** | PID for residual VOCs | Validating sterilization (EtO < 1 ppm) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Beer–Lambert Law for NDIR CO₂ Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation moves from the **differential form** of intensity loss to the **integrated exponential form** used in every NDIR data sheet.

**Step 1 — Start with a thin slab of gas of thickness $dx$.**

The fraction of photons absorbed in this slab is proportional to (a) the number density of molecules in it, and (b) the path length:

$$dI = -\alpha(\lambda)\, c\, I \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha c \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $x=0$ (where $I=I_0$) to $x=L$ (where $I$ is measured).**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I}\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha c \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$\ln I - \ln I_0 = -\alpha c L$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides to recover the Beer–Lambert equation.**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha c L}$$

**Step 5 — Define absorbance** $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$.

Using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$A = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{\alpha c L}{2.303} = \varepsilon c L$$

where $\varepsilon = \alpha / 2.303$ is the **molar extinction coefficient** (**L/mol·m** or **ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor 2.303 matters in KTU numericals:** If a question states "ε = 1500 L/mol·cm", you must convert to consistent units *before* substituting. Common student error: mixing ppm and mol/L without conversion. Use $c \text{ (mol/L)} = \dfrac{ppm \times \rho_{\text{gas}}}{M_{\text{gas}} \times 10^6}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Nernst Equation for the Zirconia Oxygen Sensor

**Step 1 — Electrochemical cell notation.**

The cell is represented as:
$$\text{Pt, } p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}} \,\vert\, \text{YSZ (ZrO}_2\text{-Y}_2\text{O}_3) \,\vert\, p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}, \text{ Pt}$$

**Step 2 — Half-reactions.**

At the cathode (reference side, high $p_{O_2}$):
$$\text{O}_2^{\text{ref}} + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{O}^{2-}$$

At the anode (sample side, low $p_{O_2}$):
$$2\text{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{O}_2^{\text{sample}} + 4e^-$$

**Step 3 — Net reaction** ($n = 4$ electrons):
$$\text{O}_2^{\text{ref}} \rightarrow \text{O}_2^{\text{sample}}$$

**Step 4 — Free-energy change.**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}\right)$$

Since $\Delta G^\circ = 0$ for an ideal gas at the standard state, and at equilibrium $\Delta G = -nFE$:

$$-nFE = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E$ (Nernst form).**

$$\boxed{\,E = \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right)\,}$$

**Step 6 — Insert constants** $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $n=4$:

$$E = \frac{8.314 \cdot T}{4 \cdot 96{,}485}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right) = 2.154 \times 10^{-5}\,T \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right)$$

Converting to mV and base-10 logarithm:

$$E\,(\text{mV}) = 49.6 \cdot T \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Standard Numerical:** This is the **exact problem type** seen in previous ESE papers.

**Problem Statement (Composite):**
A 15 L stirred-tank bioreactor is aerated with **1.2 L/min** of air. Off-gas analysis using a **paramagnetic O₂ sensor** reads **18.2% O₂** and an **NDIR CO₂ sensor** reads **1.85% CO₂** (dry basis). Ambient conditions: $T = 30^\circ$C, $P = 1$ atm.
Mole fraction of O₂ in inlet air = 0.2095, CO₂ in inlet = 0.0004 (ambient background).

Calculate:
1. **Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)** in mmol/L·h
2. **CO₂ Evolution Rate (CER)** in mmol/L·h
3. **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)**
4. **Comment on the metabolic state.**

---

**Step 1 — Convert the inlet air flow to molar flow rate.**

$$Q_{\text{in}} = 1.2\;\text{L/min} = 0.0200\;\text{L/s} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Using ideal gas at 303.15 K, 1 atm:

$$n_{\text{total}} = \frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{101325 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314 \times 303.15} = 0.02894\;\text{mol/s}$$

Convert to mmol/min:
$$n_{\text{total}} = 0.02894 \times 60 \times 1000 = 1736.4\;\text{mmol/min}$$

**Step 2 — Assume $\text{N}_2$ is conservative** (no consumption, no generation), so $Q_{N_2,\text{in}} = Q_{N_2,\text{out}}$.

$$Q_{N_2} = Q_{\text{in}}\,(1 - 0.2095 - 0.0004) = 0.7901 \times 1736.4 = 1371.9\;\text{mmol/min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outlet flow calculation:** $Q_{\text{out}} = Q_{N_2} + Q_{O_2,\text{out}} + Q_{CO_2,\text{out}}$. Since $N_2$ is conserved, $Q_{N_2}$ is the bridge between inlet and outlet mass balances.

**Step 3 — Compute outlet O₂ and CO₂ molar flows.**

Total outlet flow:
$$Q_{\text{out}} = \frac{Q_{N_2}}{(1 - 0.182 - 0.0185)} = \frac{1371.9}{0.7995} = 1715.9\;\text{mmol/min}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_{O_2,\text{out}} = 0.182 \times 1715.9 = 312.3\;\text{mmol/min}$$
$$Q_{CO_2,\text{out}} = 0.0185 \times 1715.9 = 31.74\;\text{mmol/min}$$

**Step 4 — Apply mass balance for O₂ (OUR).**

$$\text{OUR} = \frac{Q_{O_2,\text{in}} - Q_{O_2,\text{out}}}{V_{\text{liq}}}$$

$$Q_{O_2,\text{in}} = 0.2095 \times 1736.4 = 363.8\;\text{mmol/min}$$

$$\text{OUR} = \frac{363.8 - 312.3}{15\;\text{L}} = \frac{51.5\;\text{mmol/min}}{15\;\text{L}} = 3.43\;\text{mmol/(L·min)}$$

Convert to mmol/(L·h):
$$\text{OUR} = 3.43 \times 60 = 206.0\;\text{mmol/(L·h)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply mass balance for CO₂ (CER).**

Inlet CO₂ is from ambient (0.0004 × 1736.4 = 0.69 mmol/min).

$$\text{CER} = \frac{Q_{CO_2,\text{out}} - Q_{CO_2,\text{in}}}{V} = \frac{31.74 - 0.69}{15} = \frac{31.05}{15} = 2.07\;\text{mmol/(L·min)}$$

$$\text{CER} = 2.07 \times 60 = 124.2\;\text{mmol/(L·h)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute RQ.**

$$RQ = \frac{\text{CER}}{\text{OUR}} = \frac{124.2}{206.0} = 0.603$$

**Step 7 — Metabolic Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RQ Values — Memorize the Reference Table:**

| **RQ** | **Metabolic State** | **Substrate / Process** |
|---|---|---|
| **1.0** | Pure glucose oxidation | C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O |
| **0.7** | Pure lipid oxidation | Typical for fatty acid catabolism |
| **0.6–0.7** | Mixed substrate (oil + glucose) | Industrial fermentations using plant oils |
| **> 1.0** | Overflow metabolism / **ethanol formation** | Crabtree-positive yeasts |
| **∞** | Pure fermentation (no O₂ uptake) | Strict anaerobes (Clostridium) |

Here **RQ = 0.60**, indicating **predominantly lipolytic / oil-substrate metabolism** with partial glucose co-oxidation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture — On-Line Fermenter Off-Gas Train

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the **complete signal chain** from the fermenter to the operator interface, illustrating how gas sensors integrate with a typical bioreactor control system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reactor<br/>Headspace<br/>Gas] --> B[Condenser<br/>0 to 4 C]
    B --> C[NaOH Scrubber<br/>CO2 removal<br/>optional]
    C --> D[Glass Wool<br/>Particulate Filter]
    D --> E[Rotameter<br/>Flow Set 1.0 L/min]
    E --> F[Switching Valve<br/>3-way]
    F --> G[Paramagnetic<br/>O2 Sensor]
    F --> H[NDIR<br/>CO2 Sensor]
    F --> I[TCD<br/>H2 Sensor]
    F --> J[Electrochemical<br/>H2S Sensor]
    G --> K[Signal<br/>Conditioner]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[ADC<br/>16 bit]
    L --> M[PLC / SCADA<br/>HMI Display]
    M --> N[OUR<br/>CER<br/>RQ<br/>Computation]
    N --> O[Control Loop<br/>Aeration<br/>Agitation<br/>Feed]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The gas leaving the reactor passes through a **condenser** (removes water vapour that would interfere with IR and TCD), an **optional CO₂ scrubber** (for O₂-only streams in chemostat studies), and a **particulate filter**. The **switching valve** lets a single sample line visit multiple sensors sequentially, saving cost. All four transducer outputs are normalized to 4–20 mA, digitized, and processed by a PLC.

---

## 4.2 Internal Construction — Paramagnetic Sensor (Dumb-Bell Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Paramagnetic<br/>Sample Gas In] --> Q[Non-uniform<br/>Magnetic Field<br/>Pole Pieces]
    Q --> R[Glass Spheres<br/>Filled with N2]
    R --> S[Torsion<br/>Wire]
    S --> T[Mirror<br/>and<br/>Photocell]
    T --> U[Feedback<br/>Coil]
    U --> V[Output<br/>Current<br/>mA]
    subgraph Reference [Reference Path]
        X[Diamagnetic<br/>Reference Gas] --> Y[Compensation<br/>Chamber]
    end
    Y -.-> S
    style P fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style V fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Operating logic:** O₂ is pulled into the magnetic field → sphere displaces → mirror tilts → photocell detects displacement → feedback coil applies counter-torque → coil current becomes the **O₂-proportional** output.

---

## 4.3 Internal Construction — NDIR Sensor (Lübcke-type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IR Source<br/>Filament 700 K] --> B[Chopper<br/>Wheel 50 Hz]
    B --> C[Sample<br/>Cell<br/>Length L]
    C --> D[Optical<br/>Filter<br/>lambda 4.26 um]
    D --> E[Thermopile<br/>Detector]
    B -.-> F[Reference<br/>Cell<br/>N2 filled]
    F --> G[Optical<br/>Filter]
    G --> H[Reference<br/>Detector]
    E --> I[Differential<br/>Amplifier]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Output<br/>Voltage<br/>proportional to c]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Why dual-beam design?** Any drift in source intensity, detector aging, or window fouling affects both sample and reference paths equally, so the **ratio $(I_{\text{sample}} - I_{\text{ref}})$** cancels these errors — a classic **ratiometric architecture**.

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Sensor for a Bioprocess

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need a Gas Sensor] --> Q1{Target Gas?}
    Q1 -->|O2 only| Q2{Temperature?}
    Q2 -->|Ambient 25 to 40 C| P1[Paramagnetic]
    Q2 -->|High T greater than 600 C| P2[Zirconia]
    Q2 -->|Portable handheld| P3[Electrochemical]
    Q1 -->|CO2| R1{Need ppm resolution?}
    R1 -->|Yes| P4[NDIR]
    R1 -->|Low cost only| P5[MOS with filter]
    Q1 -->|H2| S1{Concentration range?}
    S1 -->|ppm to percent| P6[TCD]
    S1 -->|percent only| P7[Thermal Mass Flow]
    Q1 -->|Total hydrocarbons| P8[FID]
    Q1 -->|VOCs| P9[PID]
    Q1 -->|H2S / NH3 / acid gases| P10[Electrochemical<br/>3 electrode]

    style P4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style P9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Selection tip for KTU design questions:** Default to **paramagnetic O₂ + NDIR CO₂** for aerobic fermenters, and **NDIR CH₄ + TCD H₂** for anaerobic digesters. Electrochemical sensors are for safety alarms only, not for control loops.
