# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module 4, KTU Pattern)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT521 Q1a]**
> **"List the working principles of any THREE gas sensors used for off-gas analysis in a fermentation process. Compare their selectivity."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| **Sensor** | **Principle** | **Selectivity** |
|---|---|---|
| **Paramagnetic O₂ analyzer** | Exploits O₂'s unique paramagnetic susceptibility ($\chi_m \approx +3449 \times 10^{-6}$ cgs); only O₂ is strongly paramagnetic among common gases | **Excellent** for O₂ vs. N₂, CO₂, H₂O (all diamagnetic) |
| **NDIR (CO₂, CO, CH₄)** | IR absorption at gas-specific vibrational wavelength (Beer–Lambert law) | **High** — each gas absorbs at a unique λ; cross-sensitivity moderate |
| **TCD (H₂, He)** | Difference in thermal conductivity of gas relative to reference (N₂) | **Poor** — any two gases with similar $k$ cannot be distinguished |

> **Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded 1 per sensor for principle + 0.5 each for selectivity. Mentioning the *underlying equation* (Beer–Lambert / Curie / Nernst) earns a bonus 0.5.

---

### Question 2 (Module 4, KTU Pattern)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT521 Q2b]**
> **"The off-gas from a yeast fermentation shows 14% O₂ and 1.2% CO₂. Comment on the metabolic state of the cells."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Given outlet O₂ = 14% (below inlet 20.95%) and measurable CO₂, the cells are **aerobic**. If RQ is computed using the full off-gas equation and yields **RQ > 1.0**, the cells are exhibiting the **Crabtree effect** — fermentative (ethanol/glycerol) metabolism *despite* presence of O₂. This is characteristic of *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* at high glucose (>5 g/L) and elevated growth rates (μ > 0.25 h⁻¹).

> **Examiner's Note:** A complete 3-mark answer must include (a) computation hint, (b) metabolic inference, (c) industrial consequence (ethanol yield vs. biomass yield trade-off). Skipping any of the three costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **The #1 deduction reason in this module** is *mixing up inlet and outlet mole fractions* in the N₂-balance equation. Always anchor N₂ as the **inert tie** ($N_{2,\text{in}} = N_{2,\text{out}}$).
> - The **second most common deduction** is forgetting the $10^3$ conversion between mol and mmol. A student computing OUR as "3.43" instead of "3.43 mmol/L·min" loses **2 marks** outright.
> - For NDIR questions, students forget that Beer–Lambert is a **logarithmic** law. A plot of $I$ vs $c$ is **not linear**; a plot of $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ vs $c$ **is linear**. Mentioning this distinction scores 1 bonus mark.
> - For Nernst equation, do **not** use $n = 2$ for O₂ — the correct electron count is $n = 4$ per O₂ molecule (because each O atom takes 2 e⁻).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Paramagnetic + Mass Balance

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT521 Q8a]**
>
> **(a)** A **200 L working volume** stirred-tank bioreactor is sparged with **3.0 m³/h** of filtered air. Off-gas analysis (after condenser) shows **16.4% O₂** and **2.10% CO₂** (dry basis). Inlet air contains **20.95% O₂** and **0.04% CO₂**. Ambient: $T = 32^\circ$C, $P = 1$ atm. Calculate:
>   (i) Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR) in **mmol/L·h**.
>   (ii) CO₂ Evolution Rate (CER) in **mmol/L·h**.
>   (iii) Respiratory Quotient (RQ) and metabolic interpretation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **working principle of a paramagnetic oxygen analyzer** with a neat sketch. Discuss why it is preferred over an electrochemical sensor for **continuous OUR monitoring**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Mass Balance**

**Step 1 — Convert inlet air flow to molar flow at T = 305.15 K, P = 1 atm.**

$$Q_{\text{in}} = 3.0 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h} = \frac{3.0}{3600} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$n_{\text{total,in}} = \frac{P \cdot Q_{\text{in}}}{R \cdot T} = \frac{101325 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314 \times 305.15} = 0.03324\;\text{mol/s}$$

Convert to **mmol/h**:
$$n_{\text{total,in}} = 0.03324 \times 3600 \times 1000 = 119{,}664\;\text{mmol/h} \approx 1.197 \times 10^5\;\text{mmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute N₂ flow (conservative tracer).**

$$Q_{N_2} = n_{\text{total,in}} \times (1 - 0.2095 - 0.0004) = 119{,}664 \times 0.7901 = 94{,}543\;\text{mmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute outlet total flow (assuming dry-basis O₂ + CO₂ + N₂ sums to 1).**

Dry-basis fractions: $y_{O_2,\text{out}} = 0.164$, $y_{CO_2,\text{out}} = 0.021$, so $y_{N_2,\text{out}} = 1 - 0.164 - 0.021 = 0.815$.

$$n_{\text{total,out}} = \frac{Q_{N_2}}{0.815} = \frac{94{,}543}{0.815} = 116{,}004\;\text{mmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Individual outlet flows.**

$$Q_{O_2,\text{out}} = 0.164 \times 116{,}004 = 19{,}025\;\text{mmol/h}$$
$$Q_{CO_2,\text{out}} = 0.021 \times 116{,}004 = 2436\;\text{mmol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Inlet flows (for completeness).**

$$Q_{O_2,\text{in}} = 0.2095 \times 119{,}664 = 25{,}070\;\text{mmol/h}$$
$$Q_{CO_2,\text{in}} = 0.0004 \times 119{,}664 = 47.9\;\text{mmol/h}$$

**Step 6 — OUR and CER.**

$$\text{OUR} = \frac{Q_{O_2,\text{in}} - Q_{O_2,\text{out}}}{V} = \frac{25{,}070 - 19{,}025}{200} = \frac{6045}{200} = 30.23\;\text{mmol/(L·h)}$$

$$\text{CER} = \frac{Q_{CO_2,\text{out}} - Q_{CO_2,\text{in}}}{V} = \frac{2436 - 47.9}{200} = \frac{2388}{200} = 11.94\;\text{mmol/(L·h)}$$

**Step 7 — RQ and interpretation.**

$$RQ = \frac{\text{CER}}{\text{OUR}} = \frac{11.94}{30.23} = 0.395$$

> **Metabolic Inference:** RQ = 0.40 is *below* the lowest possible value for any substrate oxidation (pure lipid gives RQ ≈ 0.7). This is **physiologically impossible** for normal aerobic metabolism. It indicates either (a) an **instrument calibration error** (paramagnetic cell drift or NDIR zero-drift), or (b) **CO₂ retention** in the broth (e.g., due to sparging with CO₂-rich gas), or (c) **N₂O/CO production** skewing the N₂ balance. The student must flag this as an **anomaly** rather than fabricate a "lipid metabolism" answer.

---

### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Paramagnetic Sensor**

**Principle (Müllen Dumb-bell Cell):**
- O₂ is the *only* common gas with significant **paramagnetic susceptibility** ($\chi_m = +3449 \times 10^{-6}$ cgs).
- Two N₂-filled glass spheres suspended in a non-uniform magnetic field experience a torque when O₂ displaces them.
- A **feedback coil** applies a counter-torque; the **coil current** is the measurement output.

**Why preferred over electrochemical for OUR monitoring:**

| **Aspect** | **Paramagnetic** | **Electrochemical** |
|---|---|---|
| **Lifetime** | 10+ years, no consumables | 2–3 years (electrolyte dry-out) |
| **O₂ consumption** | **Zero** — non-depleting | Consumes O₂ → drift in low-pO₂ |
| **Response time** | 2–5 s | 15–30 s |
| **Accuracy at low pO₂** | Excellent (down to 0.1%) | Poor below 5% |
| **Cross-sensitivity** | None for H₂, CO₂, CH₄ | Sensitive to NO₂, Cl₂ |
| **Maintenance** | Annual calibration only | Quarterly electrolyte refill |

**Sketch (text representation since this is text-only):**
> The sensor contains a horizontal **dumb-bell** of two N₂-filled glass bulbs on a thin platinum suspension wire in a non-uniform magnetic field between **pole pieces**. A **mirror** on the wire reflects a beam onto a **photocell**; the photocurrent drives a **feedback coil** wound around the dumb-bell, restoring it to null position. The coil current is digitized as the O₂ reading.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — NDIR + Zirconia / Electrochemical

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PECHT521 Q8b]**
>
> **(a)** A **NDIR analyzer** for CO₂ uses an **optical path length of 0.20 m** and a **molar extinction coefficient ε = 1600 L/(mol·cm)** at λ = 4.26 µm. The measured transmittance is **T = I/I₀ = 0.78**. Calculate the **CO₂ concentration in ppm** at STP. Take the molar volume at STP = **22.414 L/mol**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a labelled diagram, describe the **working of a zirconia-based oxygen sensor** in the flue gas of a bioprocess incinerator. Derive the **Nernst equation** and state how the cell voltage indicates the **air-fuel ratio (λ)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — NDIR CO₂ Beer–Lambert**

**Step 1 — Convert ε to SI-consistent units.**

$$\varepsilon = 1600 \;\frac{\text{L}}{\text{mol·cm}} = 1600 \times \frac{1000\;\text{cm}^3/\text{L}}{100\;\text{cm/m}} \cdot \frac{1}{1000}\;\text{cm}^3/\text{m}^3 = 1.6 \times 10^4 \;\frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{mol·m}}$$

Wait — let us re-derive carefully. $\varepsilon$ has units (L/mol·cm). For Beer–Lambert in the form $A = \varepsilon c L$, when $L$ is in cm and $c$ in mol/L, $A$ is dimensionless.

Using the textbook form: $A = -\log_{10} T = 0.108$ (since $T = 0.78$).

**Step 2 — Compute absorbance.**

$$A = -\log_{10}(0.78) = 0.108$$

**Step 3 — Solve for concentration.**

$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot L}$$

If $L$ is in cm, $L = 20$ cm. Then $c$ comes out in **mol/L**:

$$c = \frac{0.108}{1600 \times 20} = \frac{0.108}{32{,}000} = 3.375 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{mol/L}$$

**Step 4 — Convert mol/L to ppm using molar volume.**

$$\text{ppm (v/v)} = c\,(\text{mol/L}) \times V_m\,(\text{L/mol}) \times 10^6$$

$$\text{ppm} = 3.375 \times 10^{-6} \times 22{,}414 \times 10^6 = 75{,}647\;\text{ppm} \approx 7.56\%\;\text{v/v}$$

**Final Answer:** CO₂ concentration ≈ **75,650 ppm** (or **7.56% v/v**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** A common error is to write $c$ in mol/m³ while using $L$ in cm. Always write the units *alongside* the equation: $A = \varepsilon c L$ where $\varepsilon$ is in (mol/L)⁻¹·cm⁻¹ ⟹ $c$ in mol/L and $L$ in cm. *Never* mix metres and centimetres in the same equation.

---

### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Zirconia Sensor**

**Cell Setup:**
- A **yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ)** ceramic disc is sandwiched between two **porous platinum electrodes**.
- Reference side: ambient air, $p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}} = 0.2095$ atm.
- Sample side: flue gas at **800 °C**.

**Reactions:**
$$\text{Cathode (air):} \quad \text{O}_2 + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{O}^{2-}$$
$$\text{Anode (flue):} \quad 2\text{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4e^-$$

**Nernst Derivation (abbreviated — see full derivation in SECTION 3.2):**

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}}}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right) = 49.6 \cdot T \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2095}{p_{O_2}^{\text{sample}}}\right)\;\text{mV}$$

**Air-Fuel Ratio Indication:**
- **Lean burn** (excess air, λ > 1): $p_{O_2,\text{flue}} \approx 0.02 - 0.05$ atm → $E \approx 700$–900 mV.
- **Stoichiometric** (λ = 1): $E \approx 450$ mV.
- **Rich burn** (excess fuel, λ < 1): $p_{O_2,\text{flue}} \approx 10^{-15}$ atm → $E \approx 0$–50 mV.

**Use in bioprocess incinerator:**
- A zirconia probe in the stack ensures **complete combustion** of biowaste, maintaining λ ≈ 1.1–1.2 for minimal CO/HC emissions and acceptable NOₓ.
- The probe signal drives the **secondary-air damper** in a closed loop.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

### A. Sensor-Family Cheat Cards
- [ ] **Paramagnetic → O₂ only** (Curie's law, $\chi_m \propto 1/T$)
- [ ] **NDIR → CO₂, CO, CH₄** (Beer–Lambert, $\lambda = 4.26\;\mu$m for CO₂)
- [ ] **TCD → H₂, He** (thermal conductivity ranking; $k_{H_2} = 6.7 \times k_{N_2}$)
- [ ] **Electrochemical → O₂, H₂S, CO, NO₂** ($I_{\text{lim}} = nFADc/\delta$)
- [ ] **Zirconia → O₂ at high T** (Nernst with $n = 4$)
- [ ] **MOS → VOCs, H₂, CH₄** (Taguchi: $R_s = R_0(1 + Kc^n)$)
- [ ] **FID → Total HC**; **PID → VOCs** (UV ionization)

### B. Equations to Reproduce Cold
- [ ] Beer–Lambert: $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha c L}$
- [ ] Nernst for Zirconia: $E = (RT/4F)\ln(p_{\text{ref}}/p_{\text{sample}})$
- [ ] Limiting current: $I = nFADc/\delta$
- [ ] OUR = $(Q_{O_2,\text{in}} - Q_{O_2,\text{out}})/V$
- [ ] CER = $(Q_{CO_2,\text{out}} - Q_{CO_2,\text{in}})/V$
- [ ] RQ = CER / OUR

### C. Fingerprint Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] $\lambda_{\text{CO}_2} = 4.26\;\mu$m; $\lambda_{\text{CO}} = 4.67\;\mu$m
- [ ] $k_{H_2} = 0.182\;\text{W/m·K}$ (≈ 7× $k_{N_2}$)
- [ ] LEL of CH₄ in air = **5% v/v**
- [ ] RQ(glucose) = 1.0; RQ(lipid) ≈ 0.7
- [ ] YSZ operating T ≥ 600 °C; λ = 1 gives E ≈ 450 mV
- [ ] Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

### D. Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] **N₂ is conservative** — always use it as the link between inlet and outlet.
- [ ] **n = 4 for O₂** in Nernst equation (not 2).
- [ ] **ε has units** — never drop them.
- [ ] **N₂ as reference** means $p_{O_2}^{\text{ref}} = 0.2095$, not 1.0.
- [ ] **TCD cannot distinguish** H₂ and He.
- [ ] **Paramagnetic reading drifts** 3.4% per 10 °C — hence thermostatted cells.
- [ ] **Beer–Lambert is logarithmic**: plot $A$ vs $c$, not $I$ vs $c$.

### E. Real-World Application Anchors (for viva)
- [ ] Penicillin fermentation → Paramagnetic + NDIR → OUR/CER/RQ
- [ ] Anaerobic biogas → TCD (H₂) + NDIR (CH₄, CO₂)
- [ ] Activated sludge aeration → Zirconia → 30–40% energy saving
- [ ] Breweries → NDIR CO₂ in headspace → degree of attenuation
- [ ] Cleanroom / sterilization → PID for residual EtO < 1 ppm

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: On-line Sensors and Offline Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT521 - BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** On-line Sensors and Offline Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Bioprocess Engineering and Fermentation Technology, **sensors** are analytical devices used to monitor critical process parameters and biochemical variables within a bioreactor system. The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies them into two fundamental categories based on their **physical location**, **sampling mode**, and **data acquisition time**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On-line Sensors (In-situ / In-line Sensors):** Analytical devices that are *physically integrated* within the bioreactor vessel (or in a side-stream loop directly connected to it) and provide **real-time, continuous, automated measurements** of process variables without requiring manual withdrawal of the sample from the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Off-line Sensors (At-line / Off-line Sensors):** Analytical instruments that require **manual sampling** of the broth/fermentation liquid, followed by sample preparation, transport, and discrete (batch) analysis in a **separate laboratory setup** away from the bioreactor.

> [!NOTE]
> A third hybrid category, the **At-line sensor**, is sometimes mentioned. It is located physically near (adjacent to) the bioreactor, requires manual sampling, but the measurement is automated. However, the primary KTU 2024 classification strictly divides sensors into **On-line** and **Off-line**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a modern **smartwatch with a heart-rate monitor** versus a visit to the **hospital's ECG lab**:

| Real-World Analogy | Sensor Type |
|---|---|
| **Smartwatch heart-rate monitor** – Worn 24/7, continuously measures pulse from your wrist without any needle or blood test, sends data instantly to your phone. | **On-line Sensor** (e.g., in-situ pH probe inside a bioreactor) |
| **Hospital blood-test report** – You must travel to a lab, give a blood sample, technicians centrifuge it, run it through machines, and you get results 6-24 hours later. | **Off-line Sensor** (e.g., HPLC analysis of glucose concentration) |

**The Engineering Takeaway:** Just as a smartwatch cannot measure your cholesterol level, *not every variable can be measured on-line*. Some biochemical quantities (like specific protein product concentration) still require complex sample preparation, making them inherently off-line.

### 1.3 Physical Standards & Key Metrics (Industry Standards)

- **Steam-sterilizable in-situ probes** must withstand **121 °C at 1.03 bar (15 psi)** for at least 20-30 minutes (standard autoclave conditions).
- **Response time (τ₉₀)** for industrial on-line bioprocess sensors: **< 30 seconds** for optimal feedback control.
- **Sterilization compatibility:** On-line probes must be **SIP (Steam-In-Place)** and **CIP (Clean-In-Place)** compatible as per **ASME BPE-2019** and **ISPE Baseline® Guide** standards.
- **Bio-compatibility:** Materials in contact with broth must be **USP Class VI** certified (non-toxic, non-hemolytic).
- **Hygienic process connections:** Standard fittings include **Ingold port (25 mm)**, **PG13.5 thread**, and **Tuchenhagen Varivent** connections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The single most important distinction examiners test is the **location of the sensor** relative to the bioreactor wall and whether **real-time** data is generated. Always mention whether the probe is *inserted directly into the vessel* (on-line) or if the sample is *withdrawn and taken elsewhere* (off-line).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of On-line Sensors

On-line sensors operate on **three foundational principles**:

1. **Direct Immersion Principle:** The sensor's active element is physically submerged in the fermentation broth. Examples: pH glass electrode, polarographic Dissolved Oxygen (DO) probe, Pt-100 RTD.
2. **Side-Stream Loop Principle:** A small fraction of broth is continuously withdrawn through a recirculation loop containing the sensor, then returned to the vessel. Examples: In-line NIR spectroscopy flow cell, on-line HPLC with automated sampling valve.
3. **Non-Invasive / Ex-situ Optical Principle:** Light is passed *through a transparent window* on the bioreactor wall (no broth contact). Examples: UV/Vis turbidity probes, fluorescence-based biomass sensors, Raman probes.

**Why this matters:** A side-stream loop allows sensors to operate in **cleaner, cell-free conditions** (using a tangential flow microfilter), drastically extending sensor life.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Off-line Sensors

Off-line measurements follow a **discrete workflow**:

1. **Manual Sampling:** Aseptically withdraw 5-50 mL of broth using a syringe through a sampling port.
2. **Sample Pre-treatment:** Centrifugation (10,000 × g, 10 min, **4 °C**), filtration (0.22 µm), dilution, or chemical quenching.
3. **Storage (if needed):** Samples stored at **-20 °C** or **-80 °C** to halt metabolic activity.
4. **Laboratory Analysis:** Measurement using bench-top instruments.
5. **Data Recording:** Results logged manually or via LIMS (Laboratory Information Management System).

**The 'Why' behind off-line analysis:** It offers **higher specificity, accuracy, and broader analyte range** (proteins, secondary metabolites, complex carbohydrates) — capabilities that no in-situ probe can currently match.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Phenomenon | Equation | Application | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Beer-Lambert Law (Optical Sensors)** | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | Optical density / turbidity probes; on-line NIR | $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless), $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), $c$ = concentration (mol·L⁻¹), $l$ = path length (cm) |
| **Nernst Equation (pH Probe)** | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \cdot \ln \left( a_{H^+} \right)$ | Glass-membrane pH electrode | $E$ = measured potential (V), $R$ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $T$ = temperature (K), $F$ = 96485 C·mol⁻¹, $n = 1$ |
| **Simplified Nernst (pH)** | $E = E^0 - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH} \quad (\text{at } 25\,^\circ\text{C})$ | Direct pH calculation | $E^0$ = standard potential, slope = **-59.16 mV/pH** at 25 °C |
| **Henry's Law (DO Probe)** | $pO_2 = k_H \cdot x_{O_2}$ | Dissolved oxygen saturation | $pO_2$ = partial pressure (atm), $k_H$ = Henry's constant, $x_{O_2}$ = mole fraction |
| **Polarographic DO Current** | $i_d = nFA \cdot \dfrac{D \cdot C_s}{\delta}$ | Galvanic/polarographic probe | $i_d$ = diffusion current (A), $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s), $C_s$ = surface concentration, $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness (m) |
| **Calibration Curve (Linear)** | $y = m \cdot x + b$ | Sensor calibration | $m$ = sensitivity (slope), $b$ = intercept (offset) |
| **Optical Density (Biomass)** | $\text{OD}_{600} = -\log_{10} \left( \dfrac{I}{I_0} \right)$ | Spectrophotometric biomass | $I$ = transmitted intensity, $I_0$ = incident intensity |
| **Dry Cell Weight (Offline)** | $\text{DCW} = \dfrac{(W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}})}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | Off-line biomass | g·L⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Notice that on-line sensors rely on **physical/electrochemical laws** (Nernst, Henry, Beer-Lambert) that can be miniaturized and made robust. Off-line sensors use **separative techniques** (chromatography, mass spectrometry) that are physically large and destructive to the sample.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **GMP Pharmaceutical Production (e.g., Insulin, mAbs):** On-line DO, pH, and temperature probes feed into **PAT (Process Analytical Technology)** frameworks mandated by the **US FDA (2004 Guidance for Industry)**. Real-time data ensures batch consistency.
- **Beer Brewing (SABMiller, Heineken):** On-line density and alcohol sensors control fermentation in real time.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** On-line OUR (Oxygen Uptake Rate) sensors using DO probes monitor BOD removal efficiency.
- **Vaccine Manufacturing:** Off-line ELISA, HPLC, and qPCR quantify antigen and impurity levels due to their unmatched specificity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Slope for pH Sensor (From First Principles)

The pH sensor is a concentration cell with a glass membrane that selectively exchanges H⁺ ions. We derive the potential difference:

**Step 1: Chemical potential of H⁺ on each side of the glass membrane**

$$\mu_{H^+, \text{inside}} = \mu^0_{H^+} + RT \cdot \ln(a_{H^+, \text{inside}})$$

**Step 2: Chemical potential on the broth side**

$$\mu_{H^+, \text{outside}} = \mu^0_{H^+} + RT \cdot \ln(a_{H^+, \text{outside}})$$

**Step 3: Gibbs free energy change for transferring 1 mole of H⁺ across the membrane**

$$\Delta G = \mu_{H^+, \text{outside}} - \mu_{H^+, \text{inside}} = RT \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{a_{H^+, \text{outside}}}{a_{H^+, \text{inside}}} \right)$$

**Step 4: Relate free energy to electrical work done ($\Delta G = -nFE$)**

$$-nFE = RT \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{a_{H^+, \text{outside}}}{a_{H^+, \text{inside}}} \right)$$

**Step 5: Solve for potential E (the measured EMF)**

$$E = -\dfrac{RT}{nF} \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{a_{H^+, \text{outside}}}{a_{H^+, \text{inside}}} \right)$$

**Step 6: Take negative log of activity ratio using pH definition (pH = -log₁₀(a_{H⁺}))**

By definition: $a_{H^+, \text{outside}} = 10^{-\text{pH}_{\text{outside}}}$ and similarly for inside (reference buffer at pH = 7).

**Step 7: Simplify using log identities (ln x = 2.303 × log₁₀ x)**

$$E = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \cdot (\text{pH}_{\text{outside}} - \text{pH}_{\text{inside}})$$

**Step 8: Substitute constants at T = 298.15 K, n = 1, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96485 C/mol**

$$E = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{1 \times 96485} \cdot \Delta\text{pH} = 0.05916 \cdot \Delta\text{pH}$$

**Final Result:**

$$E \,(\text{in V}) = 0.05916 \cdot (\text{pH}_{\text{reference}} - \text{pH}_{\text{sample}})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** This **59.16 mV/pH** slope is *temperature dependent*. At 37 °C (mammalian cell culture), it increases to **61.5 mV/pH**. This is why all industrial pH controllers include **Automatic Temperature Compensation (ATC)**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Beer-Lambert Law for Optical Density (On-line Biomass Sensor)

The optical density sensor measures scattered/transmitted light. Deriving the relationship between concentration and absorbance:

**Step 1: Define the intensity of light entering a thin slab dx of the broth**

$$dI = -I \cdot k \cdot c \cdot dx$$

where $k$ is a proportionality constant (combines molar absorptivity and geometry), $c$ is the cell concentration, $I$ is light intensity at position $x$.

**Step 2: Separate variables to prepare for integration**

$$\dfrac{dI}{I} = -k \cdot c \cdot dx$$

**Step 3: Integrate from x = 0 to x = l (total path length)**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI}{I} = -k \cdot c \cdot \int_{0}^{l} dx$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals**

$$\ln \left( \dfrac{I}{I_0} \right) = -k \cdot c \cdot l$$

**Step 5: Convert natural log to log base 10 using ln x = 2.303 · log₁₀ x**

$$\log_{10} \left( \dfrac{I}{I_0} \right) = -\dfrac{k \cdot c \cdot l}{2.303}$$

**Step 6: Define Absorbance A = -log₁₀(I/I₀) and let ε = k/2.303**

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

**Final Result (Beer-Lambert Law):**

$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation in Bioprocesses:** This law is **linear only for OD₆₀₀ ≤ 0.4**. Beyond this, multiple scattering causes deviations. Industrial probes use **back-scatter (180°) geometry** to extend the linear range up to OD ~ 200 (g·L⁻¹).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (On-line vs Off-line)

| Parameter | On-line Sensor | Off-line Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling** | Continuous, in-situ or in side-stream loop | Manual, discrete, destructive |
| **Response Time** | Seconds to minutes | Hours (5 min - 24 h typical) |
| **Sterilization** | SIP/CIP compatible, autoclavable | Samples are aseptically removed; instrument is outside sterile boundary |
| **Contamination Risk** | Low (no sampling port breach) | Higher (each sample = potential entry) |
| **Automation** | Fully integrated with DCS/PLC | Requires human operator |
| **Specificity** | Moderate (1-2 analytes per probe) | Very high (multi-analyte, e.g., LC-MS) |
| **Cost per Measurement** | Low (no consumables after installation) | High (reagents, columns, technician time) |
| **PAT Compatibility (FDA)** | Excellent | Limited (delayed data defeats real-time release) |
| **Examples** | pH, DO, temperature, OD, capacitance, NIR | HPLC, ELISA, DCW, GC-MS, qPCR, plate counts |
| **Capital Cost** | High initial (probe + transmitter) | Lower initial, high per-sample cost |
| **Calibration Frequency** | Weekly to monthly | Before every batch / daily |
| **Data Quality** | Continuous trend, no missing data | Sparse data points, interpolation needed |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A pH probe in a fed-batch bioreactor at 30 °C reads **+0.213 V** relative to its internal reference buffer (pH = 7.00). Calculate the broth pH.

**Solution:**

$$E = 0.05916 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{T - 25}{273 + 25}\right) \cdot (\text{pH}_{\text{ref}} - \text{pH}_{\text{sample}})$$

Wait — the Nernst slope is directly proportional to T, so:

$$\text{Slope at } 30\,^\circ\text{C} = 0.05916 \times \dfrac{303.15}{298.15} = 0.06015 \text{ V/pH}$$

Rearranging the Nernst equation:

$$\text{pH}_{\text{sample}} = \text{pH}_{\text{ref}} - \dfrac{E}{\text{Slope}}$$

$$\text{pH}_{\text{sample}} = 7.00 - \dfrac{0.213}{0.06015}$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH}_{\text{sample}} = 7.00 - 3.54 = 3.46}$$

**Interpretation:** The fermentation broth is highly acidic — typical of an organic acid (e.g., lactic or citric acid) production phase. Control action: increase ammonia/NaOH feed rate.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"Differentiate between on-line and off-line sensors in a bioreactor. Give two examples of each."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | On-line Sensors | Off-line Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Inside or directly attached to bioreactor | Separate laboratory setup |
| Sampling | Automatic, continuous | Manual, discrete |
| Data Delay | Real-time | Hours to days |
| Examples | Steam-sterilizable pH probe, polarographic DO probe | HPLC, Dry Cell Weight (DCW) |

On-line sensors provide **continuous, real-time process data** enabling feedback control, while off-line sensors offer **higher specificity and accuracy** for complex analytes like products, byproducts, and detailed metabolite profiling, but introduce significant time delay.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**"Explain the principle of operation of a polarographic dissolved oxygen (DO) probe. Why is it classified as an on-line sensor?"** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A polarographic (Clark-type) DO probe consists of a **cathode** (Pt) and an **anode** (Ag/AgCl) separated from the broth by a **gas-permeable Teflon membrane**. A polarizing voltage of **0.6 - 0.8 V** is applied.

Oxygen diffuses through the membrane and is reduced at the cathode:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$

The resulting **diffusion current ($i_d$)** is directly proportional to dissolved oxygen concentration. Since the probe is **directly immersed inside the sterile bioreactor** and provides **continuous, real-time readings** of DO, it is classified as an **on-line sensor**.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various types of **on-line sensors** used for monitoring critical parameters in a stirred-tank bioreactor. Explain the principle, working, and industrial significance of **pH and dissolved oxygen probes**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the operational workflow of **off-line analytical methods** for bioprocess monitoring. Compare their advantages and limitations with on-line sensors, with reference to **HPLC, dry cell weight (DCW), and ELISA techniques**. *(7 Marks)*

---

**(a) Model Answer:**

**On-line sensors** can be classified into three categories based on placement:

**(i) In-situ Immersion Probes:**
The probe is inserted directly into the fermentation broth through an Ingold port (25 mm standard) on the bioreactor top-plate. They are steam-sterilizable (121 °C, 20 min) and operate in real time.

- **pH Probe (Glass Membrane Electrode):** Based on the Nernst equation:
$$E = E^0 - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH} \quad (\text{at } 25\,^\circ\text{C})$$
A special **steam-sterilizable double-junction reference electrode** prevents Ag⁺ poisoning of the broth. It is **ATC (Automatic Temperature Compensated)**.

- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Probe (Polarographic Clark-type):** Cathode Pt, anode Ag/AgCl. Oxygen diffuses through the gas-permeable membrane (PTFE, ~25 µm thick) and is reduced:
$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$$
The current is proportional to DO; calibration is performed in air-saturated broth (100%) and N₂-sparged broth (0%).

- **Temperature Probe:** Pt-100 RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector), 100 Ω at 0 °C, with 0.385 Ω/°C sensitivity.

**(ii) Side-stream Loop Probes:**
Broth is continuously withdrawn through a recirculation loop containing a sensor. A **tangential flow microfilter (0.2 µm)** removes cells, enabling on-line HPLC of glucose, lactate, or ethanol.

**(iii) Non-invasive Optical Probes:**
A sapphire window on the bioreactor wall allows light-based measurement without broth contact. Examples: NIR (Near-Infrared), fluorescence (GFP-based biomass), Raman spectroscopy.

**Industrial Significance:** Real-time data enables **closed-loop feedback control** of pH, DO, temperature, and feeding rate — critical for PAT compliance and product quality consistency.

---

**(b) Model Answer:**

**Operational Workflow of Off-line Analysis:**

1. **Sampling:** Aseptically withdraw 5 - 20 mL broth through a steam-sterilized sampling valve.
2. **Pre-treatment:** Centrifugation (10,000 × g, 4 °C, 10 min) to separate cells; supernatant filtered (0.22 µm) for substrate/product assays.
3. **Storage:** Snap-frozen in liquid N₂ and stored at -80 °C if not analyzed immediately.
4. **Quantification:** Bench-top instruments provide readings.
5. **Data Logging:** Manual entry into LIMS or spreadsheet.

**Comparison of Off-line Techniques:**

| Technique | Analyte | Principle | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HPLC (High Performance Liquid Chromatography)** | Glucose, ethanol, organic acids, amino acids | Reverse-phase or ion-exchange separation with RI/UV detection | High specificity, multi-analyte | Slow (15-30 min), expensive columns |
| **DCW (Dry Cell Weight)** | Biomass | Centrifuge, wash, dry at 105 °C for 24 h, weigh | Gold standard for biomass | Long delay (24+ h), destructive |
| **ELISA (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay)** | Specific protein products (e.g., insulin, mAb) | Antibody-antigen binding with colorimetric detection | Extremely high specificity, low detection limit (pg/mL) | Multi-step, 4-6 h, expensive kits |

**Advantages of Off-line Sensors:** Higher accuracy, broader analyte range, ability to measure products not amenable to in-situ probes.
**Limitations:** Time delay (defeats real-time control), contamination risk at sampling port, labor-intensive, higher cost per data point.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch/flow diagram**, explain the **classification of bioprocess sensors** into on-line and off-line categories. Describe the principle of **capacitance-based on-line biomass sensors** and **NIR spectroscopy probes**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A pH probe at **37 °C** in a mammalian cell culture bioreactor reads **+0.187 V** relative to its internal reference (pH 7.00). The Nernst slope at 25 °C is **59.16 mV/pH**. Calculate the broth pH. Discuss why on-line pH control is **critically important** in monoclonal antibody (mAb) production. *(7 Marks)*

---

**(a) Model Answer:**

**Classification Diagram** (already shown in Section 4.1, can be redrawn by student).

**Capacitance-Based On-line Biomass Sensor:**

This probe measures the **dielectric permittivity** of the broth at radio frequencies (typically **0.1 - 10 MHz**). Viable cells with intact plasma membranes act as tiny capacitors because their membranes separate conducting intracellular fluid from the external medium.

The measured capacitance is **proportional to the volume fraction of viable cells** — providing a real-time, on-line measurement of **viable biomass concentration** (typically 10⁶ - 10⁹ cells/mL).

**Advantages:** Distinguishes viable from dead cells, non-destructive, CIP/SIP compatible.
**Disadvantage:** Affected by changes in medium conductivity.

**NIR (Near-Infrared) Spectroscopy Probe:**

NIR light (wavelength **780 - 2500 nm**) is passed *through a sapphire window* in the bioreactor wall. Different chemical bonds (C-H, O-H, N-H) absorb at characteristic wavelengths. Using **chemometric models (e.g., PLS — Partial Least Squares)**, the spectra are correlated to concentrations of:
- Glucose, lactate, ammonia
- Biomass
- Product (e.g., ethanol, mAb)

**Advantages:** Non-invasive, multi-analyte, real-time, no consumables.
**Disadvantage:** Requires extensive initial calibration with HPLC reference data.

---

**(b) Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Adjust Nernst slope for 37 °C (310.15 K)**

$$\text{Slope}_{37\,^\circ\text{C}} = 0.05916 \times \dfrac{310.15}{298.15} = 0.06153 \text{ V/pH}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange the Nernst equation**

$$\text{pH}_{\text{sample}} = \text{pH}_{\text{ref}} - \dfrac{E}{\text{Slope}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values (E = 0.187 V)**

$$\text{pH}_{\text{sample}} = 7.00 - \dfrac{0.187}{0.06153}$$

$$\text{pH}_{\text{sample}} = 7.00 - 3.039$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH}_{\text{sample}} = 3.96}$$

**Critical Importance of On-line pH Control in mAb Production:**

1. **CHO Cell Viability:** Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO) cells used for mAb production have a narrow optimal pH range of **6.8 - 7.2**. Deviation of just 0.2 units reduces viable cell density by **15-30%**.
2. **Glycosylation Profile:** mAb glycosylation (critical for Fc-receptor binding and half-life) is **pH-sensitive**. pH shifts alter the distribution of G0, G1, G2 glycoforms, affecting **bioactivity and ADCC (Antibody-Dependent Cell-mediated Cytotoxicity)**.
3. **Aggregation and Fragmentation:** Off-target pH promotes protein misfolding, leading to **aggregation** (immunogenic risks) or **clipping** (loss of activity).
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** **FDA 21 CFR Part 11** and **ICH Q7** require documented real-time pH monitoring of critical quality attributes (CQAs).
5. **Closed-Loop Control:** CO₂ sparging (lowers pH) and Na₂CO₃/NaHCO₃ addition (raises pH) are automatically activated based on on-line pH readings, ensuring **consistent product quality batch after batch**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where students lose marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "At-line" with "On-line":** At-line sensors require *manual sampling* but are *near* the bioreactor — they are NOT on-line. Many students write "on-line" for HPLC with an auto-sampler.
> 2. **Forgetting the Nernst Slope Temperature Correction:** Examiners award only partial marks (often 1/7) if you use 0.05916 V/pH at 37 °C without recalculating.
> 3. **Writing "pH probe measures H⁺ ions" — INCORRECT:** The glass membrane does *not* allow H⁺ to pass through; it develops a **potential difference** proportional to log[H⁺]. Use the word "potential" or "EMF."
> 4. **Mixing up DCW and OD:** DCW is a *gravimetric* off-line method; OD is a *spectrophotometric* measurement (which can be on-line or off-line).
> 5. **Not mentioning sterilization compatibility** of on-line probes — a key KTU examiner's checkpoint. Always add "SIP/CIP compatible" or "steam-sterilizable at 121 °C."
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "On-line and Off-line Sensors":**
> 
> - **On-line sensors** are in-situ, real-time, and provide continuous data; **off-line sensors** require manual sampling and external lab analysis.
> - **Three placement modes** of on-line sensors: **in-situ immersion**, **side-stream loop**, and **non-invasive optical**.
> - **pH probe** operates on the **Nernst equation**: $E = 0.05916 \cdot \Delta\text{pH}$ at 25 °C (slope changes with temperature).
> - **DO probe (Clark-type)** is **polarographic**: reduction of O₂ at Pt cathode gives diffusion current ∝ [O₂].
> - **Temperature probe**: Pt-100 RTD, 100 Ω at 0 °C.
> - **Biomass on-line sensors** include capacitance (viable cell volume), turbidity (OD), and NIR (multi-analyte).
> - **Off-line workhorse techniques:** **HPLC** (substrates/products), **DCW** (gravimetric biomass), **ELISA/qPCR** (specific proteins/DNA), **GC-MS** (metabolomics).
> - **Beer-Lambert Law** $A = \varepsilon c l$ governs all optical on-line sensors; linear only for OD ≤ 0.4.
> - **PAT (Process Analytical Technology)** is the FDA-driven framework that heavily relies on on-line sensors for **Real-Time Release Testing (RTRT)**.
> - **On-line sensor advantages:** fast response, automation, PAT compliance, closed-loop control.
> - **On-line sensor disadvantages:** limited analytes, high capital cost, frequent calibration.
> - **Off-line sensor advantages:** high specificity, broad analyte range, well-established.
> - **Off-line sensor disadvantages:** time delay, contamination risk, manual labor.
> - **Standard pH probe** must be **steam-sterilizable** at **121 °C, 15 psi, 20 min** for SIP bioreactors.
> - **Reference electrode** in pH probe uses **double-junction** design to prevent Ag⁺ contamination in protein-rich broths.
> - **NIR probes** require **chemometric calibration models (PLS/PCR)** trained against HPLC reference data.
> - **Capacitance probes** measure only **viable cells** (intact membrane capacitance) — a key advantage over OD measurement.
> - **At-line sensors** (e.g., YSI biochemistry analyzer near the bioreactor) — manual sample, but automated reading; *not* truly on-line.
> - **Soft sensors** combine on-line data with mathematical models (e.g., mass balance) to *estimate* off-line variables in real time.
> - **USP Class VI** certification is required for all wetted materials in pharmaceutical bioprocess sensors.
> - **Ingold port (25 mm)** is the industry standard port for probe insertion in stainless-steel bioreactors.
> - **Nernst slope** at **37 °C** = **61.53 mV/pH** — remember to recalculate for mammalian cell culture.
> - **DO probe calibration:** 100% in air-saturated broth, 0% in N₂-sparged broth.
> - **Raman and FTIR** are emerging non-invasive PAT tools for real-time multi-analyte monitoring.
